USR-001.risk: This check flags well-known standard SAP accounts (SAP*, DDIC, SAPCPIC, EARLYWATCH, TMSADM) whose lock flag USR02-UFLAG is 0 (unlocked). These accounts ship with documented default passwords and their names and credentials are public knowledge, so they are the first thing any attacker or automated scanner tries. In an S/4HANA RISE landscape the ABAP application layer, including standard-user hygiene, is the customer's responsibility under SAP's shared-responsibility model, so an unlocked SAP*/DDIC is customer exposure, not SAP's. SAP* is especially dangerous: if its user master record is deleted and profile parameter login/no_automatic_user_sapstar is 0, the kernel activates a hard-coded emergency SAP* with password PASS and implicit SAP_ALL, bypassing the user table entirely. DDIC owns the Data Dictionary and is used during upgrades/imports; SAPCPIC is a legacy CPIC/RFC user; TMSADM holds broad transport authorizations across the entire transport domain. Attack scenario: an intruder who reaches SAP GUI, the Fiori/Web GUI logon, or the message server tries the default credentials directly, or brute-forces them because these accounts are dialog-capable. On success they immediately grant themselves SAP_ALL, read HR/finance tables, post fraudulent documents, create backdoor users, run OS commands via SM49, or pivot across the transport domain into DEV/QA/PRD. A real incident looks like an off-hours SAP* or DDIC dialog logon from an unusual terminal followed by SU01 user creation and profile assignment. This is a textbook critical audit finding under SOX ITGC, and unauthorized access to financially-relevant and personal data also drives GDPR/data-breach exposure.
USR-001.mitigation:
1. Run report RSUSR003 (via SA38 or SUIM) in every client (000, 001, 066 and all productive clients) to list SAP*, DDIC, SAPCPIC, EARLYWATCH, TMSADM and see which still have default passwords or are unlocked.
2. For each client, in SU01 set a strong random password and apply an administrator lock to SAP* and DDIC (this sets USR02-UFLAG); only unlock DDIC temporarily for upgrades/imports, then re-lock it.
3. Secure the SAP* emergency logon: set profile parameter login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = 1 in RZ10 (DEFAULT/instance profile), activate the profile, and restart the instance in a maintenance window; verify the runtime value with RZ11.
4. Ensure a real SAP* user master record exists in every client so the hard-coded fallback cannot activate; never delete SAP*.
5. For TMSADM, follow SAP Note 1414256 to change its password consistently across the whole transport domain, then restrict its authorizations to the minimum transport scope.
6. Lock SAPCPIC and EARLYWATCH if unused; if EarlyWatch is active, restrict it to minimal monitoring authorizations.
7. Use SU10 for mass lock/password changes when many clients/accounts are affected.
8. Verify by re-running RSUSR003 (no default passwords, standard users locked) and confirming USR02-UFLAG > 0 for SAP*/DDIC.
9. Enable the Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG / SM19) with a filter to alert on any dialog logon by these accounts.
10. Caution: locking DDIC can break scheduled imports/upgrades and the SAP* parameter change needs an instance restart, so test in DEV/QA and schedule a maintenance window with Basis.

USR-002.risk: This check flags users assigned the critical profiles SAP_ALL, SAP_NEW or S_A.SYSTEM. SAP_ALL grants every authorization in the system; SAP_NEW is open by design and grants all authorizations newly delivered by upgrades so that existing processes do not break; S_A.SYSTEM is the broad system-administration profile. A dialog user holding any of these effectively has unrestricted access, defeating least-privilege and segregation of duties. Attack scenario: a compromised or malicious holder of SAP_ALL can post or alter financial documents (fraudulent FB01 postings and payment runs), read and exfiltrate HR/PII and bank data (tables such as PA0008, BSEG, LFBK), create and empower users to establish persistence, execute OS-level commands, and deactivate or clear the audit log to cover tracks, all from a single account. Because SAP_ALL includes S_DEVELOP, the holder can also debug-and-replace values at runtime to bypass any residual control. In RISE/S4 this is squarely a customer-owned control. SAP_NEW is insidious: after an upgrade it can silently re-grant powerful new authorizations to whoever still holds it. A real incident: a temporary go-live SAP_ALL grant to a consultant that was never removed, later abused (or the consultant phished) to create a hidden admin ID and run mass vendor bank-detail changes for payment fraud. This is among the most severe SOX ITGC and DSAG audit findings and a direct SoD violation.
USR-002.mitigation:
1. Enumerate holders with RSUSR002 (users by complex selection) or SUIM filtered on profiles SAP_ALL, SAP_NEW, S_A.SYSTEM; cross-check table UST04/AGR_USERS and run RSUSR008_009_NEW for critical authorization combinations.
2. For each production dialog user, remove the profile in SU01 (Profiles tab) or SU02 and replace it with a PFCG role scoped to the user's actual task set.
3. Never assign SAP_NEW permanently; after upgrades, process new authorizations properly via SU25/SU24 instead of leaving SAP_NEW assigned.
4. Restrict SAP_ALL to break-glass only by implementing SAP GRC Access Control Emergency Access Management (Firefighter/EAM) so elevated access is time-boxed, logged and reviewed.
5. For genuine administration, build least-privilege admin roles (separate Basis, user-admin and transport roles) instead of S_A.SYSTEM.
6. Document any residual emergency assignment with a validity/expiry date (SU01 validity period) and an approval ticket.
7. Verify by re-running RSUSR002/RSUSR008_009_NEW to confirm no dialog user retains the profiles and that firefighter IDs are the only path to SAP_ALL.
8. Configure a recurring SUIM/GRC report and a Security Audit Log filter to alert whenever SAP_ALL/SAP_NEW is assigned.
9. Caution: removing SAP_ALL from technical/interface users can break RFC and batch jobs, so trace the authorizations actually used with STAUTHTRACE/ST01 before trimming and test in QA.
10. Coordinate the change window with application owners and roll out per user to avoid mass lockout.

USR-003.risk: This check flags unlocked accounts (USR02-UFLAG = 0) whose last logon date (USR02-TRDAT) is older than the configured threshold (default 90 days). Dormant accounts are latent attack surface: they are typically leavers, movers, ex-consultants or old test IDs whose owners no longer watch them, yet they still carry their roles and authorizations. Attack scenario: an attacker who phished or purchased old credentials, or who guesses a weak never-rotated password, logs in with a dormant account nobody monitors; because the legitimate owner is gone, the intrusion generates no user complaint and can persist for months. Dormant privileged accounts are worse, as a dormant Basis or finance user yields instant high privilege. In an outsourced/RISE landscape with rotating consultants, orphaned external accounts are common and easy to miss. Business impact: unauthorized transactions, quiet data exfiltration, and a failed access-recertification and termination control (SOX ITGC), plus GDPR concerns where the dormant ID can still read personal data. A real incident looks like a logon on a long-idle ex-employee ID from a new IP outside business hours, followed by report execution and downloads of customer or payroll data. Even absent compromise, dormant accounts inflate the named-user license count and the audit-review burden and dilute the reliability of periodic reviews.
USR-003.mitigation:
1. Produce the authoritative list with RSUSR200 (users by logon date and password change) or RSUSR006, selecting last logon (TRDAT) older than the policy threshold and lock status = unlocked.
2. Reconcile against HR leavers and the joiner-mover-leaver feed to confirm which owners have left or changed roles.
3. Lock confirmed dormant accounts in SU01, or in bulk with SU10 (mass administrator lock), and set a validity end date so they cannot be silently reused.
4. After a defined grace period (for example 30-60 days locked with no business objection), delete or archive the users and reclaim their roles.
5. Automate detection by scheduling RSUSR200/RSUSR006 as a periodic background job in SM36, and/or use SAP GRC User Access Review for recurring recertification.
6. Establish a formal quarterly user-access review with business-owner sign-off, and integrate deprovisioning with the leaver process (ideally via SAP IPS or GRC).
7. Set login/password_max_idle_productive so unused productive passwords expire automatically, shrinking the dormant-but-usable window.
8. Verify by re-running RSUSR200 to confirm the previously dormant IDs are locked or removed, and confirm no successful logon occurred after lock via SM20 / RSAU_READ_LOG.
9. Caution: some dormant-looking IDs are legitimate technical or break-glass accounts, so confirm user type and purpose before locking to avoid breaking an interface.
10. Communicate with business owners before locking to avoid disrupting seasonal or infrequent but valid users.

USR-004.risk: This check flags accounts named like service/technical IDs (prefixes SVC_, RFC_, BATCH_, BG_, INT_, API_, TECH_) that still carry user type A (Dialog), meaning they can be used for interactive SAP GUI/Fiori logon with credentials embedded in interfaces and batch jobs. Technical accounts are dangerous when interactive because their passwords are widely known to integration teams, are seldom rotated, are often stored in cleartext in RFC destinations (SM59), config files and scripts, and frequently hold broad RFC, batch or cross-client authorizations. SAP user types exist precisely to prevent this: types C (Communication/CPIC) and B (System/background) cannot perform dialog logon, whereas A (Dialog) and S (Service) can. Attack scenario: an attacker extracts an RFC/interface password from a config file, a memory dump, a captured RFC call or a decompiled middleware job, then logs on interactively as that technical user via SAP GUI, abusing its standing authorizations to read tables, run reports and pivot; because it is a shared service ID, attribution and detection are poor. In RISE/BTP, integration users bridging BTP, SAP Integration Suite/PI-PO and third-party middleware are prime targets. Business impact: undetected privileged access, an SoD failure (a technical ID performing interactive work), and audit findings on shared-account accountability. A real incident: reuse of a leaked RFC_ password for an interactive logon that pulls a full customer-master extract.
USR-004.mitigation:
1. Inventory technical accounts and their type with RSUSR200/SUIM filtered on user type, review naming conventions, and cross-check SM59 for the users that RFC destinations authenticate as.
2. For each genuinely non-interactive account, change USR02-USTYP in SU01 (Logon Data > User Type) to C (Communication) for RFC/CPIC, B (System) for background/batch and internal jobs, or S (Service) only where a technical dialog is truly required.
3. Prefer type C or B for interface and batch IDs so dialog logon is impossible at the kernel level.
4. Rotate the passwords of all affected technical users after the type change, store them in a secrets vault rather than scripts, and update SM59 destinations and job definitions accordingly.
5. Restrict authorizations to the minimum the interface needs (for example specific S_RFC function groups) rather than broad profiles.
6. Where possible migrate RFC/interface authentication to trusted-system RFC or SNC/certificate-based authentication to remove shared passwords.
7. Verify by attempting a dialog logon with a converted account (rejected for type B/C) and confirming the interface/batch job still runs successfully in QA.
8. Enable Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG) alerts for dialog logons by technical user IDs.
9. Caution: changing a user type or password can break live interfaces and scheduled jobs, so coordinate with each interface owner, schedule during a low-volume window and test end-to-end in non-prod first.
10. Document the correct type per technical account in the interface register so future provisioning stays consistent.

USR-005.risk: This check flags users with an unusually large number of assigned roles (default threshold 30, counted from AGR_USERS), a symptom of role explosion and weak RBAC governance. The core security problem is authorization creep and segregation-of-duties (SoD) violation: a user who accumulates roles across transfers and projects almost inevitably ends up able to execute conflicting business functions, for example create a vendor and pay it, or maintain a customer and release its credit, enabling fraud. Over-entitled users also enlarge the blast radius if the account is phished, since the attacker inherits every role. Excessive assignments make access reviews impractical because reviewers rubber-stamp long lists, which hides toxic combinations. There are technical effects too: very large authorization buffers can approach the user-buffer limit (auth/auth_number_in_userbuffer) and cause spurious authorization failures, tempting admins to fix them with SAP_ALL, which makes the exposure far worse. In an S/4 RISE deployment the customer owns role design, so bloated assignments are a customer audit finding. Business/compliance impact: SoD and SOX ITGC findings, fraud exposure, and slower, less reliable recertification. A real incident: a long-tenured finance user with dozens of stacked roles quietly gains both vendor-maintenance and payment-run access and diverts payments, undetected because no single role obviously combined the duties.
USR-005.mitigation:
1. Identify over-entitled users with SUIM (roles by user assignment) or by querying AGR_USERS to count roles per user, and sort descending to target those above the policy threshold.
2. Run a proper SoD analysis with SAP GRC Access Control (Access Risk Analysis/ARA), or RSUSR008_009_NEW for critical combinations, to find and prioritise toxic role pairs rather than just high counts.
3. Rationalise roles in PFCG: consolidate overlapping single roles into composite roles and use derived roles for org-level variants to cut the number of direct assignments.
4. Remove roles the user no longer needs based on actual usage from STAUTHTRACE / ST03N rather than trimming blindly.
5. Redesign toward a least-privilege, job-function role model (role mining) so each job maps to a small, stable role set.
6. Implement periodic access recertification (GRC User Access Review) with business-owner approval and enforce assignment through a request workflow to stop future creep.
7. Check auth/auth_number_in_userbuffer via RZ11 to ensure large assignments have not overflowed the user buffer and caused false authorization errors.
8. Verify by re-running the AGR_USERS count and the GRC SoD report to confirm counts are within policy and no critical SoD conflicts remain, then confirm affected users can still perform legitimate tasks in QA.
9. Caution: removing roles can break daily work, so stage removals, communicate with users and managers and keep a rollback (re-assign) path; test in non-prod.
10. Do not resolve buffer or authorization errors by granting SAP_ALL or other wide profiles.

USR-006.risk: This check flags users whose values for high-power authorization objects are wildcard (*) or, for table access, the special &NC& (unclassified) group, i.e. effectively unrestricted. Each object is dangerous: S_DEVELOP with full activity grants the ABAP debugger with change/replace, letting a user alter variables at runtime (including authorization results and payment amounts) and write and execute arbitrary ABAP, a de-facto takeover in production. S_ADMI_FCD = * grants all Basis administrative functions; S_BTCH_ADM = * grants batch administration across users and clients (schedule jobs as anyone); S_RZL_ADM = * grants CCMS/system administration; S_USER_GRP with ACTVT=* grants full user administration (create users, assign any role/profile), a direct privilege-escalation primitive; S_TABU_DIS with DICBERCLS=&NC& bypasses table authorization groups and exposes unclassified tables via SE16/SM30. Attack scenario: a user with S_DEVELOP debug-replace sets an authorization return value to bypass a check or edits a running payment program to redirect funds; a user with S_USER_GRP=* self-assigns SAP_ALL; a user with S_TABU_DIS &NC& reads or edits business and config tables directly, circumventing application controls. In RISE, developer-style access in production is a classic finding. Business impact: RCE-equivalent capability, fraud, mass data exposure and complete loss of SoD and change-control integrity, all critical SOX and GDPR failures. A real incident: an over-broad support role carrying S_DEVELOP=* used to debug-replace a posting block and push an unauthorized journal entry.
USR-006.mitigation:
1. Find the holders with RSUSR002 (by authorization values) or RSUSR008_009_NEW and SUIM (by authorization object) for each object (S_DEVELOP, S_ADMI_FCD, S_BTCH_ADM, S_RZL_ADM, S_USER_GRP, S_TABU_DIS), filtering on * and &NC& values.
2. In PFCG, edit the underlying roles to replace * with the minimum specific values; for S_DEVELOP limit ACTVT to 03 (display) and specific object types and remove change/debug-replace (activity 02) in production.
3. For S_TABU_DIS, assign real authorization groups (DICBERCLS) maintained via SE54/table TDDAT, grant only the groups a role needs, eliminate &NC&, and prefer the finer S_TABU_NAM only where table-level access is justified.
4. For S_USER_GRP, restrict ACTVT and the user-group class so admins manage only their scoped groups, and separate user-admin from role/profile-admin duties (S_USER_AGR/S_USER_PRO).
5. For S_ADMI_FCD, S_RZL_ADM and S_BTCH_ADM, grant only the specific function codes/values each admin role needs, never *.
6. Enforce a policy that S_DEVELOP change/debug-replace is not assigned in production and route developer changes through the transport path DEV to QA to PRD.
7. Enable the Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG) to record debugging (S_DEVELOP) and direct table-maintenance events.
8. Verify by re-running RSUSR002/RSUSR008_009_NEW to confirm no * or &NC& remains on these objects, and run a positive test (SU53/STAUTHTRACE) that legitimate admin tasks still work in QA.
9. Caution: tightening these objects can break Basis operations and support workflows, so trace real usage first with ST01/STAUTHTRACE, change in DEV/QA and roll out via transport with owner sign-off.
10. Re-baseline the role after the change and remove the wildcard from any role template so it cannot reappear.

USR-007.risk: This check flags unlocked accounts with no recorded logon (USR02-TRDAT empty). These are pre-staged or orphaned provisioning artifacts, such as bulk-created user shells, aborted onboarding records, or old test IDs, that still carry an initial password and whatever roles were assigned at creation. They are lower severity than an abused live account but remain attractive footholds: initial passwords are often weak, predictable, or set to a shared default across a batch, and because no one has ever used the account, no legitimate owner will notice a takeover. Attack scenario: an attacker who learns the provisioning convention, such as a common initial password or a derivable username scheme, activates a never-used account, changes the initial password, and gains a valid, low-noise identity; if the shell was pre-assigned roles, or worse a broad profile, the attacker inherits them instantly. Never-logged-in privileged IDs are the worst case. Business/compliance impact: unauthorized access, inflated named-user license counts, and access-review findings for accounts that exist without a validated business need. A real incident: a batch of onboarding accounts created with a uniform initial password, one of which is activated weeks later from an external IP and used to browse HR data before anyone notices the shell was ever live.
USR-007.mitigation:
1. List them with RSUSR200 (select users who have never logged on) or SUIM, filtered to unlocked accounts with no last-logon date, and capture creation date (USR02-ERDAT) and assigned roles.
2. Reconcile with HR/onboarding and the provisioning system to determine which shells are still needed versus abandoned.
3. Lock unneeded accounts immediately in SU01 or in bulk via SU10, and set a validity end date so unused shells expire automatically.
4. Delete or archive shells that have no confirmed business owner after a short grace period.
5. Fix provisioning hygiene by enforcing unique strong initial passwords and a short initial-password validity via login/password_max_idle_initial so unused initial passwords expire quickly, and avoid shared/default initial passwords in bulk creation.
6. Do not pre-assign broad roles or profiles at creation; grant entitlements only on activation through a request workflow (GRC/IPS).
7. Verify by re-running RSUSR200 to confirm never-logged-in unlocked accounts are locked or removed and any remaining shells have an expiry and minimal roles.
8. Add a periodic scheduled RSUSR200 job (SM36) so newly stranded shells are caught each cycle.
9. Caution: some never-used IDs are legitimately pre-staged for an imminent go-live or seasonal hires, so confirm with owners before deleting.
10. Track provisioning-to-first-logon time as a metric so orphaned shells are cleaned up routinely.

USR-008.risk: This check flags unlocked dialog users (type A) whose password has not changed in more than the policy window (USR02-PWDCHGDATE older than the threshold, default 180 days). This usually means password expiry is not enforced (login/password_expiration_time = 0 or too high) or these users are exempt. Stale, long-lived passwords are more likely to have been exposed via phishing, reuse across systems, a prior breach dump, or shoulder-surfing, and the longer they persist the larger the window for offline cracking of a captured hash. Where weak downward-compatible hashing is still active (login/password_downwards_compatibility not disabled, legacy BCODE hashes present), captured hashes crack quickly. Attack scenario: an attacker obtains an old credential through credential-stuffing from a public breach, a phishing capture, or a cracked USR02 hash, and because the password was never rotated it still authenticates for interactive SAP GUI/Fiori logon, letting the attacker operate as that business user. Long-lived passwords also undermine the assumptions behind lockout and audit controls. Business/compliance impact: account takeover, fraud and data access under a real user's identity, and direct policy, SOX and CIS findings on password lifecycle. A real incident: a finance clerk's years-old password, reused on a breached website, is replayed against the Fiori launchpad and used to run a payment proposal. In RISE/BTP many dialog logons should be moving to SSO via SAP Cloud Identity (IAS), which changes but does not remove this control for accounts still using local passwords.
USR-008.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current policy by checking login/password_expiration_time, login/password_max_idle_productive, login/min_password_lng, the login/min_password_* complexity parameters, and login/password_downwards_compatibility via RZ11 / report RSPFPAR.
2. Set an enforced rotation window (for example login/password_expiration_time = 90) and adequate length/complexity in RZ10 (DEFAULT profile), activate it, and set login/password_compliance_to_current_policy = 1 so existing non-compliant passwords must change at next logon.
3. Disable weak legacy hashes by setting login/password_downwards_compatibility = 0 (after confirming no old kernels/systems depend on it) so only strong PWDSALTEDHASH is stored, and clean up history per SAP guidance.
4. Identify stale accounts with RSUSR200 (by password change date) and, for those past the threshold, force a change by setting a new productive/initial password via SU01 or SU10 with change-at-next-logon.
5. Reduce password dependence by adopting SSO: SAML/OIDC via SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) for Fiori, or SNC/Kerberos for SAP GUI, so interactive logon uses a central identity with MFA rather than static SAP passwords.
6. Configure lockout parameters login/fails_to_user_lock and login/failed_user_auto_unlock to blunt credential-stuffing.
7. Verify by re-running RSUSR200 and RSPFPAR to confirm no dialog user exceeds the age threshold and the parameters are active at runtime, then test a logon to confirm the change-at-next-logon prompt fires.
8. Enable Security Audit Log monitoring for repeated failed logons and logons after long inactivity.
9. Caution: forcing mass password changes can generate helpdesk load and break any dialog account misused as a technical account, so first re-type genuine technical IDs (see USR-004) to exclude them and communicate the change to users.
10. Roll out complexity/expiry changes in a maintenance window and confirm no critical interface relies on a never-expiring dialog password.

IAM-SOD-000.risk: This finding fires when the scanner receives no usable Segregation-of-Duties input at all — no pre-computed SoD matrix, no role-to-transaction mapping (AGR_1251), no user-role assignment extract (AGR_USERS) and no GRC ruleset. The weakness is not a single toxic access combination but a total blind spot: the organization cannot demonstrate that conflicting duties (e.g. create a vendor AND pay it, post a journal entry AND maintain the GL master) are prevented or detected. In an S/4HANA RISE model the customer, not SAP, owns application-layer access governance, so an empty feed usually means the extract job or GRC connector is broken and nobody is watching. The concrete abuse scenario: an insider who has quietly accumulated both sides of a conflict over several role changes commits and conceals fraud — a duplicate/fictitious vendor paid through F110, or a ghost employee run through payroll — with no automated control ever flagging the combination. Business impact is direct financial loss plus a control-environment failure. For SOX/J-SOX, ITGC and IT-general-controls testing, 'unable to test SoD' is itself reportable and can escalate to a significant deficiency or material weakness, undermining the CFO's internal-control certification. A realistic incident: at year-end the audit team requests SoD evidence, none can be produced, an emergency review is launched, and a long-running procurement fraud that the missing control would have surfaced months earlier finally comes to light.
IAM-SOD-000.mitigation:
1. Extract user-to-role assignments from table AGR_USERS via SE16N or SUIM report RSUSR070, exporting UNAME, AGR_NAME, FROM_DAT, TO_DAT to user_roles.csv.
2. Extract role authorization content from table AGR_1251 (SE16N) to role_tcodes.csv (AGR_NAME, OBJECT, FIELD, LOW/HIGH); for permission-level precision export the full AGR_1251 as role_auth_values.csv so the Access Risk Analysis module can supersede this coarse check.
3. If SAP GRC Access Control is licensed, run batch Access Risk Analysis (report GRAC_BATCH_RA) and export the violation list as sod_matrix.csv (USERNAME, TCODES).
4. Place the file(s) in the scanner input directory and re-run; confirm IAM-SOD-000 no longer triggers.
5. Validate scope: ensure the extract covers all productive clients and includes technical/service users, not only dialog users.
6. Verify completeness by reconciling CSV row counts against SU01 user totals and AGR_DEFINE role totals.
7. Rollout caution: pull the extract from production (or a same-day system copy) during a read-only window so results reflect live access.
8. Institutionalize the feed by scheduling the extract as a recurring background job in SM36 (monthly) so SoD data never goes stale.

IAM-SOD-FIN-001.risk: Users flagged here hold both the ability to create or change vendor master data (FK01/FK02/XK01/XK02/BP, object F_LFA1_BUK) and the ability to execute or configure outgoing payments (F110/F-53/F-58/FBZP, object F_BKPF_BUP). This is the classic 'create-a-vendor-and-pay-it' toxic combination and the single most exploited financial SoD conflict in SAP. The attacker is typically a trusted AP or finance clerk, not an external hacker. Step by step: the user creates a fictitious or duplicate vendor (or edits a legitimate vendor's bank account in table LFBK to their own account), enters or uploads an invoice, then runs the payment program F110 to disburse funds to the account they control, and finally reverts the bank details to conceal the trail. Because one person owns the whole cycle, no second pair of eyes ever sees the fraud. Business impact is direct cash theft that can run to millions before detection, plus financial-statement misstatement. For SOX this is a textbook material-weakness combination; auditors specifically test for it every cycle. A real incident looks like a series of round-sum payments to a newly created vendor whose bank account matches an employee's, surfacing only during a bank reconciliation or whistleblower tip — often months and many payment runs later.
IAM-SOD-FIN-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the conflict for each user in SUIM (RSUSR070 by the listed t-codes, or RSUSR002 by authorization) or via GRC Access Risk Analysis; eliminate display-only false positives by checking activity (ACTVT 01/02 vs 03).
2. Decide which side to strip per job function — a payment processor should not maintain vendor master, and vice versa.
3. In PFCG, remove the offending transactions from the role Menu and restrict the relevant object (F_LFA1_BUK or F_BKPF_BUP) on the Authorizations tab; regenerate the profile and run PFUD (user master comparison).
4. Where business genuinely requires both, register a mitigating control in GRC: mandatory dual control in F110 (separate payment-proposal and payment-run approvers via FBZP), plus monitoring of vendor bank-detail changes.
5. Enable and review change documents for vendor bank data (table LFBK / CDHDR-CDPOS) and configure sensitive-field logging so any bank-account change is logged and reviewed.
6. Verify remediation by re-running the SoD analysis and confirming zero remaining conflicts for these users.
7. Rollout caution: test the modified role in QA, coordinate with the AP process owner, and schedule the change outside the payment-run window to avoid breaking a live F110 cycle.

IAM-SOD-FIN-002.risk: These users can both create/release purchase orders (ME21N/ME22N/ME28/ME29N, object M_BEST_BSA) and post goods receipts (MIGO/MB01/MB0A, object M_MSEG_BWA), defeating the three-way match (PO / GR / invoice) that underpins procure-to-pay integrity. A buyer who can also confirm receipt can raise a PO to a colluding or fictitious vendor and then 'receive' goods that were never delivered, which triggers the invoice-verification and payment chain automatically. The abuse path: create the PO (or self-approve it if release strategy is weak), post a goods receipt against it, let MIRO/ERS generate the liability, and payment follows — all without any independent confirmation that material actually arrived. Impact is inventory shrinkage, inflated cost of goods, fraudulent disbursement and understated margins. It also masks kickback schemes with real vendors, where over-received quantities are split. Under SOX and ITGC this is a high-severity procure-to-pay conflict. A real incident presents as recurring GR/IR imbalances, goods-receipts with no corresponding delivery notes, and inventory counts that never reconcile — usually caught during a physical stock take long after the cash has left.
IAM-SOD-FIN-002.mitigation:
1. Validate each flagged user in SUIM (RSUSR070) or GRC ARA, checking the movement types and activities actually granted in M_MSEG_BWA and M_BEST_BSA.
2. Segregate the roles: purchasing users keep ME2x, warehouse/receiving users keep MIGO — remove the crossover transactions from the composite role in PFCG and regenerate.
3. Strengthen PO release strategy in customizing (SPRO: Materials Management > Purchasing > Purchase Order > Release Procedure) so PO creators cannot release their own orders; enforce it via object M_EINK_FRG.
4. Where combined access is unavoidable (small teams), implement a mitigating control: independent review of GR-without-delivery-note exceptions and periodic GR/IR clearing analysis (MB5S / transaction MR11).
5. Configure and monitor goods-movement logs and require inbound delivery references for high-value materials.
6. Verify by re-running SoD analysis and confirming the conflict is cleared, and by spot-checking that a purchaser can no longer post MIGO.
7. Rollout caution: coordinate with logistics/warehouse process owners and test in QA, since MIGO authorization changes can block legitimate receiving if movement types are over-restricted.

IAM-SOD-FIN-003.risk: Users here can both post journal entries (FB01/FB50/F-02, object F_BKPF_BUK) and maintain the GL account master (FS00/FSP0/FSS0, object F_SKA1_BUK). Whoever controls both the chart of accounts and the postings to it can engineer and hide financial-statement manipulation. The scenario: the user creates or unblocks a GL account (or changes its field-status/line-item settings), posts adjusting or top-side journal entries to move amounts between periods or accounts, and then re-blocks or deletes the account to obscure the activity — classic earnings management and fraud concealment. Because master-data and transactional control sit with one person, the entries look legitimate. Impact is materially misstated financials, improper revenue/expense timing, and potential securities-law exposure; this is precisely the kind of top-side journal-entry risk external auditors are required to scrutinize under SOX and PCAOB standards. A real incident looks like quarter-end manual journals to unusual or newly created accounts posted by the same user who set those accounts up, discovered only during journal-entry testing or a restatement.
IAM-SOD-FIN-003.mitigation:
1. Confirm the conflict in SUIM (RSUSR070 on the listed t-codes) or GRC ARA; distinguish create/change (ACTVT 01/02) from display.
2. Separate duties: GL master maintenance should belong to a master-data/controlling team, journal posting to accounting operations — remove FS00/FSP0/FSS0 from posting roles in PFCG and regenerate.
3. Restrict object F_SKA1_BUK so posting users cannot maintain account master, and tighten F_BKPF_BUK by company code where appropriate.
4. Where separation is impractical, implement a mitigating control: mandatory review of all manual journals via the GL line-item/journal-entry monitoring (e.g. document change logs in CDHDR/CDPOS and periodic review of FB03 postings), and workflow approval for parked documents (FBV0).
5. Enable change-document logging for GL master data and review it periodically.
6. Verify by re-running the SoD check and confirming the user can no longer open FS00 in change mode.
7. Rollout caution: coordinate with the controlling/record-to-report process owner and test in QA so period-end close is not disrupted by removed authorizations.

IAM-SOD-FIN-004.risk: These users can both create/change customer master data (FD01/FD02/XD01/XD02/BP, object F_KNA1_BUK) and create sales orders or billing documents (VA01/VA02/VF01/VF02, object V_VBAK_AAT). This order-to-cash conflict enables fictitious-revenue and credit-abuse fraud. The abuse path: the user sets up a bogus or related-party customer with an inflated credit limit, books sales orders and billing documents against it to recognize revenue, or issues unauthorized credit memos (VF01 for credits) to write off receivables to colluding parties. Because master data and the transactions flow through one person, there is no independent check on customer legitimacy or credit terms. Impact includes channel-stuffing/revenue-recognition fraud that inflates reported sales, bad-debt losses from uncollectable fictitious sales, and unauthorized discounts or credits that leak margin. Under SOX and revenue-recognition (IFRS 15 / ASC 606) scrutiny this is a high-risk combination. A real incident looks like end-of-quarter sales spikes to newly created customers with generous credit limits set by the same salesperson, followed by returns or write-offs in the next period — surfacing during revenue-cut-off testing or DSO analysis.
IAM-SOD-FIN-004.mitigation:
1. Confirm each conflict in SUIM (RSUSR070) or GRC ARA and verify the activities granted (create/change vs display).
2. Split the duties: customer master maintenance to a master-data team, order/billing to sales operations — remove FD0x/XD0x/BP change access from sales-transaction roles in PFCG and regenerate.
3. Move credit-limit maintenance into SAP Credit Management / FSCM and restrict it away from order creators so limits cannot be self-set.
4. Where combined access persists, add a mitigating control: independent review of new-customer setups and of all credit memos (VF01 credit process), plus blocked-order release handled by a separate approver (VKM1/VKM3).
5. Enable change-document logging on customer master (KNA1/KNB1) and review periodically.
6. Verify by re-running the SoD analysis and confirming zero residual conflicts for these users.
7. Rollout caution: coordinate with the order-to-cash process owner, test in QA, and stage the change to avoid blocking legitimate order entry during a billing run.

IAM-SOD-HR-001.risk: Users flagged here can both maintain HR master data (PA20/PA30/PA40, object P_ORGIN) and execute payroll (PC00_M99_RUN / PC00_M10_CALC / PU03, object P_PYEVRUN). This is the 'ghost employee' payroll-fraud combination. A single person can create a fictitious employee or alter an existing employee's bank details (infotype 0009) and salary infotypes (0008), then run payroll to disburse funds to an account they control, and revert the data afterward. Because HR master data and payroll processing are not separated, there is no independent validation of who is being paid or how much. Beyond fraud, this concentration exposes highly sensitive personal data — salaries, bank accounts, national IDs, health/absence data — creating a GDPR/BDSG exposure if the account is compromised or misused, since one user can read and change the entire workforce's PII. Impact is direct payroll theft, unauthorized salary changes, and privacy-regulation breach with notification and fine risk. A real incident looks like a recurring payment to an 'employee' with no cost-center manager, no time records and a bank account shared with a payroll administrator, discovered during a headcount-to-payroll reconciliation long after multiple pay cycles.
IAM-SOD-HR-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm each conflict in SUIM (RSUSR070) or GRC ARA, checking the infotype/subtype authorizations in P_ORGIN and the payroll-area scope in P_PYEVRUN.
2. Separate the duties: HR administrators maintain master data; a distinct payroll team runs PC00_* — remove payroll-run transactions from HR-admin roles in PFCG and regenerate.
3. Protect sensitive infotypes (0008 basic pay, 0009 bank details) with structural/context authorizations (P_ORGINCON / P_PERNR) so administrators cannot edit their own or unrelated records.
4. Enforce payroll control record (PA03) release by a separate role so the person maintaining data cannot start the run.
5. Where separation is impractical, implement a mitigating control: independent review of bank-detail (IT0009) change logs and a payroll-to-headcount reconciliation each cycle.
6. Verify by re-running the SoD analysis and confirming HR admins can no longer start a payroll run.
7. Rollout caution: test in QA, coordinate with the HR/payroll process owner, and treat all HR authorization data as GDPR-relevant — restrict who can even view the finding's affected-user list.

IAM-SOD-SEC-001.risk: These users hold both user-administration authority (SU01/SU01D/SU10, object S_USER_GRP) and role/profile-administration authority (PFCG/SU02/SU03, object S_USER_AGR). This is a security-administration SoD conflict that directly enables self-service privilege escalation and is one of the most dangerous internal combinations in an SAP system. A single administrator can build or modify a role in PFCG to include any powerful authorization (for example S_TABU_DIS/DISPLAY on sensitive tables, or SAP_ALL-equivalent objects), then assign that role to themselves or an accomplice through SU01/SU10 — all without any second person approving either the role content or the assignment. The same power lets them create a hidden back-door account, grant it broad access, use it, and delete it. Impact is a complete breakdown of the authorization control model: any access restriction elsewhere in the system becomes meaningless because this user can grant themselves around it, enabling downstream financial fraud, data theft or sabotage while covering their tracks. Under SOX ITGC and the SAP Security Baseline this is a critical control. A real incident looks like a user whose own role was edited and re-assigned by that same user shortly before a suspicious set of financial or data-export actions, uncovered only through security audit-log (SM20) and change-document review.
IAM-SOD-SEC-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the conflict in SUIM (RSUSR070 for the t-codes; RSUSR002 for objects S_USER_GRP and S_USER_AGR) and check the activity/values granted.
2. Split security administration into two roles held by different people: a user-administrator role (SU01/SU10, S_USER_GRP scoped to specific user groups) and a role-administrator role (PFCG, S_USER_AGR) — never both to one person.
3. Remove PFCG/SU02/SU03 from the user-admin role (and vice versa) in PFCG and regenerate; scope S_USER_GRP to defined user groups and S_USER_AGR to a role namespace rather than '*'.
4. Implement a four-eyes control for role changes and assignments — ideally route provisioning through GRC Access Request Management (ARM) with workflow approval so no one self-provisions.
5. Enable table logging and change documents on user/role tables and monitor SU01/PFCG activity in the Security Audit Log (SM19/SM20, transaction RSAU_CONFIG).
6. Verify by re-running the analysis and confirming no single user retains both S_USER_GRP and S_USER_AGR with change activity.
7. Rollout caution: coordinate a maintenance window, retain at least one emergency break-glass admin (monitored), and test the split roles in QA before production cutover.

IAM-SOD-BASIS-001.risk: Users flagged here can both develop ABAP (SE38/SE80/SE24/SE37, object S_DEVELOP) and manage/release transports (STMS/SE09/SE10, object S_CTS_ADMI), collapsing the change-management control that keeps unreviewed or malicious code out of production. A developer who can also import transports can write arbitrary ABAP — including a program that grants SAP_ALL, reads or exfiltrates data, or opens a remote back door — and push it straight to production without any independent code review, QA sign-off or approval. Because ABAP runs with full application privileges, this is effectively remote code execution within the ERP: the developer can bypass every authorization check at the source-code level. Impact spans fraud (code that silently posts or approves transactions), data breach (mass extract via a custom report), sabotage, and audit failure — SOX and change-management controls require strict separation between who writes code and who moves it to production. A real incident looks like an unauthorized or undocumented transport imported to PRD outside the change calendar, later traced to a custom program that manipulated financial postings or created a privileged account — discovered during transport-log (STMS import history) review or a code audit.
IAM-SOD-BASIS-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the conflict in SUIM (RSUSR070 on the listed t-codes) or GRC ARA and check S_DEVELOP activity and S_CTS_ADMI values.
2. Enforce the standard landscape rule: developers work only in DEV with S_DEVELOP; transport import to QA/PRD is performed by a separate Basis/transport-admin team — remove STMS/SE09/SE10 release+import authority from developer roles in PFCG and regenerate.
3. Keep production clients closed to changes via client settings (SCC4) and the system change option (SE06), so code cannot be created directly in PRD.
4. Route all transports through a change-management approval (ChaRM/Solution Manager or an equivalent CAB workflow) so no developer self-approves a production import.
5. Restrict S_CTS_ADMI and the transport-import authorization to the Basis transport team only, and review STMS import history regularly.
6. Verify by re-running the SoD analysis and confirming developers can no longer import to PRD, and by checking STMS that imports require the transport-admin role.
7. Rollout caution: coordinate with the Basis team and release manager, test in QA, and ensure an approved emergency-transport procedure exists so urgent fixes are not blocked.

IAM-SOD-HEUR-001.risk: This is the heuristic (role-name-pattern) counterpart to the vendor-maintenance vs payment conflict, raised when precise t-code data was not available and the scanner matched role names containing patterns such as VENDOR/FK0x/SUPPLIER on one side and PAYMENT/F110/DISBURS on the other. The underlying risk is identical and severe: a user who can both maintain vendor master data and process outgoing payments can create a fictitious vendor (or hijack a real vendor's bank account) and pay it, committing and concealing disbursement fraud without a second reviewer. The important caveat is confidence: because the match is by naming convention rather than resolved authorizations, some hits may be false positives (a role named 'VENDOR_DISPLAY' is not really conflicting) and, conversely, poorly named roles could hide real conflicts this heuristic misses. It should therefore be treated as a strong lead requiring validation, not a confirmed violation. Business impact once confirmed is the same as the precise rule — cash theft, financial misstatement, SOX material-weakness exposure. A realistic outcome is that validation confirms a subset of these users genuinely hold both capabilities and have been operating without compensating controls, which then becomes an auditable finding.
IAM-SOD-HEUR-001.mitigation:
1. Do not remediate blindly — first validate each flagged user at the authorization level in SUIM (RSUSR070 by the actual vendor and payment t-codes) or GRC Access Risk Analysis to separate true conflicts from name-only false positives.
2. Improve the data feed by exporting role-to-t-code content from AGR_1251 (role_tcodes.csv) so future scans use the precise IAM-SOD-FIN-001 logic instead of the heuristic.
3. For confirmed conflicts, remove one side (vendor maintenance or payment) from the user's roles in PFCG and regenerate; apply the same remediation and mitigating controls described for IAM-SOD-FIN-001 (F110 dual control, vendor bank-change monitoring).
4. Remediate role naming: adopt a consistent PFCG naming convention so business function is unambiguous and heuristic analysis is reliable.
5. Verify by re-running the analysis with resolved t-code data and confirming the conflict is cleared for validated users.
6. Rollout caution: because these are candidates, coordinate with the process owner and confirm business impact before stripping access, and always test role changes in QA first.

IAM-SOD-HEUR-002.risk: This heuristic flags the purchasing vs goods-receipt conflict based on role naming patterns (PURCHAS/ME21/BUYER on one side, GR_POST/MIGO/WAREHOUSE on the other) when precise t-code resolution was unavailable. The real risk it approximates is the procure-to-pay three-way-match breakdown: a user who can both raise purchase orders and confirm goods receipt can order from a colluding or fictitious vendor and 'receive' goods that never arrived, triggering invoice verification and payment automatically. As a naming-based match it carries lower certainty — a display-only receiving role or an inconsistently named role can produce false positives or false negatives — so each hit needs authorization-level confirmation. Where confirmed, the impact is inventory shrinkage, fraudulent disbursement, inflated cost of goods and kickback concealment, all high-severity for SOX and procure-to-pay ITGC. The likely real-world outcome is that a portion of the flagged users are validated as genuinely holding both ME2x and MIGO, revealing that the three-way match has been relying on trust rather than enforced segregation.
IAM-SOD-HEUR-002.mitigation:
1. Validate each flagged user at the authorization level in SUIM (RSUSR070 by ME21N/ME22N and MIGO/MB01) or GRC ARA before acting.
2. Export AGR_1251 role content (role_tcodes.csv) so subsequent scans use the precise IAM-SOD-FIN-002 rule rather than the heuristic.
3. For confirmed conflicts, separate purchasing and receiving roles in PFCG (buyers keep ME2x, warehouse keeps MIGO), regenerate, and run PFUD.
4. Reinforce PO release strategy (SPRO: MM > Purchasing > Purchase Order > Release Procedure; object M_EINK_FRG) so creators cannot release their own POs.
5. For unavoidable combined access, add a mitigating control: review of goods-receipts without delivery notes and periodic GR/IR clearing analysis (MR11 / MB5S).
6. Standardize role naming so future heuristic runs are accurate.
7. Verify by re-running with resolved data and confirming zero residual conflicts; rollout caution — test in QA and coordinate with logistics owners so receiving is not blocked.

IAM-SOD-HEUR-003.risk: This heuristic flags the user-administration vs security-administration conflict from role naming patterns (USER_ADMIN/SU01/IDM_ADMIN on one side, ROLE_ADMIN/PFCG/SECURITY_ADMIN on the other) when precise authorization data was not supplied. It is rated critical because the approximated conflict — one person able to both create/modify users and build/modify roles — is a direct self-privilege-escalation path: such a user can craft a role granting any authorization and assign it to themselves or a back-door account with no independent approval, dissolving the entire access-control model. As a name-based match it needs validation (a role called 'USER_ADMIN_DISPLAY' is not a real conflict), but the downside of a true positive is so severe that it warrants urgent verification. Confirmed cases mean any other control in the system can be bypassed by this administrator, exposing the organization to fraud, mass data exfiltration and sabotage, and constituting a critical SOX ITGC and SAP Security Baseline failure. A realistic outcome is discovering that a Basis/security administrator effectively holds unchecked god-mode over both users and roles, which auditors will treat as a significant deficiency until segregated.
IAM-SOD-HEUR-003.mitigation:
1. Treat as urgent but validate first: confirm each user at object level in SUIM (RSUSR002 for S_USER_GRP and S_USER_AGR) or GRC ARA to rule out display-only false positives.
2. Export precise role content (AGR_1251) so future scans use the exact IAM-SOD-SEC-001 logic rather than heuristics.
3. For confirmed conflicts, split security administration into a user-admin role (SU01/SU10, S_USER_GRP scoped) and a separate role-admin role (PFCG, S_USER_AGR), each held by different individuals; remove the crossover in PFCG and regenerate.
4. Route role changes and user provisioning through GRC Access Request Management with four-eyes approval so no one self-provisions.
5. Enable change documents and Security Audit Log monitoring (RSAU_CONFIG/SM20) for SU01 and PFCG activity.
6. Standardize role naming to make future heuristic detection reliable.
7. Verify by re-running with resolved data and confirming no user holds both objects with change activity; rollout caution — retain one monitored break-glass admin and test the split in QA before cutover.

IAM-FF-000.risk: This finding means accounts matching firefighter/emergency naming patterns (FF_, FIRE, EMERG, BREAKGLASS, EAM_) exist in the user master, but no usage log was provided, so their activity cannot be reviewed. Firefighter (emergency access / GRC Superuser Privilege Management) IDs carry very broad, often SAP_ALL-class authorizations that are meant to be checked out temporarily under supervision and fully logged. If these powerful IDs exist but their sessions are not being logged, exported or reviewed, the core compensating control around privileged emergency access is either absent or invisible to audit. The abuse scenario: someone checks out a firefighter ID (or logs on with it directly if it is a standing dialog account with a known password), performs privileged actions — posting entries, changing configuration, creating users, exporting data — and because no log is captured or reviewed, the action is untraceable to the real person and never scrutinized. Impact is unaccountable superuser activity, which is both a fraud/data-breach enabler and a direct SOX/ITGC deficiency because privileged access without logging and review is a classic material-weakness pattern. A real incident looks like an unexplained privileged change in production that cannot be attributed to any individual because the firefighter session was never logged.
IAM-FF-000.mitigation:
1. Identify the flagged IDs and determine whether they are managed through SAP GRC Emergency Access Management (EAM/SPM) or are standing privileged accounts outside any framework.
2. If GRC EAM is in place, export the consolidated firefighter log (EAM Log Report / report GRAC_SPM_LOG_SYNC) to firefighter_log.csv with FF_USER, ACTUAL_USER, LOGIN_TIME, LOGOUT_TIME, REASON, REVIEWED, REVIEWER, then re-run the scanner.
3. If no framework exists, implement one: bring emergency IDs under GRC EAM (or an equivalent controlled check-out with logging) so every session captures who, when, why and what.
4. Ensure firefighter IDs are not usable as ordinary standing logons — lock them (SU01/SU10) between check-outs and remove shared static passwords.
5. Enable the Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG) for these IDs as a backstop so their actions are captured even outside EAM.
6. Verify by confirming the exported log now covers all emergency IDs and that IAM-FF-000 no longer fires.
7. Rollout caution: coordinate with control owners; ensure at least one working emergency-access path remains available during the transition so operations are not stranded.

IAM-FF-001.risk: This flags emergency/firefighter sessions that ran longer than the configured maximum (default 4 hours). Firefighter access grants far-reaching privileges for a specific, time-boxed emergency; a session that stays open for many hours (or overnight) is a control failure because the window during which someone can perform unrelated, unmonitored privileged actions expands dramatically. Long sessions often indicate the ID was checked out and then left logged on, shared, or used for routine work it was never intended for. The abuse scenario: a user checks out a firefighter ID for a genuine incident, then keeps the session alive to perform additional, unlogged-in-context activities — configuration changes, data extracts, transaction postings — well beyond the original justification, blending legitimate and illegitimate actions in one hard-to-review session. Impact is increased exposure to privileged misuse and a weakened compensating control; extended sessions also make the controller's after-the-fact review far harder because there is more activity to sift and less correlation to a single incident. For SOX this undermines the 'time-boxed, reviewed emergency access' assertion. A real incident looks like a firefighter session spanning a full day during which an unauthorized master-data or authorization change was made, buried among legitimate incident-response steps.
IAM-FF-001.mitigation:
1. Review each long session in the GRC EAM log against its incident/change ticket to confirm whether the extended duration was justified or indicates misuse.
2. Enforce a session time-limit: in GRC EAM configuration (SPRO > Governance, Risk and Compliance > Access Control > Emergency Access Management) set the firefighter validity/timeout so IDs auto-expire after the approved window (align to the 4-hour target or your policy).
3. Configure SAP profile parameter rdisp/gui_auto_logout (and comparable idle-timeout controls) so idle privileged sessions are terminated.
4. Require re-checkout for continued work rather than keeping one session open, so each interval carries its own justification and log.
5. Investigate any session that materially exceeded the limit for unauthorized activity, using the EAM transaction log and Security Audit Log (SM20).
6. Verify by re-running the analysis after the next review cycle and confirming sessions now fall within the threshold.
7. Rollout caution: test the timeout in a non-production GRC/EAM setting first and communicate to firefighter users so an auto-logout does not interrupt a genuine critical fix mid-step.

IAM-FF-002.risk: This flags emergency/firefighter sessions that have no documented reason or justification. A defining requirement of controlled emergency access is that every check-out carries a traceable business reason — typically an incident or change ticket — so the privileged activity can be explained and defended in audit. Sessions with a blank reason break the accountability chain: nobody can later establish why superuser access was used, whether it was authorized, or whether the actions taken matched a legitimate need. The abuse scenario is straightforward — a user checks out the firefighter ID without stating a reason and performs privileged actions that are effectively unexplained, giving cover to unauthorized changes because there is no stated purpose to compare the actual activity against. Impact is a failed compensating control and an audit exception: SOX/J-SOX and internal control frameworks require documented justification for privileged access, and undocumented emergency use is routinely cited as a deficiency. A real incident looks like an auditor sampling firefighter logs, finding sessions with empty justification, and being unable to obtain any after-the-fact explanation — turning a procedural gap into a reportable control weakness and prompting a wider look for actual misuse.
IAM-FF-002.mitigation:
1. Review the listed sessions and retroactively obtain and document the business reason/ticket for each, recording it in the EAM log so the audit trail is complete.
2. Make justification mandatory: in GRC EAM configuration enable the reason-code requirement and maintain a controlled list of reason codes (EAM 'Maintain Reason Codes') so no session can start without one.
3. Require an incident/change ticket reference in the reason field via workflow, linking each firefighter use to ITSM.
4. Configure the firefighter log-on screen/workflow to reject blank justifications.
5. Train firefighter users and controllers on the documentation standard and the audit consequences of blank reasons.
6. Verify by re-running the analysis for the next period and confirming all new sessions carry a justification.
7. Rollout caution: test the mandatory-reason enforcement in non-production EAM first so it does not block a genuine emergency, and grandfather/annotate historical sessions rather than leaving them blank.

IAM-FF-003.risk: This flags emergency/firefighter sessions that were never reviewed by a controller — rated critical because controller review is the primary compensating control that makes broad emergency access acceptable in the first place. The model is: firefighter IDs may carry very high privileges, but every session is logged and an independent controller reviews the captured activity afterward to confirm it matched the stated emergency and contained no unauthorized actions. If sessions go unreviewed, that control has effectively not operated — powerful privileged activity occurred with no one checking what was done. The abuse scenario: a user (or someone who obtained the ID) performs unauthorized privileged actions during a session that is never examined, so fraud, data exfiltration or a planted back door passes silently. Impact is severe on two fronts: real risk of undetected superuser misuse, and a direct SOX Section 404 failure because the compensating control auditors rely on to accept emergency access is demonstrably not being performed. A real incident looks like a post-breach investigation revealing that the malicious change was made during a firefighter session weeks earlier that no controller ever reviewed, meaning the organization had the log evidence all along but never looked.
IAM-FF-003.mitigation:
1. Immediately assign controllers to review every outstanding session in the list, comparing the logged activity to the stated justification and escalating anomalies.
2. Ensure each firefighter ID has a designated, independent controller in GRC EAM (Firefighter ID owner/controller assignment) and that the assignment is current.
3. Configure automated notification/escalation in EAM so controllers receive the log for review promptly and reminders fire if review is not completed.
4. Establish and enforce an SLA (e.g. review within 48 hours of session end) and track compliance.
5. Use the EAM Log Report / consolidated log review workflow as the standard mechanism rather than ad-hoc checks.
6. Verify by re-running the analysis and confirming the backlog is cleared and new sessions are reviewed within SLA.
7. Rollout caution: allocate sufficient controller capacity before tightening SLAs, and ensure controllers are independent of the firefighter users (see IAM-FF-004) so the review is meaningful.

IAM-FF-004.risk: This flags emergency/firefighter sessions that were reviewed or approved by the same person who used the firefighter ID — a self-review that voids the control entirely and is therefore critical. Controller review works only if the controller is independent of the requestor; when the user reviews their own session, there is no oversight at all, only the appearance of one. This is a segregation-of-duties failure inside the control process itself. The abuse scenario: a user checks out the firefighter ID, performs unauthorized privileged actions, then signs off their own session as 'reviewed — no exceptions', producing a clean audit trail that conceals the very misuse the review was meant to catch. It is especially dangerous because it can be entirely deliberate — the person committing the misuse is also the person certifying it was fine. Impact is undetected privileged fraud combined with fabricated assurance, and it is a direct SOX/ITGC control-design deficiency because the compensating control lacks independence. A real incident looks like an insider who both used a firefighter ID to make fraudulent postings and closed out the review themselves, so every periodic control test passed until an unrelated investigation exposed the self-approval pattern.
IAM-FF-004.mitigation:
1. Immediately re-review every self-approved session in the list using a genuinely independent controller, treating them as unreviewed.
2. Enforce controller ≠ requestor in GRC EAM: assign firefighter controllers who are organizationally independent of the firefighter users and never assign a user as their own controller.
3. Configure EAM so the workflow prevents the log reviewer from being the session's actual user (segregate owner/controller/firefighter roles).
4. Review the firefighter owner/controller matrix (GRC EAM master data) to eliminate any circular assignments across the team.
5. Add a periodic control that specifically tests for reviewer = requestor so any recurrence is caught quickly.
6. Verify by re-running the analysis and confirming no session shows the same person as user and reviewer.
7. Rollout caution: ensure enough independent controllers exist before enforcing the rule so emergencies are not left without any valid reviewer, and document the reassignment for audit.

IAM-FF-005.risk: This flags users who invoked emergency/firefighter access more often than the configured monthly threshold (default 5 sessions). Emergency access is meant to be exceptional — for genuine, unforeseen incidents. When a user relies on it repeatedly, it signals that firefighter access has become a routine substitute for properly designed day-to-day roles, which erodes the whole model: frequent use normalizes privileged access, dilutes controller scrutiny (reviewers become desensitized to yet another session), and expands the time these users spend operating with broad, SAP_ALL-class authorizations. The abuse angle is subtle rather than a single break-in: a user who is 'always firefighting' effectively holds standing elevated access under the guise of emergencies, blending routine work into privileged sessions where individual actions get less attention, and gaining ongoing capability to act beyond their assigned role. Impact is heightened privilege-misuse exposure and a weakened control narrative for audit — a recertification reviewer will question why standing needs are met through emergency access. It also masks a role-design gap. A real incident looks like a power user who quietly runs most of their job through firefighter IDs, so a fraudulent action hides among dozens of 'normal' emergency sessions nobody scrutinizes closely.
IAM-FF-005.mitigation:
1. Review each high-frequency user's firefighter usage in the GRC EAM log to understand what recurring tasks are driving the check-outs.
2. Where the need is legitimate and recurring, redesign the user's permanent role in PFCG to cover it (least privilege) so emergency access is no longer the routine path.
3. Reserve firefighter IDs strictly for genuine emergencies and set/enforce a monthly usage expectation; flag outliers for owner review.
4. Tighten the scope of firefighter IDs so they are not a catch-all superuser — provision task-specific firefighter roles where possible.
5. Track firefighter-usage trends over time and feed them into periodic access review so recurring users are re-justified or re-provisioned.
6. Verify by re-running the analysis after role redesign and confirming usage frequency drops below the threshold.
7. Rollout caution: validate the redesigned permanent role in QA and confirm it does not itself create a SoD conflict, and coordinate with the process owner before removing the user's reliance on emergency access.

IAM-EXP-001.risk: This flags role assignments with no expiration date (validity-to open-ended, e.g. 31.12.9999) in AGR_USERS. Indefinite role assignments defeat time-based access governance: access that never expires accumulates silently as people change jobs, cover for colleagues, or complete projects, producing the classic 'privilege creep' where long-tenured users end up with far more access than their current role needs. Because the assignment never lapses, it also bypasses one of the simplest recertification levers — forcing periodic renewal by design. The abuse scenario is opportunistic: a user who retains an old, forgotten but still-valid role can use residual access (e.g. to a financial transaction or sensitive table) long after any business need ended, and because nothing ever prompted a review, the access is invisible to controls. Impact is an ever-growing attack surface and SoD-conflict likelihood, plus an ITGC/SOX weakness because recertification cannot be evidenced as effective when assignments are perpetual. A real incident looks like a mover-not-leaver who accumulated financial and admin roles across several transfers — none time-boxed — and exploited a legacy authorization that should have expired years earlier, discovered only during a belated access review.
IAM-EXP-001.mitigation:
1. Quantify the population from AGR_USERS (SE16N: entries with TO_DAT = 99991231) and prioritize privileged and financially significant roles.
2. Set validity-end dates on assignments: use PFCG (user tab) or mass maintenance via SU10 to apply a policy-based expiry, or drive it through SAP Identity Management / GRC Access Request Management for automated enforcement.
3. Define and document a maximum validity policy (e.g. 12 months for business roles; longer, justified periods for technical/service accounts).
4. Implement periodic recertification (GRC User Access Review campaigns) so renewals require explicit re-approval rather than defaulting to perpetual.
5. For assignments provisioned via GRC ARM, enforce mandatory validity end-dates at request time.
6. Verify by re-extracting AGR_USERS and confirming the count of open-ended assignments has dropped to the approved exceptions only.
7. Rollout caution: do not blanket-expire technical/RFC/service users that would break interfaces — coordinate with interface owners, exempt and document them, and stage changes in QA before production.

IAM-EXP-002.risk: This flags role assignments whose validity end-date has already passed but which are still physically present in the user master (AGR_USERS). SAP does enforce validity dates at runtime, so an expired assignment does not by itself grant access — which is why the severity is low — but leaving expired rows in place still matters. It pollutes the access baseline: SoD analysis, access reviews and audits become noisy and error-prone because the reported assignment set does not reflect effective access, and reviewers may waste effort on or be confused by stale entries. It can also mask reactivation risk — an administrator re-opening the validity window (or a mass job doing so) could silently restore access that everyone assumed was gone. The practical impact is data-quality and control-hygiene: accumulated expired assignments bloat AGR_USERS, complicate reporting, and reduce confidence that the access model is clean. A real incident is less a breach and more an audit friction point — reviewers cannot reconcile 'who has what' against the raw table, and an expired-but-present privileged role gets accidentally reactivated during a role-copy or mass-change operation.
IAM-EXP-002.mitigation:
1. Confirm the population from AGR_USERS (SE16N: TO_DAT earlier than today) and separate genuinely expired assignments from those with future or open validity.
2. Run report PRGN_COMPRESS_TIMES (SA38/SE38) with the option to delete expired assignments; it compresses and removes past-dated role/user links in AGR_USERS.
3. Run it in the correct system — on the CUA central system if Central User Administration is in use — and review its log of changes.
4. Schedule PRGN_COMPRESS_TIMES as a periodic background job (SM36) to keep the assignment table clean going forward.
5. After cleanup, run PFUD (user master comparison) so profiles are consistent.
6. Verify by re-extracting AGR_USERS and confirming expired rows are gone and no active access was affected.
7. Rollout caution: first run in simulation/QA and review the log, because compression can also merge overlapping validity intervals — confirm no still-valid assignment is inadvertently removed, and back up AGR_USERS before the first production run.

IAM-EXP-003.risk: This flags role assignments whose validity window exceeds the configured maximum (default 365 days). Very long validity periods are a weaker form of the 'no expiry' problem: the assignment does eventually lapse, but the multi-year window delays recertification and stretches the time during which access remains unexamined. Long-lived grants let privilege creep and dormant access persist far longer than governance intends, and they blunt the effectiveness of periodic access reviews because a role granted for three years simply will not resurface for renewal within a normal review cadence. The abuse scenario is again slow and opportunistic: a user retains a powerful role across job changes for the full extended window, and because renewal is not forced, misuse of that residual access (a financial posting capability, a sensitive-data read) can go unchecked for a long time. Impact is elevated standing risk and a control-effectiveness gap for SOX/ITGC recertification, which expects access to be re-justified on a defined, reasonably short cycle. A real incident looks like an over-long grant to a role that later becomes SoD-conflicting after other changes, with the conflict persisting for years because the assignment was never up for renewal.
IAM-EXP-003.mitigation:
1. Identify assignments where TO_DAT minus FROM_DAT exceeds policy (from AGR_USERS) and prioritize privileged/financial roles.
2. Reduce validity to the policy maximum (e.g. 12 months) via PFCG or mass maintenance (SU10), or reprovision through GRC ARM with a bounded validity.
3. Establish a recertification workflow (GRC User Access Review) so role renewals require explicit re-approval at each cycle rather than a single long grant.
4. Define documented exceptions for service/technical accounts that legitimately need longer validity, with named approval.
5. Enforce a maximum validity default at provisioning time in GRC ARM so future requests cannot exceed policy without approval.
6. Verify by re-extracting AGR_USERS and confirming validity windows now conform, exceptions aside.
7. Rollout caution: shortening validity forces earlier renewals — coordinate with process/role owners so business users are not unexpectedly cut off, and exempt documented technical accounts to avoid breaking interfaces; test mass changes in QA first.

IAM-XID-001.risk: This flags SAP BTP subaccount users that have no matching identity in the S/4HANA user master. In a RISE landscape, BTP is the extension and integration layer that often reaches back into the S/4 backend through destinations, APIs and the Cloud Connector. A BTP user with no S/4 counterpart may be a legitimate external developer or consultant, but it may equally be an orphaned account left after a project, a shadow identity created directly in BTP outside central governance, or a leaver who was removed from S/4 but not from BTP. The danger is that these accounts fall outside the joiner-mover-leaver process and periodic reviews that govern S/4 users, so they persist unnoticed and unreviewed. The abuse scenario: an external contractor's BTP account remains active after engagement end, still holding role collections that allow access to apps, APIs or destinations that proxy into backend data — the contractor (or anyone who obtains the credentials) can continue to reach business functions with no S/4 login ever appearing. Impact is unmanaged standing access, potential data exposure through BTP services, and a governance gap that identity audits will flag. A real incident looks like a former consultant retaining BTP access months after leaving, used to pull data through an OData API that routes to S/4.
IAM-XID-001.mitigation:
1. Export the BTP subaccount user and role-collection list (BTP cockpit > Security > Users, or via the SCIM/authorization API) and reconcile against the S/4 user master to review each BTP-only account.
2. For each, confirm business justification with the responsible team; remove orphaned or unrecognized accounts in the BTP cockpit.
3. Establish centralized identity governance across S/4 and BTP using SAP Cloud Identity Services — Identity Authentication (IAS) as the single IdP and Identity Provisioning (IPS) with SCIM to provision/deprovision consistently.
4. Bring external/consultant identities into the same lifecycle (sponsored accounts with expiry) rather than standalone BTP entries.
5. Add BTP users to the periodic access review scope so BTP-only identities are re-justified each cycle.
6. Verify by re-running the comparison after cleanup and confirming remaining BTP-only users are documented exceptions.
7. Rollout caution: confirm an account is truly orphaned (not a needed technical/integration user) before deletion, coordinate with the extension/development team, and change in a controlled window to avoid breaking running BTP apps or destinations.

IAM-XID-002.risk: This flags users who are locked in S/4HANA but still active in BTP — a direct indicator of incomplete offboarding, rated high. When an account is locked in the S/4 backend (UFLAG set) the organization has decided that person should not have access, typically because they left or their access was suspended. If the same identity remains active in BTP, the intended access revocation is only half done: the user can still authenticate to BTP, use subscribed applications and APIs, and — critically in RISE — potentially reach the S/4 backend indirectly through destinations, OData/REST APIs or the Cloud Connector that run under technical or principal-propagation identities. The abuse scenario: a terminated employee whose S/4 account was locked still logs into a BTP Fiori app or calls a BTP-exposed API that proxies data from S/4, continuing to view or extract business data despite the backend lock, or a suspended insider retains a BTP path to act while believed to be cut off. Impact is a real, exploitable gap in deprovisioning that defeats the control, plus GDPR/leaver-access and SOX termination-control failures. A real incident looks like a departed contractor or employee accessing customer or financial data through BTP for weeks after their S/4 account was locked, uncovered during an access review or an anomalous-login investigation.
IAM-XID-002.mitigation:
1. Immediately revoke BTP access for every listed user — remove their role collections and disable the account in the BTP cockpit (and in IAS if that is the IdP).
2. Correlate with HR/leaver records to confirm each is a genuine leaver or suspension and document the removal.
3. Implement synchronized deprovisioning: use SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS as IdP, IPS for provisioning) so an S/4 lock/termination automatically propagates to BTP and removes access.
4. Where IPS reads the ABAP system as a source, configure the lock/deletion in S/4 to trigger BTP deprovisioning so the two systems cannot drift.
5. Review BTP destinations and technical users to ensure a locked person cannot reach S/4 through a shared technical identity.
6. Verify by re-running the cross-system comparison and confirming no S/4-locked user remains active in BTP.
7. Rollout caution: act quickly given the exposure, but confirm you are not disabling a still-needed shared/technical account that merely shares a naming collision; coordinate with the BTP admin and record every change for audit.

IAM-XID-003.risk: This flags BTP subaccount users holding administrative role collections (e.g. Subaccount Administrator, Global Account Administrator, Space Developer, Security Administrator, Connectivity/Destination Administrator). BTP administrative access is highly powerful in a RISE landscape: these role collections can change trust configuration and identity-provider settings, create and edit destinations and the Cloud Connector configuration that bridge BTP to the S/4 backend, provision services, and manage other users' access. Excessive or unjustified admin assignments concentrate control over the entire extension and integration layer in too many hands, and each admin is a high-value target. The abuse scenario: an over-privileged BTP admin (or an attacker who phishes one) reconfigures a destination or trust to point at an attacker-controlled endpoint, creates a destination that exposes S/4 data, weakens authentication, or grants themselves further access — effectively compromising the bridge between cloud and core ERP. Impact spans data breach, integration tampering, and potential lateral movement into S/4, plus a least-privilege and SoD failure (e.g. one person holding both Subaccount Admin and Security Admin).
