A real incident looks like a BTP admin account being used to add a rogue destination or modify trust so that data or tokens flow to an external system, discovered during a BTP security configuration review.
IAM-XID-003.mitigation:
1. Export the list of admin role-collection holders (BTP cockpit > Security > Role Collections / Users) and review each against least privilege and documented business need.
2. Remove admin role collections from users who do not require them; assign the minimal collection needed (e.g. developer vs administrator) rather than blanket admin.
3. Separate duties within BTP administration — do not combine Subaccount Administrator with Security Administrator on one person; split platform, security and connectivity administration.
4. Restrict and monitor Connectivity/Destination Administrator, since those roles control the S/4 bridge (destinations, Cloud Connector, trust).
5. Bring BTP admin access under privileged-access governance and periodic review, and document a business justification for each remaining admin.
6. Verify by re-exporting role collections and confirming the admin population is reduced to the approved, segregated set.
7. Rollout caution: retain at least one break-glass global-account/subaccount admin (monitored) so you are not locked out, coordinate with the BTP platform team, and test role-collection changes in a non-production subaccount first.

IAM-REV-001.risk: This flags access-review (user access recertification) campaigns that are past their due date and not completed. Periodic access review is a foundational detective control: at defined intervals, managers or role owners re-attest that each user's access is still appropriate, catching privilege creep, orphaned access, SoD drift and leaver residue. When campaigns run overdue, that control is not operating on schedule — inappropriate access that a timely review would have removed stays live, and the organization loses its recurring checkpoint on the access model. The abuse angle is indirect but real: access that should have been revoked (a mover's old financial role, a dormant admin grant) persists because no one re-examined it, extending the window for misuse and SoD conflict. For SOX Section 404 and ITGC, timely periodic recertification is an explicitly tested control, and overdue campaigns are a common audit exception that can escalate if pervasive. Impact is both elevated standing risk and a documented control deficiency. A real incident looks like an auditor finding a quarter's recertification campaign months overdue, then discovering during their own sample that several users retained access they should have lost — turning a scheduling lapse into a finding about the effectiveness of the entire access-governance program.
IAM-REV-001.mitigation:
1. Escalate the overdue campaigns to the responsible managers and control owners immediately and set a firm completion deadline (e.g. within two weeks).
2. Complete the outstanding reviews — in GRC Access Control run/resume the User Access Review (UAR) or SoD Review campaign and ensure each item is actioned (keep/remove) by the reviewer.
3. Configure automated reminders and escalation in GRC ARM so approaching and missed deadlines notify reviewers and their managers.
4. Enforce the defined review cycle (e.g. 90 days) with scheduled campaign generation so a new cycle starts automatically.
5. Track completion metrics and report overdue campaigns to governance so accountability is visible.
6. Verify by re-extracting the campaign status and confirming the flagged campaigns are now completed and the next cycle is scheduled.
7. Rollout caution: ensure reviewers have adequate time and guidance so 'rubber-stamp' approvals do not replace genuine review; stagger campaigns to avoid overloading reviewers and undermining quality.

IAM-REV-002.risk: This flags access-review campaigns marked as completed/closed but whose recorded completion percentage is below 100%. A campaign closed with incomplete coverage gives false assurance: the control's output says 'access has been reviewed and validated' when in fact a subset of users, roles or line-items was never examined. Those unreviewed items retain whatever access they had, unvalidated, while the audit record claims otherwise — which is arguably worse than a visibly-overdue campaign because it hides the gap. The scenario: reviewers work through most items, the campaign is administratively closed to meet a deadline, and the skipped items (often the hardest or most privileged) are exactly where inappropriate access hides — a leaver's residual role or a SoD-conflicting grant slips through under the cover of a 'completed' review. Impact is a misstated control result: for SOX/ITGC this undermines the reliability of the recertification evidence and can be a more serious finding than lateness, because it calls the control's integrity into question. A real incident looks like an auditor reconciling the campaign's item count to its attestations, finding a shortfall behind a 'closed' status, and expanding testing on the assumption that other campaigns may be similarly incomplete.
IAM-REV-002.mitigation:
1. Reopen each affected campaign in GRC Access Control and identify the specific line-items/users that were not reviewed.
2. Complete the review of all outstanding items so coverage reaches 100%, with reviewers explicitly deciding keep/remove on each.
3. Investigate why items were skipped (reviewer unavailability, unassigned owners, data errors) and fix the root cause.
4. Enforce completion rules in GRC ARM/UAR so a campaign cannot be set to Completed/Closed while any item is unactioned.
5. Add a reconciliation check that compares attested items to total scope before allowing closure.
6. Verify by re-extracting campaign data and confirming closed campaigns show 100% completion.
7. Rollout caution: closing the enforcement gap may surface a backlog of genuinely incomplete campaigns — plan reviewer capacity, and communicate that 'closed' now means fully reviewed so deadlines are met by doing the work, not by early closure.

IAM-REV-003.risk: This flags access-review campaigns with no assigned reviewer or owner. A recertification campaign only produces a control outcome if a responsible person actually performs the review; an unowned campaign is inert — nobody is accountable for examining the access, so it will simply never be completed, silently defeating the periodic-review control for whatever population it covers. The weakness is organizational rather than technical, but its effect is a coverage hole: the users or roles in that campaign go unreviewed indefinitely while the program assumes they are covered. The scenario: a campaign is generated for a business area whose owner left or was never mapped, so it sits with no reviewer; access that should be re-attested (including privileged or SoD-relevant grants) is never looked at, and the gap is invisible unless someone checks for unassigned ownership. Impact is unreviewed standing access plus an ITGC/SOX gap — auditors expect every in-scope population to have a named accountable reviewer, and unowned campaigns indicate the review program is not comprehensively assigned. A real incident looks like a whole department's access going years without recertification because its review campaign was never assigned an owner, discovered when someone finally maps campaigns to responsible managers.
IAM-REV-003.mitigation:
1. Assign a named reviewer/owner to each unassigned campaign, based on business-process or role ownership (the business manager for user access, the role owner for role content).
2. Configure automatic reviewer determination in GRC ARM/UAR using the organizational structure (manager hierarchy) or role-owner mapping so campaigns are never generated without an owner.
3. Ensure role and user-manager ownership data is maintained (GRC role owner assignment; up-to-date manager relationships in the HR/identity source).
4. Add a pre-launch check that blocks or flags any campaign lacking a reviewer before it starts.
5. Complete the now-assigned reviews so the previously unowned population is finally recertified.
6. Verify by re-extracting campaign metadata and confirming every campaign has an assigned reviewer.
7. Rollout caution: validate that auto-assigned owners are correct (a stale manager mapping can route reviews to the wrong person), and coordinate with HR to keep the org structure current so future assignment stays accurate.

IAM-ROLE-001.risk: This flags custom (Z/Y) roles that have no description text. On its own a missing description is low severity, but it is a meaningful governance and maintainability weakness at scale. A role whose purpose is not documented cannot be effectively reviewed, recertified or safely modified: an access reviewer cannot judge whether the role is appropriate for a user if they cannot tell what it does, and a security administrator maintaining it risks adding or removing authorizations blindly. Undocumented roles tend to become 'mystery grants' that reviewers rubber-stamp because challenging them is too hard, which is exactly how inappropriate or over-broad access survives recertification. The scenario is slow decay rather than a direct attack: over years, undocumented custom roles proliferate, accumulate authorizations through copy-and-tweak maintenance, and nobody can confidently clean them up because their intent is lost — so privilege creep and SoD risk grow under a veil of poor documentation. Impact is degraded auditability and higher long-term risk of over-provisioning, and auditors do cite role documentation as part of access-governance quality. A real incident is an access review where dozens of Z-roles are approved without scrutiny because no one can explain them, later found to contain excessive or conflicting authorizations.
IAM-ROLE-001.mitigation:
1. Extract custom roles lacking descriptions from AGR_TEXTS / AGR_DEFINE (SE16N) or via SUIM to produce the work list.
2. In PFCG, open each custom role and add a meaningful description on the role definition, covering business process, intended user group and the approval/change reference.
3. Establish a role-documentation standard and enforce it in your role-build/change procedure so no new custom role is transported without a description.
4. Where GRC Business Role Management is used, capture the business rationale and owner alongside the technical role.
5. Prioritize privileged and financially significant roles first.
6. Verify by re-extracting AGR_TEXTS and confirming the custom-role description gap is closed for the prioritized set.
7. Rollout caution: description changes are low-risk but still travel by transport in a governed landscape — make them in DEV and transport through QA to PRD rather than editing directly in production, and align text with any existing naming/role-catalog conventions.

IAM-ROLE-002.risk: This flags custom (Z/Y) roles that have no designated owner. Role ownership is the linchpin of access governance: the owner is the accountable person who approves who may hold the role, attests to its content during recertification, and authorizes changes to its authorizations. An ownerless role has no one to include it in access-review campaigns and no one accountable for its scope, so it drifts — authorizations get added over time through copy-and-modify maintenance with no owner challenging the growth, and the role never gets re-attested because there is nobody to attest. The scenario is progressive over-provisioning: an ownerless Z-role gradually accumulates powerful or conflicting authorizations and is assigned to more users, all without governance, becoming a concentration of unmanaged access that no review ever validates. Impact is elevated privilege-creep and SoD risk plus a direct recertification gap — GRC User Access Reviews and role-content reviews depend on a named owner, so ownerless roles are effectively outside the control. For SOX/ITGC, undefined ownership and accountability is a recognized deficiency. A real incident looks like a widely-assigned custom role with no owner turning out to contain excessive authorizations that were never reviewed because no campaign could route to a responsible approver.
IAM-ROLE-002.mitigation:
1. Extract custom roles lacking an owner (from role metadata / GRC role master or CREATED_BY as a stopgap) to build the work list.
2. Assign a business owner to every custom role — ideally the business-process lead who uses it, not an IT administrator — using GRC Business Role Management role-ownership features or a maintained RACI/role catalog.
3. Make owner assignment a mandatory step in the role-creation and change procedure so no new custom role goes live without one.
4. Wire ownership into recertification so each owner attests to their roles' content and assignments each cycle (GRC UAR / role-content review).
5. Prioritize privileged and high-assignment roles for immediate owner assignment.
6. Verify by re-extracting role ownership data and confirming all custom roles now have a named owner.
7. Rollout caution: confirm assigned owners accept and understand the accountability (approvals, attestations) rather than assigning nominally; validate that owners map to current staff, since a stale owner is as ineffective as none.

IAM-ROLE-003.risk: This flags custom (Z/Y) roles whose menu/transaction count is zero — roles that contain no transactions. Empty roles are typically leftover artifacts: remnants of a role-redesign project, half-built roles that were never completed, or roles whose content was stripped but the shell was never deleted. On their own, empty roles grant little or nothing, so severity is low, but they degrade the cleanliness and trustworthiness of the role catalog. They inflate the number of roles that must be reviewed and maintained, confuse access reviewers and administrators, and occasionally mask risk — an 'empty' role may still carry authorization-tab objects even with no menu, or may later be re-populated and assigned without fresh review, quietly reintroducing access. The scenario is mostly hygiene and audit-noise: over time, dozens of empty Z-roles clutter the catalog, some remain assigned to users (implying assignments that convey no function and should be removed), and the sheer clutter makes it harder to reason about the access model. Impact is reduced maintainability and review efficiency, and a minor audit observation about catalog hygiene. A real incident is an access review bogged down by empty and near-empty roles, or an empty role being reused as a template and unexpectedly given content that bypasses the normal build/approval path.
IAM-ROLE-003.mitigation:
1. Identify empty custom roles (menu/transaction count zero) via SUIM or by inspecting AGR_1251/AGR_DEFINE for roles with no menu entries.
2. For each, confirm in PFCG that the role truly has no menu and check the Authorizations tab in case objects exist without menu transactions.
3. Verify no users are assigned (AGR_USERS via SE16N); if users are assigned to a genuinely empty role, remove those pointless assignments (SU10).
4. Delete confirmed-empty, unused roles in PFCG following your change/transport process.
5. Add a step to your role-redesign/decommission procedure so retired roles are deleted, not just emptied.
6. Verify by re-extracting the catalog and confirming the empty roles are gone and no assignments were left dangling.
7. Rollout caution: ensure a role is truly obsolete before deletion (some 'empty' roles are deliberate org-level or value-role containers, or are referenced by composite roles); make changes in DEV and transport through QA, and keep the transport as a rollback point.

IAM-ORPH-001.risk: This flags user-role assignments that reference roles which no longer exist in the role catalog (present in AGR_USERS but not in AGR_DEFINE/role_details). These orphaned assignments are the residue of roles that were deleted without cleaning up the user linkages. While a truly deleted role usually conveys no live authorizations, orphaned assignments corrupt the integrity of the access model and create several concrete risks. First, they make 'who has what' unreliable — reporting, SoD analysis and access reviews operate on a user-role table that references phantom roles, undermining audit assertions. Second, they create a reactivation hazard: if a role name is later recreated (e.g. a role with the same technical name is rebuilt for a different purpose), every user still carrying the orphaned assignment silently inherits the new role's authorizations without any provisioning or approval — an unintended, unreviewed grant. Third, they indicate a broken role-deletion process, which itself is a control weakness. Impact is data-integrity and potential unauthorized-access risk, plus audit exceptions on access-baseline accuracy. A real incident looks like a decommissioned role name being reused months later, instantly granting the new (possibly privileged) access to a set of users who were never provisioned for it, discovered only when someone notices unexpected access tied to a recreated role.
IAM-ORPH-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the orphaned assignments by reconciling AGR_USERS (SE16N) against AGR_DEFINE — assignments whose AGR_NAME has no matching role definition.
2. Remove the orphaned assignments: use SU10 mass maintenance or targeted SU01 changes to strip the non-existent roles from affected users.
3. Run PFUD (user master comparison, background report RHAUTUPD_NEW) so user buffers/profiles are reconciled after cleanup.
4. Run PRGN_COMPRESS_TIMES to compress and tidy AGR_USERS, reviewing its log.
5. Fix the root cause: adopt a role-decommission procedure that removes all user assignments before or as part of deleting the role, so orphans are not created again.
6. Verify by re-reconciling AGR_USERS to AGR_DEFINE and confirming no assignment references a missing role.
7. Rollout caution: before deleting assignments, confirm the role is genuinely deleted (not merely missing from the extract); test the mass change in QA, take a backup of AGR_USERS, and coordinate with role owners in case a role is due to be recreated intentionally.

IAM-USRGRP-001.risk: This flags active (unlocked) users who sit in the default or an unassigned user group (blank, 000, DEFAULT or SUPER) in the CLASS field of USR02. The user group ('User Group for Authorization Check') is more than a label: it is an administrative-segmentation control enforced through authorization object S_USER_GRP, which governs who may maintain which users. When users are left in a default/blank group, that control layer is unusable for them — any administrator with broad S_USER_GRP can maintain them, and it becomes impossible to delegate or restrict user administration by segment (e.g. letting a regional admin manage only their region's users, or ring-fencing highly privileged accounts). Concentrating users in a catch-all group therefore weakens both least-privilege delegation and the protection of sensitive accounts. The scenario: because sensitive or privileged accounts are not segregated into a protected user group, a helpdesk-level administrator whose S_USER_GRP is not tightly scoped can reset passwords or modify high-value accounts (including admin IDs) they should not be able to touch, enabling privilege escalation or account takeover. Impact is weakened administrative segregation and a CIS SAP benchmark gap. A real incident looks like a lower-tier administrator resetting a privileged account's password because no user-group boundary existed to stop them, then using it to escalate.
IAM-USRGRP-001.mitigation:
1. Extract users with blank/default user groups from USR02 (CLASS field, via SE16N or SUIM RSUSR002 by user group) and prioritize privileged accounts.
2. Define a meaningful user-group scheme (by region, function, or sensitivity — e.g. a protected group for admin/technical IDs) in transaction SUGR.
3. Assign users to the appropriate group via SU01 (Logon Data tab, 'User Group for Authorization Check') or mass via SU10.
4. Scope administrators' S_USER_GRP authorization to only the groups they should manage, so user administration is properly segmented and privileged accounts are protected.
5. Ensure highly privileged/technical accounts sit in a restricted group only senior admins can maintain.
6. Verify by re-extracting USR02 and confirming active users carry meaningful groups and that a test lower-tier admin can no longer maintain a protected account.
7. Rollout caution: changing user groups affects who can administer whom — coordinate with all user-administration teams, test the S_USER_GRP scoping in QA, and stage changes so you do not accidentally lock every admin out of a group of users.

IAM-REF-001.risk: This flags dialog users (type A) that are assigned as reference users for other accounts. A reference user is an authorization-inheritance mechanism: any user pointing to it 'inherits' all of the reference user's authorizations. SAP intends reference users to be dedicated type L (Reference) accounts that cannot log on, precisely so that the inherited authorization set is a controlled, non-interactive object. Using a live dialog account as a reference user is dangerous because that account is a real, logon-capable identity whose authorizations change as its owner's job changes — and every such change silently propagates to all dependent accounts. The scenario: a dialog user who happens to be someone's active account is used as a reference for a group of users; when that person is granted a new powerful role (or their access is broadened for a project), every dependent account instantly inherits the same elevated authorizations with no provisioning event, approval or review — a stealthy mass privilege escalation. Worse, if the dialog account is compromised, the blast radius includes all inheriting users. Impact is uncontrolled, invisible authorization propagation, broken auditability of 'who has what', and a real escalation/breach vector. A real incident looks like a routine role addition to one employee unexpectedly granting sensitive access to dozens of other users because that employee's dialog account was serving as a reference user, uncovered during an authorization-inheritance review.
IAM-REF-001.mitigation:
1. Identify the misused reference relationships from USR02 (REF_USER / USTYP) and list every dependent account inheriting from each dialog reference user.
2. Create dedicated type L (Reference) users in SU01 to hold the intended inherited authorizations, and repoint the dependent accounts (SU01 Roles tab, or mass via SU10) to the new reference users.
3. Remove the reference-user linkage from the live dialog accounts so no account inherits from a logon-capable identity.
4. Enforce the standard: set the customizing switch REF_USER_CHECK to 'E' in table PRGN_CUST so only users of type Reference can be assigned as reference users; if reference users are not used at all, deactivate the field per SAP Note 330067.
5. Audit the authorizations inherited by the dependent accounts to confirm they are still appropriate after the repoint.
6. Verify by re-extracting USR02 and confirming no dialog user is assigned as a reference user and that REF_USER_CHECK=E is active.
7. Rollout caution: repointing changes effective authorizations for dependent users — test in QA, communicate with affected user owners, and run user master comparison (PFUD) so buffers reflect the new inheritance before enforcing the check in production.

IAM-PRIV-001.risk: This flags users who hold two or more of the top security-administration authorization objects at full ('*') scope — S_USER_AGR (role administration via PFCG), S_USER_GRP (user administration via SU01) and S_USER_PRO (profile assignment) — which together constitute a self-service privilege-escalation capability and is rated critical. A user with, say, S_USER_AGR=* and S_USER_GRP=* can build a role in PFCG containing any authorization (including SAP_ALL-equivalent objects) and then assign it to themselves or to a newly created account, with no independent approval of either the role content or the assignment. Adding S_USER_PRO=* lets them attach powerful profiles directly, bypassing even role construction. This combination dissolves the authorization control model: every other restriction in the system is negotiable because this user can grant around it. The step-by-step abuse: create or edit a role/profile granting full access, assign it to self or a hidden back-door user, perform the intended fraud/data-theft/sabotage, then strip the access and delete the account to erase the trail. Impact is catastrophic — undetectable superuser fraud, mass data exfiltration, or destructive action — and it is a direct SAP Security Baseline and SOX ITGC critical failure because it defeats segregation of security-administration duties. A real incident looks like a security or Basis administrator quietly self-granting a powerful role, executing fraudulent financial changes, and reverting the grant, exposed only through Security Audit Log and change-document forensics.
IAM-PRIV-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm each flagged user's authorizations in SUIM (RSUSR002 for S_USER_AGR, S_USER_GRP, S_USER_PRO) and run the critical-combination report RSUSR008_009_NEW to corroborate the toxic combinations.
2. Split security administration across different individuals: a role administrator (S_USER_AGR, no user-admin), a user administrator (S_USER_GRP scoped to groups, no role-admin), and remove direct profile-assignment power (S_USER_PRO) from all but a tightly controlled exception.
3. In PFCG, restrict these objects away from '*' — scope S_USER_AGR to a role namespace, S_USER_GRP to specific user groups — and eliminate S_USER_PRO where profiles are managed via roles; regenerate and run PFUD.
4. Route all role changes and user provisioning through GRC Access Request Management with four-eyes approval so no one self-provisions.
5. Enable change documents and the Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG / SM20) for PFCG, SU01 and profile changes, and monitor them.
6. Verify by re-running RSUSR008_009_NEW and confirming no single user retains two or more of these objects at full scope.
7. Rollout caution: this touches the administrators who run the system — plan a maintenance window, retain one monitored break-glass admin so you are not locked out, test the split roles in QA, and document the segregation for audit.

PARAM-000.risk: This finding fires when the scanner received no profile-parameter export at all, so the entire security-parameter baseline (password policy, lockout, audit logging, RFC, gateway, TLS) could not be evaluated. This is a control-visibility gap rather than a single misconfiguration, but it is dangerous because 'no evidence' very often correlates with 'never hardened'. Several SAP kernel defaults are insecure out of the box: login/password_expiration_time defaults to 0 (passwords never expire), login/min_password_lng defaults to 6, and rsau/enable defaults to 0 (Security Audit Log off). If the parameters were never exported, they may also never have been reviewed, and any of these defaults could be live in production. In an S/4HANA RISE / ECS engagement the customer retains ownership of these application-layer parameters even though SAP operates the infrastructure, so the absence of an export means the customer-side hardening obligation is unverified. From an attacker's perspective, anyone who obtains even a low-privileged dialog logon can run RSPARAM/RSPFPAR themselves and immediately learn which controls are weak (short passwords, no lockout, no audit trail) and pivot accordingly. From a compliance perspective an auditor cannot attest to ITGC password, logging or access controls, which directly threatens SOX ITGC sign-off, ISO 27001 A.8/A.12 evidence, and GDPR Art. 32 'appropriate technical measures'. A real incident would surface post-breach when forensics finds no Security Audit Log and no documented baseline, making root-cause and scope analysis impossible.
PARAM-000.mitigation:
1. Log on to each ABAP system and client with a Basis-authorized user (S_RZL_ADM / S_ADMI_FCD).
2. Run report RSPARAM via SA38 or SE38 to list every active parameter with its current, default and profile value; RSPFPAR gives an alternative flat list.
3. Alternatively open RZ11 for single-parameter display, or RZ10 -> Utilities to view/export the DEFAULT and instance profiles.
4. Export the output to CSV/spreadsheet and feed it back into this scanner so the baseline can actually be evaluated.
5. Repeat the export per application-server instance, because parameters can differ between the DEFAULT profile and individual instance profiles.
6. In RISE/ECS, if you do not have RZ10/RZ11 yourself, raise a request to the SAP-managed operations team for a full profile-parameter export.
7. Verify completeness by confirming the login/*, rsau/*, gw/*, rfc/*, icm/* and ssl/* families are all represented in the export.
8. Store the signed export as audit evidence for SOX/ISO and attach it to the assessment.
9. This step is read-only reporting only - no parameter change and no kernel restart is required, so it can be executed in production safely.
10. Re-run the assessment and confirm PARAM-000 no longer fires.

PARAM-MISSING.risk: This finding lists CRITICAL/HIGH baseline parameters that were entirely absent from the export. Missing does not mean safe: when a parameter is not set in any profile the system silently uses the kernel default, and for many security parameters that default is weak or off. Concretely, if login/no_automatic_user_sapstar, rsau/enable, gw/sec_info or gw/reg_info are absent, the system may be exposed to the SAP* hard-coded backdoor, may have no security audit trail, and may have an unrestricted RFC gateway that permits remote registration and start of external programs - each of which is independently exploitable. The danger is compounded because operators frequently assume 'we didn't change it, so it's fine', when in fact the SAP-delivered default is the insecure state. In a RISE/ECS context these are precisely the application-layer controls the customer still owns, so leaving them at default is a customer-side gap that SAP's infrastructure controls do not cover. Attack scenario: an adversary who reaches the gateway port (3300/33NN) of a system with no reg_info/sec_info can register a malicious RFC server or start OS-level programs, achieving remote code execution as the <sid>adm user; separately, if no_automatic_user_sapstar is unset on an older release, deleting the SAP* master record re-activates SAP* with the default password PASS. Business impact spans full system compromise, undetectable fraud (no audit log), and audit findings for missing ITGC. A real incident would look like unexplained external program starts in SMGW logs, or an SAP* logon from an unexpected terminal, with no Security Audit Log to reconstruct events.
PARAM-MISSING.mitigation:
1. Take the affected-items list from this finding as the work list of parameters to explicitly set.
2. In RZ10 open the DEFAULT profile (system-wide) or the relevant instance profile in extended maintenance.
3. For each missing parameter, add it with the secure target value: login/no_automatic_user_sapstar=1, rsau/enable=1 (or configure via RSAU_CONFIG on 7.50+), gw/sec_info and gw/reg_info pointing to hardened ACL files, gw/reg_no_conn_info per SAP Note 1444282, and the password/lockout minimums from the other findings.
4. Confirm each parameter's exact meaning and allowed values first in RZ11 (it shows official documentation and whether the parameter is dynamically switchable).
5. Save and activate the profile; schedule the required instance restart during a maintenance window for static parameters (RZ11 flags which are dynamic).
6. For gateway ACL parameters, after placing the files reload them via SMGW -> Goto -> Expert Functions -> Reread (no restart needed).
7. Verify with report RSPARAM after restart that every previously-missing parameter now shows an explicit, compliant value rather than 'default'.
8. Roll out in DEV/QA first and coordinate with interface owners, because gateway and RFC hardening can break registered external programs if ACLs are wrong.
9. In RISE/ECS raise a change request to the SAP operations team for any parameter you cannot maintain directly.
10. Re-export parameters and re-run the scanner to confirm PARAM-MISSING is cleared.

PARAM-login/min_password_lng.risk: login/min_password_lng sets the minimum length of ABAP dialog/logon passwords; the SAP kernel default is only 6 characters (allowed range 3-40). A short minimum dramatically shrinks the password keyspace and makes both online guessing and, more importantly, offline cracking of captured password hashes fast and cheap. SAP historically stored password hashes in table USR02 (fields BCODE/PASSCODE and the newer PWDSALTEDHASH); the legacy hash formats are weak, and even the salted iSSHA-1 hash succumbs quickly to GPU cracking when the underlying password is only 6-8 characters. An attacker who obtains a USR02 dump - via a database read, an over-privileged S_TABU_DIS/S_TABU_NAM authorization, a backup, or SQL injection in a custom report - can crack short passwords to plaintext and then log on interactively as those users, including privileged ones. In S/4HANA RISE the same risk applies to any password-based dialog/communication users that remain (SSO/SAML reduces but rarely eliminates them). Business impact: account takeover of Finance/Basis users, fraudulent postings, data exfiltration, and privilege escalation, all appearing as 'legitimate' logons. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP, SAP Security Baseline password strength expectations, and SOX/ISO password-strength controls. A real incident looks like a valid-credential logon from an unusual source shortly after a database or backup exposure, with the compromised account traced back to a trivially crackable short password.
PARAM-login/min_password_lng.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current value in RZ11 for login/min_password_lng and review related parameters login/password_downwards_compatibility and login/password_hash_algorithm.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/min_password_lng to at least 8, with 10-12 recommended per SAP Security Baseline and DSAG guidance.
3. Also harden the password-composition parameters (login/min_password_digits, _letters, _specials) so length increases do not push users to predictable patterns.
4. Set login/password_downwards_compatibility = 0 once no legacy/older-release RFC partners require the old hash, to eliminate the weak BCODE hash from USR02.
5. Save and activate the profile; the login/* parameters are dynamically switchable, but persist them in the profile and restart the instance during a maintenance window for durability.
6. Force adoption by setting login/password_compliance_to_current_policy = 1 so existing non-compliant passwords must be changed at next logon.
7. Verify with RSPARAM that the active value is >= 8 and with RZ11 that it is applied on every instance.
8. Restrict read access to USR02 by reviewing S_TABU_DIS/S_TABU_NAM assignments (SUIM) so hashes cannot be dumped.
9. Roll out first in DEV/QA and notify the service desk of increased password-reset volume before production.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm PARAM-login/min_password_lng is compliant.

PARAM-login/min_password_digits.risk: login/min_password_digits enforces the minimum number of numeric characters a new password must contain; the default is 0, meaning purely alphabetic (often dictionary-word) passwords are permitted. Without a digit requirement, users overwhelmingly choose predictable words, names or seasonal patterns, which fall immediately to dictionary and rule-based cracking and to targeted online guessing. Combined with a short min length this collapses effective password entropy far below the nominal length. In an S/4HANA landscape any remaining password-based dialog, service or communication users become the weak link: an attacker running a dictionary attack against the logon (or cracking a USR02 hash dump offline) recovers such passwords in seconds. The abuse path is straightforward - enumerate valid user IDs (via SU01 error differences, RFC, or leaked lists), spray common words, and log on as a legitimate user. Because the logon is credential-valid it bypasses many detective controls unless the Security Audit Log is active. Business impact includes account takeover, segregation-of-duties bypass, and fraudulent transactions attributable to the victim. Compliance impact: fails CIS SAP password-complexity benchmarks and the SAP Security Baseline. A real incident would appear as a successful logon following a burst of failed attempts, tied to an account whose password was a single dictionary word with no digit.
PARAM-login/min_password_digits.mitigation:
1. Check the current value in RZ11 for login/min_password_digits.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/min_password_digits >= 1 (a value of 1-2 is typical; do not over-constrain, which pushes users to predictable substitutions).
3. Harden the companion parameters in the same change: login/min_password_letters >= 1 and login/min_password_specials >= 1, and ensure login/min_password_lng >= 8.
4. Optionally maintain a prohibited-password list in table USR40 (via SM30) to block trivial patterns such as the company name.
5. Save and activate the profile; login/* parameters are dynamic but persist them in the profile for durability.
6. Set login/password_compliance_to_current_policy = 1 so existing weak passwords are forced to comply on next change.
7. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and per-instance with RZ11.
8. Communicate the new complexity rule to end users and the service desk ahead of rollout.
9. Test in DEV/QA first to confirm no automated/interface logons rely on non-compliant fixed passwords.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the parameter is compliant.

PARAM-login/min_password_letters.risk: login/min_password_letters enforces the minimum number of alphabetic characters in a new password; the default is 0. When set to 0 the policy permits all-numeric passwords such as PIN-style strings, which have an extremely small keyspace (an 8-digit numeric password has only 10^8 combinations and is brute-forced almost instantly, online or offline). All-numeric or near-numeric passwords are also common user choices (dates of birth, employee numbers, phone fragments) that are guessable with minimal target knowledge. In an S/4HANA context this weakens every password-based account, and the risk multiplies for technical/communication users whose passwords are rarely rotated. The attack pattern is trivial: an adversary who obtains a USR02 hash dump cracks numeric passwords near-instantly, or performs online guessing against known user IDs; success yields a valid interactive or RFC logon under the victim's identity. Because the credential is genuine, the intrusion masquerades as normal activity and evades controls that rely on anomalous credentials. Business impact: identity takeover, fraudulent financial postings, and lateral movement using the compromised user's authorizations. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP and SAP Security Baseline complexity requirements and general ITGC password-strength expectations. A real incident would present as a valid logon whose account password turned out to be an easily guessed numeric string, with no complexity control having blocked it.
PARAM-login/min_password_letters.mitigation:
1. Review the current value in RZ11 for login/min_password_letters.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/min_password_letters >= 1 (typically 1).
3. Apply the full complexity set together: login/min_password_digits >= 1, login/min_password_specials >= 1, login/min_password_lng >= 8.
4. Consider maintaining forbidden trivial passwords in table USR40 (SM30) to block numeric patterns like dates.
5. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; the login/* parameters are dynamically switchable but keep them in the profile so they survive restarts.
6. Enforce on legacy passwords via login/password_compliance_to_current_policy = 1.
7. Verify the active value using RSPARAM and confirm consistency across instances with RZ11.
8. Notify users/service desk of the composition change before enforcing.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA to ensure no interface or batch user depends on a non-compliant password.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm compliance.

PARAM-login/min_password_specials.risk: login/min_password_specials sets the minimum number of special (non-alphanumeric) characters required in a password; the default is 0. Requiring at least one special character meaningfully expands the character set an attacker must brute-force and defeats simple dictionary and mask attacks that assume alphanumeric-only passwords. With the value at 0, users default to word-plus-number patterns that modern cracking rules exhaust rapidly, so despite a nominal length the real strength is low. In S/4HANA landscapes this matters most for the residual password-based dialog, communication and RFC users that persist even after SSO adoption; those are exactly the accounts adversaries target for reuse and lateral movement. Concrete abuse: after obtaining a USR02 hash export (via excessive table-read authorizations, a database/backup exposure, or SQL injection in custom ABAP), the attacker runs rule-based cracking; passwords lacking special characters fall first, yielding plaintext credentials for direct logon. The resulting access is credential-valid and blends into normal traffic. Business impact includes account takeover, SoD circumvention, and fraudulent or unauthorized transactions performed under a legitimate identity. Compliance impact: non-conformance with CIS SAP and SAP Security Baseline complexity guidance and typical ITGC standards. A real incident would show a legitimate-looking logon whose credential was later found to be a simple word-plus-digit password with no special character requirement in force.
PARAM-login/min_password_specials.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current value in RZ11 for login/min_password_specials.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/min_password_specials >= 1.
3. Apply as part of the complete complexity policy (digits >= 1, letters >= 1, length >= 8) so the controls reinforce rather than push users to predictable substitutions.
4. Optionally block trivial/predictable passwords via table USR40 (SM30).
5. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; login/* parameters are dynamic but persist them in the profile.
6. Force legacy passwords to comply with login/password_compliance_to_current_policy = 1.
7. Verify with RSPARAM (active value) and RZ11 (per instance).
8. Warn users and the service desk that new passwords must contain a special character before enforcing.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and check that no automated logon uses a fixed non-compliant password.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the parameter is compliant.

PARAM-login/password_expiration_time.risk: login/password_expiration_time defines, in days, how long a password remains valid before the user must change it; the SAP default is 0, which means passwords never expire. When passwords never expire, a single credential compromise is effectively permanent: a password captured months ago from a phishing lure, a keylogger, a reused-credential breach, or an old USR02 hash dump remains valid indefinitely, and terminated employees or rotated contractors may retain working credentials well past their access-review date. In S/4HANA RISE landscapes this is most acute for password-based dialog and communication users that are not integrated with SSO/central IdP-driven rotation. Attack scenario: an adversary obtains a credential once (breach dump, malware, or hash crack), and because it never expires, they can return at any time to log on as that user, exfiltrate data or post transactions, with the access indistinguishable from normal use. Long-lived static passwords on technical/RFC users are especially prized for durable persistence. Business impact: prolonged undetected access, fraud, data breach, and failure of the periodic-credential-rotation control that auditors expect. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP benchmark 1.1.6, the SAP Security Baseline, and SOX/ISO password-lifecycle controls. A real incident would look like a dormant account reactivating months after an employee left, or credentials from an old breach still granting access.
PARAM-login/password_expiration_time.mitigation:
1. Check the current value in RZ11 for login/password_expiration_time.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/password_expiration_time to 90 or fewer days (per policy; many organisations use 60-90).
3. Pair this with login/password_max_idle_productive to invalidate productive passwords that go unused, and login/min_password_diff to force meaningful changes.
4. For technical/RFC/communication users where interactive rotation is impractical, prefer SNC/certificate-based authentication or move them to SSO rather than exempting them from expiry.
5. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; login/* parameters are dynamic but keep them in the profile so they persist across restarts.
6. Communicate the change well ahead of time - a large first-wave password-reset spike is expected once expiry is enabled.
7. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and confirm on all instances via RZ11.
8. Identify accounts that would break on rotation (interface users) beforehand via SUIM and plan alternative authentication for them.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and coordinate with interface owners before production enforcement.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm compliance.

PARAM-login/password_max_idle_initial.risk: login/password_max_idle_initial controls how many days an initial (administrator-set or reset) password remains usable if it is never used to log on; after that window the initial password becomes invalid and must be reset again. The default is effectively unbounded/large, which means a password an administrator set during user creation or a help-desk reset can sit unused and valid for a long time. This is dangerous because initial passwords are the weakest link in the lifecycle: they are frequently a predictable pattern (a standard 'welcome' string, the SID, or a formula the help desk reuses), they are transmitted over email/tickets, and they are known to at least one other person. If such an initial password is not consumed and expired quickly, an attacker who learns it - by guessing the help-desk convention, reading a ticketing system, or intercepting the notification - can activate a dormant or newly-created account before the legitimate user ever logs on. In S/4HANA onboarding at scale, many freshly created accounts may sit with initial passwords, presenting a broad set of easily-activated identities. Business impact: account takeover of new or reset users, including potentially privileged ones, and creation of a persistence foothold. Compliance impact: fails CIS SAP 1.1.7 and SAP Security Baseline initial-password guidance. A real incident would look like a never-onboarded or long-idle account being logged into using its original help-desk password.
PARAM-login/password_max_idle_initial.mitigation:
1. Review the current value in RZ11 for login/password_max_idle_initial.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/password_max_idle_initial to a short window, 14 days or fewer (7 or less is stronger), so unused initial passwords expire quickly.
3. Also set login/password_max_idle_productive to a sensible limit for productive passwords that go unused.
4. Standardise on strong, unique initial passwords (avoid a fixed help-desk pattern) and require change-on-first-logon, which SAP enforces for admin-set passwords by default.
5. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; these login/* parameters are dynamically switchable but keep them in the profile.
6. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and per-instance with RZ11.
7. Use SUIM/RSUSR200 to identify existing users still on an initial password and follow up on stale ones.
8. Coordinate with the onboarding/service-desk team so newly created accounts are activated within the shortened window.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA to confirm the shortened window does not disrupt batch user provisioning.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm compliance.

PARAM-login/password_history_size.risk: login/password_history_size defines how many previous passwords the system remembers and forbids a user from reusing. When the history is too small (or 0), users cycle back to a handful of familiar passwords - or immediately re-set the same password after a forced expiry - which defeats the purpose of password rotation entirely. The security value of expiry (login/password_expiration_time) depends on this control: without a meaningful history, a mandatory 90-day change becomes theatre because the user simply reverts to the old, possibly already-compromised, password. In an S/4HANA environment this means a credential leaked in a prior breach or cracked from an old hash dump can be reinstated by the user at the next change, restoring the attacker's access. It also enables the well-known trick of changing the password N times rapidly to flush the history and return to a favourite. Attack scenario: an adversary holding a previously valid credential waits for rotation and finds the same password back in use because history was too shallow to block it. Business impact: rotation controls provide false assurance, compromised credentials persist, and auditors may rely on an ineffective control. Compliance impact: weakens conformance with CIS SAP 1.1.8 and the SAP Security Baseline password-reuse expectation. A real incident would look like a compromised password reappearing on an account shortly after a mandated change, granting renewed access. This is rated lower severity but materially undermines the other password controls.
PARAM-login/password_history_size.mitigation:
1. Check the current value in RZ11 for login/password_history_size.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/password_history_size to at least 5 (values of 5-24 are common; higher is stronger).
3. To close the rapid-cycling loophole, set login/password_change_waittime to a small positive value (e.g. 1 day) so users cannot change the password many times in a row to flush history.
4. Ensure login/min_password_diff > 0 so a new password must differ meaningfully from the old one.
5. Keep this aligned with login/password_expiration_time so rotation and reuse-prevention reinforce each other.
6. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; login/* parameters are dynamic but persist them in the profile.
7. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and confirm across instances via RZ11.
8. Communicate to users that recent passwords cannot be reused.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA before enforcing in production.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the parameter is compliant.

PARAM-login/fails_to_session_end.risk: login/fails_to_session_end determines how many consecutive incorrect logon attempts are tolerated within a single session before the SAP GUI/logon session is terminated (the connection is dropped). The default is 3. If this value is set too high (or the control is effectively disabled), an attacker can keep hammering credentials within one session without the session being torn down, materially accelerating online password-guessing and credential-spraying against a target user ID. While login/fails_to_user_lock is the stronger sibling control (it locks the account), fails_to_session_end is the first-line brake that forces the attacker to re-establish sessions and slows automated tooling. In an internet-adjacent or broadly-reachable S/4HANA / Fiori landscape, a permissive value lets an adversary iterate through dictionaries against known accounts far more efficiently. Attack scenario: an attacker scripts logon attempts against a privileged user ID; with a high fails-to-session-end the tooling never gets interrupted and can exhaust a wordlist quickly, especially when combined with weak password complexity. Business impact: increased likelihood of successful brute-force account takeover, which then leads to fraud, data exfiltration or privilege escalation. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP 1.2.1 and SAP Security Baseline logon-attempt limits. A real incident would present as a burst of failed logons that were never throttled, culminating in a successful logon on the same user ID.
PARAM-login/fails_to_session_end.mitigation:
1. Review the current value in RZ11 for login/fails_to_session_end.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/fails_to_session_end to 3 or fewer.
3. Set the stronger companion control login/fails_to_user_lock to 5 or fewer so the account is actually locked, not just the session ended.
4. Set login/failed_user_auto_unlock = 0 so a locked account stays locked until an administrator unlocks it (prevents automatic midnight unlock that resets the attacker's budget).
5. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; login/* parameters are dynamically switchable but keep them in the profile for durability.
6. Ensure the Security Audit Log (rsau/enable) captures failed logons so brute-force patterns are detectable in SM20/RSAU_READ_LOG.
7. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and confirm across all instances via RZ11.
8. Front-end Fiori/web logons should additionally be protected at the Web Dispatcher/WAF layer against distributed guessing.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and confirm no legitimate automated logon repeatedly mistypes credentials before enforcing.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm compliance.

PARAM-login/fails_to_user_lock.risk: login/fails_to_user_lock sets the number of consecutive failed logon attempts after which SAP locks the user account; the default is 5. This is the primary defence against online password brute-forcing and credential spraying. If the value is too high or the lockout is weakened, an attacker can make a large number of guesses against a user ID before being stopped, and with weak password complexity that is often enough to succeed. A related pitfall is login/failed_user_auto_unlock = 1, which automatically unlocks accounts at midnight, effectively giving the attacker a fresh budget of attempts every day and allowing slow-and-low guessing to eventually break in without ever tripping a permanent lock. In an S/4HANA landscape reachable by many users (and via RFC/web front-ends), a permissive lockout policy is one of the most directly exploitable weaknesses. Attack scenario: an adversary enumerates valid user IDs and sprays common passwords across them, staying under the per-account limit, or targets one privileged ID with a dictionary while relying on nightly auto-unlock. Success yields a valid logon and full use of the victim's authorizations. Business impact: account takeover leading to fraud, unauthorized data access, and lateral movement. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP 1.2.2 and the SAP Security Baseline. A real incident would look like repeated failed logons across many accounts (spray) or a single account, followed by a successful authentication.
PARAM-login/fails_to_user_lock.mitigation:
1. Check the current value in RZ11 for login/fails_to_user_lock.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/fails_to_user_lock to 5 or fewer.
3. Set login/failed_user_auto_unlock = 0 so locked accounts remain locked until an administrator unlocks them via SU01.
4. Keep login/fails_to_session_end at 3 or fewer as the complementary session-drop control.
5. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; login/* parameters are dynamic but persist them in the profile.
6. Enable the Security Audit Log (rsau/enable=1) and create SM20/RSAU_READ_LOG monitoring for repeated failed logons and lock events, ideally forwarded to the SIEM.
7. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and confirm on every instance via RZ11.
8. Establish a help-desk process for verified unlock requests so the stricter lockout does not create denial-of-service for legitimate users, and monitor for lockout-based DoS against specific IDs.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and communicate the tighter lockout to users before production enforcement.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm compliance.

PARAM-login/no_automatic_user_sapstar.risk: login/no_automatic_user_sapstar controls SAP's hard-coded emergency super-user SAP*. When this parameter is 0 (or unset on older releases), the kernel's built-in SAP* becomes usable: if the SAP* user master record is deleted from a client, the system falls back to the hard-coded SAP* which has the well-known default password PASS and, critically, is a member of no authorization checks - it is an unrestricted super-user with SAP_ALL-equivalent power and is exempt from normal authorization enforcement. This is one of the most classic and severe SAP backdoors. Attack scenario: an attacker (or malicious insider) with the ability to delete a user master record in any client - or who simply targets a client where SAP* has been deleted or never properly secured - logs on as SAP* with password PASS and obtains complete, unrestricted control of that client, including creating privileged users, reading all data, and posting any transaction. Even without deletion, a value of 0 signals the safety net is off. In S/4HANA RISE this is a customer-owned parameter, and because it must be set in the DEFAULT profile to apply to all clients and instances, a gap here exposes every client. Business impact: total system and data compromise, undetectable fraud, and full loss of segregation of duties. Compliance impact: violates SAP Note 68048, CIS SAP 1.2.5, and any ITGC baseline. A real incident would look like a SAP* logon from an unexpected terminal followed by mass privilege changes. This is correctly rated CRITICAL.
PARAM-login/no_automatic_user_sapstar.mitigation:
1. Verify the current value in RZ11 for login/no_automatic_user_sapstar; the secure value is 1 (automatic SAP* disabled).
2. In RZ10 set login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = 1 in the DEFAULT profile so it applies to ALL clients and instances (per SAP Note 68048).
3. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile and restart the instances during a maintenance window so it takes effect system-wide.
4. Independently, ensure a real SAP* master record exists in every client (including 000, 001, and any custom clients), is assigned to a locked-down group, has a strong unique password, is locked in SU01, and holds no wide profiles - deleting SAP* is not the fix.
5. Change the password of SAP* and DDIC in all clients away from any default; verify with report RSUSR003 that no standard user still has a default password.
6. Confirm SAP* is excluded from being an emergency backdoor by keeping the parameter at 1 even though a real SAP* exists.
7. Verify with RSPARAM that the active value is 1 on every instance and re-check after the restart.
8. Monitor SAP* logons via the Security Audit Log (SM20/RSAU_READ_LOG).
9. Roll out via DEV/QA first, but treat production remediation as high priority given the CRITICAL rating.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the parameter is compliant.

PARAM-login/disable_multi_gui_login.risk: login/disable_multi_gui_login controls whether a single user ID may hold multiple simultaneous SAP GUI dialog sessions. When multiple concurrent logons are allowed (value 0), it becomes difficult to detect and prevent credential sharing and stolen-credential reuse: a legitimate user can be actively working while an attacker who has obtained the same credentials logs on in parallel, and both sessions appear valid. This undermines accountability and non-repudiation, complicates SoD enforcement (one physical person effectively multiplying their presence), and can mask an intruder's activity behind an active legitimate session. It also has licensing implications, but the security concern is the loss of a one-user-one-session invariant that helps surface anomalous concurrent access. In S/4HANA landscapes where a compromised dialog credential is the typical entry point, disallowing concurrent GUI sessions raises the chance that a parallel malicious logon is noticed or blocked. Attack scenario: an attacker phishes a controller's credentials and logs on to post fraudulent journal entries while the controller is simultaneously logged on for normal work, with nothing preventing the second session. Business impact: harder incident detection, weakened accountability, potential fraud under a shared identity. Compliance impact: relates to CIS SAP 1.2.6 and general access-accountability expectations. This is a lower-severity, defence-in-depth control rather than a critical exposure, and a legitimate exception list is often required for administrators.
PARAM-login/disable_multi_gui_login.mitigation:
1. Review the current value in RZ11 for login/disable_multi_gui_login.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and set login/disable_multi_gui_login = 1 to prevent multiple concurrent GUI sessions per user.
3. Where specific accounts genuinely need multiple sessions (certain admins or technical monitors), list them in login/multi_login_users as a controlled, documented exception rather than disabling the whole control.
4. Save and activate the DEFAULT profile; the login/* parameters are dynamically switchable but persist them in the profile.
5. Verify the active value with RSPARAM and per-instance via RZ11, and confirm the exception list contents.
6. Use SM04/AL08 to review current active sessions and identify accounts routinely holding concurrent logons before enforcing, to avoid disrupting legitimate workflows.
7. Communicate to users that concurrent GUI sessions will be blocked and provide the exception process.
8. Combine with Security Audit Log monitoring so any concurrent-logon exceptions are visible.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and coordinate with teams that use shared or multi-session workflows.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm compliance.

PARAM-rfc/reject_insecure_logon.risk: This check flags that RFC logons are permitted over insecure (unauthenticated/unencrypted) channels. Note that rfc/reject_insecure_logon is not a standard SAP profile-parameter name - the real, SAP-supported enforcement of 'reject insecure RFC logon' is achieved through the SNC parameters (snc/enable, snc/accept_insecure_rfc, snc/only_encrypted_rfc) and related RFC hardening (rfc/reject_expired_passwd), so the underlying risk is that inbound RFC connections can authenticate without Secure Network Communications. When RFC traffic is not protected by SNC, credentials and payloads traverse the network in a form that can be intercepted or replayed, and the system accepts logons that are not cryptographically protected. In an S/4HANA landscape with many RFC destinations (S/4-to-BW, PI/PO, Solution Manager, third-party middleware), an attacker positioned on the network can sniff RFC logon data, capture trusted-system assertions, or perform man-in-the-middle attacks, then reuse the captured authentication to log on to the target system with the source system's rights. This directly enables lateral movement across the SAP landscape and is the class of weakness exploited by RFC capture-replay attacks. Business impact: cross-system compromise, data exfiltration, and privilege escalation via trusted-RFC chains. Compliance impact: violates the SAP Security Baseline's secure-communication requirements. A real incident would look like an RFC logon to a production system originating from captured/relayed credentials, invisible unless SNC and auditing are in place.
PARAM-rfc/reject_insecure_logon.mitigation:
1. Because the exact parameter as named is non-standard, base remediation on the real SNC controls: verify current values in RZ11 for snc/enable, snc/accept_insecure_rfc, snc/only_encrypted_rfc and snc/log_unencrypted_rfc.
2. In RZ10 (DEFAULT profile) set snc/enable = 1 to activate Secure Network Communications for the instance.
3. Set snc/accept_insecure_rfc = 0 so unprotected inbound RFC logons are rejected (use U with a documented allow-list only during migration).
4. Set snc/only_encrypted_rfc = 1 to force encryption on RFC, and snc/log_unencrypted_rfc = 2 first to discover any remaining cleartext callers before you enforce.
5. Set rfc/reject_expired_passwd = 1 so RFC logons with expired passwords are refused.
6. Configure the SNC PSE/credentials in STRUST and the SNC name mappings, and update SM59 destinations to use SNC (activate 'SNC active' with the partner's SNC name).
7. Save/activate the profile and restart the instance in a maintenance window (SNC activation requires restart).
8. Verify with RSPARAM that the SNC parameters are active and test each SM59 destination's 'Connection Test' and 'Authorization Test'.
9. Roll out landscape-wide in coordination with every interface owner - enabling snc/accept_insecure_rfc=0 will break any partner not yet on SNC, so migrate and log first, then enforce.
10. Re-run the scanner and confirm insecure RFC logon is no longer possible.

PARAM-rfc/reject_insecure_logon_data.risk: This check flags that RFC data transmission may occur over an insecure (unencrypted) channel. As with its sibling, rfc/reject_insecure_logon_data is not a standard SAP profile-parameter name; the genuine SAP mechanism for enforcing encrypted RFC data transport is SNC (snc/enable, snc/only_encrypted_rfc, snc/data_protection/min|max|use set to 3 = privacy/encryption). The underlying risk is therefore that RFC payloads - which routinely carry business data, credentials embedded in destinations, and trusted-system tickets - move across the network without confidentiality or integrity protection. An attacker with network visibility (a compromised host on the same segment, a rogue admin, or a poorly segmented cloud VPC in a RISE deployment) can passively capture sensitive data in transit (financial records, PII, HR data) and can tamper with or replay RFC calls. Capture-replay of trusted-RFC assertions specifically enables logging on to a trusting system with the source system's authorizations, chaining one compromised system into many. In S/4HANA RISE, where connectivity between the ABAP system, BTP services and on-premise systems may traverse various network paths, unencrypted RFC is a real exposure. Business impact: data breach (GDPR-relevant if PII is carried), integrity loss on interface data, and lateral movement. Compliance impact: fails SAP Security Baseline transport-protection and GDPR Art. 32 encryption-in-transit expectations. A real incident would look like sensitive interface data recovered from a network capture, or manipulated RFC payloads producing fraudulent records.
PARAM-rfc/reject_insecure_logon_data.mitigation:
1. Treat this as an SNC encryption-in-transit control; verify current values in RZ11 for snc/enable, snc/only_encrypted_rfc and snc/data_protection/min, /max and /use.
2. In RZ10 (DEFAULT profile) set snc/enable = 1.
3. Set snc/data_protection/min = 3, snc/data_protection/max = 3 and snc/data_protection/use = 3 to enforce privacy protection (encryption) on SNC-protected connections.
4. Set snc/only_encrypted_rfc = 1 (and snc/only_encrypted_gui = 1 where applicable) so RFC/GUI data is always encrypted.
5. Use snc/log_unencrypted_rfc = 2 during the migration phase to identify any destinations still sending cleartext before you enforce.
6. Configure the SNC PSE and credentials in STRUST and update each SM59 destination to activate SNC with quality of protection 'privacy'.
7. Save/activate the profile and restart the instance in a maintenance window; SNC changes require restart.
8. Verify with RSPARAM and by running SM59 connection/authorization tests on all destinations; check that data protection is applied.
9. Coordinate the rollout with all interface and middleware owners (PI/PO, BW, SolMan, third parties) - forcing encryption breaks partners not configured for SNC, so log first, migrate, then enforce.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm insecure RFC data transmission is eliminated.

PARAM-rfc/allowoldticket4tt.risk: rfc/allowoldticket4tt governs whether the SAP system still accepts the old-format tickets/assertions used by the legacy trusted-RFC (trusted/trusting-system) mechanism. Note the parameter is text-valued (values such as 'no', 'both', 'yes'), not the numeric 0/1 the scanner compares against, but the security intent is sound: as long as old-format trusted-RFC tickets are accepted, the system remains vulnerable to the weaknesses that the reworked trusted/trusting concept was designed to fix. The legacy method did not uniquely bind the ticket to a specific source system, which is the root of CVE-2023-0014 (addressed by SAP Security Notes 3089413 and 3318850): an attacker able to capture the authentication from normal communication between trusting SAP systems can replay it to impersonate the trusted source system and log on to the trusting system with elevated rights, without knowing any password. In a connected S/4HANA landscape with trusted-RFC relationships (e.g., Solution Manager to managed systems, or hub-and-spoke S/4 to satellites), a single foothold plus captured trusted-RFC traffic can be pivoted into privileged access on multiple systems - a classic lateral-movement and privilege-escalation chain. Business impact: multi-system compromise, fraud, and data breach spanning the landscape. Compliance impact: leaving weak crypto/legacy tickets enabled contradicts SAP's security notes and the SAP Security Baseline. A real incident would look like a trusted-system logon on a production system that no legitimate source actually initiated.
PARAM-rfc/allowoldticket4tt.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current value in RZ11 for rfc/allowoldticket4tt (secure target is 'no'; a temporary migration value of 'both' may be needed while old and new methods coexist).
2. First implement the reworked trusted/trusting-RFC method: apply SAP Security Notes 3089413 and 3318850 and prerequisites, then re-establish trust relationships using the new migration in transactions SMT1 (trusted) and SMT2 (trusting).
3. Migrate every trusted-RFC connection in the landscape to the new method; use the relevant migration/report guidance in the notes to confirm each relationship is upgraded on both sides.
4. Only after all connections are migrated, set rfc/allowoldticket4tt = no in the DEFAULT profile (DEFAULT.PFL) via RZ10 so old-format tickets are rejected system-wide.
5. Also review rfc/selftrust = 0 and the trusted-system entries in table RFCSYSACL for stale or overly broad trust.
6. Save/activate the profile and restart the instances during a maintenance window.
7. Verify with RSPARAM that the value is 'no' and test each trusted-RFC destination (SM59) still authenticates under the new method.
8. Roll out carefully across the whole landscape in coordination with every trusting/trusted partner - setting 'no' prematurely will break connections still on the old method, so migrate all sides first.
9. Monitor trusted-system logons via the Security Audit Log for anomalies during and after cutover.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the legacy-ticket acceptance is disabled.

PARAM-gw/sec_info.risk: gw/sec_info points the SAP Gateway to its secinfo access-control-list file, which governs which external programs the gateway is allowed to start and which hosts may do so (the classic CPIC/RFC 'start of external programs' control). If gw/sec_info is not configured (empty), the gateway falls back to permissive behaviour and, combined with a missing reginfo, effectively allows arbitrary external program registration and start. This is one of the most serious and actively exploited SAP exposures: an attacker who can reach the gateway TCP port (sapgw<nr>, e.g. 3300+instance) can instruct the gateway to start an operating-system program on the application server, executing code as the <sid>adm user - full remote code execution and host compromise, no SAP credentials required. Public exploit tooling (e.g. the '10KBLAZE'-class attacks) automated exactly this against systems with default/empty secinfo/reginfo. In S/4HANA RISE the gateway is customer-relevant, and while network exposure may be reduced, internal attackers or a compromised jump host can still reach it. Business impact: complete server and database compromise, ransomware deployment, data theft, and pivot into the rest of the landscape - among the highest-impact SAP findings, correctly rated CRITICAL. Compliance impact: violates SAP Note 1408081, CIS SAP 3.1, and the SAP Security Baseline. A real incident would look like unexpected OS processes spawned by <sid>adm and gateway logs (SMGW) showing external program starts from untrusted hosts.
PARAM-gw/sec_info.mitigation:
1. Verify the current value in RZ11 for gw/sec_info and check the gateway status/ACL state in SMGW (Goto -> Expert Functions).
2. Create a restrictive secinfo file on the application server (default location referenced by gw/sec_info, typically under the instance work/DATA directory) following SAP Note 1408081, using explicit USER/HOST/TP rules and a default-deny posture (deny all, then allow only required programs/hosts).
3. In RZ10 set gw/sec_info to the full path of that secinfo file, and configure gw/reg_info in the same effort (they work together).
4. Set gw/reg_no_conn_info per SAP Note 1444282 and gw/acl_mode = 1 to enforce ACL evaluation.
5. Save/activate the profile; you can reload the ACL files without a restart via SMGW -> Goto -> Expert Functions -> Reread, which limits disruption.
6. Use the SMGW logging/simulation mode (gw/sim_mode) to observe what legitimate programs need access before enforcing default-deny, so you do not break RFC server programs.
7. Verify by attempting a disallowed external program start and confirming it is rejected, and by reviewing SMGW logs for denied entries.
8. Ensure the gateway port is not exposed beyond the trusted network (firewall / network policy), as ACLs plus network isolation are complementary.
9. Roll out in DEV/QA first and coordinate with owners of registered RFC server programs (e.g. tax engines, external converters) to enumerate required rules.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm gw/sec_info is configured.

PARAM-gw/reg_info.risk: gw/reg_info points the SAP Gateway to its reginfo access-control-list file, which governs which external RFC server programs may register with the gateway and from which hosts. If gw/reg_info is empty/unconfigured, the gateway permits unrestricted registration of external programs. An attacker who can reach the gateway port can register a rogue RFC server program under the name of a legitimate registered server (or any name), and then either intercept RFC calls intended for the real server or be invoked to execute attacker-controlled logic - and in concert with a missing/permissive secinfo, this extends to starting OS programs, i.e. remote code execution as <sid>adm. Unrestricted registration also enables denial of service (registering to displace a legitimate server) and man-in-the-middle against RFC data. This is the same class of exposure exploited by automated SAP gateway attacks against default configurations. In an S/4HANA landscape with registered external servers (tax, output management, EDI converters), a missing reginfo is a direct path to compromise. Business impact: RCE and host takeover, interception/tampering of business-critical RFC traffic, and interface outages - a CRITICAL exposure. Compliance impact: violates SAP Note 1408081, CIS SAP 3.2, and the SAP Security Baseline. A real incident would look like an unknown program registered in SMGW's logged-on clients, RFC calls being answered by a rogue server, or OS-level code execution originating from a gateway registration.
PARAM-gw/reg_info.mitigation:
1. Verify the current value in RZ11 for gw/reg_info and inspect registered servers in SMGW (Logged On Clients / Goto -> Expert Functions).
2. Create a restrictive reginfo file on the application server per SAP Note 1408081, with explicit TP/HOST/ACCESS/CANCEL rules and a default-deny stance, allowing only the specific external server programs and hosts that must register.
3. In RZ10 set gw/reg_info to the full path of the reginfo file and configure gw/sec_info together (both are required for effective gateway hardening).
4. Set gw/acl_mode = 1 and gw/reg_no_conn_info per SAP Note 1444282, and set gw/rem_start = DISABLED where remote start is not required.
5. Save/activate the profile and reload ACLs live via SMGW -> Goto -> Expert Functions -> Reread (no restart needed).
6. Use gateway simulation/logging first to capture the legitimate registrations before switching to default-deny, so registered servers are not cut off.
7. Verify by confirming only the intended programs can register (SMGW) and that denied registrations appear in the gateway log.
8. Combine with network controls so the gateway port is reachable only from hosts that legitimately register servers.
9. Roll out in DEV/QA first and coordinate with every external-server owner to enumerate the required reginfo entries before production enforcement.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm gw/reg_info is configured.

PARAM-gw/reg_no_conn_info.risk: gw/reg_no_conn_info is a bit-mask (not a simple logging level, despite the baseline description) that switches on a set of hardening/security fixes in the SAP Gateway's handling of the secinfo/reginfo ACLs and of registered-server connection information, as documented in SAP Note 1444282 and the follow-on notes it references. Each corrective note adds a specific bit value; leaving the parameter too low means several of those gateway security corrections are simply not active, so even a present secinfo/reginfo may be evaluated with known weaknesses (for example, older gateways applied ACL checks inconsistently to already-registered connections). In practice this weakens the very ACL enforcement that protects against rogue external-program registration and start - the RCE-class exposure described in the gw/sec_info and gw/reg_info findings. In an S/4HANA landscape this is a defence-in-depth hardening parameter that ensures the gateway's ACL logic behaves securely; without it, an attacker may find edge cases (e.g. connection-info handling) that bypass the intended restrictions. Business impact: residual gateway attack surface that can contribute to remote code execution, MITM, or DoS on RFC interfaces. Compliance impact: relates to SAP Note 1408081/1444282 and the SAP Security Baseline gateway hardening. Note: the scanner's threshold (>=255) sets all bits; the correct value is release-specific per SAP Note 1444282 (commonly 1, 9, or 129 depending on kernel), and setting an inappropriate value can affect registered programs, so it must be chosen deliberately rather than maximised blindly.
PARAM-gw/reg_no_conn_info.mitigation:
1. Read SAP Note 1444282 (and the referenced follow-on notes) to determine the correct bit-mask value for your exact kernel release - do not blindly set 255.
2. Verify the current value in RZ11 for gw/reg_no_conn_info and note your kernel patch level.
3. In RZ10 set gw/reg_no_conn_info to the value recommended by SAP Note 1444282 / the SAP Security Baseline for your release (frequently 129 on current kernels; confirm per note).
4. Ensure this is done together with a valid secinfo (gw/sec_info) and reginfo (gw/reg_info) and gw/acl_mode = 1, since this parameter hardens how those ACLs are enforced.
5. Save/activate the profile; reload gateway settings via SMGW -> Goto -> Expert Functions where supported, and restart the instance if the note indicates a restart is required.
6. Test all registered external RFC server programs after the change, because stricter connection-info handling can affect long-lived registrations.
