7. Verify with RSPARAM that the intended bit-mask value is active on every instance.
8. Monitor SMGW logs for newly denied connections that indicate a legitimate program needs an ACL adjustment.
9. Roll out in DEV/QA first and coordinate with interface owners; because this touches live registrations, schedule a controlled window.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the parameter meets the required value.

PARAM-icm/HTTPS/verify_client.risk: icm/HTTPS/verify_client controls how the Internet Communication Manager handles X.509 client certificates on inbound HTTPS connections. The values are: 0 = do not request/use a client certificate; 1 = request a certificate but allow the connection without one (optional); 2 = require a valid client certificate (strict). (Note the scanner's remediation text reverses 1 and 2.) If the value is 0 where certificate-based authentication is intended, then client-certificate (PKI/SSO) authentication is effectively disabled and connections fall back to weaker password-based logon, and any design that relies on mutual TLS is silently not enforced. Conversely, on endpoints that are supposed to require certificates (e.g. system-to-system HTTPS, certain Fiori/gateway front-ends, secure B2B interfaces), a value below 2 means a client without a valid certificate can still connect, weakening authentication. In an S/4HANA / Fiori / BTP-connected landscape exposed via the Web Dispatcher, weak client-certificate verification undermines strong-authentication assumptions and can allow an attacker to bypass an intended PKI control and reach the application with only a password (which may itself be weak). Business impact: authentication bypass or downgrade on web channels, increasing the chance of account takeover and unauthorized access to Fiori apps and web services. Compliance impact: contradicts SAP Note 510007 secure-communication guidance and strong-authentication requirements. A real incident would look like access to a supposedly certificate-protected endpoint using only a password, or an SSO/PKI control that auditors find was never actually enforced.
PARAM-icm/HTTPS/verify_client.mitigation:
1. Determine the intended authentication design for each HTTPS endpoint before changing anything - whether client certificates are required, optional, or unused.
2. Verify the current value in RZ11 for icm/HTTPS/verify_client and review it alongside the ICM/Web Dispatcher setup (SMICM) and certificate configuration in STRUST.
3. For endpoints that must enforce client-certificate (mutual TLS) authentication, set icm/HTTPS/verify_client = 2 (require); where certificate auth is offered but password fallback is acceptable, use 1 (request/optional); use 0 only where client certificates are genuinely not used.
4. Configure the trusted CA certificates in STRUST (SSL server PSE / trust list) so presented client certificates can be validated, and map certificates to users (transaction/EXTID or rule-based mapping) as required.
5. Set this in RZ10 (instance profile) and remember icm/HTTPS/verify_client can be overridden per port in the icm/server_port_<n> definitions - check those too.
6. Save/activate and restart the ICM (SMICM -> Administration -> ICM -> Restart) or the instance so the change takes effect.
7. Verify by testing HTTPS access with and without a client certificate and confirming the ICM enforces the intended behaviour; check SMICM traces.
8. In RISE deployments coordinate with the Web Dispatcher / SAP-managed layer, since TLS termination and client-cert forwarding may occur there (also review icm/accept_forwarded_cert_via_http).
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and coordinate with owners of the affected web services/interfaces before production.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm the value matches the intended secure configuration.

PARAM-ssl/ciphersuites.risk: ssl/ciphersuites (and its client counterpart ssl/client_ciphersuites) defines which TLS protocol versions and cipher suites the SAP server offers/accepts for HTTPS and other TLS traffic via the CommonCryptoLib. If it is left unset/at defaults, the system may negotiate weak or legacy protocols and ciphers - older TLS versions, non-PFS key exchange, or weak algorithms - exposing encrypted channels to downgrade and interception attacks. Weak TLS on an S/4HANA / Fiori / Web Dispatcher front-end means an attacker able to intercept traffic (network foothold, rogue proxy, or a poorly isolated cloud path in a RISE deployment) can potentially decrypt or manipulate sessions, harvesting credentials, session cookies (SAP logon/assertion tickets) and business data in transit. Downgrade attacks force the connection to the weakest mutually supported suite, so a single legacy option can undermine the whole endpoint. Business impact: interception of authentication material and PII/financial data (GDPR-relevant), session hijacking, and man-in-the-middle tampering. Compliance impact: fails CIS SAP 5.1, the SAP Security Baseline transport-security requirements, and PCI/GDPR encryption-in-transit expectations. A real incident would look like credentials or ticket cookies captured off a weakly-encrypted session and replayed to gain access, or an external assessment flagging TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak ciphers on the public HTTPS endpoint.
PARAM-ssl/ciphersuites.mitigation:
1. Ensure the system uses a current CommonCryptoLib (SAPCRYPTOLIB) patch level, as cipher-string capabilities depend on it (see SAP Note 510007 and the CommonCryptoLib notes).
2. Verify the current values in RZ11 for ssl/ciphersuites and ssl/client_ciphersuites.
3. In RZ10 set a strong, PFS-first cipher string, for example ssl/ciphersuites = 135:PFS:HIGH::EC_P256:EC_HIGH and ssl/client_ciphersuites = 150:PFS:HIGH::EC_P256:EC_HIGH (the leading number selects the minimum TLS protocol/behaviour per SAP documentation), restricting to TLS 1.2+ and strong AES/ECDHE suites.
4. Confirm the exact recommended cipher string for your release and any regulatory requirement (TLS 1.2/1.3, AES-256) using SAP's CommonCryptoLib/ssl documentation rather than guessing values.
5. Save/activate the profile and restart the ICM (SMICM) or instance so the new suites take effect.
6. If a Web Dispatcher fronts the system (typical in RISE), apply matching strong ssl/ciphersuites there too, since TLS is often terminated at that layer.
7. Verify with an external TLS scan (or SMICM trace) that only TLS 1.2+ and strong ciphers are offered and that legacy protocols are refused.
8. Check for legacy clients/interfaces that may only support old ciphers before enforcing, to avoid breaking connectivity.
9. Pilot in DEV/QA and coordinate with interface and Web Dispatcher owners before production rollout.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm ssl/ciphersuites is explicitly and securely configured.

PARAM-rsau/enable.risk: rsau/enable turns on the SAP Security Audit Log (SAL), the primary forensic and detective control for the ABAP system - it records security-relevant events such as successful/failed logons, RFC calls, transaction/report starts, user-master and authorization changes, and debug activity. If rsau/enable is 0 (the kernel default) or the SAL is otherwise not active, the system generates no reliable security audit trail. This is dangerous because it blinds both real-time monitoring and post-incident forensics: an attacker who takes over an account or abuses privileges leaves little trace, and investigators cannot reconstruct what was accessed or changed. In an S/4HANA RISE landscape the SAL is a customer-owned control and the key data source feeding SIEM/SOC detections; without it, detections for brute force, SAP* usage, debug-and-replace fraud, and mass downloads simply have no telemetry. Attack scenario: an insider posts fraudulent payments or exfiltrates data via debugger field manipulation, and because the SAL is off there is no record of the debug events or the RFC calls, so the fraud is neither detected nor provable. Business impact: undetectable fraud/data breach, inability to meet breach-notification and evidentiary obligations, and failed audits. Compliance impact: violates SAP Note 2191612, CIS SAP 6.1, SAP Security Baseline logging requirements, and SOX/GDPR monitoring/accountability duties - correctly rated CRITICAL. A real incident would surface as a suspected fraud that cannot be investigated because no audit trail exists.
PARAM-rsau/enable.mitigation:
1. On S/4HANA (SAP_BASIS 7.50+) configure the Security Audit Log via RSAU_CONFIG (the successor to SM19); on older releases use SM19. Note that on 7.40+ SAL 'kernel parameters' set in RSAU_CONFIG override the rsau/* profile parameters.
2. Activate logging: in RSAU_CONFIG set 'Static security audit active' (equivalent to rsau/enable = 1) and, if using profile parameters, set rsau/enable = 1 in RZ10.
3. Configure sufficient filters/selection slots (rsau/selection_slots, e.g. 10+) to capture logons (esp. failed), RFC logons/calls, transaction/report starts, user and authorization changes, and debug events - at minimum for critical users (SAP*, DDIC, admins, emergency IDs) and all users for high-severity events.
4. Enable log integrity protection (rsau/integrity = 1) and set DIR_AUDIT / FN_AUDIT (or the kernel-parameter equivalents) so logs are written to a protected location with adequate retention.
5. Save/activate; rsau parameters are dynamically switchable via RSAU_CONFIG, but persist the profile values in RZ10 so the setting survives restarts.
6. Verify events are being recorded by generating a test failed logon and reading it back in SM20 / RSAU_READ_LOG.
7. Forward the SAL to the SIEM/SOC (e.g. via RSAU integration or log shipping) so detections have data.
8. In RISE/ECS confirm log retention and access are aligned with the SAP-managed operations responsibilities.
9. Roll out in DEV/QA first to size the filters and avoid excessive log volume, then enable in production.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm rsau/enable / SAL is active.

PARAM-rec/client.risk: rec/client controls table logging - specifically which client(s) have change logging active for tables flagged as log-relevant (those whose technical settings have the 'Log data changes' flag set). It accepts values such as OFF, ALL, or a specific client/list. When rec/client is not configured (or set to OFF), changes to critical customizing and configuration tables are not recorded even if the tables are marked for logging, removing a key audit trail for configuration and master-data-control changes. This matters because many high-impact fraud and sabotage actions are configuration changes rather than transactions - e.g. altering tolerance groups, payment/banking configuration, number ranges, or currency/exchange-rate tables. Without table change logging, such changes cannot be attributed or reconstructed. In an S/4HANA landscape subject to SOX, change-management evidence for these tables is expected; missing table logging undermines both detective control and audit evidence. Attack scenario: a malicious insider with configuration access changes a payment-relevant customizing table to redirect funds or suppress a control, then reverts it - with rec/client off there is no record the change ever occurred. Business impact: undetectable configuration fraud, loss of change-audit evidence, and SOX ITGC/change-management findings. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP 6.2 and change-logging expectations of the SAP Security Baseline and SOX. A real incident would look like an unexplained configuration state change with no history in the change-document/table-log records to explain who did it or when.
PARAM-rec/client.mitigation:
1. Verify the current value in RZ11 for rec/client.
2. In RZ10 set rec/client to log the relevant client(s) - use rec/client = ALL to log all clients, or list the productive client number(s) explicitly (e.g. rec/client = 100) per policy.
3. Ensure the tables you care about actually have the 'Log data changes' flag enabled in their technical settings (SE13 / the ABAP Dictionary technical settings) - rec/client only takes effect for tables marked log-relevant; SAP delivers many critical customizing tables pre-flagged.
4. Save/activate the profile; rec/client generally requires an instance restart, so schedule a maintenance window.
5. Verify by making a test change to a logged table and reviewing the log via SCU3 (report RSVTPROT) to confirm the change was recorded with user and timestamp.
6. Confirm log retention and periodic review of table-change logs are in place (and forwarded to SIEM where feasible) so the trail is actually used.
7. Assess performance/volume impact in DEV/QA first, since broad logging on high-write tables adds overhead; scope table flags sensibly rather than enabling logging on transactional mass tables.
8. Coordinate with the change-management and audit teams to align logged tables with SOX-relevant configuration.
9. In RISE/ECS coordinate the restart with the SAP-managed operations team.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm rec/client is configured.

PARAM-rdisp/wpdbug_max_no.risk: rdisp/wpdbug_max_no sets the maximum number of work processes that may be used simultaneously for ABAP debugging. It is relevant in production because the ABAP debugger is not merely a viewer - with the 'Debug & Replace' capability a user can change the value of internal variables at runtime, altering program flow to bypass authorization checks, force a control to pass, or manipulate financial figures before they are posted. If debugging is broadly permitted in production (and combined with debug authorizations), a user can, for example, stop in a posting or approval program, overwrite the amount or the authorization result, and let execution continue - producing fraudulent postings that look legitimate. Setting rdisp/wpdbug_max_no = 0 disables the use of debug work processes and is a strong technical brake on production debugging (it complements, but does not replace, restricting the S_DEVELOP authorization for DEBUG with change activity). In an S/4HANA production system, uncontrolled debugging is a direct fraud and privilege-escalation vector. Attack scenario: an insider with debug-change authorization halts execution in a payment run, uses Replace to change a vendor bank field or an authorization flag in memory, and completes the run - the Security Audit Log may record the debug event, but the fraudulent outcome is posted. Business impact: financial fraud, authorization bypass, data integrity loss. Compliance impact: violates CIS SAP 7.1 and SoD/production-change controls expected under SOX. A real incident would look like debug-and-replace activity in the SAL correlated with an anomalous posting.
PARAM-rdisp/wpdbug_max_no.mitigation:
1. Verify the current value in RZ11 for rdisp/wpdbug_max_no and review the intended production-support model.
2. In RZ10 set rdisp/wpdbug_max_no = 0 on production instances to disable debug work processes (allow debugging only in DEV/QA).
3. Save/activate the profile; this parameter requires an instance restart, so schedule a maintenance window.
4. Independently and importantly, restrict the debug authorization: ensure no production dialog user holds S_DEVELOP with object type DEBUG and activity 02 (change / Debug & Replace) - display-only debug (activity 03) is far less dangerous but should still be tightly limited; review assignments via SUIM.
5. Also restrict S_DEVELOP for other development activities and lock down SE38/SE24/SE80/SE16-family transactions in production via role design.
6. Ensure the Security Audit Log (rsau/enable) records debugging and debug-replace events and that these are monitored/alerted in the SIEM.
7. Verify by attempting to start a debugging session in production and confirming debug work processes are unavailable, and by re-checking S_DEVELOP DEBUG assignments.
8. Provide a controlled firefighter/emergency-access process (e.g. via GRC firefighter) for the rare legitimate production debug need, with logging and approval.
9. Roll out in DEV/QA first, and coordinate with support teams who may currently rely on production debugging so alternative procedures are in place.
10. Re-run the scanner to confirm rdisp/wpdbug_max_no = 0 in production.

NET-001.risk: This check flags RFC destinations (maintained in SM59, persisted in table RFCDES) that store a fixed logon user and password. SAP encrypts the stored password with a system key, but it is decryptable at runtime and recoverable from a database/system copy, a table export, or by anyone able to read RFCDES. The core danger is credential inheritance: any user or process on the source system that can trigger a call through such a destination silently authenticates on the target as the stored user, with that user's full authorization set and no separate logon. This is the classic RFC-hopping lateral-movement path. In an S/4HANA RISE landscape there are many RFCs radiating from the central ABAP instance to satellite systems, Solution Manager, BW, and outward via the Cloud Connector to BTP, so a single destination carrying a powerful technical user (or worse SAP_ALL, DDIC, or a wide batch user) becomes a crown-jewel pivot. The attack is concrete: a low-privilege dialog user who can start a function module that uses the destination, or an attacker who has compromised the source host, hops to the target with the stored user's rights and then reads or posts data, dumps tables, or calls further RFCs. If the destination runs from a lower-trust tier (sandbox or development) into production, it collapses the segregation between environments. Business impact includes fraudulent financial postings, mass data exfiltration under GDPR, segregation-of-duties bypass, and failure of SOX IT general controls over access. A real incident typically looks like a forgotten dev-to-prod destination holding an FI power user that is abused to create unauthorized journal entries with no attributable dialog logon.
NET-001.mitigation:
1. Inventory the exposure: run report RSRFCCHK (or review SM59 and table RFCDES) to list every destination that carries stored logon data, and record the stored user and target system for each.
2. Prefer trust over stored passwords: where source and target are your own systems, replace the stored credential with a Trusted RFC relationship defined in SMT1 (trusted/trusting systems) so the caller identity flows and the target performs an S_RFCACL check rather than relying on a stored password.
3. Where trust is not possible, switch the SM59 destination to certificate/SNC single sign-on on the Logon and Security tab instead of user and password.
4. If a technical user must remain, create a dedicated account in SU01 of type System or Communication (never Dialog), and scope its role tightly, restricting authorization object S_RFC to only the required function groups; never assign SAP_ALL or use DDIC.
5. Set a long generated password, store it in a secrets vault, and rotate it on a schedule.
6. Lock down who can maintain destinations: restrict transaction SM59 and object S_RFC_ADM to the Basis team only.
7. Enforce inbound RFC authorization on targets by setting profile parameter auth/rfc_authority_check to 6 or 9 so the target validates S_RFC for the called function group.
8. Optionally deploy Unified Connectivity (transaction UCONCOCKPIT) to allow-list which function modules are callable externally.
9. Verify by re-running RSRFCCHK and confirming the count drops; test each interface in non-production first and coordinate with interface owners before removing stored credentials. SM59 changes take effect immediately, but SNC or parameter changes may require an instance restart.

NET-002.risk: This check flags RFC destinations whose target host falls outside the recognized internal RFC-1918 ranges, i.e. destinations that point to external or unrecognized systems. Each such destination is a potential data-egress channel and a trust boundary where credentials and business payloads may cross an untrusted network. In an S/4HANA RISE model some external targets are legitimate (BTP services reached through the SAP Cloud Connector, sanctioned SaaS endpoints), but an unmanaged or undocumented external destination is exactly what an attacker or a malicious insider would create to redirect traffic. The abuse scenario has two shapes. First, an actor who can maintain destinations repoints or creates a destination toward an attacker-controlled host; if the destination stores logon data or uses basic auth, the logon handshake and payload are delivered straight to the attacker, harvesting credentials and data. Second, a legitimate-looking external destination silently exfiltrates business data (HR, payroll, customer master, pricing) to a third party, or acts as a man-in-the-middle relay. Because RFC and HTTP destinations can carry sensitive fields, an uncontrolled external endpoint is also an uncontrolled cross-border data flow, which raises GDPR sub-processor and data-residency concerns and undermines the RISE shared-responsibility boundary. The business impact ranges from silent data breach to compliance failure for undeclared data transfers. A real incident often looks like a destination to an unfamiliar public IP that, on investigation, has been quietly siphoning master data for months, discovered only because network egress logging finally flagged the connection.
NET-002.mitigation:
1. Enumerate all destinations in SM59 (or table RFCDES) and extract the target host for each connection type.
2. Compare every target against an approved allow-list of internal subnets plus sanctioned SaaS and BTP endpoints; treat anything else as an exception to be justified.
3. For each external host, obtain documented business justification and interface-owner sign-off and record it in the interface register.
4. Ensure every external connection is encrypted: type 3 and T destinations via SNC, and HTTP destinations (type G/H) via HTTPS with server-certificate validation using certificates imported in STRUST.
5. For BTP or cloud targets in RISE, route through the SAP Cloud Connector with an explicit resource allow-list rather than configuring direct outbound destinations.
6. Constrain the gateway: maintain secinfo and reginfo (via SMGW or the ACL files) to restrict which external programs may register or be started.
7. Enforce network-layer egress firewall rules so only approved destinations can be reached.
8. Deactivate and delete obsolete or unowned external destinations, and restrict SM59 maintenance authorization to Basis.
9. Verify by re-scanning and confirming every remaining external destination is on the allow-list and encrypted; test changes in non-production with the interface owner before touching production.

NET-003.risk: This check flags RFC destinations of connection type 3 (ABAP-to-ABAP over TCP/IP), T (external TCP/IP program), or W/HTTP that do not have SNC (Secure Network Communication) enabled, meaning the traffic is not cryptographically protected. The SAP DIAG and RFC protocols are only lightly obfuscated, not encrypted, so without SNC the logon credentials and all business payload traverse the network in effectively clear form. Any actor positioned on the network path can capture and decode this traffic. The attack is straightforward: an adversary with a foothold in the same network segment, or able to perform ARP spoofing, tap a switch span port, or observe traffic within a shared cloud tenancy, records the RFC session, recovers the technical user's password and the transported data, and then replays or reuses those credentials for lateral movement. In an S/4HANA RISE deployment the links between the customer-managed components and the SAP-managed application and HANA layers, and the links out to BTP, cross provider-controlled network fabric, so unencrypted RFC directly contradicts the SAP Security Baseline requirement for encrypted internal and external communication. The consequences are credential theft feeding onward compromise, disclosure of confidential business data in transit, and a documented control gap against the Security Baseline and DSAG audit guidance. A realistic incident is a packet capture of routine batch RFC traffic that yields a technical user's password, which turns out to be reused across several systems, giving the attacker a landscape-wide key.
NET-003.mitigation:
1. Install and configure the SNC cryptographic library (CommonCryptoLib / sapcryptolib) on all application servers and create the system SNC PSE via STRUST and transaction SNC0.
2. Set the enabling profile parameters: snc/enable=1, snc/data_protection/use=3, snc/data_protection/min=3, snc/data_protection/max=3, and snc/r3int_rfc_secure=1 to force internal system-to-system RFC over SNC.
3. During migration, temporarily allow legacy clients with snc/accept_insecure_rfc and related snc/accept_insecure_* parameters, then tighten them to 0 (or a named allow-list) once all partners are converted.
4. In SM59, for each type 3 and T destination, open the Logon and Security tab, activate SNC, and set the Quality of Protection to Privacy Protection (encryption; value 3, or 9 for maximum).
5. For type W/G/H destinations, change the target to HTTPS and import and trust the server certificate in STRUST, enforcing certificate validation.
6. Because the enabling parameters require an instance/kernel restart, schedule a maintenance window, validate in non-production first, and coordinate SNC name mapping (SNC0 / external identity mapping) with interface owners.
7. Verify using the SM59 Connection Test (it should report SNC active), a fresh scanner run, and a test-system packet capture confirming no cleartext credentials remain.

NET-004.risk: This check flags Internet Communication Framework (ICF) services that are active and known to be high-risk, such as /sap/public/info, /sap/public/icman/ping, /sap/bc/soap/rfc, /sap/bc/webrfc, /sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui, and various BSP/WebDynpro nodes. These endpoints are reachable over HTTP(S) and dramatically enlarge the attack surface. /sap/public/info and the ICM ping page leak kernel, patch, operating-system, and database details that let an attacker fingerprint the system and select matching CVEs. /sap/bc/soap/rfc and /sap/bc/webrfc are HTTP-to-RFC bridges that allow remote function-module invocation over the web, a path historically abused to reach dangerous modules and achieve remote code execution. WebGUI exposes a full SAP GUI inside a browser. The abuse chain, well documented by SAP security researchers, is to enumerate the ICF tree, hit the info page to fingerprint, then use the SOAP/Web RFC bridge with anonymous or captured credentials to call function modules that read data or execute code. In an S/4HANA RISE landscape the Fiori and gateway front-end sits close to the internet edge, so an exposed ICF node is directly reachable external attack surface rather than a purely internal concern. The impact spans information disclosure, remote code execution, full system compromise, and consequent data breach. A realistic incident resembles the ICF-driven exploitation classes that have led to complete SAP takeovers, where an exposed service was the initial entry point before privilege escalation and data theft.
NET-004.mitigation:
1. In transaction SICF (or via table ICFSERVLOC) review the service tree and identify which high-risk nodes are active.
2. Deactivate services that are not required by right-clicking the node and choosing Deactivate Service; specifically evaluate /sap/public/info, /sap/public/icman/ping, /sap/bc/soap/rfc, /sap/bc/webrfc, /sap/bc/bsp/sap/it00, and webgui.
3. Align the deactivation list with SAP KBA 2661761 (ICF services: mandatory vs. deactivatable) and the SAP Security Baseline ICF recommendations, and use the standard SICF status/reporting tools to confirm state across clients.
4. For services that must stay active, enforce authentication on the SICF Logon Data tab (use Standard logon, not an alternative/anonymous logon) and protect the node with authorization object S_ICF.
5. Remove any stored anonymous service user, or replace it with a minimally-authorized technical user.
6. Add an edge control: configure the SAP Web Dispatcher or reverse proxy to URL-filter and block public diagnostic paths from external networks.
7. Verify by attempting to reach each path from an external client (it should return 403/404) and by re-running the scanner; document a business justification for anything left active.
8. Caution: deactivating ICF nodes can break Fiori apps and interfaces, so map dependencies and test thoroughly in non-production before changing production.

NET-005.risk: This check flags ICF services that are active and configured without authentication (anonymous or an alternative stored-user logon), excluding the intentionally public /sap/public/* tree. This is the most dangerous ICF misconfiguration because it grants unauthenticated execution of the service handler: anyone who can reach the URL runs the underlying application logic with the anonymous or stored service user's authorizations, with no logon at all. The attack requires no credentials. An adversary, whether on the internet for an edge-exposed system or an unprivileged insider internally, simply requests the URL; if the anonymous context carries any meaningful authorization, they can read or write business data, trigger RFC calls, or dump table contents, and if the stored service user is powerful the outcome escalates to mass data extraction or code execution. Because the access is unauthenticated, it also produces no user-attributable trail, creating an audit and forensic gap on top of the exposure. In an S/4HANA RISE context an internet-adjacent Fiori or gateway host with an anonymous non-public service is effectively an open door that automated scanners routinely discover. The business impact is a direct data breach with no attribution, GDPR exposure for personal data reachable through the service, and SOX control failure for uncontrolled access to financially relevant functions. A realistic incident is an anonymous BSP or WebDynpro service exposing customer or employee data that is harvested at scale by a crawler before anyone notices.
NET-005.mitigation:
1. In SICF, identify active services whose Logon Data tab is set to anonymous or an alternative stored-user logon, especially any node outside /sap/public/*.
2. For each non-public service, change the logon procedure to Standard so authentication is required, and remove the stored anonymous user.
3. Where a service genuinely needs a technical user, use a minimally-authorized account of type System/Communication and protect the node with authorization object S_ICF (fields ICF_FIELD/ICF_VALUE).
4. Deactivate outright any service that has no business need (right-click, Deactivate Service).
5. Enforce transport-layer protection: require HTTPS (SSL-enabled ICM port via icm/server_port) and, where feasible, client-certificate or SSO authentication.
6. For internet-facing scenarios add Web Dispatcher or reverse-proxy authentication and URL allow-listing in front of the ABAP stack.
7. Verify that an unauthenticated request to each affected URL now returns HTTP 401, confirm the access is recorded in the Security Audit Log (RSAU_READ_LOG / SM20), and re-run the scanner.
8. Caution: a few standard services legitimately permit anonymous logon (for example certain launchpad or logon services); validate against SAP KBA 2661761 (ICF services: mandatory vs. deactivatable) and the Security Baseline before disabling, and test in non-production.

NET-006.risk: This check flags transport requests that remain in a modifiable state (status D or L) in a production context, which is a symptom of weak change management and, more seriously, of a production system that is open to direct changes. The underlying risk is that the production client and/or system change option permits repository and customizing changes outside the governed development-to-quality-to-production transport path. When a system is open, a person with development and transport authorizations can alter code or customizing directly in production without a released, reviewed transport: no four-eyes approval, no test evidence, and a broken chain of custody. The abuse scenario is a developer or an attacker who has acquired S_TRANSPRT and S_DEVELOP making a direct change in production, for example inserting a backdoor into a program, weakening an authority check, or altering pricing, tax, or payment configuration, and then leaving the change effectively unreviewed. Open transports also mean uncontrolled objects sitting in production whose contents were never validated. The business impact is severe: fraud, persistent backdoors, system instability, and a headline SOX IT-general-controls failure for change management, which is one of the most common and most heavily weighted audit findings. A realistic incident is a direct production change to a payment or vendor-master program that reroutes funds, discovered only during a later audit precisely because there was no transport and no approval record to catch it earlier.
NET-006.mitigation:
1. Close the production client in SCC4: set the client to Not modifiable and disallow changes to repository and cross-client customizing.
2. Close the system in SE06/SE03 (System Change Option): set the global setting to Not modifiable so the software components cannot be changed directly.
3. Review outstanding modifiable requests in SE01/SE09/SE03 (Transport Organizer and its tools): release or delete illegitimate open requests and investigate their owners for unauthorized activity.
4. Enforce a controlled import path through TMS (STMS) with a QA approval step, and where available use ChaRM (Solution Manager) or cCTS to govern approvals.
5. Restrict change-capability authorizations in production: object S_TRANSPRT, S_CTS_ADMI, and S_DEVELOP should not grant developers the ability to change production.
6. Turn on Security Audit Log monitoring for change-option toggles and direct changes so any reopening is detected.
7. Verify that SCC4 and SE06 both show Not modifiable, that SE03 lists no open requests originating in production, and that the audit report is clean.
8. Caution: coordinate with Basis to define and document a break-glass emergency-change procedure so legitimate urgent fixes have a controlled, logged path.

NET-007.risk: This check flags transports whose descriptions contain terms like debug, replace, breakpoint, or hotfix direct, which suggests use of the ABAP debugger's debug-and-replace capability against a production system. Debug-and-replace lets a suitably authorized user change the value of variables in a running program at runtime, which is one of the most powerful and abusable techniques in SAP. Because it operates on the live process, it can bypass controls that appear intact in the code: an attacker can force the result of an authority check (for example set the return code to success after a failed AUTHORITY-CHECK), unlock a blocked function, or alter an amount, quantity, or bank account mid-transaction, all without any persistent code change and with only a faint trace. The realistic abuser is an insider holding authorization object S_DEVELOP with OBJTYPE=DEBUG and ACTVT=02 (change) who manipulates a running transaction, for example to approve a purchase order above their release limit, change a vendor bank account, or defeat a segregation-of-duties block. Because the change is transient and leaves little behind beyond a Security Audit Log entry (only if debug auditing is enabled), it is a favored fraud and privilege-escalation technique that is hard to reconstruct after the fact. The business impact is direct financial fraud, integrity loss on financially relevant data, privilege escalation, and SOX control failure. A realistic incident is a finance user using debug-replace to release a payment that the application had deliberately blocked, with the manipulation invisible in normal transaction logs.
NET-007.mitigation:
1. Investigate each flagged transport and its owner immediately to determine what was changed at runtime and whether it was authorized.
2. Remove runtime-change capability in production by restricting authorization object S_DEVELOP so that no standard user holds OBJTYPE=DEBUG with ACTVT=02 (change/replace); ideally OBJTYPE=DEBUG is absent entirely except for tightly controlled emergency access.
3. Where debug display is genuinely needed, permit only ACTVT=03 (display) and grant even that through time-boxed emergency access (for example a GRC firefighter user).
4. Optionally cap debug work processes with profile parameter rdisp/wpdbug_max_no, but treat the S_DEVELOP restriction as the real control since the parameter only limits resources.
5. Enable Security Audit Log recording of debugging events (RSAU_CONFIG) and route alerts to the SOC/SIEM.
6. Keep the system Not modifiable (SE06) and the client Not modifiable (SCC4) so debug-based changes to persistent objects are further constrained.
7. Verify with SUIM (Users by Complex Selection Criteria on S_DEVELOP with OBJTYPE=DEBUG, ACTVT=02): the result set should be zero outside controlled firefighter users, and a test debug event should appear in RSAU_READ_LOG/SM20.
8. Caution: coordinate with support teams and provide a documented firefighter path so genuine production incidents can still be debugged under supervision.

NET-008.risk: This check flags the absence of any active Security Audit Log (SAL) filters, meaning the ABAP Security Audit Log (configured via SM19 on older releases or RSAU_CONFIG on S/4HANA) is effectively not recording security-relevant events. Without active filters the system does not capture dialog and RFC logon successes and failures, transaction and report starts, RFC function-module calls, user-master and role/authorization changes, debugging activity, or audit-configuration changes. The consequence is a near-total loss of the forensic and detection trail. From an attacker's perspective this is ideal operating conditions: reconnaissance, credential abuse, privilege escalation, and data theft can all proceed with no durable record, and any tracks in transient logs are easy to lose. For defenders it means incident response is blind, alerting is impossible, and there is no evidence to support investigations, non-repudiation, or regulatory inquiries. In an S/4HANA RISE model the customer generally retains responsibility for configuring the ABAP-layer Security Audit Log, so its absence is a critical governance and shared-responsibility gap rather than something the provider covers. The impact includes an undetectable breach, failure of SOX logging and monitoring controls, inability to demonstrate GDPR-required accountability, and adverse audit opinions. A realistic incident is a post-breach investigation that finds no audit log at all: the team cannot scope which data was accessed or exfiltrated, cannot identify the actor, and must conservatively assume worst-case data loss, which magnifies breach-notification obligations and cost.
NET-008.mitigation:
1. Enable the log by setting the profile parameters: rsau/enable=1, rsau/selection_slots to at least 10, rsau/user_selection=1, rsau/integrity=1, and size the storage with rsau/max_diskspace/local (or log to the database); confirm which parameters are dynamic via RZ11 versus needing a restart per SAP Note 2191612.
2. Configure filters in RSAU_CONFIG (S/4HANA; SM19 on releases before BASIS 755) and activate a static profile.
3. Cover at minimum: dialog and RFC logon failures and successes for privileged users, transaction and report starts, RFC function-module calls, user-master record changes, role and authorization changes, debugging events, and changes to the audit configuration itself.
4. Ensure all events are captured for SAP*, DDIC, and any emergency/firefighter users, with critical events captured for all users.
5. Turn on integrity protection (rsau/integrity=1) so the log is tamper-evident, noting that logs must then be read via RSAU_READ_LOG/SM20 rather than at OS level (SAP Notes 2765858 and 2191612).
6. Forward the log to a SIEM for retention and alerting, and define a retention period meeting policy and regulatory minimums.
7. Verify by confirming RSAU_CONFIG shows an active profile, generating a deliberate failed logon, and confirming it appears in RSAU_READ_LOG/SM20.
8. Caution: broad filters can generate high volume, so size storage appropriately, test in non-production, and schedule any required restart in a maintenance window.

RISE-001.risk: The default SAP identity provider (SAP ID Service, origin sap.default / sap.ids) is still enabled as a logon-capable trust on the BTP subaccount, alongside or instead of the corporate IdP. SAP ID Service is a public, internet-facing identity store that anyone holding an S-user or a self-registered P-user can access; it is NOT governed by the organization's joiner/mover/leaver process, corporate password policy, MFA enforcement, or conditional-access rules. While it remains 'Available for User Logon', an attacker who obtains or self-provisions an SAP ID Service account and is granted (or inherits, via shadow-user creation) any role collection can authenticate to BTP apps and, through destinations and principal propagation, reach the connected S/4HANA backend, completely bypassing the corporate IdP, its MFA, and its central deprovisioning. Because the default IdP survives corporate-IdP outages and is routinely forgotten after a custom-IdP go-live, it becomes a durable, low-visibility backdoor. In a real incident, a former contractor whose corporate account was disabled still logs on through SAP ID Service, or a phished S-user password is replayed against the subaccount; the SOC sees a 'valid' BTP logon with no failed corporate-IdP events, and the actor pivots to Fiori launchpad tiles exposing financial or HR data. From a compliance standpoint this defeats SOX access-control assertions and GDPR least-privilege/deprovisioning obligations, and it is a standard SAP Security Baseline audit finding. The weakness is architectural, not a single stolen password: it is a permanently-open alternate authentication path that neither central IAM nor the SIEM has full visibility over.
RISE-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the corporate IdP (SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication, IAS) is fully configured and tested end-to-end for the subaccount before touching the default trust.
2. In the BTP Cockpit open the affected Subaccount -> Security -> Trust Configuration.
3. Locate the 'Default Identity Provider' entry (SAP ID Service, origin sap.default/sap.ids) and choose the Edit (pencil) icon.
4. Uncheck 'Available for User Logon' and Save. Note: the default IdP cannot be deleted - hiding it from logon is the SAP-supported hardening step; it stays as an administrative fallback only.
5. Repeat for every subaccount (DEV/QA/PROD) and for both the platform and application trust where applicable.
6. If you administer via CLI, verify and set with 'btp list security/trust' and 'btp update security/trust'.
7. Synchronise the trust list into any SAP Cloud Connector: Cloud To On-Premise -> Principal Propagation -> Synchronize, so the on-prem trust list matches BTP.
8. Verify: open the subaccount/application logon URL in a fresh private browser session and confirm the SAP ID Service tile no longer appears and only the corporate IdP is offered.
9. Verify no active role collections remain assigned to sap.default shadow users (Security -> Users, filter by origin) and clean up any found.
10. Rollout caution: retain one break-glass platform administrator reachable via the global account or a separately governed emergency path BEFORE disabling default logon, and perform the change in a non-production subaccount first to avoid a lock-out.

RISE-002.risk: The trust is configured to auto-create BTP shadow users on first logon (createShadowUsers / auto_create_shadow_users = true). A shadow user is the local BTP representation that carries role-collection assignments; with on-the-fly creation the mere ability to authenticate at the trusted IdP is enough to materialise an account in the subaccount with no approval or provisioning workflow. This collapses the crucial separation between 'can authenticate' and 'is authorized to use this system'. The danger is amplified when the IdP is a large corporate directory (every employee can now instantiate an account in a Finance or HR subaccount) or when a permissive default IdP is also enabled. If any 'default' role collection is assigned at the subaccount level or via IdP group-to-role-collection mapping, freshly created shadow users may immediately inherit access. Attack scenario: an internal user outside the ERP population, or an attacker who has compromised any federated identity, logs on once; a shadow user is silently created and, via group mapping, receives access to OData APIs or Fiori apps - with no request ticket, no approver, and no joiner record to trip detection. The outcome is uncontrolled user-base growth, orphaned and over-entitled accounts, broken segregation-of-duties and named-user licence compliance, and an audit finding that user provisioning is ungoverned. A breach looks like dozens of never-approved shadow users appearing in the user list, several holding sensitive role collections that no one can trace to a request, discovered only during an access recertification.
RISE-002.mitigation:
1. Decide the target model: explicit provisioning via SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Provisioning (IPS/SCIM) rather than logon-time auto-creation.
2. In BTP Cockpit -> Subaccount -> Security -> Trust Configuration, edit the relevant trust and set shadow-user creation to a controlled value (disable automatic creation where exposed); via CLI use 'btp update security/trust' with the auto-create-shadow-users parameter set to false.
3. In the corporate IdP (IAS admin console) restrict the BTP/XSUAA application's User Access to 'Only Corporate Users' or 'Private' so only assigned users can authenticate.
4. Remove any broad 'default' role-collection assignment at the subaccount and replace it with explicit, request-based assignment under Security -> Role Collections.
5. Configure IPS jobs (or SCIM) to provision only entitled users and to deprovision leavers, tied to an approval workflow.
6. Clean up existing shadow users: Security -> Users, identify auto-provisioned accounts, revoke unneeded role collections and delete orphans.
7. Verify: attempt logon with a deliberately non-entitled test identity and confirm no shadow user is created and access is denied.
8. Reconcile the BTP user list against the authoritative HR/IGA source and document any exceptions.
9. Rollout caution: coordinate with app/integration owners so legitimate first-time users are pre-provisioned before you disable auto-creation, and pilot in a non-production subaccount to avoid locking out real users.

RISE-003.risk: One or more communication arrangements bundle a large number of inbound/outbound services (more than ten) under a single communication scenario and a single communication user. In S/4HANA a communication arrangement instantiates an SAP-delivered scenario (SAP_COM_*) and grants the bound communication user access to every API/service the scenario contains. When a scenario is broad, or several are consolidated onto one arrangement/user, a single credential can call dozens of OData/SOAP APIs spanning finance, procurement, sales and master data. This violates least privilege and dramatically enlarges the blast radius: if that one credential leaks (hard-coded in middleware, committed to a repo, or exposed in logs) the attacker inherits the union of all those services at once - read/write of business partners, posting of journal entries, creation of purchase or sales orders. It also destroys traceability, because activity from many logically distinct integrations all appears under the same arrangement and user, so an incident cannot be scoped to one interface. Attack scenario: a developer stores the arrangement's client secret in a CI pipeline; it is exfiltrated; the attacker enumerates the arrangement's services and posts fraudulent supplier bank-detail changes via API_BUSINESS_PARTNER while simultaneously pulling GL data via API_JOURNAL_ENTRY, all with one token. Compliance impact spans SoD violations, SOX ITGC failures over interface access, and an inability to contain the breach. Auditors flag this as a classic over-entitlement/least-privilege interface finding.
RISE-003.mitigation:
1. Inventory arrangements in the 'Maintain Communication Arrangements' Fiori app and list every service active under each (Inbound/Outbound Services sections).
2. For each arrangement, confirm which services the connected system actually consumes; set the unneeded individual inbound/outbound services inactive within the arrangement.
3. Split multi-purpose arrangements into separate arrangements per integration scenario / per external system, each bound to the narrowest suitable SAP_COM_* scenario.
4. Assign a dedicated communication user to each arrangement (see RISE-006) so scope and identity are 1:1 with the integration.
5. Prefer narrower SAP-delivered scenarios over broad ones; do not extend a scenario's services beyond documented need.
6. For S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition / on-prem equivalents, review activated OData services in transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and web-service bindings in SOAMANAGER, removing unused ones.
7. Verify: re-open each arrangement and confirm the service count reflects only required services; regression-test each integration after pruning.
8. Document each arrangement's business owner, consuming system and service list for audit evidence.
9. Rollout caution: coordinate with interface owners and change management; deactivate services in a test tenant first, because removing a service in active use will break the integration.

RISE-004.risk: One or more communication arrangements accept inbound calls with no authentication, anonymous access, or only HTTP Basic authentication (username/password). This is the credential front door to the ERP's APIs. 'None/anonymous' means the API can be invoked without proving identity at all - a direct path for any party that can reach the endpoint to read or post business data. Basic authentication means a static, replayable secret guards write access to financial and master-data services; it is vulnerable to interception if TLS is ever mis-terminated, to brute force and credential spraying, and to silent reuse, because a stolen password produces 'valid' traffic with no token expiry or scoping. In a RISE context these arrangements typically expose OData services such as API_BUSINESS_PARTNER, API_JOURNAL_ENTRY or API_PURCHASEORDER, so a compromised or absent credential leads directly to financial fraud, data exfiltration, and integrity damage (fake postings). Attack scenario: an attacker discovers a Basic-auth arrangement, sprays common or reused passwords against the communication user, succeeds, then automates supplier bank-account changes and pushes payment-relevant master data - all appearing as legitimate integration traffic. An unauthenticated arrangement is worse: no credential is needed at all. Business impact includes payment fraud, GDPR-reportable personal-data exposure (business-partner/HR fields), SOX control failure over interface authentication, and a critical audit/pen-test finding. A real incident manifests as anomalous API volume from an unexpected source IP against a single communication user, or postings with no corresponding user session.
RISE-004.mitigation:
1. Treat this as critical/priority remediation and identify every affected arrangement in the 'Maintain Communication Arrangements' app.
2. In the associated 'Maintain Communication Systems' configuration set the inbound authentication method to OAuth 2.0 (client credentials / SAML Bearer) or SSL Client Certificate (X.509), and disable 'User Name and Password' (Basic) for inbound.
3. In the arrangement's Inbound Communication section open OAuth 2.0 Details, generate client credentials or bind the certificate, and distribute secrets to the caller via a secrets manager (never hard-coded).
4. For any arrangement that must temporarily keep Basic auth, enforce TLS end-to-end, set a strong unique password on the communication user, and schedule migration to OAuth/X.509.
5. Ensure no arrangement remains anonymous/none; if an endpoint genuinely needs no auth, remove it.
6. For Private Edition / on-prem services, remove any anonymous logon alias at the ICF node in transaction SICF and require token/cert auth on gateway services via /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE and SOAMANAGER.
7. Rotate the communication users' passwords/secrets after the change and revoke the old Basic credentials.
8. Verify: send an unauthenticated call and an old-Basic call and confirm HTTP 401; confirm the OAuth/certificate flow succeeds for the legitimate client.
9. Enable inbound call logging and monitor for failed-auth spikes.
10. Rollout caution: coordinate the cutover with each interface owner (they must switch auth method at the same time) and pilot in non-production; an uncoordinated switch breaks the interface.

RISE-005.risk: Sensitive OData/REST services matching finance, HR/payroll, procurement, sales or master-data patterns (for example API_BUSINESS_PARTNER, API_JOURNAL_ENTRY, API_SALES_ORDER, /sap/opu/odata/sap/, PAYROLL, HCM_) are published and active. Every activated service is reachable attack surface: even with authentication in place, an exposed sensitive service can be abused by an over-entitled or compromised user/communication account, probed for authorization gaps and injection, or enumerated for bulk data. In S/4HANA these APIs expose highly regulated data and powerful write operations - posting journal entries, changing supplier bank details, reading personnel data. Services left active 'just in case', or activated during a project and never deactivated, are a recurring root cause: they widen the surface with no business owner and no monitoring. Attack scenario: an attacker with a foothold (a leaked communication credential, or a low-privilege dialog user holding an over-broad OData authorization) enumerates active services, finds API_BUSINESS_PARTNER exposed, and mass-exfiltrates supplier/customer PII and bank data via large $batch/$top reads, then attempts writes; because the service was unused and unmonitored the abnormal volume goes unnoticed. Business impact includes a GDPR-reportable data breach, financial fraud and IP loss; from an audit view it is a least-privilege and API-governance failure. A real incident looks like sustained bulk OData reads against a sensitive entity set from a single principal over a short window, only discovered later in gateway logs.
RISE-005.mitigation:
1. Produce an inventory of published/active sensitive services: in S/4HANA Cloud via the communication arrangements/scenarios that expose them; in Private Edition/on-prem via /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE (Activate and Maintain Services), /IWFND/V4_ADMIN for OData V4, and the ICF tree in transaction SICF.
2. For each sensitive service confirm a documented business owner and active consumer; deactivate services with no legitimate consumer (remove/deactivate the arrangement, or set the service inactive in /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE or SICF).
3. Front externally reachable sensitive APIs with SAP API Management and apply OAuth scopes, rate limiting/quota, payload-size limits and IP-allowlist policies.
4. Tighten authorizations so only intended users/communication users can call each service - review SU24 defaults and the roles granting the service's authorization objects, and trace with SU53/ST01.
5. Restrict network exposure through the SAP Cloud Connector access-control allowlist, exposing specific resource paths rather than broad prefixes.
6. Enable gateway/API logging and alerting (/IWFND/ERROR_LOG and access logs) and alert on abnormal volume against sensitive entity sets.
7. Verify: attempt to reach a deactivated service and confirm HTTP 404/403; confirm remaining services enforce authentication and scopes.
8. Re-run the inventory after cleanup and record the approved exposed-service baseline for audit.
9. Rollout caution: validate each deactivation against live interfaces in a test tenant and coordinate with app/interface owners before turning services off in production.

RISE-006.risk: A single communication (technical/system) user is reused across more than three arrangements. Sharing one identity across many integrations breaks accountability and multiplies blast radius. Because all those interfaces authenticate as the same principal, backend and gateway logs cannot distinguish which integration performed a given call, so forensic attribution during an incident is effectively impossible and SoD analysis is muddied. The shared user typically accumulates the union of authorizations required by all the arrangements it serves, becoming an over-privileged account whose single credential unlocks a wide swath of APIs. If that one secret leaks or must be rotated, every dependent interface is simultaneously at risk or disrupted, which discourages timely rotation and encourages long-lived static secrets. Attack scenario: a shared communication user's password/secret is exposed in one poorly secured middleware system; the attacker now wields access to all the finance, procurement and master-data services aggregated on that user, and defenders cannot tell which interface was abused because every call carries the identical identity. Compliance impact includes failure of SOX ITGC around unique user identification and audit trail, GDPR accountability gaps, and standard audit findings on shared/technical-account governance. A real incident looks like the credential appearing in traffic from an unexpected system while the legitimate interfaces continue using the same account, effectively camouflaging the intrusion inside normal integration noise.
RISE-006.mitigation:
1. In the 'Maintain Communication Users' Fiori app list all communication users and cross-reference them to arrangements via 'Maintain Communication Arrangements'.
2. Create a dedicated communication user per integration scenario / per external system (Maintain Communication Users -> New).
3. Re-point each arrangement to its own dedicated user in the 'Maintain Communication Arrangements' app and remove the shared user from arrangements it should no longer serve.
4. Grant each new user only the scope of its single arrangement (least privilege) so its authorizations are no longer the union of many interfaces.
5. Prefer OAuth 2.0 client credentials / X.509 per user over shared static passwords, and store each interface's secret separately in a secrets manager.
6. For Private Edition/on-prem technical users, review type and usage via SU01/SUIM and table USR02 (user type Communications/System) and split shared service users similarly.
7. Establish a secret-rotation schedule now that rotation impacts only one interface at a time.
8. Verify: confirm each arrangement shows a unique communication user and that backend logs now attribute calls to distinct users; regression-test each interface after the change.
9. Rollout caution: coordinate credential cutover with each interface owner and stage in non-production - repointing to a new user requires the calling system to adopt the new credential simultaneously.

RISE-007.risk: API/OData endpoints are configured with no authentication, anonymous access, or only Basic authentication. This is the endpoint-side view of the same weakness as RISE-004 but assessed at the individual service/ICF-node level, and in a RISE landscape every such endpoint is directly reachable attack surface. An anonymous or unauthenticated endpoint lets any party that can route to it invoke the service and read or manipulate data with no identity at all; a Basic-only endpoint protects potentially powerful operations with a single static, replayable secret susceptible to interception, brute force and credential spraying, and it provides no token scoping or expiry. Attackers routinely scan for active OData paths (for example under /sap/opu/odata/sap/) and probe their authentication; a weakly protected endpoint that reads or writes business data is a direct route to data exfiltration, fraudulent postings, or a pivot deeper into the system. Attack scenario: a scanner finds an OData service whose SICF node carries an anonymous logon alias; the attacker reads an entity set full of personal or financial data without credentials, then tests write operations; nothing in the logs shows a failed logon because none was required. Business impact spans GDPR-reportable breaches, financial fraud, and SOX interface-control failures, and it is a standard critical pen-test/audit finding. A real incident looks like data pulls or writes against an OData service from an unexpected source with no corresponding authenticated user session.
RISE-007.mitigation:
1. Enumerate endpoints and their authentication: in S/4HANA Cloud via the communication arrangements/systems; in Private Edition/on-prem via /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE (and /IWFND/V4_ADMIN) and the ICF service tree in transaction SICF.
2. Enforce OAuth 2.0 (client_credentials or authorization_code/SAML Bearer) or X.509 certificate authentication on every endpoint and disable Basic and anonymous access.
3. In SICF remove any anonymous or stored-logon alias from the service nodes so each call must authenticate, and set the correct logon procedure.
4. For gateway/OData services ensure the system alias and service require authentication and that no service is published without an auth method.
5. Place external-facing APIs behind SAP API Management and enforce OAuth scope, quota/rate-limit and IP-allowlist policies centrally.
6. Restrict reachability via the SAP Cloud Connector access-control allowlist to only the required resource paths.
7. Rotate any credentials previously protected only by Basic auth and remove the legacy Basic secrets after cutover.
8. Verify: send an unauthenticated request and a stale-Basic request and confirm HTTP 401/403; confirm the OAuth/certificate flow works for the legitimate client and review /IWFND/ERROR_LOG.
9. Rollout caution: coordinate with each API consumer to switch auth methods together and validate in a non-production tenant first to avoid breaking live integrations.

BTP-CC-001.risk: The SAP Cloud Connector (SCC) tunnels on-premise backend systems into BTP, and each exposed backend carries a list of 'Resources' (URL path prefixes) whose access policy is either 'Path' (exact node) or 'Path And All Sub-Paths'. A wildcard or root-level mapping ('/', '/*', or a broad '/sap' set to sub-paths) exposes the entire ICF/HTTP namespace of the S/4HANA or ECC backend to any BTP application bound to that connection. This defeats the whole point of the SCC allowlist: instead of publishing only the two or three OData services an extension app needs, the tunnel now reaches every ICF node - SICF administration, WebGUI, ABAP Development Tools (/sap/bc/adt), the SOAP-to-RFC bridge, WebRFC, BSP applications and Web Dynpro. Attack scenario: an adversary who compromises a bound BTP app (leaked service key, SSRF in an extension, a vulnerable custom app) or a malicious insider with a destination over this SCC pivots through the tunnel and reaches interactive and code-level functions on the core ERP. With WebGUI they obtain a dialog session as the propagated or technical user; with ADT they read and deploy ABAP; with the RFC bridge they invoke function modules. Business impact is a direct cloud-to-core lateral-movement path: data exfiltration, fraudulent financial postings, creation of backdoor users, and effective remote code execution - precisely the network-segmentation control that RISE is meant to guarantee. It is a clear breach of least-privilege and defense-in-depth expectations under SOX ITGC, ISO 27001 A.13, and the SAP Security Baseline. A real incident surfaces as anomalous ICF access from the SCC service user to /sap/bc/gui or /sap/bc/adt, originating from a cloud IP, well outside the small set of services the business actually integrates.
BTP-CC-001.mitigation:
1. Open the Cloud Connector administration UI (https://<scc-host>:8443) and log on with an Administrator account.
2. Navigate to 'Cloud To On-Premise' > select each Subaccount > 'Access Control' tab.
3. For every mapped backend, review the 'Resources Accessible On <virtual host>' list.
4. Remove any resource whose URL Path is '/', '/*', or a broad '/sap' entry configured as 'Path And All Sub-Paths'.
5. Re-add only the genuinely required APIs as specific paths (for example /sap/opu/odata/sap/<SERVICE>) and set the access policy to 'Path' (exact) for single-node consumers; use 'Path And All Sub-Paths' only for a tightly scoped service root.
6. Verify the protocol (HTTP/HTTPS/RFC) and virtual-to-internal host mapping for each retained entry.
7. On the backend ABAP system, deactivate unneeded ICF services in transaction SICF and enforce RFC allowlisting via UCON (transaction UCONCOCKPIT) so an over-broad mapping cannot reach sensitive nodes even if reintroduced.
8. Save; the SCC applies changes immediately with no restart required.
9. Verify from a bound BTP destination that only intended paths respond and that '/' returns HTTP 403/404 through the tunnel.
10. Rollout caution: tighten the DEV/QA subaccount first and confirm with extension/interface owners, because removing a path an iFlow silently depends on will break the integration; record a business justification per mapping and schedule a recurring SCC access-control review.

BTP-CC-002.risk: A defined subset of Cloud Connector-exposed paths is inherently dangerous regardless of how narrowly the rest of the tunnel is scoped: /sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui (interactive SAP GUI for HTML, full transaction access), /sap/bc/soap/rfc and /sap/bc/srt/rfc (SOAP-to-RFC bridges that invoke RFC-enabled function modules), /sap/bc/webrfc (WebRFC), /sap/bc/adt (ABAP Development Tools - read/write source, execute classes and reports), /sap/bc/bsp (Business Server Pages) and broad /sap/opu/odata/sap or /sap/es5 gateways. Publishing these through the SCC turns it into a cloud-reachable interactive gateway to the ERP core. Attack scenario: an attacker who lands on a BTP app bound to a destination over this SCC can, via WebGUI, drive dialog transactions (SE38, SE16, SM59, SU01, PFCG) as far as the propagated or technical user's authorizations permit - enabling data theft, master-data manipulation, or creation of a privileged backdoor user; via the SOAP/WebRFC path they can call powerful function modules for effective remote code execution; via ADT they can inject ABAP that later runs with high privilege. Business impact is a straight cloud-to-core RCE and fraud path that bypasses the network isolation RISE is designed to provide, and is exactly the exposure that leads to ransomware staging, sabotage of financial data, or large-scale exfiltration of personal and financial records (GDPR/SOX exposure). A real incident manifests as GUI, ADT, or RFC calls entering the ABAP stack from the SCC service user outside the handful of OData services the business genuinely consumes, frequently at unusual times.
BTP-CC-002.mitigation:
1. In the SCC admin UI, open 'Cloud To On-Premise' > 'Access Control' and audit each backend's resource list against the high-risk paths above.
2. Remove /sap/bc/gui/sap/its/webgui, /sap/bc/adt, /sap/bc/webrfc, /sap/bc/soap/rfc, /sap/bc/srt/rfc and /sap/bc/bsp unless a documented cloud use case requires them.
3. Where an OData gateway is needed, expose only the specific service path /sap/opu/odata/sap/<SERVICE> with policy 'Path', never the whole /sap tree.
4. On the backend, deactivate the corresponding ICF nodes in transaction SICF for services that must not be reachable at all.
5. For RFC traffic that must legitimately flow, restrict callable function modules with UCON allowlists in transaction UCONCOCKPIT.
6. Enforce principal propagation so calls run as the real end user, and confirm those users lack SE38/SE16/S_DEVELOP/debug authorizations (analyse with SUIM and check S_TCODE, S_DEVELOP, S_RFC).
7. Save SCC changes (applied live) and re-test from the bound BTP destination.
8. Verify each removed path now returns HTTP 403 through the tunnel.
9. Rollout caution: confirm with integration and remote-support owners that WebGUI/ADT are not used by a legitimate Fiori or support scenario before removal, and stage in non-production.
10. Document any remaining exposed high-risk path with a named business owner, compensating controls, and a quarterly re-review date.

BTP-CC-003.risk: The Cloud Connector is configured with an unusually large number of backend systems (above the review threshold). Each configured backend is a live doorway from BTP into the on-premise network, so the aggregate count is a direct measure of attack surface and of operational blast radius. A sprawling SCC typically accumulates entries for decommissioned sandboxes, retired test systems, one-off proof-of-concept backends, and duplicated dev/test/prod targets that nobody owns. The danger is threefold. First, forgotten backends often retain permissive resource lists and stale technical users, so a single compromised BTP binding can reach systems the security team no longer monitors. Second, mixing dev, QA and production backends behind one SCC removes environment isolation, so a weakly protected non-prod app can be used as a stepping stone toward production. Third, the larger the configuration, the less likely each mapping receives a proper least-privilege review, and misconfigurations hide in the noise. Business impact is elevated breach likelihood and a governance gap: auditors expect a documented, owned, minimal inventory of connections between the cloud and the ERP, and an unbounded SCC fails that test under ISO 27001 asset-management and the SAP Security Baseline connectivity guidance. A real incident often begins with an attacker enumerating tunnelled hosts from a compromised app and finding an unpatched, unmonitored legacy backend still reachable through the connector.
BTP-CC-003.mitigation:
1. In the SCC admin UI export or review the full list of mapped systems under 'Cloud To On-Premise' for each subaccount.
2. Cross-reference every backend against the current system inventory/CMDB and confirm an active business owner and integration use case.
3. Delete mappings for decommissioned, sandbox, or proof-of-concept systems.
4. Separate environments: run distinct Cloud Connector instances (or at least distinct subaccounts) for DEV/QA versus PROD so non-prod cannot tunnel to production backends.
5. For remaining backends, re-verify each resource list is minimal (see BTP-CC-001/002) and that principal propagation is used.
6. Deploy the SCC in a master/shadow high-availability pair in the DMZ, and restrict at the network firewall which internal hosts the SCC server may reach.
7. Enable and forward SCC audit logs to the SIEM to detect access to rarely used backends.
8. Establish a recurring (quarterly) SCC inventory review with sign-off by connection owners.
9. Verify the fix by re-running the scanner or re-exporting the config and confirming the backend count is within the agreed threshold with every entry owned.
10. Rollout caution: archive/export each configuration before deletion and confirm with interface owners, since removing an active but low-traffic backend can silently break a scheduled integration.

BTP-CC-004.risk: The Cloud Connector access-control model is the primary gate that decides which internal hosts and resources the tunnel is permitted to reach; the module found ACL entries with no host restriction (empty allowlist or wildcards such as '*', '0.0.0.0/0', 'any'). Without an explicit, minimal host allowlist, a bound BTP service can be pointed at any internal host and port the SCC server can route to, turning the connector into an open reverse proxy into the corporate network. This is dangerous because the SCC by design sits between the untrusted cloud and the trusted on-premise zone: its allowlist is often the last enforceable boundary. Attack scenario: an attacker who obtains a valid BTP destination or service binding over this SCC (leaked service key, compromised app, insider) crafts requests to internal hosts that were never meant to be cloud-reachable - domain controllers, databases, management interfaces, other SAP systems - performing internal reconnaissance and SSRF-style pivoting from the cloud. Combined with weak backend authentication this yields lateral movement well beyond the intended integration. Business impact is loss of the cloud-to-on-prem trust boundary, expanded ransomware/exfiltration reach, and failure of network-segmentation controls demanded by SOX ITGC and ISO 27001. A real incident looks like the SCC service account initiating connections to internal IPs and ports that correspond to no documented integration.
BTP-CC-004.mitigation:
1. In the SCC admin UI, review 'Cloud To On-Premise' access control for each subaccount and confirm that only explicitly required internal host:port entries are mapped (the SCC does not support wildcard hosts by design - remove any over-broad virtual-to-internal mappings).
2. For each mapped backend, ensure the resource allowlist is minimal (align with BTP-CC-001/002) so the host is reachable only on the intended paths.
3. Restrict at the network layer: configure the firewall so the SCC host can egress only to the specific internal SAP backends it must serve, denying all other internal destinations.
4. Enforce principal propagation so end-user identity, not a shared technical user, governs backend access.
5. Validate that each connected subaccount is one you own and expect under the SCC 'Subaccounts' registration; remove unknown subaccount trusts.
6. Place the SCC in a hardened DMZ segment with OS hardening and restricted admin access.
7. Enable SCC audit logging and forward to the SIEM with alerts on connections to non-allowlisted internal hosts.
8. Save changes (applied immediately) and test that a bound destination can reach only the intended host/port and is refused elsewhere.
9. Verify via the SCC 'Monitoring' view and firewall logs that no unexpected internal destinations are reachable.
10. Rollout caution: coordinate firewall tightening with the network team and stage in non-production, as an overly aggressive egress rule can break legitimate interfaces.

BTP-CC-005.risk: The Cloud Connector relies on X.509 certificates for two critical functions: the system certificate that authenticates and secures the tunnel to the SAP BTP region, and the CA/system certificate used for principal propagation (the short-lived user certificates the SCC issues and the backend trusts via STRUST/CERTRULE). The scan found certificates that are expired or expiring within the warning window. An expired tunnel or CA certificate has an immediate operational and security effect: the SCC tunnel drops or principal propagation stops working, breaking every integration that flows through it (Fiori extensions, iFlows, replication jobs). During the outage, teams under pressure frequently apply unsafe workarounds - disabling TLS validation, reissuing certificates without proper CA governance, or temporarily widening access - which introduce lasting weaknesses. From a pure security standpoint, an expired principal-propagation trust can also cause silent fallback to a shared technical user, collapsing user-level accountability and auditability on the backend. Business impact includes unplanned integration downtime affecting order-to-cash and other core processes, plus loss of end-user attribution for backend actions (a SOX and GDPR accountability gap). A real incident typically presents as a sudden mass integration failure at the exact certificate expiry timestamp, followed by a rushed, poorly documented certificate replacement.
BTP-CC-005.mitigation:
1. In the SCC admin UI, open 'Configuration' and review the system/CA certificates and, under each subaccount, the tunnel and principal-propagation certificates and their validity dates.
2. Identify all certificates that are expired or within 90 days of expiry (align the warning window with your policy).
3. Generate a new key pair and CSR for the affected certificate using RSA 2048+ (or ECDSA P-256+) and obtain a certificate from your enterprise/managed CA.
4. Import the renewed certificate in the SCC and, for principal propagation, update the trusted CA in the backend via transaction STRUST and the certificate-to-user mapping rules in CERTRULE.
5. Renew at least 30 days before expiry to allow validation, and test a live tunnel connection and a principal-propagated backend call after replacement.
6. Implement automated certificate-expiry monitoring (SCC alerting plus an external monitor/SIEM alert) so expirations are caught weeks ahead.
7. Document the CA hierarchy and renewal runbook so replacements never require unsafe workarounds.
8. Verify by confirming the SCC tunnel status is 'Connected' and that backend calls arrive as the propagated end user (not a fallback technical user), checked in the backend security audit log (SM20/RSAU_READ_LOG).
9. Rollout caution: schedule renewals in a maintenance window, coordinate with interface owners, and keep the outgoing certificate trusted in STRUST until the new one is fully validated to enable rollback.

BTP-CC-006.risk: One or more Cloud Connector certificates use weak cryptography - key sizes below 2048 bits or deprecated signature algorithms such as SHA-1 or MD5. These certificates secure the tunnel between BTP and the on-premise landscape and, for principal propagation, vouch for the identity asserted to the backend. Weak keys and hashes are vulnerable to practical cryptographic attacks: SHA-1 has demonstrated collision attacks and RSA keys under 2048 bits are considered forgeable by well-resourced adversaries. If an attacker can forge or collide a certificate that the backend trusts, they can mount a man-in-the-middle attack on the tunnel or, worse, forge principal-propagation assertions that impersonate arbitrary end users on the ERP - authenticating as a privileged user without ever knowing a password. Attack scenario: a network-positioned adversary (or one who has compromised an intermediary) uses the weak trust anchor to intercept or inject traffic, or to present a fraudulent client certificate that the backend's STRUST/CERTRULE accepts, gaining authenticated access as a high-privilege identity. Business impact is compromise of the confidentiality and integrity of all cloud-to-core traffic and a total breakdown of identity assurance, enabling fraud and data theft while defeating audit trails. It is a direct violation of the SAP Security Baseline and modern TLS guidance (SAP Note 510007). A real incident may be nearly invisible, as forged-identity access blends into legitimate propagated traffic.
BTP-CC-006.mitigation:
1. In the SCC admin UI, inventory all certificates (system, tunnel, principal-propagation CA) under 'Configuration' and each subaccount, noting key size and signature algorithm.
2. Replace every certificate below RSA 2048 bits (prefer RSA 3072/4096 or ECDSA P-256+) and any signed with SHA-1/MD5, reissuing with SHA-256 or stronger.
3. Generate fresh key pairs and CSRs in the SCC and obtain new certificates from a CA that enforces modern algorithms.
4. On the backend, update the trusted CA and system PSE in transaction STRUST and revalidate the certificate-mapping rules in CERTRULE (avoid wildcard mappings; map on a strong attribute such as email/UID).
