Because GET_ENTITYSET/GET_ENTITY or the FM body never calls AUTHORITY-CHECK on the relevant object (e.g., F_BKPF_BUK for company code, or a business object authorization), the user reads or posts data they are not authorized for.
This is a classic horizontal/vertical privilege escalation: display roles become effectively write roles, and company-code / plant segregation is bypassed. RFC-enabled FMs are especially dangerous because they are reachable from other systems and from external callers holding an RFC user.
Business impact: broken segregation of duties, unauthorized access to HR/finance/PII data, and audit findings against SOX access controls and GDPR least-privilege. A real incident manifests as a user performing actions their role review says are impossible - detectable only if the object is later instrumented, since no failed-auth trace (SU53/ST01) is ever generated when the check is simply absent.
CODE-INJ-002.mitigation:
1. Review each flagged object in SE37/SE80/ADT and identify the sensitive operation (data read, posting, config change) that must be gated.
2. Add AUTHORITY-CHECK OBJECT '...' for the correct authorization object(s) at method/FM entry - e.g., S_TCODE plus business objects like F_BKPF_BUK, and check ACTVT for the actual operation; SY-SUBRC <> 0 must abort.
3. Use SU22/SU24 to maintain authorization default values so the objects flow into PFCG roles via SU25/SU24 proposals.
4. For SEGW/OData services, implement the check in the DPC_EXT GET_ENTITYSET/GET_ENTITY (and CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE) methods; for RAP business objects, define authorization control in the Behavior Definition.
5. For RFC-enabled FMs, also confirm S_RFC and S_RFCACL are appropriately restricted so external callers cannot reach the FM broadly.
6. Run SAP Code Vulnerability Analyzer 'missing authorization check' variant in transaction ATC over the affected packages to find all instances.
7. Rebuild affected PFCG roles (transaction PFCG), regenerate profiles, and re-run SU25 step 2 where SU24 defaults changed.
8. Verify with a low-privileged test user: attempt the operation and confirm it is now denied, and inspect SU53/ST01 to see the expected failed AUTHORITY-CHECK trace.
9. Confirm authorized users still succeed (no over-restriction) via QA regression with the app owner.
10. Transport DEV->QA->PRD and coordinate role changes with the authorization team; monitor the Security Audit Log (SM20/RSAU) for auth failures post go-live.

CODE-INJ-003.risk:
Credentials are embedded directly in ABAP source: passwords, API keys, bearer tokens, client secrets or private keys as string literals. Because ABAP source is stored in the repository and moved through transports, a hardcoded secret is exposed to everyone with SE38/SE80/SE84 display, anyone reading the transport data files (E070/E071 / cofiles/data files on the transport directory), and anyone with version-history access. Rotating such a secret is slow and often forgotten, so it tends to live for years.
Attack scenario: an insider with only display-level developer access, or an attacker who has compromised the transport filesystem or a QA/sandbox copy, greps the source (or the exported transport) for PASSWORD/SECRET/API_KEY and recovers a working credential. If it is an RFC/technical user password, a HANA/DB credential, or a BTP/cloud API key, the attacker pivots to the target system with that identity - potentially a high-privilege service account.
Because the same code is copied to non-production systems (which are usually less protected and more widely accessible), the blast radius includes every landscape tier and every refresh copy.
Business impact: credential theft leading to lateral movement, data breach, and non-repudiation loss (actions performed under a shared technical identity cannot be attributed). It also breaches PCI-DSS, GDPR and SAP Security Baseline requirements to never store secrets in clear text. A real incident looks like a technical/RFC account authenticating from an unexpected host, discovered only after the fact in the Security Audit Log.
CODE-INJ-003.mitigation:
1. Treat every flagged secret as already compromised: identify the target system/account and rotate the credential immediately, in parallel with code fixes.
2. Remove the literal from source; for RFC/system credentials store them in an SM59 destination (which holds the logon data encrypted) and have code call via the destination, not embedded logon.
3. For application secrets/keys, use the ABAP Secure Storage (SECSTORE / class-based secure store) or STRUST/SSF PSEs for certificates and private keys - never a source literal.
4. On SAP BTP, move secrets to the Credential Store service or the Destination service / XSUAA bindings and read them at runtime.
5. Purge the secret from history: because it persists in transport data files and prior versions, coordinate with Basis to ensure old transports containing it are not re-imported, and restrict the transport directory.
6. Enable CVA (report RSLIN_SEC_LICENSE_SETUP) and run the ATC hardcoded-credential/'password in source' checks across all Z*/Y* packages to catch remaining instances.
7. Tighten access: restrict S_DEVELOP and table/source display (S_TABU_*) in production and non-production copies to limit who can read source.
8. Verify by re-grepping source and re-running ATC to confirm no literal secrets remain, and confirm the app still authenticates using the externalized credential.
9. Transport DEV->QA->PRD; coordinate the credential rotation window with the interface owner to avoid outage.
10. Add a standing ATC/CVA gate so future transports with embedded secrets are blocked at release.

CODE-STMT-001.risk:
Custom code uses dangerous or obsolete ABAP statements. This family covers: CALL 'SYSTEM' / CALL 'C_SAPGPARAM' (direct kernel calls that run OS commands or read system parameters), GENERATE SUBROUTINE POOL / GENERATE REPORT (runtime code generation), INSERT/DELETE/READ REPORT (reading or modifying ABAP source at runtime), EDITOR-CALL (opening the ABAP editor at runtime), EXPORT ... TO MEMORY (cross-session data leakage), and CALL TRANSACTION / SUBMIT ... VIA JOB used without a preceding authority check.
The kernel and code-generation statements are the most dangerous: CALL 'SYSTEM' executes operating-system commands under the SAP service account (typically <sid>adm), and GENERATE/INSERT REPORT let an attacker construct and run arbitrary ABAP at runtime. If any of these consume user-controlled input, the result is remote code execution on the application server - the highest-impact SAP compromise.
Attack scenario: an attacker who can influence a parameter that flows into CALL 'SYSTEM' or a generated program injects an OS command or ABAP snippet, gaining a shell as <sid>adm or executing privileged ABAP, then reads/writes the HANA database, secure store, or transport directory. CALL TRANSACTION/SUBMIT without AUTHORITY-CHECK/WITH AUTHORITY-CHECK allows starting transactions the user is not entitled to.
Business impact: full system takeover, data destruction, planting of backdoors, and complete audit-trail bypass (an OS-level attacker can alter logs). Even without user input, these statements defeat static review and upgrade safety. A real incident looks like unexpected OS processes or files owned by <sid>adm, or unexplained new/modified programs in TRDIR.
CODE-STMT-001.mitigation:
1. Inventory each occurrence via ATC/Code Inspector (transaction ATC/SCI) 'critical statements'/security variant and the SLIN extended program check; rank CALL 'SYSTEM', GENERATE*, INSERT/DELETE REPORT, EDITOR-CALL first.
2. Eliminate CALL 'SYSTEM': replace ad-hoc OS calls with defined external commands in SM69, executed via SM49/SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE, which enforce authorization object S_LOG_COM and an allow-list of commands/parameters.
3. Remove GENERATE SUBROUTINE POOL/GENERATE REPORT/INSERT REPORT patterns; refactor to BAdI/enhancement-framework implementations, predefined method dispatch, or configuration tables instead of runtime code generation.
4. Remove READ/DELETE REPORT and EDITOR-CALL from productive code; these have no legitimate place in business logic.
5. For every retained CALL TRANSACTION add ... WITH AUTHORITY-CHECK, and precede SUBMIT/CALL TRANSACTION with an explicit AUTHORITY-CHECK on the target transaction (S_TCODE) and business objects.
6. Replace EXPORT ... TO MEMORY with session-scoped, keyed shared memory or explicit parameter passing to prevent cross-session leakage.
7. Harden the platform: restrict S_LOG_COM, S_DATASET (file access), S_DEVELOP; and constrain runtime debugging via profile parameter rdisp/wpdbug_max_no (coordinate the value/restart with SAP ECS under RISE).
8. Re-run ATC/SLIN to confirm the critical statements are gone or exempted with documented justification.
9. Verify functionally in QA that refactored logic behaves identically and that the SM69 command path enforces S_LOG_COM.
10. Transport DEV->QA->PRD; add an ATC gate at release so these statements cannot re-enter productive code.

CODE-ATC-001.risk:
The ABAP Test Cockpit / Code Inspector scan reports Critical (priority 1 / error) findings that remain in an unresolved state (not fixed, exempted, or marked false-positive). Critical ATC findings from the security variant typically map to genuinely exploitable defects - injection, missing authority checks, directory traversal, hardcoded secrets, insecure dynamic calls - present in code that is live in production.
The danger is twofold. First, each unresolved critical finding is an individual, exploitable vulnerability (see the injection and auth-check risks). Second, a large backlog of unresolved criticals indicates the organization has no effective secure-SDLC gate: vulnerable code ships freely, and there is no evidence of remediation for auditors.
Attack scenario: an attacker or red-team enumerates custom Z-objects (many are RFC- or OData-exposed), then targets the exact weaknesses ATC already documented internally - effectively a pre-written vulnerability catalog. Because custom code often runs with powerful technical/DB context, one exploited critical can yield data theft, unauthorized posting, or RCE.
Business impact: elevated breach likelihood, failure of SOX ITGC change-management/secure-development controls, and GDPR exposure. A real incident is an exploited custom object whose flaw was already listed as an open critical ATC finding that no one actioned - a governance failure that materially worsens regulatory and legal consequences.
CODE-ATC-001.mitigation:
1. Export the current ATC result set (transaction ATC, or the central ATC quality-governance run) and confirm the count of open Priority 1 / error security findings.
2. Enable the full CVA security check set (report RSLIN_SEC_LICENSE_SETUP) if not already active, so criticals reflect the complete security ruleset.
3. Triage each critical: assign an owner (development team), and classify as fix / true false-positive / risk-accepted.
4. Remediate genuine findings at the source (see CODE-INJ-001/002/003 and CODE-STMT-001 procedures) with the highest-severity, most-exposed (RFC/OData) objects first.
5. Use the formal ATC exemption process (with approver and expiry) only for confirmed false positives - never to silence real issues.
6. Establish a remediation SLA (e.g., criticals within 30 days) tracked in the ATC quality governance dashboard.
7. Integrate ATC into the transport workflow (ATC check on transport release) so any new Priority 1 finding blocks release in DEV; configure the central check system and check variant accordingly.
8. Re-run the ATC mass run after fixes and confirm the open-critical count trends to zero.
9. Verify representative fixes functionally in QA (drive the affected app/service) before production import.
10. Report residual accepted risks to the security/GRC owner and transport fixes DEV->QA->PRD on the normal change path.

CODE-ATC-002.risk:
The ATC/Code Inspector scan shows High-severity (priority 2 / warning) findings that are unresolved. While individually lower-impact than criticals, High findings still represent real security and robustness weaknesses - for example incomplete authority checks, unsafe dynamic constructs, weak input handling, or performance/robustness issues that can be chained into a larger exploit or lead to denial of service.
A persistent High backlog signals that secure-coding standards are not enforced and that technical debt is accumulating in code that runs with business-critical data access. Attackers frequently chain a High-severity weakness (e.g., partial authorization coverage or an information-disclosure defect) with another flaw to reach a critical outcome; defenders who ignore Highs leave those chains intact.
Attack scenario: an authenticated user leverages a High-rated missing/partial check in a custom OData service to enumerate data they should not see, or triggers an unhandled dynamic call to cause a short dump / resource exhaustion affecting availability of a productive Fiori app.
Business impact: gradual erosion of the control environment, audit findings on secure development lifecycle maturity, and increased likelihood that a future change introduces a critical. A real incident is rarely a single High alone, but Highs are the raw material for the incidents that do occur, and their neglect is itself an ITGC deficiency.
CODE-ATC-002.mitigation:
1. Extract the open Priority 2 / warning findings from transaction ATC and group them by object, package, and finding type.
2. Assign each group to the responsible development owner and set a remediation target (SAP practice: resolve within ~90 days of detection).
3. Prioritize High findings on RFC/OData-exposed and finance/HR-relevant objects, since these are the most reachable and sensitive.
4. Remediate at source following secure-ABAP guidance; convert repeated patterns into reusable secure helper methods to prevent recurrence.
5. Use the ATC exemption workflow (with justification and expiry) only for validated false positives.
6. Track progress in the ATC quality-governance dashboard and review the trend in security/change-advisory meetings.
7. Add ATC to the transport release check so new High findings are surfaced (warn or block per policy) before code leaves DEV.
8. Re-run the ATC mass scan after each remediation sprint and confirm the open-High count is decreasing.
9. Verify fixes in QA by exercising the affected functionality; ensure no regression for authorized users.
10. Transport fixes DEV->QA->PRD on the standard change path and report residual accepted Highs to the GRC owner.

CODE-TMS-001.risk:
The Transport Management System (STMS) transport routes are configured so that a development system delivers directly to production, with no consolidation/delivery route through a QA/quality tier. This collapses the mandatory three-system (DEV->QA->PRD) landscape and removes the integration-test and quality gate between coding and production.
The danger is both technical and governance. Technically, untested code and customizing reach production without regression, security (ATC), or performance validation - raising the chance of outages, data corruption, and shipping exploitable defects straight to the live system. From a control standpoint, a direct dev->prod route is the enabling condition for unreviewed and unapproved change: a developer can move objects to production without any independent step.
Attack/abuse scenario: a developer (or an attacker who has compromised a developer account) creates a transport containing malicious or fraudulent logic - e.g., a backdoor FM, a payment-redirection change, or a weakened authority check - and releases it straight into production, bypassing QA review and four-eyes approval entirely.
Business impact: loss of segregation between development and production, SOX ITGC change-management failure, elevated fraud and RCE risk, and inability to demonstrate a controlled promotion path to auditors. A real incident looks like an unauthorized production behavior change traced back to a transport that never passed through QA.
CODE-TMS-001.mitigation:
1. In STMS (domain controller) -> Transport Routes -> graphical editor, document the current route topology and identify every consolidation/delivery route whose target is the production SID.
2. Redesign to a strict DEV -> QA(consolidation) -> PRD(delivery) path: define the standard transport layer with a consolidation route DEV->QA and a delivery route QA->PRD; remove any direct DEV->PRD route.
3. Distribute and activate the corrected route configuration to all systems in the transport domain.
4. Activate the 'Single transports' strategy and disable 'Import All' shortcuts so ad-hoc bulk imports cannot bypass the path.
5. Turn on the QA Approval Procedure on the QA system ('Delivery after confirmation') so transports must be confirmed before they can be delivered onward to PRD.
6. Restrict STMS/route administration authorizations (S_CTS_ADMI, S_TRANSPRT) to a small Basis group so routes cannot be re-opened.
7. Verify by inspecting the route graph (no DEV->PRD edge) and by test-transporting a dummy request end-to-end, confirming it must pass QA before PRD is offered.
8. Confirm import queues on PRD only accept requests that traversed QA (STMS_IMPORT).
9. Coordinate the change window with Basis and, under RISE, with SAP ECS since TMS domain changes affect the managed landscape.
10. Retain the before/after route configuration as audit evidence of the corrected promotion path.

CODE-TMS-002.risk:
Transports were imported into the production system with no recorded approval. Change management requires that every production change be explicitly authorized before import; an unapproved import means code/customizing entered production outside the control framework, with no evidence that a change owner, CAB, or QA confirmation ever sanctioned it.
The danger is that unapproved imports are the mechanism by which fraudulent, malicious, or simply untested changes reach production undetected. Without an approval gate, a developer or Basis operator can push arbitrary objects live, and there is no independent checkpoint to catch a payment-redirection change, a backdoor, a weakened role, or a risky config toggle.
Attack/abuse scenario: an insider prepares a transport containing a self-benefiting change (e.g., altering a tolerance/approval limit, adding themselves to a role via a role transport, or inserting a backdoor RFC), then imports it into production during an unmonitored window. Because no approval was required, the change looks like routine activity in the STMS import history.
Business impact: direct SOX ITGC change-approval control failure (a reportable deficiency), heightened fraud and sabotage risk, and inability to prove change legitimacy during audit or incident response. A real incident is a production defect or fraud whose root-cause transport shows a blank approver field - demonstrating the control never operated.
CODE-TMS-002.mitigation:
1. Extract the production import history (STMS_IMPORT import history / table records) and reconcile every prod import against an approval record.
2. Implement an enforced approval gate: activate the STMS QA Approval Procedure on the pre-prod system, or adopt SAP Change Request Management (ChaRM) in Solution Manager / SAP Cloud ALM so imports require a released, approved change document.
3. Define the approval RACI: change owner + technical approver (four-eyes) must sign off before any production import.
4. Restrict production import authorization (S_CTS_ADMI import functions, STMS import) to a named Basis group that operates only against approved changes.
5. For the existing unapproved imports, perform retroactive review: validate each change is legitimate, and document a formal after-the-fact approval or raise an exception.
6. Enable/verify the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU_CONFIG) and transport logging so future imports are traceable to a user and time.
7. Verify the control by test: attempt a production import of a request that has no approval and confirm it is blocked/queued pending approval.
8. Establish an emergency-change (break-glass) process that still captures a post-hoc approval and review, so 'urgent' is not a bypass.
9. Roll out with Basis and, under RISE, coordinate with SAP ECS for any TMS domain configuration change.
10. Report residual unapproved imports and the remediation to the GRC/audit owner as evidence the gap is closed.

CODE-TMS-003.risk:
One or more transports were both released (in the source system) and imported (into the target/production system) by the same user. This violates the maker-checker / four-eyes principle and segregation of duties: the person who authors and releases a change must not also be the person who promotes it to production. When one identity controls both ends, there is no independent verification between development and go-live.
The danger is that a single actor can unilaterally push their own change into production. Combined with any authoring capability, this lets a developer bypass review entirely - the exact condition that enables insider fraud and sabotage, because the same person defines the change, releases it, and lands it live.
Attack/abuse scenario: a developer with both release authorization and production-import authorization crafts a transport that, for example, raises their own approval limit, adds a critical authorization to their role, or plants a backdoor, then releases and imports it themselves within minutes - no other human ever sees it.
Business impact: a textbook SOX segregation-of-duties deficiency, high fraud/insider-threat exposure, and failure of the change-management control that auditors specifically test. A real incident looks like a production change where the STMS release user and import user are identical and no third party reviewed it, undermining non-repudiation and control assurance.
CODE-TMS-003.mitigation:
1. From the transport history, list all requests where the release user equals the import user for production targets and treat them as SoD exceptions to review.
2. Separate the roles: developers hold release authorization in DEV (S_TRANSPRT) but must NOT hold production import authorization; Basis operators hold import authorization (S_CTS_ADMI import functions) but do not develop/release.
3. Review PFCG roles (transaction PFCG) and remove the combination of transport-release and production-import authorizations from any single role/user; use SUIM/RSUSR008_009_NEW to detect the toxic combination.
4. Enforce technically via the STMS QA Approval Procedure and/or ChaRM so the importer/approver must differ from the releaser.
5. For the flagged historical transports, validate each change was legitimate and document compensating review; escalate anything suspicious.
6. Restrict who can perform STMS imports to a named operations team, and log imports via the Security Audit Log.
7. Verify by testing that a user who released a transport cannot also import it into production (import is blocked or requires a different approver).
8. Re-run the SoD/critical-combination report to confirm no user retains both capabilities.
9. Coordinate role changes with the authorization team and communicate the new release/import responsibilities to developers and Basis.
10. Report the closed exceptions and the enforced control to GRC/audit as evidence.

CODE-TMS-004.risk:
Transports were imported into the target system on weekends / outside normal change windows. Off-hours imports are not inherently malicious, but they are a recognized indicator of change-control weakness: they often coincide with reduced oversight, absent approvers, skipped CAB review, and thin monitoring, which is precisely when unauthorized or emergency-disguised changes are most likely to slip through.
The danger is reduced detective control. A change made at 02:00 on a Sunday is less likely to be observed, correlated, or challenged in real time; if it is malicious or erroneous, the window before anyone notices is longer, increasing dwell time and impact. Attackers and malicious insiders deliberately choose low-staffing windows for exactly this reason.
Abuse scenario: an insider schedules or manually triggers a production import during a weekend maintenance slot, relying on the absence of the normal approval/monitoring staff to land a change (e.g., a config or role modification) that would draw scrutiny during business hours. By the time it is reviewed on Monday, the change has already executed against live data.
Business impact: weakened change-window governance, potential SOX finding if off-hours changes lack the same approval rigor, and slower incident detection. A real incident is a problematic production change that was imported outside the sanctioned window and correlated only after downstream effects appeared, having evaded timely review.
CODE-TMS-004.mitigation:
1. Extract the off-hours/weekend imports from the transport history and reconcile each against an approved change record and an authorized maintenance window.
2. Define and publish formal change windows and change-freeze periods in the change policy (and in ChaRM / SAP Cloud ALM if used).
3. Require an explicit emergency/exception approval for any import outside the standard window, captured before the import.
4. For each flagged import, confirm it had valid emergency justification; document a post-implementation review for those that did, and raise exceptions for those that did not.
5. Ensure off-hours imports still pass the same QA Approval Procedure and four-eyes controls as business-hours imports - the window must not lower the bar.
6. Enable/verify the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU_CONFIG) and alert on production imports occurring outside defined windows (e.g., via monitoring on STMS import events).
7. Restrict production import authorization to the on-call operations team and log all activity.
8. Verify the control by reviewing the next scheduled off-hours change and confirming approval + review evidence exists before and after.
9. Coordinate window definitions with application/interface owners so legitimate batch/maintenance work is scheduled and pre-approved.
10. Report the reconciliation results (justified vs. unjustified off-hours imports) to the change/GRC owner.

CODE-TMS-005.risk:
Production import records show transports that were imported into production directly from a development or sandbox source, bypassing the QA/staging tier. This is the executed instance of the routing weakness in CODE-TMS-001: not merely that a direct route could exist, but that changes actually reached production without passing through integration testing, ATC security scanning, and the QA quality gate.
The danger is that untested, unscanned code and customizing are already live. Skipping QA means no regression testing, no performance validation on production-like data, and no independent security review - so functional defects, data-integrity bugs, and exploitable vulnerabilities are shipped straight to the business-critical system. It also means the change likely skipped the approval and four-eyes steps that the consolidation path enforces.
Abuse scenario: a developer or compromised developer account releases a transport from DEV/sandbox and it is imported into PRD without ever existing in QA, delivering a backdoor, a fraudulent logic change, or a weakened control directly to production while evading every intermediate checkpoint.
Business impact: production instability, data corruption, and directly deployed vulnerabilities, plus a SOX change-management failure (no evidence of testing/promotion controls). A real incident is a production outage or fraud whose originating transport has a DEV/sandbox source SID and no corresponding QA import - proof the promotion path was circumvented.
CODE-TMS-005.mitigation:
1. From the transport history, list all production imports whose source SID is a DEV/sandbox system and confirm there is no prior QA import of the same request.
2. Investigate each: validate legitimacy, assess whether the change was tested elsewhere, and remediate/roll back anything unauthorized or defective.
3. Close the routing gap per CODE-TMS-001: enforce a DEV->QA->PRD route in STMS and remove any direct DEV/sandbox->PRD path.
4. Activate the QA Approval Procedure ('Delivery after confirmation') on QA and the 'Single transports' strategy so requests must be QA-confirmed before PRD is offered.
5. Restrict production import authorization (S_CTS_ADMI, STMS import) so operators cannot manually import an arbitrary request from a dev source into PRD.
6. Define a controlled hotfix/emergency process that still routes through QA (even if expedited) and requires post-deploy QA validation and approval.
7. Enable transport/import logging and the Security Audit Log to detect future direct-source imports.
8. Verify by attempting to import a DEV-sourced request that has not been through QA and confirming it is blocked.
9. Coordinate STMS domain changes with Basis and, under RISE, with SAP ECS; test the corrected path with a dummy transport.
10. Report the remediated historical cases and the enforced path to GRC/audit as evidence of a controlled landscape.

CODE-CLIENT-001.risk:
The production client is not locked in SCC4 (table T000): it permits direct client-specific customizing changes (CCCORACTIV), and/or cross-client customizing/repository changes (CCNOCLIIND), and/or is not protected against overwriting (CCCOPYLOCK). A production client must be closed so that the ONLY way to change it is a controlled transport import; leaving it open defeats the entire transport-based change-management model.
The danger is that with the client open, a user holding the right customizing/development authorizations can change configuration, tables, or even repository objects directly in production - with no transport, no QA, and no approval. Cross-client settings are especially severe: a change made from an open production client can affect all clients on the system, including sensitive cross-client repository and system tables.
Abuse scenario: an insider (or attacker on a privileged account) uses SM30/SE16N/SPRO in the open production client to alter a control table directly - e.g., change a tolerance group, a tax setting, an output/bank determination, or a security-relevant customizing entry - instantly and without any change record beyond table logging. Because it never went through a transport, there is no request, no approver, and no QA evidence.
Business impact: complete breakdown of segregation and change control, high fraud and error risk against financial configuration, and a serious SOX ITGC deficiency. A real incident is an unexplained configuration change in production that maps to a direct table maintenance in an open client rather than to any transport.
CODE-CLIENT-001.mitigation:
1. In transaction SCC4 for the production client, set 'Changes and Transports for Client-Specific Objects' to 'No changes allowed' (T000-CCCORACTIV) so only transport imports can change client-dependent customizing.
2. Set cross-client object protection to 'No changes to Repository and cross-client Customizing objects' (T000-CCNOCLIIND).
3. Enable client copy/comparison protection (CCCOPYLOCK) and set 'Protection: level 1/no overwriting' as appropriate to prevent accidental overwrite.
4. Confirm the client role (CCCATEGORY) is set to Production (P); production role interacts with these locks.
5. Set the system-wide change option (transaction SE06 / SE03 'Set System Change Option') for production to 'Not modifiable' so repository/global objects cannot be changed even if a client were opened.
6. Restrict authorizations to open the client (S_TABU_DIS/S_TABU_CLI on the client-admin tables, and S_TABU_CLI CLIDMAINT) to a very small emergency group; opening the client must be an audited, time-boxed exception.
7. Ensure table logging is active (see CODE-CHG-001) so any change is recorded, and enable the Security Audit Log for client-open events.
8. Verify by inspecting T000 for the production client and by attempting a direct SM30 change - it must be rejected as 'client not modifiable'.
9. Coordinate the lock with functional teams to confirm no legitimate process relies on direct production maintenance (migrate any such config to transports); under RISE, align with SAP ECS.
10. Capture the SCC4/SE06 settings as audit evidence and re-check after any client copy, since a refresh can silently reset them (see SAP KBA on SCC4 changing after client copy).

CODE-CHG-001.risk:
Change documents are absent for one or more critical object types (e.g., USER/PFCG role changes, T000 client config, TADIR repository directory, RFCDES RFC destinations, CDHDR itself). Missing change history for these objects means either that change logging is switched off for them or that the audit export is incomplete - in both cases the forensic trail for the most security-relevant objects in the system is broken.
The danger is loss of detective and forensic control precisely where it matters most. User and role changes, RFC destination changes, and client/config changes are the actions an attacker or malicious insider takes to escalate privilege, create backdoors, or open the system - and if these are not logged, such actions become invisible. An attacker who knows logging is off for these objects can operate with impunity.
Abuse scenario: an insider grants themselves a powerful role or creates a trusted RFC destination to a target system; with change documents/table logging disabled for PFCG/RFCDES, no CDHDR/CDPOS or DBTABLOG record is produced, so a later review finds no evidence the change ever happened, defeating both real-time detection and post-incident investigation.
Business impact: inability to detect or investigate privilege escalation and configuration tampering, and failure of SOX/GDPR requirements for an auditable change trail. A real incident is discovered late (or never) because the very objects used in the attack had no change history, forcing reliance on incomplete secondary evidence.
CODE-CHG-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm whether the gap is a logging gap or an export gap: check whether change documents/table logging are actually active for the listed object types before assuming missing data.
2. Verify the system-wide table-logging switch: parameter rec/client (check via RZ11/RSPFPAR) must be set to log the production client (e.g., ALL); coordinate the value and any restart with SAP ECS under RISE.
3. For sensitive tables, ensure 'Log data changes' is enabled in the technical settings (SE13) so changes are captured in DBTABLOG and viewable via SCU3.
4. Confirm change-document objects are active for user/role/config changes (SU01/PFCG changes are logged in CDHDR/CDPOS by standard; validate they are present).
5. Enable and configure the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU_CONFIG; read via SM20/RSAU_READ_LOG) to record security events for users, RFC, and debugging as a complementary trail.
6. For any object type where logging was genuinely off, enable it and document the change-control baseline of logged objects.
7. Re-run the data export to ensure completeness, then re-assess coverage against the critical object list.
8. Verify by making a test change to a covered table/object and confirming a corresponding record appears in SCU3/DBTABLOG or CDHDR/CDPOS.
9. Protect the logs: restrict deletion authorizations (table logging deletion, RSAU log admin) so the trail cannot be tampered with.
10. Report the corrected logging baseline to GRC/audit and re-check after client copies, which can reset technical settings.

CODE-CHG-002.risk:
Change documents are attributed to empty, BATCH, SYSTEM, or DDIC users. When changes to business or configuration data are recorded against a generic/technical identity rather than a named person, the audit trail cannot establish who actually made the change. This breaks non-repudiation and undermines every downstream investigation and SOX control that depends on user attribution.
The danger is twofold. First, legitimate automation running under a shared generic account produces changes no one can be held accountable for. Second, an attacker or insider can deliberately hide behind a generic/technical user (or a job that runs as one) so that malicious changes appear as anonymous system activity, frustrating detection and attribution. DDIC in particular is a powerful standard account whose use for data changes is a red flag.
Abuse scenario: an insider schedules or triggers a data/config change through a batch job or a shared technical user so the change document shows only 'BATCH'/'SYSTEM'; during a fraud investigation the change is real and damaging, but it cannot be pinned to any individual, so accountability - and the ability to prosecute or remediate access - is lost.
Business impact: failure of SOX Section 404 change-attribution controls, weakened forensic readiness, and potential masking of fraud. A real incident is a confirmed unauthorized change whose change document names only a generic user, leaving investigators unable to prove who performed it and forcing reliance on circumstantial system logs.
CODE-CHG-002.mitigation:
1. Extract the change documents attributed to empty/BATCH/SYSTEM/DDIC users and identify, for each, the originating program, job, or interface.
2. For legitimate automation, replace shared generic users with named service/system accounts of type 'System' or 'Communication' (SU01), one per interface/job, so activity is attributable.
3. Ensure background jobs (SM36/SM37) are defined to run under a specific named service account rather than a generic one; update the job steps accordingly.
4. Lock down and stop using DDIC and other standard users for routine changes; restrict and monitor DDIC per SAP Security Baseline, and rotate its password.
5. For system-to-system (RFC/IDoc/OData) flows, configure dedicated technical users per connection so inbound changes carry a traceable identity.
6. Verify the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU_CONFIG) captures logon and change events for these technical users to reinforce attribution.
7. Investigate any empty-user or DDIC change that cannot be tied to a known job as a potential incident and escalate.
8. Verify remediation by triggering the affected job/interface and confirming the resulting change document now shows the named service account.
9. Coordinate the account changes with interface/job owners and test in QA to avoid breaking automated flows.
10. Report the cleaned-up attribution model and residual generic-user usage to GRC/audit.

CODE-DEV-001.risk:
Users hold development access in the production system - development transactions (SE38, SE80, SE37, SE24, SE11, SE16/SE16N, SM30/SM31, CMOD/SMOD, SE09/SE10) and/or the authorization object S_DEVELOP, particularly with ACTVT create/change (01/02) or debug-and-replace. In a controlled landscape, all development happens in DEV and reaches production only via transport; developer capability in production breaks that model and hands users the ability to change or inspect live code and data directly.
S_DEVELOP with change/debug rights is one of the most dangerous productive authorizations: it enables the ABAP debugger with 'replace' (letting a user alter field values - including authorization results and SY-SUBRC - at runtime to bypass checks), direct code changes, and reading of sensitive source. SE16/SE16N and SM30 add direct table display/maintenance of business and control data.
Abuse scenario: a user with S_DEVELOP debug-replace runs a restricted transaction, sets a breakpoint at an AUTHORITY-CHECK, and forces SY-SUBRC to 0, executing an action they are not authorized for; or edits/creates a report in production to plant a backdoor or exfiltrate data - all without a transport and with minimal trace.
Business impact: privilege escalation, fraud, data theft, and a severe SOX segregation-of-duties/change-management deficiency (developers must not have change capability in production). A real incident is an authorization bypass or unexplained production data change traced to a user who held S_DEVELOP/debug-replace in PRD.
CODE-DEV-001.mitigation:
1. From the flagged users, separate those with S_DEVELOP ACTVT 01/02/* and debug-replace from those with only display (ACTVT 03).
2. Remove S_DEVELOP with create/change and debug-replace, and the development transactions (SE38/SE80/SE37/SE24/SE11/SE16N/SM30/CMOD/SMOD/SE09/SE10) from all production roles via PFCG; keep display-only S_DEVELOP (ACTVT 03) only where genuinely needed for support.
3. Restrict direct table maintenance: constrain S_TABU_DIS/S_TABU_NAM to display, and remove SM30/SM31/SE16N change access from productive roles.
4. Provide legitimate emergency development/support access through a firefighter / Emergency Access Management process (SAP GRC EAM) that is time-boxed, logged, and reviewed - not standing access.
5. Use SUIM / report RSUSR002 and the critical-authorization report RSUSR008_009_NEW to enumerate all users still holding S_DEVELOP/dev tcodes in production and drive them to zero.
6. Ensure the production system change option (SE06) is 'Not modifiable' so even residual S_DEVELOP cannot change repository objects.
7. Enable the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU_CONFIG) to record debugging (debug-replace) and development activity for detection.
8. Verify by re-running the critical-auth report and by attempting SE38/debug-replace with a test production user to confirm denial (check SU53).
9. Coordinate role changes with the authorization team and business to avoid disrupting legitimate support; stage in QA first.
10. Report the cleaned production access baseline and the firefighter process to GRC/audit as the sanctioned emergency path.

CODE-MOD-001.risk:
SAP standard objects have been modified without registration against an SAP Note / correction number (no SSCR / Modification Assistant record). Unregistered modifications are undocumented deviations from SAP standard: no one knows why the change was made, what it affects, or whether it is still needed, and the Modification Adjustment tools (SPAU/SPDD) rely on registration to surface them at upgrade time.
The security danger is that undocumented modifications can silently alter standard behavior - including security-relevant logic - and are easily lost or mishandled during Support Package and upgrade cycles. A modification that weakens a standard check may be reintroduced or dropped unpredictably, and because it is unregistered, it evades the structured upgrade review, potentially re-opening a vulnerability or breaking a control without anyone noticing.
Abuse/failure scenario: during an S/4HANA upgrade or SP import, an unregistered modification to a standard program is either overwritten (removing a needed control or fix) or blindly re-applied (reintroducing a weakness), and because it never appeared in the modification browser, it is not reviewed - leaving the system in an unknown, possibly insecure state.
Business impact: upgrade instability, unmanaged security drift, and a governance/audit finding that the organization cannot account for changes to standard code. A real incident is a post-upgrade defect or security regression traced to an unregistered modification that the upgrade process could not properly adjust because it was never registered.
CODE-MOD-001.mitigation:
1. Enumerate the modified standard objects via the Modification Browser (transaction SE95) and SPAU/SPDD, and cross-check against the flagged list.
2. For each modification, establish and document the business reason, the exact behavior change, and whether it is still required.
3. Bring modifications under the Modification Assistant so they are tracked and adjustable at upgrade; register them properly (SE95) rather than leaving them as raw, unregistered changes.
4. Where the functionality can be achieved via a supported extension point, replace the modification with a BAdI / enhancement-framework / user-exit implementation and revert the core modification.
5. Retire modifications that are no longer needed by resetting the object to SAP standard.
6. Give special scrutiny to any modification touching security-relevant standard code and route it through CODE-MOD-002 handling.
7. Ensure all modifications are captured in transports and documented in the change record so they survive and are reviewed at the next SP/upgrade (SPAU).
8. Verify by re-running SE95/SPAU and confirming every remaining modification is registered and documented.
9. Test reverted/replaced logic in QA to confirm no functional regression before transporting DEV->QA->PRD.
10. Record the modification register as audit evidence and review it before each upgrade project.

CODE-MOD-002.risk:
SAP standard programs in security-critical areas have been modified - namespaces/programs related to authorization (SAPLAUTH), user management (SAPLSU*), transport/system administration (SAPLSTMS, SAPMSSY*, SAPLSMSY), and security services (SAPLSECUR). Modifying these standard components can directly weaken or bypass the platform's built-in security controls, and because it is a change to core code, its effects are global and largely invisible to normal application-level review.
The danger is that a modification here can neutralize the very mechanisms that protect the system: an altered authorization routine can make AUTHORITY-CHECK always succeed, a modified user-management program can suppress logging or allow forbidden user changes, and a modified transport routine can bypass approval or route controls. Unlike custom Z-code, these changes hide inside trusted standard programs, so they are rarely suspected and hard to detect.
Abuse scenario: an insider or attacker with modification capability patches the standard authorization check so a specific condition returns 'authorized' regardless of the user's roles, creating a universal backdoor that no PFCG role review would ever reveal; or patches user-maintenance code to silently create/elevate accounts without a change document.
Business impact: catastrophic - a compromised security kernel means every other control (roles, SoD, logging, transport approval) can be silently defeated, enabling undetected fraud, data theft, and full system compromise. It is also a critical SOX/audit failure. A real incident is the discovery that the system's authorization or user-management logic was altered to grant hidden access, invalidating all prior access-control assurance.
CODE-MOD-002.mitigation:
1. Treat each security-critical modification as a potential backdoor: freeze and review it immediately with the security team, prioritizing SAPLAUTH/SAPLSU*/SAPLSECUR/SAPLSTMS changes.
2. Diff the modified code against SAP standard (SE95 Modification Browser / version management) to determine exactly what behavior was changed and whether any authorization/logging logic was weakened.
3. Validate whether the modification is authorized and documented; any undocumented change to security code should be handled as a suspected security incident and escalated to IR.
4. Reverse illegitimate or unnecessary modifications - reset the object to SAP standard - and where a genuine need exists, re-implement via a supported BAdI/enhancement rather than a core modification.
5. Request an SAP security review / open a support case for any modification that must remain in security-critical standard code.
6. Rotate credentials and re-review privileged access if a security-kernel modification is found, since prior controls may have been bypassed.
7. Enable the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU_CONFIG) and change logging to detect further tampering, and restrict S_DEVELOP so such modifications cannot recur in production.
8. Verify by re-running SE95 and confirming no unauthorized modifications remain in the security namespaces, and by testing that AUTHORITY-CHECK behaves correctly for a low-privileged user.
9. Test reverted logic thoroughly in QA (authorization/user-management flows) before transporting DEV->QA->PRD; coordinate with SAP ECS under RISE.
10. Report findings and remediation to security/GRC as a high-priority item and retain evidence of the review.

CODE-MOD-003.risk:
SAP standard modifications more than five years old remain in place. Very old modifications are a maintenance and upgrade liability: the original developer and business rationale are usually gone, the modification predates numerous SAP corrections and Support Packages, and it may now conflict with, or silently re-open issues fixed by, current SAP code. Although lower severity, aged modifications accumulate into an unmanaged risk surface.
The security-relevant danger is drift: a five-plus-year-old modification may have been made against an old version of a standard program that has since received security fixes. If the modification overrides or is reapplied over patched code, it can reintroduce a defect that SAP already remediated, or hold the object at an outdated, less-secure logic level. Because it is old and unreviewed, no one is watching for this interaction.
Abuse/failure scenario: an SP or upgrade adjustment (SPAU) reapplies a stale modification over freshly patched standard code, reverting a security fix; because the modification is ancient and unexamined, the regression ships to production and remains until independently rediscovered.
Business impact: upgrade friction and cost, and a latent chance of security regression or defect reintroduction. It is primarily a technical-debt and hygiene issue with a governance dimension: the organization cannot demonstrate that all deviations from standard are current and justified. A real incident is a post-upgrade regression traced to a long-forgotten modification that should have been retired years earlier.
CODE-MOD-003.mitigation:
1. List the stale modifications via the Modification Browser (transaction SE95) and confirm the age and current relevance of each.
2. For each, determine whether the original requirement still exists; contact the owning functional/development team for confirmation.
3. Retire modifications that are no longer needed by resetting the object to SAP standard (via SE95/SPAU).
4. For still-needed modifications, re-implement using a supported extension (BAdI/enhancement framework) where possible, then revert the core modification.
5. Re-validate any retained modification against the current standard code so it aligns with the latest SAP corrections and does not override a security fix.
6. Ensure all retained modifications are registered and documented so SPAU handles them correctly at the next upgrade.
7. Fold this review into the S/4HANA upgrade / custom-code adaptation project so stale items are actively decisioned rather than carried forward.
8. Verify by re-running SE95 and confirming the stale count is reduced and remaining items are documented and current.
9. Regression-test any reverted/replaced logic in QA before transporting DEV->QA->PRD.
10. Maintain a modification register and re-review ages periodically so modifications do not silently become stale again.

CODE-DEAD-001.risk:
The custom-code inventory contains a large number (above the configured threshold) of unreferenced or never-executed Z*/Y* objects. Dead code is not benign: every unused report, function module, or class is still installed, potentially still callable (directly, via RFC, or via a generic runner), and still part of the attack and maintenance surface - but with no owner actively watching it, no test coverage, and no reason for anyone to review it.
The security danger is that dead code frequently harbors old, unpatched vulnerabilities - missing authority checks, injection flaws, hardcoded credentials, dangerous statements - written to older, less secure standards. Because it is 'unused', it is excluded from functional testing and often from security review, yet it can still be invoked. Attackers specifically hunt for such forgotten, callable objects because they are the least monitored.
Abuse scenario: an attacker enumerates custom objects and finds a dead but still RFC-enabled or transaction-startable Z-program with no authority check and a direct table read; they invoke it to exfiltrate data or as a foothold, and because the object was considered dead, no one is monitoring it and its use goes unnoticed.
Business impact: an inflated, poorly understood attack surface that complicates security assessments and S/4HANA migration, plus ongoing maintenance cost. A real incident is exploitation of a long-forgotten custom object that everyone assumed was inert but was still reachable and vulnerable.
CODE-DEAD-001.mitigation:
1. Validate 'unused' with runtime evidence, not just the static flag: run the ABAP Call Monitor (transaction SCMON) over a representative period (ideally spanning period-end/quarter-end) and aggregate with SUSG to confirm objects are truly not executed.
2. Cross-check references with the where-used list (SE84 / cross-reference) and the SAP Custom Code Migration app / ATC usage data before deciding an object is dead.
3. For confirmed-dead, still-callable objects, first neutralize exposure: remove them from menus/roles, disable any RFC-enabled flag, and remove S_TCODE start authorizations so they cannot be invoked.
4. Decommission in a controlled way: deprecate, then archive the source, then delete via the transport organizer (SE80/SE38 delete, captured in a transport) so removal is auditable and reversible.
5. Prioritize decommissioning objects that also carry security findings (missing auth checks, injection, hardcoded secrets) from the ATC/CVA scan.
6. Keep objects that SCMON shows are used but rarely, and ensure they are owned and in scope for security review (see CODE-DEAD-002).
7. Tune the scanner threshold (max_dead_code_alert) only after establishing a real cleanup baseline, not to suppress the finding.
8. Verify by re-running SCMON/where-used after removal to confirm the objects are gone and nothing broke.
9. Test in QA and monitor for missing-object short dumps before transporting deletions DEV->QA->PRD; coordinate with functional owners in case of hidden dependencies.
10. Fold this into a governed custom-code lifecycle process so dead code is retired continuously rather than accumulating.

CODE-DEAD-002.risk:
Custom code objects have no designated owner (empty owner/responsible/created-by). Ownerless code is unmanaged code: when no team or individual is accountable for an object, it is not maintained, not patched, not reviewed for security, and not properly included in assessments or upgrade planning. It falls into a governance blind spot where problems accumulate unaddressed.
The security relevance is that unowned objects are the ones most likely to retain latent vulnerabilities - missing authority checks, injection, hardcoded secrets, dangerous statements - because there is no one to triage or remediate findings against them. During a security assessment or S/4HANA migration, ownerless code cannot be validated (no one can confirm whether it is still needed, what it does, or whether it is safe), so it is often left in place by default, perpetuating risk.
Abuse/failure scenario: an ATC/CVA scan flags a serious finding in an unowned object; because no owner exists, the finding is never assigned, never fixed, and eventually aged out or ignored - and the vulnerable object stays live and callable indefinitely. Attackers benefit from exactly this remediation gap.
Business impact: weakened custom-code governance, slower and less reliable security remediation, and audit findings on change/ownership controls. While lower severity on its own, ownerlessness is a force-multiplier for every other custom-code risk. A real incident is a vulnerability that persisted only because the object it lived in had no owner to drive the fix.
CODE-DEAD-002.mitigation:
1. Extract the list of ownerless objects and reconcile against the custom-code inventory and the SAP Custom Code Migration / ATC metadata.
2. Determine responsibility from available signals: created-by user, package/development-class assignment, transport history (who last changed it), and functional area.
3. Assign a named owning team (and ideally an individual/product owner) to every custom object; record ownership in the package/application metadata and in the code inventory register.
4. Where no owner can be found and the object is confirmed unused (via SCMON, see CODE-DEAD-001), route it for decommissioning rather than leaving it ownerless.
5. Establish a custom-code governance standard that no object may be transported without a designated owner, and enforce it as part of the transport/ATC release checklist.
6. Feed ownership into ATC/CVA so future security findings are automatically routed to the responsible team for remediation.
7. Include ownership review in the S/4HANA custom-code adaptation project so every retained object has an accountable owner.
8. Verify by re-running the inventory/ownership report and confirming the ownerless count is driven to zero (assigned or decommissioned).
9. Communicate assigned ownership to the development teams so they accept maintenance and remediation responsibility.
10. Periodically re-audit ownership (e.g., after reorganizations or offboarding) so objects do not become orphaned again.

LOG-AUD-001.risk:
The Security Audit Log (SAL) is SAP's single most important forensic control, and this finding means it is either completely absent from the assessment data or, worse, active with zero effective filters (rsau/enable = 0 or no active selection slots). When SAL is not recording, none of the security-relevant events occur in any trail: dialog and RFC logons, user-master changes via SU01, role changes via PFCG, program execution via SE38, direct table maintenance via SE16/SE16N, and authorization-check failures all happen invisibly.
In an S/4HANA RISE model, SAP hosts the infrastructure but the customer owns the application-layer security configuration, so activating and filtering SAL is squarely the customer's responsibility - a fact often missed, leaving prod with no audit trail at all.
Attack scenario: an attacker phishes a dialog account, or an insider with SE16N reads USR02 password hashes or maintains vendor bank details in LFA1, then reverts the change. With SAL off, the SOC receives no event, brute-force attempts are undetectable, and post-incident forensics cannot reconstruct who did what or when.
Business and compliance impact is severe: SOX IT general controls require an audit trail over financially relevant changes; GDPR Articles 32/33 require the ability to detect and report breaches within 72 hours; the SAP Security Baseline and DSAG guidance both mandate SAL be active. A real incident looks like fraudulent F110 payment runs or a self-granted SAP_ALL profile discovered weeks later, with no log evidence, a stalled investigation, and regulatory penalties.
LOG-AUD-001.mitigation:
1. Open RSAU_CONFIG (transaction SM19 on releases prior to NetWeaver 7.50) to inspect and build the audit configuration.
2. In RZ10, set the profile parameters on the DEFAULT/instance profile: rsau/enable = 1, rsau/selection_slots = 10 (or higher), rsau/user_selection = 1, and rsau/integrity = 1 (tamper-evident checksums).
3. Create at least one STATIC audit profile (or Kernel Parameters) so recording survives restarts, with filters for the audit classes: Dialog logon, RFC/RFC login, Transaction start, Report start, User master change, System events, and 'Other events'; set critical/severe severities for user *.
4. Activate the profile; dynamic filters apply immediately, while static profile / kernel parameters take effect after the next restart.
5. Verify recording by generating a test event (a failed logon and an SE16 read of a logged table), then confirm the entries appear in RSAU_READ_LOG (SM20).
6. Confirm active status in RSAU_CONFIG > Display Active Status; ensure it reads active on every application server.
7. Rollout caution: SAL can generate high volume - size rsau/max_diskspace/local or switch recording to the database, and validate in a non-production system first. Coordinate storage sizing with SAP RISE operations. Reference SAP Notes 2191612 and 539404.

LOG-AUD-002.risk:
This finding means the Security Audit Log has no STATIC audit profile, only dynamic (runtime) filters, if any. The distinction is critical: static profiles (and kernel parameters) are persisted and automatically re-armed by the kernel at system start, whereas dynamic filters set at runtime are lost on any restart.
In a RISE/ECS environment SAP performs routine maintenance, kernel patches, and failovers, so restarts are frequent and often outside the customer's direct control. With dynamic-only filtering, every restart silently disarms SAL until an administrator manually re-enables it - an audit blind window nobody is alerted to.
This is especially dangerous because maintenance windows coincide with the highest-risk activity: elevated firefighter access, transport imports, and configuration change. The precise period when the most sensitive actions occur is the period least likely to be logged.
Attack scenario: an attacker who can trigger, or simply wait for, a scheduled restart obtains a logging blackout, then performs privilege escalation or data theft during the gap. Because no one notices SAL is off, the actions are never recorded.
Compliance impact: SOX and internal audit require continuous, gap-free audit coverage as evidence that the control operated throughout the period; a restart-induced gap breaks that continuity and typically results in a control-deficiency finding. A real incident is fraud discovered two weeks after a support-pack import, with no logs for the affected window.
LOG-AUD-002.mitigation:
1. In RSAU_CONFIG (SM19 pre-7.50), create a STATIC audit profile rather than relying on dynamic filters.
2. Populate it with the same critical filter classes (Dialog logon, RFC login, RFC function call, Transaction start, Report start, User master change, System) for user * at critical/severe severity.
3. To make activation persistent, set rsau/enable = 1 as a profile parameter in RZ10 (or use the Kernel Parameters tab, which persists across restarts).
4. Save and activate the static profile.
5. In a non-production system, perform a controlled restart and confirm that SAL re-arms automatically - check RSAU_CONFIG > Display Active Status after the restart shows the static profile active on all instances.
6. Verify events are being written post-restart via RSAU_READ_LOG (SM20).
7. Repeat the parameter and profile setup in production during a planned window.
8. Rollout caution: coordinate the validation restart with SAP RISE operations, document the static profile as the system-of-record configuration, and add a monitoring check that alerts if SAL active status is ever false. Reference SAP Notes 2191612 and 539404.

LOG-AUD-003.risk:
Here the Security Audit Log is active but its filters do not cover the full set of security-critical event types the module checks for: failed dialog logon, RFC logon, critical transaction starts (SU01, PFCG, SE38, SM59), user-master changes, authorization-check failures, report/program execution, RFC function-module calls, direct table access (SE16/SE16N), system events, and changes to the audit configuration itself. Every uncovered class is a detection blind spot an attacker can deliberately choose.
Concrete abuse cases follow directly from the gaps. If RFC logon and RFC function-call events are not audited, lateral movement over a trusted RFC destination or S_RFCACL abuse from a lower-trust system into production is invisible. If audit-configuration-change events are not covered, an attacker can disable SAL itself as an anti-forensic step without leaving a trace. If SE16/SE16N table access is unmonitored, silent bulk reads of USR02 (hashes), PA0008 (payroll bank data), or BSEG (financial documents) exfiltrate data with no alert. If authorization-check failures are not logged, an attacker probing for privilege gaps enumerates freely.
In RISE/BTP the exposed HTTP and OData surface makes transaction-start and report-start monitoring essential to catch web-driven attacks. Compliance impact: the SAP Security Baseline requires monitoring of these specific event classes, so partial coverage is a baseline deviation and weakens SOX and GDPR breach-detection posture. A real incident is an RFC-based compromise that spreads across the landscape while the SOC dashboard stays green.
LOG-AUD-003.mitigation:
1. In RSAU_CONFIG (SM19 pre-7.50), open the active profile and review filter coverage against the required classes.
2. Add or extend filters so all needed audit classes are captured: Dialog logon (incl. failures), RFC login, RFC function call, Transaction start, Report start, User master change, and System events.
3. For high-value users (SAP*, DDIC, WF-BATCH, firefighter IDs) add a filter with user * and all severities to guarantee full capture.
4. Explicitly enable transaction-start auditing for the sensitive codes (SU01, PFCG, SE38, SE16/SE16N, SM59); for comprehensive coverage select all audit classes at critical/severe.
5. Protect the audit config from tampering: restrict S_ADMI_FCD (AUDA) and RSAU authorizations, and confirm audit-configuration changes are themselves recorded.
6. Verify by generating each event type (failed logon, SE16 read of a logged table, PFCG change) and confirming they appear in RSAU_READ_LOG (SM20).
7. Rollout caution: tune filters to avoid log flooding, size rsau/max_diskspace/local or use DB recording, and test in non-production first. Reference SAP Notes 2191612 and 539404 and the SAP Security Baseline monitoring section.

LOG-SIEM-001.risk:
This finding fires when there is no SIEM integration configured, or a connector exists but is disabled. Either way, SAP security events (SAL/SM20, system log/SM21, change documents, HTTP and gateway logs) remain trapped inside the SAP system and are never correlated with network, endpoint, and identity-provider telemetry. The security operations centre therefore has no real-time visibility into the SAP estate.
This is a common and dangerous misconception in RISE: SAP hosts and monitors the infrastructure, but it does not monitor the customer's application-layer security events - the customer must forward them to their own SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, IBM QRadar) or deploy SAP Enterprise Threat Detection (ETD). A disabled connector is arguably worse than none, because it creates a false sense of coverage.
Attack scenario: an attacker chains a VPN login from an anomalous geography, then an SAP RFC logon, then a mass OData/GUI download of finance or HR data over several weeks. Each step is individually low-signal; only cross-domain correlation in a SIEM catches the chain. Without forwarding there are no alerts, and because SAL sits locally, an admin who compromises the host can also tamper with the evidence.
Business and compliance impact: breaches are found late or only via third-party notification, blowing the GDPR 72-hour reporting window; SOX and ISO 27001 expect continuous security monitoring. A real incident is finance data exfiltrated for a month while every SAP dashboard shows nothing.
LOG-SIEM-001.mitigation:
1. Deploy a log-forwarding capability: either SAP Enterprise Threat Detection (ETD / ETD cloud edition) or a connector to the enterprise SIEM (Splunk, Sentinel, QRadar).
2. For ABAP/RISE, forward the Security Audit Log (from DB or file), the system log (SM21) via syslog, change documents (CDHDR/CDPOS), and HTTP/gateway logs into the SIEM.
3. For BTP, enable the Audit Log service, pull events with the Audit Log Retrieval API, and route Alert Notification Service (ANS) events by webhook to the SIEM.
4. Set the connector to enabled/active and confirm the lastSync/lastForward timestamp is current (not stale).
5. Validate end-to-end: generate a test SAL event and confirm it arrives in the SIEM within minutes.
6. Build correlation rules and alerts for critical SAP events (failed-logon spikes, SAP_ALL grants, RFC anomalies, config changes).
7. Rollout caution: secure the forwarding channel with TLS, monitor connector health, and alert on sync gaps so a silent forwarding failure is itself detected. Reference SAP ETD documentation and the SAP Security Baseline monitoring requirements.

LOG-SIEM-002.risk:
Here a SIEM connector is active but only a subset of the required log sources is being forwarded - the module checks for SM20 (Security Audit Log), SM21 (system log), CDHDR (change documents), BTP_AUDIT (BTP audit log), ICM_LOG (HTTP access), and GATEWAY_LOG (RFC gateway). Partial coverage yields partial visibility, and each missing source is a specific, exploitable detection hole.
Concrete gaps: if SM20 is forwarded but CDHDR is not, the SOC sees logons but not the actual configuration and master-data changes, so a fraudulent vendor bank-detail change in LFA1 followed by a payment run goes undetected. If ICM access logging is missing, web-tier attacks against the Fiori launchpad and OData services - path traversal, injection, credential stuffing - are invisible. If the gateway log is absent, RFC gateway attacks (reg_info/sec_info bypass, registration of rogue external programs) are unseen. If the BTP audit log is not forwarded, high-impact cockpit actions (role-collection assignment, destination edits, service-key creation) are untracked.
In a hybrid S/4HANA RISE plus BTP landscape, attackers pivot across these tiers, so uneven coverage lets them pick the unmonitored path. Business and compliance impact: incomplete monitoring undermines the SAP Security Baseline, weakens SOX change-tracking evidence, and slows breach detection. A realistic incident is an OData injection via an exposed service that never reaches the SIEM because ICM logging was never forwarded, resulting in an undetected data breach.
LOG-SIEM-002.mitigation:
1. Inventory the required sources and confirm each is both generated and forwarded: SAL (SM20), system log (SM21), change documents (CDHDR/CDPOS), ICM HTTP access log, RFC gateway log, and BTP audit log.
2. Enable ICM access logging via RZ10 parameter icm/HTTP/logging_0 (define log file and fields); enable gateway logging via the gw/logging parameters and review in SMGW.
3. Ensure change documents are extracted (via ETD/extractor) and system log is syslog-forwarded.
4. For BTP, enable Audit Log Retrieval and route Alert Notification Service events by webhook to the SIEM.
5. Map every source into SIEM ingestion with a clear source label, and build parsers per source.
6. Verify each source individually by generating a representative event (an HTTP request, an RFC call, a config change) and confirming it lands in the SIEM.
7. Rollout caution: HTTP and gateway logging can be high-volume, so tune the captured fields, and coordinate gateway/ICM parameter changes with interface owners and SAP RISE operations. Reference the SAP Security Baseline and SAP SIEM integration guidance.

LOG-RET-001.risk:
This finding means one or more security log types retain data for fewer than the configured minimum (default 365 days). Short retention directly limits forensic reach, and that matters because sophisticated attacks and internal fraud are frequently discovered months after the fact - industry dwell times routinely exceed 200 days. If the Security Audit Log rotates after 30-90 days, then by the time a breach or fraud surfaces the evidence has already been overwritten.
The consequences are concrete: investigators cannot scope the breach (what was accessed, by whom, over what period), auditors cannot obtain evidence that SOX controls operated throughout the audit period, and the organisation cannot meet GDPR accountability obligations. In RISE and BTP the risk is heightened because the BTP Audit Log Retrieval service retains only about 90 days by default - longer retention requires an explicit export - and local SAL files are overwritten once rsau/max_diskspace is reached.
Attack/abuse scenario: an insider commits fraud in January; a routine SOX walkthrough in Q4 requests the change history for a critical role or a vendor bank-detail change; the logs were purged after 90 days, so the control cannot be evidenced. This becomes an audit control deficiency and, if pervasive, a potential material weakness. A real incident also looks like an external breach notification arriving five months late, with SAP logs already gone, making impact assessment impossible and regulatory fines more likely.
LOG-RET-001.mitigation:
1. Define a documented retention standard for security logs - at least 365 days, and longer where SOX, GDPR, or legal hold require it (align the exact figure with Legal/Compliance).
2. For the Security Audit Log, switch recording to the database in RSAU_CONFIG for durable retention (or substantially increase rsau/max_diskspace/local for file-based recording).
3. Use RSAU_ADMIN to manage and reorganise SAL data, and set up archiving via SARA for long-term storage.
4. For change documents and table logs, schedule archiving (SARA archive objects) rather than deletion.
5. For BTP, schedule periodic Audit Log Retrieval API exports to long-term storage (SIEM or object store), since the native retention is only ~90 days.
6. Verify the fix by confirming the oldest retrievable event date for each log type meets the standard.
7. Rollout caution: plan storage capacity for the increased volume, and balance retention against GDPR data-minimisation so personal data is not held longer than justified. Reference the SAP Security Baseline retention guidance and SOX log-retention requirements.

LOG-RET-002.risk:
This finding indicates one or more security log types have an online retention period but no archiving configured, so once retention expires the data is deleted permanently with no long-term copy anywhere. While day-to-day monitoring may be satisfied by online retention, several obligations require multi-year availability that online storage will not provide.
Examples include SOX-adjacent financial record expectations, industry regulations, and litigation legal holds that can span several years. Without an archive, a legal hold or a long-running investigation simply cannot be satisfied - the evidence no longer exists. Online-only logs are also more exposed to tampering by a privileged administrator and to loss under storage pressure, whereas archives can be written to immutable/WORM media.
Abuse and impact scenario: litigation triggers a legal hold requiring three-year-old access and change logs; only one year is available online and nothing is archived, creating spoliation risk and the possibility of an adverse-inference ruling. From a fraud-investigation angle, the absence of an immutable archive also means a determined insider who tampers with or reorganises online logs can destroy the only copy. Although rated lower severity because online logs may still serve near-term detection, the long-tail compliance and evidentiary exposure is real and typically surfaces at the worst possible moment - during an audit or a lawsuit.
LOG-RET-002.mitigation:
1. Configure archiving for each security log type instead of allowing outright deletion at retention expiry.
2. For the Security Audit Log, use RSAU_ADMIN reorganisation together with a SARA archive process for long-term storage.
3. For change documents, archive via the SARA archive object CHANGEDOCU; for table change logs, archive the DBTABLOG data.
4. Define an archive retention period aligned to compliance (for example seven years where financial-record rules or legal hold apply).
5. Store archives on immutable/WORM or otherwise access-restricted storage, and encrypt them.
6. For BTP, export retrieved audit logs to immutable object storage for the required horizon.
7. Verify the control by performing a test retrieval of an archived record (read SAL from archive, or view archived table changes via SCU3) to confirm the archive is usable and indexed.
8. Rollout caution: index archives for retrievability, and balance long retention against GDPR data-minimisation obligations. Reference SAP archiving (SARA) documentation and the applicable retention policy.

LOG-TBL-001.risk:
This finding means one or more critical tables lack change logging. The module's list covers the crown jewels: user master and authorization (USR02, USR04, USR21, AGR_USERS, AGR_DEFINE, AGR_1251), RFC destinations (RFCDES), authorization customizing (PRGN_CUST), client control (T000 - which holds the production client change-protection flags), transport configuration (TMSPCONF, TADIR, E070, E071), HR/payroll master including bank data (PA0000, PA0001, PA0002, PA0008), customer and vendor master with bank details (KNA1, LFA1), and financial documents (BKPF, BSEG).
Table change logging requires two things: the profile parameter rec/client set to log the relevant client(s), and the 'Log Data Changes' flag on each table in SE13. If either is missing, direct changes - via SE16N, debugger replace, an RFC/BAPI, or backend SQL - leave no change-document trail in DBTABLOG.
Classic fraud scenario: an attacker or malicious insider with S_TABU_DIS edits vendor bank details in LFA1 via SE16N, triggers an F110 payment run to their own account, then reverts the field - completely invisible without table logging. Equally severe: flipping T000 to open the production client for changes, or editing RFCDES to repoint a trusted RFC destination at an attacker-controlled system for lateral movement.
Business and compliance impact: SOX IT general controls over master-data and security changes fail without this trail; GDPR integrity/accountability is undermined; auditors will raise a finding. A real incident is a payment-fraud loss that cannot be traced because the master-data change was never logged.
LOG-TBL-001.mitigation:
1. Set the profile parameter rec/client in RZ10 to log the production client(s) - for example rec/client = <prod client> (or ALL to log every client). This activates client-dependent table logging and requires a restart.
2. For each critical table, open SE13 (Maintain Technical Settings) and enable the 'Log Data Changes' checkbox; many SAP standard tables ship with this on, so verify rather than assume.
3. Use SAP Note 112388 as the reference list of recommended tables to log and align your scope to it.
4. Reduce the underlying exposure by tightening direct table maintenance: restrict S_TABU_DIS / S_TABU_NAM and remove SE16N change access in production.
5. Verify which tables are logged with report RSTBHIST, and review actual changes via transaction SCU3 or report RSVTPROT (data stored in DBTABLOG).
6. Rollout caution: logging adds database writes, so enable it selectively on the critical tables rather than universally, test performance impact in a non-production system, and coordinate the restart required for rec/client with SAP RISE operations. Reference CIS SAP table-logging guidance and SAP Note 112388.

LOG-LOGON-001.risk:
This critical finding flags accounts whose failed-to-successful logon ratio is very high (greater than 5 with at least the failure threshold met), a pattern strongly consistent with an active credential attack - brute force or password spraying against SAP GUI, RFC, or the web/Fiori/BTP logon endpoints. If the password policy is weak or account lockout is not enforced (login/fails_to_user_lock unset or set high), the attacker eventually succeeds.
In a RISE deployment the exposed HTTP surface (Fiori launchpad, OData services) makes internet-adjacent brute force realistic, and BTP identity via IAS/XSUAA is subject to credential stuffing. The blast radius depends on which account cracks: a standard dialog user is bad, an RFC/technical user with broad authorizations is worse, and a default or emergency account (SAP*, DDIC, a firefighter ID) is catastrophic.
