3. Configure and test key backup/escrow: back up the instance SSFS and system PKI SSFS every time keys change and before enabling any encryption, and store these backups separately from the data/log backups with strict access control.
4. For certificate keys, schedule renewal via STRUST ahead of expiry (see CRYPTO-CERT-002) and rotate SNC PSE keys periodically.
5. Where an external KMS is in use (SAP Data Custodian / hyperscaler KMS, or SAP BTP Credential Store for BTP-side secrets), enable its automated rotation and centralised audit logging.
6. Automate monitoring/alerting on rotation due-dates and on backup success so lapses are detected.
7. Verify recoverability by performing a test restore of a key backup (and a backup encrypted under a rotated key) in a non-production system.
8. Rollout caution: test every rotation and recovery procedure in non-prod first, keep verified backups at each step, and align the documented process with NIST SP 800-57; in RISE coordinate root-key handling and backups with SAP ECS.

HANADB-USER-001.risk: The finding is that the built-in SYSTEM superuser is still active. SYSTEM is created at database provisioning and implicitly holds every system privilege; it is not subject to the role model, object ownership, or analytic-privilege filtering. An active SYSTEM account is effectively a single master key to the entire HANA database that underpins S/4HANA. It is dangerous because the credential is typically set during installation, is often shared among the project team, is weakly rotated, and frequently ends up embedded in install scripts, secure-store (hdbuserstore) keys, or interface connection strings. Attack scenario: an adversary who phishes or brute-forces the SYSTEM password, or finds it hard-coded in a script or backup, connects to the SQL port and gains unrestricted control - reading all business data (financials, HR, GDPR-relevant PII), minting hidden privileged users, changing ini parameters to disable auditing or TLS, and touching encryption/credential material. Because SYSTEM bypasses every compensating control, there is no secondary gate once it is used. In S/4HANA this equals total compromise of the ERP data layer beneath every Fiori/SAP GUI authorization. Compliance impact is severe: least-privilege and SoD violations (SOX ITGC), unattributable privileged access, and audit failure because actions cannot be tied to a named person. A real incident looks like anomalous SYSTEM logons from an app or jump host at odd hours, mass SELECTs against SAPHANADB tables, and new users suddenly holding DATA ADMIN.
HANADB-USER-001.mitigation:
1. Ensure named administrator accounts already exist, each provisioned with only the roles they need; never keep using SYSTEM for daily work.
2. In RISE/PCE, confirm via the Roles and Responsibilities RACI whether SAP ECS owns SYSTEM; if the customer copy is active, raise a service ticket rather than acting unilaterally.
3. Verify status: SELECT USER_NAME, USER_DEACTIVATED FROM SYS.USERS WHERE USER_NAME='SYSTEM'; (or SAP HANA Cockpit -> User and Role Management, or transaction DBACOCKPIT from the ABAP stack).
4. Deactivate from a different admin user: ALTER USER SYSTEM DEACTIVATE USER NOW;
5. Store the SYSTEM password in a sealed break-glass vault, rotate it, and record custody.
6. Verify the change: re-query SYS.USERS and confirm USER_DEACTIVATED = TRUE.
7. Create an audit policy that fires on SYSTEM CONNECT so any future break-glass use is alerted to the SIEM.
8. Rollout caution: before deactivating, confirm no interfaces, hdbuserstore keys, or backup jobs authenticate as SYSTEM (inspect connection stores and DBACOCKPIT DB connections), or those connections will break; test in non-production first.
9. Reactivate only under change control (ALTER USER SYSTEM ACTIVATE USER NOW;) and re-deactivate immediately after the emergency.
10. Document the whole procedure in the break-glass runbook and review it quarterly.

HANADB-USER-002.risk: The finding is that one or more non-technical HANA users have IS_PASSWORD_LIFETIME_CHECK_ENABLED set to FALSE, so their password never expires. This flag is set with ALTER USER ... DISABLE PASSWORD LIFETIME and is legitimately used for a narrow set of technical/interface users, but it is frequently applied to human accounts too, or copied when cloning users. A never-expiring password removes the natural forcing function to rotate credentials: a password leaked years ago in a breach dump, a screenshot, a decommissioned laptop, or a script remains valid indefinitely. Attack scenario: an adversary who obtained the credential from an old backup, a phished helpdesk interaction, or a compromised admin workstation can reuse it long after the fact; the absence of expiry also defeats the common incident-response assumption that a stale password would have changed by now. Combined with a weak password policy, static long-lived passwords are highly susceptible to offline cracking and credential stuffing against the SQL port. Business and compliance impact: it violates password-rotation requirements in SOX ITGC and ISO 27001 A.9 and most internal standards, and it enables persistent unauthorized access to the S/4HANA data layer. A real incident looks like a former contractor's DB login still working months after departure because the password never expired and dormancy monitoring missed the account.
HANADB-USER-002.mitigation:
1. List offenders: SELECT USER_NAME, IS_PASSWORD_LIFETIME_CHECK_ENABLED FROM SYS.USERS WHERE IS_PASSWORD_LIFETIME_CHECK_ENABLED='FALSE'; (or SAP HANA Cockpit -> User Management).
2. Classify each account: genuine technical/interface user (SSO, X.509, or hdbuserstore based) versus interactive human.
3. For interactive/human users, enable expiry: ALTER USER <user> ENABLE PASSWORD LIFETIME;
4. Ensure a bounded maximum_password_lifetime is set in the [password policy] section (see HANADB-PARAM-001) so that 'enabled' actually forces rotation.
5. For true technical users that must not expire, migrate authentication to X.509 certificate, SAML, Kerberos, or Secure User Store (hdbuserstore) keys, and record the exception in a risk register instead of leaving a password-based login immortal.
6. Verify: re-run the SYS.USERS query; the flag should read TRUE for every human user.
7. Rollout caution: enabling lifetime on an account whose password is already older than the maximum lifetime forces an immediate change - schedule this for interface users, coordinate with the interface owner to avoid a lockout during batch windows, and test in non-production.
8. Add this control to a recurring least-privilege/user review so it does not regress.

HANADB-USER-003.risk: The finding is that active DB users have not successfully connected in the configured threshold (default 90 days) or have never connected. Dormant accounts are effectively unmonitored by their owners, who may have left the organisation, changed roles, or simply forgotten them, yet they remain fully valid attack surface. The danger is that anomalous logons on an account that 'isn't used' produce no baseline deviation that anyone will notice, and the legitimate owner will never report a suspicious lockout or unexpected activity. Attack scenario: an adversary who compromises a dormant credential - from a breach dump, an orphaned service account, or a leaver whose account was never disabled - can operate quietly for extended periods, and dormant privileged accounts are especially damaging because they combine stealth with capability. Dormant accounts also inflate the privilege-review and licensing workload and are a classic audit finding. Business and compliance impact: leaver accounts that remain active are a recurring SOX and ISO 27001 exception and a frequent breach root cause, and unattended access to S/4HANA data is a GDPR exposure. A real incident looks like an ex-employee - or an attacker using their old credentials - logging into HANA six months after departure, exporting data, and going unnoticed for weeks because no dormancy control fired.
HANADB-USER-003.mitigation:
1. Identify candidates: SELECT USER_NAME, LAST_SUCCESSFUL_CONNECT, USER_DEACTIVATED FROM SYS.USERS WHERE USER_DEACTIVATED='FALSE' ORDER BY LAST_SUCCESSFUL_CONNECT; (a null last-connect means never connected).
2. Cross-check each account against HR leaver lists and the interface/service-account inventory.
3. Deactivate first (reversible): ALTER USER <user> DEACTIVATE USER NOW; and retain the account for a defined grace period (e.g. 30-60 days).
4. After the grace period, if no legitimate need is confirmed, drop it: DROP USER <user> RESTRICT; (resolve owned objects deliberately - use CASCADE only when you are sure no dependent objects are needed).
5. Automate dormancy review with a scheduled report (SAP HANA Cockpit, DBACOCKPIT, or a monitored SQL job) that flags accounts idle beyond the threshold and feeds the joiner-mover-leaver process.
6. Verify: re-run the query; deactivated users show USER_DEACTIVATED = TRUE.
7. Rollout caution: some technical/batch users legitimately connect only monthly or quarterly - confirm true inactivity against interface schedules before disabling, coordinate with interface owners, and validate the impact in non-production where feasible.

HANADB-PRIV-001.risk: The finding is that sensitive privileges are granted to the PUBLIC role. PUBLIC is automatically and implicitly granted to every database user - present and future - so granting a system privilege, the _SYS_BI_CP_ALL analytic privilege, or any *ADMIN privilege to PUBLIC hands that capability to the entire user population, including low-privilege technical accounts, reporting users, and any account an attacker later compromises. This is a catastrophic least-privilege break: DATA ADMIN or CATALOG READ on PUBLIC means every user can administer data or read the full catalog/metadata; _SYS_BI_CP_ALL on PUBLIC means every user can read all modeled reporting data with no row filtering. Attack scenario: an adversary only has to compromise the single weakest DB credential - a dormant reporting user, an app technical account - and instantly inherits the admin or broad-data capability granted to PUBLIC, with no separate escalation step required. Business and compliance impact: mass data exposure (financial and GDPR-relevant), trivial privilege escalation, and audit failure - because the grant is systemic it defeats per-user access reviews entirely. A real incident looks like an auditor or attacker holding only a trivial read-only account discovering they can query all HANA metadata or every analytic view, because a privilege was granted to PUBLIC during a project and never revoked.
HANADB-PRIV-001.mitigation:
1. Enumerate: SELECT GRANTEE, PRIVILEGE, OBJECT_NAME, IS_GRANTABLE FROM GRANTED_PRIVILEGES WHERE GRANTEE='PUBLIC'; and also check GRANTED_ROLES for roles granted to PUBLIC.
2. Classify which privileges are legitimately public (a small set of harmless monitoring views) versus sensitive or administrative.
3. Revoke the sensitive system privileges: REVOKE <privilege> FROM PUBLIC; and for object privileges: REVOKE <priv> ON <object> FROM PUBLIC;
4. Re-provision the capability only to the specific users or, preferably, a named role that genuinely needs it.
5. For _SYS_BI_CP_ALL granted to PUBLIC, revoke it and replace with view-specific analytic privileges (see HANADB-PRIV-005).
6. Verify: re-run the GRANTED_PRIVILEGES query - the sensitive PUBLIC rows should be gone; then smoke-test that legitimate low-privilege users still function.
7. Rollout caution: revoking a privilege from PUBLIC that an application silently relied on breaks functionality for all users at once - identify dependent applications/interfaces first, test in non-production, involve the app owners, and stage the change under change control.
8. Add a recurring check for new PUBLIC grants so the condition does not reappear after future projects.

HANADB-PRIV-002.risk: The finding is that near-omnipotent system privileges - DATA ADMIN, USER ADMIN, ROLE ADMIN, INIFILE ADMIN, DEVELOPMENT, and the encryption/credential/trust admin privileges - are granted directly to individual named users rather than through a small number of reviewed roles. This both breaks least privilege and destroys reviewability: you cannot reason about who can perform a sensitive action when privileges are scattered across ad-hoc direct grants. The concrete danger of each: USER ADMIN plus ROLE ADMIN lets a holder mint themselves any privilege and create a stealth superuser; DATA ADMIN gives read and DDL over all catalog objects; INIFILE ADMIN lets a user change security parameters to disable auditing, TLS, or the password policy; DEVELOPMENT enables creation of executable server-side objects; ENCRYPTION ROOT KEY ADMIN and CREDENTIAL ADMIN touch cryptographic and secret material. Attack scenario: a compromised or malicious holder of USER ADMIN creates a hidden privileged account, or an INIFILE ADMIN holder turns auditing off and then exfiltrates data unlogged; because the grants are direct they are easy to miss in role-centric reviews. Business and compliance impact: privilege escalation to full DB compromise, tampering with security controls, and unlogged fraud - direct SOX and GDPR exposure. A real incident looks like a developer who was left DATA ADMIN 'temporarily' for a go-live using it months later to read payroll data, the direct grant never having surfaced in the periodic role review.
HANADB-PRIV-002.mitigation:
1. List direct grants: SELECT GRANTEE, GRANTEE_TYPE, PRIVILEGE, IS_GRANTABLE FROM GRANTED_PRIVILEGES WHERE GRANTEE_TYPE='USER' AND PRIVILEGE IN ('DATA ADMIN','USER ADMIN','ROLE ADMIN','INIFILE ADMIN','DEVELOPMENT','AUDIT ADMIN','CERTIFICATE ADMIN','CREDENTIAL ADMIN','TRUST ADMIN','ENCRYPTION ROOT KEY ADMIN');
2. For each, decide the legitimate owner and design a minimal set of named admin roles that enforce SoD (e.g. separate user-admin and config-admin roles).
3. Grant the role and remove the direct privilege: GRANT <admin_role> TO <user>; then REVOKE <privilege> FROM <user>;
4. Reserve DATA ADMIN and DEVELOPMENT for exceptional, time-boxed use and prefer object-specific privileges instead.
5. Ensure INIFILE, AUDIT, and encryption/credential admin are held by a tiny, named, four-eyes-reviewed group only.
6. Verify: re-query GRANTED_PRIVILEGES (no USER-type direct grants of the critical set remain) and confirm effective access via EFFECTIVE_PRIVILEGES.
7. Enable an audit policy on grant/revoke of these privileges so future direct grants are detected.
8. Rollout caution: revoking a privilege an admin actively uses can break operational jobs - migrate to the role first, validate in non-production, and coordinate a change window; in RISE confirm ownership with SAP ECS.

HANADB-PRIV-003.risk: The finding is that powerful, though not 'keys to the kingdom', system privileges are granted directly to users rather than via roles: CATALOG READ, BACKUP ADMIN, TRACE ADMIN, IMPORT, EXPORT, and the RESOURCE/SERVICE/SESSION/MONITOR/TABLE/LOG/SAVEPOINT ADMIN family. Each still enables meaningful abuse. CATALOG READ exposes all metadata - schemas, table and column names, user and role structures - which is precisely the reconnaissance an attacker needs to plan a targeted attack. EXPORT and IMPORT can move whole tables out of or into the database, a direct data-exfiltration and data-tampering vector. BACKUP ADMIN can trigger or redirect backups. TRACE ADMIN can enable verbose traces that may capture sensitive data in trace files and can fill disks to cause a denial of service. As with the critical set, direct grants undermine reviewability and least privilege. Attack scenario: a compromised account holding EXPORT dumps sensitive tables to a filesystem location and exfiltrates them, or CATALOG READ is used to map the schema before a focused attack on the highest-value tables. Business and compliance impact: data exposure, reconnaissance enablement, potential DoS via traces, and SoD/audit findings. A real incident looks like an interface account that accumulated EXPORT and CATALOG READ across successive projects being used to bulk-export a customer master table.
HANADB-PRIV-003.mitigation:
1. Enumerate: SELECT GRANTEE, PRIVILEGE FROM GRANTED_PRIVILEGES WHERE GRANTEE_TYPE='USER' AND PRIVILEGE IN ('CATALOG READ','BACKUP ADMIN','TRACE ADMIN','IMPORT','EXPORT','RESOURCE ADMIN','SERVICE ADMIN','SESSION ADMIN','MONITOR ADMIN','TABLE ADMIN','LOG ADMIN','SAVEPOINT ADMIN');
2. Justify each grant against the user's actual role and remove any that cannot be justified.
3. Consolidate the genuinely needed privileges into purpose-built roles (e.g. a monitoring role, a backup-operator role) and grant the role: GRANT <role> TO <user>; then REVOKE <priv> FROM <user>;
4. Restrict EXPORT and IMPORT especially tightly and enable an audit policy so their use is logged and alerted.
5. Verify: re-run the query - direct grants should be replaced by role-based access - and confirm via EFFECTIVE_PRIVILEGES that required tasks still work.
6. Periodically re-review these roles as part of the access-recertification cycle.
7. Rollout caution: monitoring and backup tooling often depends on these privileges - identify the tool service accounts, migrate them to roles first, test in non-production, and coordinate with the operations team before revoking.

HANADB-PRIV-004.risk: The finding is that sensitive privileges are granted WITH ADMIN OPTION (for system privileges and roles) or WITH GRANT OPTION (for object privileges), which lets the grantee re-grant the privilege to further principals. This creates uncontrolled, self-propagating privilege sprawl: the security team grants a capability to one trusted user, who grants it onward to others outside any review, producing a grant graph that is hard to audit and hard to fully unwind. HANA adds an operational hazard - revoking a privilege the grantee has since re-granted can cascade and unexpectedly strip access from downstream users, or fail, making cleanup risky and error-prone. Attack scenario: a malicious or compromised holder of an admin-option privilege quietly grants a second account the same privilege for persistence, or distributes a sensitive privilege to accomplices; because onward granting is legitimate at the SQL level, it does not trip simple 'who did I grant to' reviews. Business and compliance impact: erosion of least privilege over time, inability to prove access boundaries to auditors, and messy remediation that can itself cause outages. A real incident looks like a 'temporary' admin-option grant seeding a web of re-grants so that, years later, dozens of users hold a sensitive privilege that nobody can explain or safely remove.
HANADB-PRIV-004.mitigation:
1. Find them: SELECT GRANTEE, GRANTEE_TYPE, PRIVILEGE, OBJECT_NAME, IS_GRANTABLE FROM GRANTED_PRIVILEGES WHERE IS_GRANTABLE='TRUE';
2. For each, determine whether delegation is genuinely required - in most cases it is not.
3. Before revoking, map downstream re-grants (query GRANTED_PRIVILEGES for the same privilege granted onward by that grantee) so the cascade impact is understood.
4. Re-issue without the option: REVOKE <priv> FROM <grantee>; then GRANT <priv> TO <grantee>; (i.e. grant again without WITH ADMIN/GRANT OPTION).
5. Where delegation truly is required, restrict it to a single governed admin role and log all grant/revoke activity with an audit policy.
6. Verify: re-run the IS_GRANTABLE='TRUE' query - only explicitly approved delegations should remain.
7. Rollout caution: revoking an admin-option grant can cascade to dependent users - plan the revoke order, test the cascade in non-production, and stage under change control with app and interface owners informed.
8. Add WITH ADMIN OPTION review to the recurring access-recertification process.

HANADB-PRIV-005.risk: The finding is that the built-in structured (analytic) privilege _SYS_BI_CP_ALL is granted to a non-technical user or role. When held, _SYS_BI_CP_ALL switches off analytic-privilege row and column filtering for all classic (XML-based) modeled calculation and analytic views. Analytic privileges are HANA's mechanism for restricting which rows of a modeled view a user may see - for example only their own company code, region, or cost centre - so a holder of _SYS_BI_CP_ALL bypasses those restrictions entirely and sees the complete result set of every such view. In an S/4HANA embedded-analytics or BW-on-HANA landscape this typically means unrestricted access to the most sensitive reporting data - financials, HR, and customer data - regardless of the carefully designed row-level security. Attack scenario: an analyst, developer, or compromised account granted _SYS_BI_CP_ALL (often copied during modeling or testing and never removed) queries the modeled views and pulls every company's or region's data, defeating the intended data-segregation controls; because the bypass is 'by design' for that privilege, the queries look entirely legitimate. Business and compliance impact: a mass confidentiality breach and a segregation-of-duties failure, GDPR exposure for personal data, and financial-misstatement risk if restricted financial views become broadly readable. A real incident looks like a report developer retaining _SYS_BI_CP_ALL after go-live and exporting all subsidiaries' P&L data that they were only ever meant to see for a single entity.
HANADB-PRIV-005.mitigation:
1. Find holders: SELECT GRANTEE, PRIVILEGE FROM GRANTED_PRIVILEGES WHERE PRIVILEGE='_SYS_BI_CP_ALL'; and check role-based grants via GRANTED_ROLES and EFFECTIVE_PRIVILEGE_GRANTEES (MODELING and CONTENT_ADMIN can carry it).
2. For each grantee, identify which modeled views they legitimately need and the correct row/column restriction.
3. Revoke the master privilege: REVOKE STRUCTURED PRIVILEGE _SYS_BI_CP_ALL FROM <user_or_role>;
4. Replace it with specific analytic privileges - preferably SQL-based analytic privileges, or classic XML analytic privileges - that enforce the intended filters, and grant those instead.
5. For genuine admin/modeling needs, time-box the access and grant it only in non-production or under change control.
6. Verify: re-run the query (no non-technical grantees remain) and functionally test that affected users now see only their permitted rows in the modeled views.
7. Rollout caution: removing _SYS_BI_CP_ALL immediately restricts data for those users - build and validate the replacement analytic privileges in non-production first, and coordinate with the BI/reporting owners so production reports do not silently return empty or partial results.

HANADB-ROLE-001.risk: The finding is that broadly powerful predefined or repository roles are granted to end users: CONTENT_ADMIN, MODELING, MONITORING, SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT, and any role ending in ADMIN or containing SUPPORT. Each over-provisions the user well beyond normal needs. CONTENT_ADMIN carries wide privileges over activated repository content and historically includes the ability to grant and revoke activated analytic privileges; MODELING typically brings _SYS_BI_CP_ALL and full modeling rights (and therefore the analytic-privilege bypass); SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT exposes internal, largely undocumented system views intended only for SAP support staff during an active incident and must never be a standing grant. Attack scenario: a user with CONTENT_ADMIN or MODELING manipulates modeled content or effectively grants themselves broad data access, while a standing SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT grant on a compromised account hands an attacker rich internal reconnaissance and capabilities beyond any normal role. Business and compliance impact: privilege escalation, data exposure, and audit findings - SAP explicitly documents SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT as temporary-only, so a standing grant is a clear control failure. A real incident looks like SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT being granted during a support case and never revoked, so months later that account is used to browse internal system state and pivot deeper into the platform.
HANADB-ROLE-001.mitigation:
1. List grants: SELECT GRANTEE, ROLE_NAME FROM GRANTED_ROLES WHERE ROLE_NAME IN ('CONTENT_ADMIN','MODELING','MONITORING','SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT') OR ROLE_NAME LIKE '%ADMIN' OR ROLE_NAME LIKE '%SUPPORT';
2. Treat SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT first - it should have zero standing grantees; revoke: REVOKE SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT FROM <user>;
3. For CONTENT_ADMIN and MODELING, confirm the user genuinely performs modeling/content activation; move day-to-day users onto narrower, task-specific custom roles.
4. Where a broad role is truly needed, grant it to the minimum set of users and time-box it (revoke after the task or support case closes).
5. Prefer building custom least-privilege roles over granting the powerful predefined ones.
6. Verify: re-run the GRANTED_ROLES query (SAP_INTERNAL_HANA_SUPPORT shows no grantees, the others only justified ones) and check EFFECTIVE_PRIVILEGES for residual _SYS_BI_CP_ALL inherited via MODELING.
7. Enable an audit policy that logs grants of these roles so future standing grants are detected.
8. Rollout caution: revoking MODELING or CONTENT_ADMIN can break a developer's activation workflow - coordinate with the modeling team and test in non-production before removing.

HANADB-AUDIT-001.risk: The finding is that global.ini [auditing configuration] global_auditing_state is not 'true', so the HANA audit trail is globally switched off. With auditing disabled, none of the security-relevant events are recorded - privilege grants and revokes, user creation and alteration, configuration (ini) changes, failed and successful privileged logons, and any sensitive data access that policies would capture. There is therefore no forensic trail, no detection of misuse, and no ability to attribute actions to an actor. Attack scenario: an adversary or malicious insider who reaches the database can create users, escalate privileges, exfiltrate data, and change configuration with zero recorded evidence; even routine incident response becomes impossible because there is nothing to reconstruct. Auditing being off also makes the other audit controls (policy coverage and trail target) moot. Business and compliance impact: this is simultaneously a direct security-control failure and a compliance breach - SOX ITGC requires logging of privileged database activity, GDPR requires the ability to detect and investigate unauthorized access to personal data, and ISO 27001 (and PCI where in scope) mandate audit logging. A real incident looks like a data-exfiltration event discovered externally - data appearing for sale - after which the team finds the HANA audit trail was never enabled, so root cause, scope, and attribution simply cannot be established.
HANADB-AUDIT-001.mitigation:
1. Check current state: SELECT * FROM M_INIFILE_CONTENTS WHERE KEY='global_auditing_state'; (or SAP HANA Cockpit -> Security -> Auditing, or DBACOCKPIT).
2. Enable auditing: ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('global.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('auditing configuration','global_auditing_state')='true' WITH RECONFIGURE;
3. Set a production-grade trail target (see HANADB-AUDIT-002): default_audit_trail_type = SYSLOGPROTOCOL (forwarded to the SIEM) or CSTABLE.
4. Create and activate audit policies for critical actions - grants/revokes, user administration, configuration changes, and authentication (see HANADB-AUDIT-003/004).
5. Forward the trail to the enterprise SIEM and alert on privileged events.
6. Verify: re-query M_INIFILE_CONTENTS (value 'true'), then perform a test auditable action such as a dummy GRANT and confirm it appears in the trail (the AUDIT_LOG view for CSTABLE, or the OS syslog for SYSLOGPROTOCOL).
7. Rollout caution: in RISE/PCE, auditing configuration may be SAP ECS's responsibility - coordinate via the RACI or a ticket. WITH RECONFIGURE applies without a restart, but validate trail sizing and retention so the chosen target does not fill up.

HANADB-AUDIT-002.risk: The finding is that the audit trail target (default_audit_trail_type or emergency_audit_trail_type) is set to CSVTEXTFILE. A CSV file audit trail is written to the operating-system filesystem and is not protected by the database, so anyone with OS or file access - a Basis admin, a compromised OS account, a backup operator - can read it (exposing the sensitive data captured in audit records) or modify and delete it to erase evidence of malicious activity. SAP explicitly documents CSVTEXTFILE (and KERNELTRACE) as unsuitable for production; only SYSLOGPROTOCOL and CSTABLE provide adequate protection and reliability. Attack scenario: an adversary who gains OS-level access after compromising the database simply edits or truncates the CSV audit files to cover their tracks, defeating the entire purpose of auditing; alternatively they read the audit CSV to harvest information such as statements containing literal values. Business and compliance impact: audit integrity is broken - the logs are neither tamper-evident nor access-controlled - which fails the log-integrity requirements of SOX and ISO 27001 and undermines any forensic investigation. A real incident looks like an investigation after a breach discovering that the CSV audit files were altered or that the relevant rows are missing, so the logs cannot be trusted or used as evidence.
HANADB-AUDIT-002.mitigation:
1. Check the target: SELECT * FROM M_INIFILE_CONTENTS WHERE KEY IN ('default_audit_trail_type','emergency_audit_trail_type','critical_audit_trail_type');
2. Choose a protected target: SYSLOGPROTOCOL (forwarded to a write-once/SIEM-protected syslog) is preferred; CSTABLE (an internal DB table queryable via the AUDIT_LOG view) is acceptable for local storage.
3. Set it: ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('global.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('auditing configuration','default_audit_trail_type')='SYSLOGPROTOCOL' WITH RECONFIGURE; and repeat for emergency_audit_trail_type.
4. If using SYSLOGPROTOCOL, harden the OS syslog and forward it off-box so a DB or OS compromise cannot erase it; if using CSTABLE, restrict access to the audit table.
5. Retain any existing CSV audit content per the retention policy, then stop writing CSVTEXTFILE.
6. Verify: re-query M_INIFILE_CONTENTS for the new value, then generate a test audit event and confirm it lands in syslog or CSTABLE.
7. Rollout caution: ensure the syslog pipeline (rsyslog/SIEM collector) is in place before switching, or events could be dropped; coordinate with the SIEM/OS team and, in RISE, with SAP ECS, and test in non-production.

HANADB-AUDIT-003.risk: The finding is that global auditing may be enabled but no audit policy is active - and in HANA an audit policy is what actually defines which actions are recorded. With zero active policies, the audit subsystem captures nothing, so despite auditing being 'on' there is no meaningful trail of privileged or security-relevant activity. This is a subtle but serious gap because it gives false assurance: status screens report that auditing is enabled, yet an investigation would find no records at all. Attack scenario: exactly as with auditing fully off - an intruder or malicious insider performs grants, user creation, configuration changes, and data access with no recorded evidence - but here the gap is easier to overlook because auditing appears configured and healthy. Business and compliance impact: the same failures as a disabled audit trail (SOX privileged-activity logging, GDPR breach detection and investigation) with the added risk of misplaced confidence during an audit or an incident. A real incident looks like the team, after a suspected insider fraud, enabling audit reporting only to discover that no policy was ever active, so months of privileged activity are permanently unrecoverable and the fraud cannot be reconstructed.
HANADB-AUDIT-003.mitigation:
1. List policies and their state: SELECT AUDIT_POLICY_NAME, IS_AUDIT_POLICY_ACTIVE, EVENT_STATUS, AUDIT_ACTION_NAME FROM AUDIT_POLICIES; (or SAP HANA Cockpit -> Security -> Auditing).
2. Design a baseline policy set covering privileged and security-relevant actions: grants/revokes, CREATE/ALTER/DROP USER and ROLE, system configuration changes, and connect/validate.
3. Create a policy, for example: CREATE AUDIT POLICY "SEC_PRIV_MGMT" AUDITING ALL GRANT PRIVILEGE, GRANT ROLE, REVOKE PRIVILEGE, REVOKE ROLE LEVEL CRITICAL; and similar policies for user administration, configuration, and connect.
4. Activate it: ALTER AUDIT POLICY "SEC_PRIV_MGMT" ENABLE;
5. Ensure global_auditing_state = 'true' and a protected trail target are set (see HANADB-AUDIT-001/002).
6. Verify: re-query AUDIT_POLICIES (IS_AUDIT_POLICY_ACTIVE = TRUE), then trigger a test action and confirm a record appears in the AUDIT_LOG view or syslog.
7. Rollout caution: over-broad policies (for example auditing all SELECTs) generate huge volumes and can affect performance and storage - scope policies to critical actions and sensitive objects, validate volume in non-production first, and align retention with the SIEM team.

HANADB-AUDIT-004.risk: The finding is that audit policies are active but do not cover one or more of the highest-value action groups: privilege GRANT/REVOKE, user administration (CREATE/ALTER/DROP USER), system configuration changes, and authentication (CONNECT/VALIDATE USER). These are precisely the events that reveal privilege escalation, backdoor-account creation, security-control tampering, and brute-force or enumeration attempts. If GRANT/REVOKE is unaudited, an attacker can escalate privileges and later clean up with no trace; if USER ADMIN is unaudited, stealth superuser creation is invisible; if configuration change is unaudited, an attacker can disable other controls such as sslenforce or the password policy unseen; if CONNECT is unaudited, brute-force and anomalous-logon detection is impossible. Attack scenario: a knowledgeable adversary deliberately operates in the uncovered category - for example performing the escalation via a path the active policies do not watch. Business and compliance impact: partial blindness that produces incomplete forensic records and weakens breach detection, and a common audit exception even where auditing is otherwise broadly configured. A real incident looks like an investigation that can see data-access events but not the grant that gave the attacker access, because privilege-management actions were outside the active policy set.
HANADB-AUDIT-004.mitigation:
1. Determine current coverage: review AUDIT_POLICIES.AUDIT_ACTION_NAME for the active policies (SAP HANA Cockpit -> Security -> Auditing shows the actions per policy).
2. Map the required baseline groups and identify the missing ones: GRANT/REVOKE (privilege and role), CREATE/ALTER/DROP USER and ROLE, SYSTEM CONFIGURATION CHANGE / SET SYSTEM LICENSE, and CONNECT/VALIDATE USER.
3. Extend or add policies for the gaps, for example: CREATE AUDIT POLICY "SEC_USER_ADMIN" AUDITING ALL CREATE USER, DROP USER, ALTER USER, CREATE ROLE, DROP ROLE LEVEL CRITICAL; plus one for SYSTEM CONFIGURATION CHANGE and one AUDITING UNSUCCESSFUL/SUCCESSFUL CONNECT for authentication.
4. Enable each: ALTER AUDIT POLICY "<name>" ENABLE;
5. Keep policies action-scoped to avoid noise while ensuring all four groups are covered.
6. Verify: re-check AUDIT_POLICIES that each critical group now maps to an active policy, then run a representative test action per group and confirm it is captured in the trail.
7. Rollout caution: auditing successful CONNECT is high-volume - at minimum audit unsuccessful connects, plus successful connects for privileged users; validate the volume in non-production and coordinate retention with the SIEM team.

HANADB-PARAM-001.risk: The finding is that one or more [password policy] parameters are weaker than recommended. minimal_password_length below 8 makes passwords far easier to brute-force or crack offline. force_first_password_change = false means initial or admin-set passwords stay in use, so provisioning-time or default passwords persist and may be widely known. maximum_invalid_connect_attempts above 6 weakens lockout, giving brute-force and password-spraying attackers more attempts before an account locks. maximum_password_lifetime above 365 days (or unset) lets passwords live too long, extending the window during which a leaked credential remains valid. Together these make DB-authenticated accounts materially easier to compromise. Attack scenario: an adversary sprays common passwords against the HANA SQL port or a reporting user; with a high lockout threshold and a short minimum length the odds of success rise sharply, and if force_first_password_change is off an account still using its provisioning password is trivially guessed. Business and compliance impact: unauthorized DB access to S/4HANA data and failure to meet SOX, ISO 27001 A.9, and enterprise password standards. A real incident looks like a service or reporting account with a 6-character, never-forced-change, never-expiring password being cracked and used to reach the database.
HANADB-PARAM-001.mitigation:
1. Review current values: SELECT KEY, VALUE FROM M_INIFILE_CONTENTS WHERE FILE_NAME='indexserver.ini' AND SECTION='password policy'; (use nameserver.ini for the system DB), or SAP HANA Cockpit -> Security -> Password Policy.
2. Set minimal_password_length >= 8 (align with the enterprise standard, e.g. 10-12): ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('password policy','minimal_password_length')='10' WITH RECONFIGURE;
3. Set force_first_password_change = true using the same statement pattern.
4. Set maximum_invalid_connect_attempts to a value <= 6 (e.g. 5) and confirm password_lock_time is set (default 1440 minutes / 24h).
5. Set a bounded maximum_password_lifetime (<= 365, commonly 90-180) and consider last_used_passwords and minimum_password_lifetime to prevent immediate reuse.
6. Also review password_layout and complexity requirements per your standard.
7. Verify: re-query M_INIFILE_CONTENTS for the new values, then create a throwaway user to confirm the policy is enforced and drop it.
8. Rollout caution: tightening lifetime or length can force mass password changes and affect technical accounts on next login - communicate to users, schedule around batch windows, migrate technical users to certificate/SSO, test in non-production, and in RISE coordinate with SAP ECS.

HANADB-PARAM-002.risk: The finding is that [password policy] detailed_error_on_connect is set to true, causing the database to return specific authentication-failure reasons to the client - distinguishing 'user does not exist' from 'wrong password' from 'user is locked/deactivated'. This is an information-disclosure weakness that directly enables user enumeration: an attacker can probe login names and learn which accounts exist, which are locked, and which are valid, live targets, then focus password-guessing and spraying on real, unlocked accounts. It also leaks account-state intelligence useful for social engineering. The secure default is false (a single generic message), but the parameter is sometimes switched on to aid troubleshooting and then left on in production. Attack scenario: an adversary scripts connection attempts against a list of likely usernames; with detailed errors on, they rapidly build an accurate inventory of valid accounts and their lock state, dramatically improving the efficiency of a subsequent brute-force or targeted phishing campaign. Business and compliance impact: it facilitates credential attacks and reconnaissance and is a recognised hardening gap in the CIS SAP HANA benchmark, contributing to broader confidentiality and access-control risk. A real incident looks like pre-attack reconnaissance logs showing systematic login probing that successfully mapped valid HANA users because detailed connect errors were enabled.
HANADB-PARAM-002.mitigation:
1. Check the value: SELECT KEY, VALUE FROM M_INIFILE_CONTENTS WHERE KEY='detailed_error_on_connect'; (section [password policy]).
2. Set it to false: ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('indexserver.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('password policy','detailed_error_on_connect')='false' WITH RECONFIGURE; (use nameserver.ini for the system DB).
3. Confirm clients now receive only a generic 'authentication failed' message.
4. Keep detailed errors only in non-production, and only temporarily while troubleshooting a specific issue - revert immediately afterwards.
5. Pair this with account lockout (maximum_invalid_connect_attempts) and connect auditing so enumeration and brute-force attempts are both throttled and detected.
6. Verify: re-query M_INIFILE_CONTENTS (value 'false'), then attempt a login with a bad username and separately with a bad password and confirm the client-side message is identical and generic in both cases.
7. Rollout caution: turning this off makes helpdesk troubleshooting slightly harder - direct admins to the server-side trace/audit for the real failure reason rather than the client message; test in non-production and, in RISE, coordinate the change with SAP ECS.

HANADB-PARAM-003.risk: The finding is that global.ini [communication] sslenforce is set to false, meaning the HANA SQL port (JDBC/ODBC, and the connection from the S/4HANA application server, SDI/DP agents, and analytics clients) will accept plaintext, non-TLS connections. Credentials and all query and result data - which for S/4HANA is effectively the entire business dataset, including financial and personal data - can then traverse the network unencrypted, exposing them to passive sniffing and active man-in-the-middle attacks by anyone positioned on the network path: a compromised host, a rogue admin, a hypervisor or network tap, or an attacker who has gained a foothold in the datacentre or VPC. This is separate from HTTPS/ICM TLS - it specifically governs the SQL layer. Attack scenario: an adversary on the same segment as the app server or a DB client captures the login handshake and subsequent SQL traffic, harvesting credentials and reading or altering data in transit; a MitM can even inject or modify statements. Business and compliance impact: a confidentiality and integrity breach of all SQL traffic, credential theft, and non-compliance with encryption-in-transit requirements (GDPR, PCI where in scope, and internal cryptography standards). A real incident looks like a network capture during an internal penetration test revealing HANA logon credentials and sensitive query results in cleartext because SQL TLS was not enforced.
HANADB-PARAM-003.mitigation:
1. Check the value: SELECT * FROM M_INIFILE_CONTENTS WHERE FILE_NAME='global.ini' AND SECTION='communication' AND KEY='sslenforce';
2. Ensure valid server certificates and the SSL key/trust stores are in place for the index/SQL server (in-database certificate collection or PSE) before enforcing.
3. Enforce TLS: ALTER SYSTEM ALTER CONFIGURATION ('global.ini','SYSTEM') SET ('communication','sslenforce')='true' WITH RECONFIGURE;
4. Configure all SQL clients to use encryption - the S/4HANA secondary DB connection, JDBC (encrypt=true and validateCertificate as appropriate), ODBC, SDI/DP agents, and analytics tools.
5. Verify: re-query M_INIFILE_CONTENTS (value 'true'), attempt a non-TLS connection (should be refused) and a TLS connection (should succeed), and check M_CONNECTIONS for encrypted sessions.
6. Rollout caution: this is a breaking change - any client not configured for TLS will lose connectivity, including the app server and interfaces. Inventory every SQL client, roll out client-side TLS first, test end-to-end in non-production, and schedule a change window.
7. In RISE/PCE, SQL-layer TLS is typically SAP ECS-managed - coordinate via the RACI or a ticket rather than changing sslenforce unilaterally.

HOTNEWS-000.risk: This finding fires when no SNOTE / applied-notes export (applied_notes.csv) was supplied, so the scanner cannot prove which HotNews (Priority 1, CVSS 9.0-10.0) SAP Security Notes are actually implemented. The risk is not a single misconfiguration but a governance blind spot: patch state is unknown, and in security an unknown patch state must be treated as vulnerable. In a RISE with SAP / S/4HANA landscape the shared-responsibility model makes this dangerous — SAP ECS operates the infrastructure, OS and DB, but application-layer ABAP/Java Security Notes remain the customer's responsibility, so a HotNews such as RECON (CVE-2020-6287) or the Visual Composer RCE (CVE-2025-31324) can sit unpatched while everyone assumes 'SAP handles patching'. Without an authoritative note inventory, the Basis team cannot demonstrate a working SAP Security Patch Day process, and there is no evidence trail for auditors. The abuse scenario is indirect but real: an attacker mass-scans internet- or network-reachable NetWeaver instances, finds one whose patch status was never tracked, and weaponises a public exploit within hours; the victim only learns the system was unpatched during incident response. Business and compliance impact is significant — this maps to failed IT General Controls for change/patch management (SOX 404), ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.8 (technical vulnerability management) and the SAP Security Baseline requirement to run a defined patch cadence. A real incident looks like an EarlyWatch or auditor asking 'prove note 2934135 is applied' and the team being unable to answer, followed weeks later by a webshell discovered on the same host. The severity is Medium only because it reflects missing evidence rather than a confirmed gap — but it can be hiding any of the Critical findings below.
HOTNEWS-000.mitigation:
1. Log into the ABAP system and open transaction SNOTE (Note Assistant); use the SAP Note Browser (Goto > SAP Note Browser) to list downloaded/implemented notes and their status.
2. For an automated, product-version-aware view, run System Recommendations in SAP Solution Manager (schedule background job SM:SYSTEM RECOMMENDATIONS, then open the Sysrec app), or use the equivalent in SAP Cloud ALM for RISE-managed systems.
3. Do NOT rely on the deprecated report RSECNOTE — SAP has superseded it with System Recommendations; its catalog is outdated.
4. Export the note status to CSV with at least the columns NOTE and STATUS (add TITLE/VERSION if available); the implementation status lives in the note store PRSTATUS field where E = Completely Implemented.
5. Cross-check AS Java / BTP components separately (SAP for Me / Maintenance Planner, or the Support Backbone) since SNOTE covers only the ABAP stack.
6. Place applied_notes.csv in the scanner input folder and re-run so HOTNEWS-001..004 can diff automatically.
7. Pull the latest SAP EarlyWatch Alert (EWA) for each production system and reconcile its security-notes section against the export.
8. Establish a recurring SAP Security Patch Day routine (SAP publishes notes the second Tuesday monthly) with a defined triage SLA for HotNews.
9. For RISE systems, confirm in writing with SAP ECS which note categories they apply versus which you must schedule, and record it in the run-book.
10. Verify: after remediation, re-export and confirm every catalog HotNews shows PRSTATUS = E in SNOTE and 'implemented' in System Recommendations.
Caution: run the System Recommendations collector job in a low-load window and ensure the managed-system RFCs to Solution Manager/Support Backbone are healthy, or results will be incomplete.

HOTNEWS-001.risk: This finding lists HotNews Security Notes (Priority 1, CVSS 9.0-10.0) from the curated catalog that are not recorded as implemented. HotNews notes fix the most severe SAP defects, and several here are pre-authentication, network-only remote-compromise flaws: RECON (CVE-2020-6287, note 2934135) in the AS Java LM Configuration Wizard, ICMAD (CVE-2022-22536, note 3123396) in the ICM/Web Dispatcher, the NetWeaver Visual Composer file-upload RCEs (CVE-2025-31324 note 3594142; CVE-2021-38163 note 3084487), the AS Java P4 missing-auth flaw (CVE-2023-23857 note 3252433), ABAP file-upload CVE-2024-33006 (note 3448171), ABA code injection CVE-2024-22131 (note 3420923) and the BTP @sap/xssec privilege escalation (CVE-2023-49583 note 3411067). The common thread is that an attacker needs only reachability to the HTTP(S)/ICM port or the Java stack — no valid SAP credentials. Concrete chain: with RECON the attacker POSTs to /CTCWebService/CTCWebServiceBean and creates a UME administrator, then pivots via trusted RFC/SSO into the connected S/4HANA ABAP system to create fictitious vendors, release payment runs (F110) or read mass HR/PII; with Visual Composer the attacker uploads a JSP webshell into servlet_jsp/irj/root/ and executes OS commands as <sid>adm, giving full read/write to the SAP database. Business and compliance impact is maximal: financial fraud, mass personal-data breach triggering GDPR Art. 32/33 notification within 72 hours, SOX ITGC failure, and ransomware staging. A real incident mirrors the April 2025 CVE-2025-31324 campaign in which threat actors and ransomware affiliates mass-scanned internet-facing NetWeaver and dropped webshells within hours of exposure. In RISE, these application-layer notes remain the customer's responsibility even though SAP runs the platform.
HOTNEWS-001.mitigation:
1. Take the affected_items list and, for each note, confirm applicability to your exact product version and Support Package in System Recommendations (Solution Manager) or SAP Cloud ALM (RISE).
2. Download and implement each correcting note in transaction SNOTE; where SAP ships the fix only in a Support Package, plan it via Maintenance Planner and deploy with SPAM/SAINT.
3. For kernel-resident fixes such as ICMAD (note 3123396), replace the disp+work kernel to the patched patch level rather than using SNOTE, then restart the instance.
4. Execute every manual pre-/post-implementation step the note specifies — e.g. RECON additionally requires locking down the LM Configuration Wizard and disabling unused services in transaction SICF; do not mark the note done until these are complete.
5. For the BTP finding (@sap/xssec, note 3411067), update the library to the fixed version in your CAP/Node.js apps and redeploy through the Cloud Foundry/Kyma pipeline.
6. Route all changes through change control: implement in DEV, regression-test interfaces/IDocs/RFCs in QA, then PROD in a maintenance window.
7. For RISE landscapes, raise the change with SAP ECS via the agreed maintenance-planning process; coordinate kernel/DB actions they own with the ABAP notes you own.
8. Where an immediate patch is impossible, apply the note's documented compensating control (e.g. block the vulnerable ICF path, restrict the port at the Web Dispatcher/firewall) as an interim measure.
9. Verify each fix: SNOTE status must read 'Completely Implemented' (PRSTATUS = E), System Recommendations must drop the note after the SM:SYSTEM RECOMMENDATIONS collector re-runs, and kernel level must be confirmed via System > Status / SM51.
10. Institutionalise a monthly SAP Security Patch Day triage with a HotNews SLA.
Caution: always test in non-prod first; kernel and Support Package changes require downtime and interface-owner coordination.

HOTNEWS-002.risk: This finding lists High-priority Security Notes (Priority 2, CVSS 7.0-8.9) that are not recorded as implemented — in the shipped catalog this includes note 3123427 (CVE-2022-22532 / CVE-2022-22533), the AS Java Memory Pipe (MPI) / ICMAD-family HTTP-smuggling and memory-exhaustion issue. While rated below HotNews, High notes still describe directly exploitable weaknesses and are routinely chained by attackers who first land through a lower-severity foothold. The MPI/smuggling class is dangerous because a crafted request sequence can desynchronise the ICM/Web Dispatcher request queue, letting an attacker smuggle a second request past front-end controls (WAF, reverse proxy, SAP Web Dispatcher ACLs) — poisoning caches, hijacking another user's authenticated session, or bypassing URL-based authorization to reach protected ICF services. The memory-exhaustion variant enables denial of service against an internet-facing gateway, which for an S/4HANA order-to-cash or treasury system is a direct availability and revenue impact. In a RISE context these notes are still the customer's to schedule, and because they are not flagged HotNews they are frequently deferred indefinitely, quietly accumulating exposure. The abuse scenario: an attacker who has mapped a reachable NetWeaver front end scripts a smuggling probe, confirms desync, then reuses it to reach an admin ICF endpoint or to force a DoS during a business-critical run. Compliance impact includes ISO 27001 A.8.8 vulnerability-management findings and, for a session-hijack outcome, GDPR/data-confidentiality exposure. A real incident looks like intermittent, hard-to-diagnose session cross-talk or gateway crashes that are eventually root-caused to an unpatched smuggling note. Severity is High rather than Critical, but these must not be allowed to age past the next patch cycle.
HOTNEWS-002.mitigation:
1. Review the affected_items list and validate each note's relevance to your kernel/Support Package level in System Recommendations (Solution Manager) or SAP Cloud ALM.
2. Schedule the notes into the next scheduled patch cycle rather than as emergency changes, but with a firm target date and owner.
3. Implement correcting notes via transaction SNOTE; for MPI/ICM-class fixes (note 3123427) apply the corresponding kernel patch and restart the instance, as the fix is typically kernel-level.
4. If an SAP Web Dispatcher sits in front of the stack, patch it to the same corrected kernel level so front-end and back-end parsing stay consistent (a mismatch is what enables smuggling).
5. Review ICM logging/limits and any reverse-proxy/WAF normalisation rules as defence-in-depth against request-smuggling.
6. Run the change through DEV > QA > PROD with regression testing of HTTP interfaces, Fiori, and any proxied traffic.
7. For RISE, coordinate kernel/Web Dispatcher patching with SAP ECS and confirm ownership boundaries.
8. Verify: confirm SNOTE status 'Completely Implemented' (PRSTATUS = E), check the running kernel patch level via System > Status, and re-run System Recommendations to confirm the note clears.
9. Add these notes to the tracked backlog with a due date so they do not silently age.
Caution: kernel replacement needs a maintenance window and instance restart; coordinate with interface owners and load-balancer/proxy teams before changing front-end parsing behaviour.

HOTNEWS-003.risk: This is the highest-urgency finding: it lists catalog vulnerabilities marked as actively exploited in the wild (public exploit code / CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities) whose correcting notes are not recorded as implemented. The exploited set includes RECON (CVE-2020-6287, note 2934135), the Solution Manager missing-authentication flaw (CVE-2020-6207, note 2890213), ICMAD (CVE-2022-22536, note 3123396), and the two Visual Composer file-upload RCEs (CVE-2025-31324 note 3594142; CVE-2021-38163 note 3084487). Unlike theoretical CVSS ratings, 'exploited' means weaponised tooling already exists — Metasploit/PoC modules, mass-scanning botnets, and in the CVE-2025-31324 case confirmed ransomware-affiliate activity — so the window between exposure and compromise is measured in hours, not weeks. Attack chain for the Solution Manager flaw: an unauthenticated attacker abuses the missing auth check in the EEM/diagnostics agent to run OS commands on the SolMan host, which by design holds trusted RFC connections to every managed production system — turning one exposed SolMan into a landscape-wide compromise. For Visual Composer, attackers POST to /developmentserver/metadatauploader and drop a JSP webshell in servlet_jsp/irj/root/, then execute OS commands as <sid>adm. The business impact is worst-case: full system takeover, data exfiltration, fraudulent financial postings, and ransomware. Compliance consequences are severe — mandatory breach notification (GDPR Art. 33), SOX material-weakness reporting, and regulatory scrutiny for ignoring a CISA-catalogued KEV. A real incident looks exactly like the 2025 NetWeaver campaign: internet-facing SAP compromised, webshells and Brute Ratel/Cobalt Strike loaders deployed, and lateral movement into the corporate network. These items warrant out-of-band emergency patching and active compromise-hunting now.
HOTNEWS-003.mitigation:
1. Treat every listed note as an emergency change — invoke your out-of-band/emergency patch process immediately rather than waiting for the next cycle.
2. In parallel with patching, hunt for indicators of compromise: for Visual Composer inspect servlet_jsp/irj/root/ and the metadatauploader/developmentserver paths for unexpected .jsp/.war/.jar files; for RECON check USR02/SU01 and the AS Java UME for unrecognised admin users and review LM Configuration Wizard / CTCWebService access logs; for ICMAD review ICM access logs for anomalous/duplicated responses.
3. Implement the correcting notes via SNOTE, or apply the required kernel patch (ICMAD 3123396) and restart, or the AS Java patch (Visual Composer) — deploy the emergency patch SAP shipped for note 3594142.
4. If patching cannot be immediate, apply the note's interim mitigation now: disable the vulnerable service (e.g. remove/disable the Visual Composer VCFRAMEWORK and the metadatauploader endpoint, deactivate unused ICF services in SICF) and block external access at the Web Dispatcher/firewall.
5. For Solution Manager (note 2890213), because SolMan holds trusted RFC into production, also review and, if needed, rotate those trusted RFC connections and check for lateral movement.
6. If any IoC is found, do NOT just patch — invoke incident response, isolate the host, preserve forensics, and assume credential/data compromise.
7. For RISE, escalate to SAP ECS as a Priority 1 / security incident and coordinate emergency maintenance.
8. Verify each fix in SNOTE (PRSTATUS = E) and confirm kernel/AS Java patch level; confirm the vulnerable endpoint now returns 403/404.
9. After remediation, monitor for re-exploitation and add detections for these endpoints.
Caution: emergency changes still need a documented back-out plan and interface-owner notification; balance speed against a restart/downtime window.

HOTNEWS-004.risk: This finding flags catalog notes that appear in SNOTE with an incomplete / not-effective implementation status (e.g. 'Incompletely Implemented' or 'Partially Implemented'). This is more insidious than a plainly missing note because it creates a false sense of security: patch dashboards, auditors and the Basis team may see the note 'in progress' or listed, and assume the vulnerability is closed, when in fact the corrective code path is not fully active. SNOTE marks a note incomplete when automatic correction instructions were applied but required manual activities were skipped, when object adjustments (SPDD/SPAU) were left unresolved after a later Support Package overwrote the note's objects, or when a dependent note/prerequisite was never implemented. For a HotNews such as RECON or a Visual Composer RCE, a half-applied fix can leave the exact vulnerable endpoint reachable while the tracking system says otherwise — the worst of both worlds. The abuse scenario: an attacker exploits the still-open flaw exactly as if the note were missing, but the defenders waste critical response time because their records show the note as handled, delaying detection and patch verification. Business and compliance impact includes an inaccurate control evidence base (a SOX/ISO auditor relying on a status that misrepresents reality), plus the same breach exposure as an unpatched HotNews. System-consistency risk is also real: a partially implemented note can leave code/customising in an inconsistent state that causes runtime errors or dumps. A real incident looks like a post-breach review discovering that the 'implemented' note had unfinished manual steps or unresolved SPAU conflicts, so protection was never actually in place.
HOTNEWS-004.mitigation:
1. Open transaction SNOTE and, for each note in the affected_items list, display the implementation log and current status to see exactly which automatic/manual steps are outstanding.
2. Re-process the note to 'Completely Implemented' (target status PRSTATUS = E), executing every manual pre-/post-implementation activity described in the note text.
3. If the incompleteness stems from a Support Package overwriting note objects, resolve the modification adjustments in transaction SPAU (and SPDD for dictionary objects) and re-confirm the note.
4. Check and implement any prerequisite/dependent notes SNOTE reports as missing; HotNews fixes often chain several notes.
5. Where objects 'cannot be implemented', investigate whether a newer note version or the correcting Support Package (via Maintenance Planner + SPAM/SAINT) is the correct path instead of forcing SNOTE.
6. Do the rework in DEV first, then transport and re-confirm through QA to PROD under change control.
7. For RISE, coordinate with SAP ECS where the completion depends on a kernel/SP action they own.
8. Verify: SNOTE must show 'Completely Implemented', the implementation log must show no open manual tasks, and System Recommendations must drop the note after the collector job re-runs.
9. For actively-exploited notes in this list, additionally run the IoC checks from HOTNEWS-003, since a partial fix does not close the exploited flaw.
Caution: re-processing notes can re-trigger object adjustments and regression risk — test thoroughly and schedule any activation/restart in a maintenance window.

AUTH-001.risk: The role grants authorization object S_DEVELOP with OBJTYPE=DEBUG and ACTVT=02, which per SAP Note 65968 is 'change authorization in the debugger' — the capability commonly called Debug & Replace. This is among the most dangerous privileges an ABAP system can hand out. A holder can set a breakpoint anywhere, halt any running program at any statement, and overwrite the contents of any variable while the work process is executing. The classic abuse: step into a program, let execution reach an AUTHORITY-CHECK, and immediately afterwards set SY-SUBRC = 0 to force a 'passed' result; or edit a field value (an amount, a vendor bank key IBAN, a company code, a document release flag) an instant before the program COMMITs it to the database. Because the manipulation happens inside the running process, it bypasses virtually every authorization check, field-level control and business validation — none of the standard controls observe it. In an S/4HANA RISE production client a user with an otherwise modest role can therefore approve their own purchase order, redirect a payment run, unblock a blocked invoice, or read and alter data they have no business authorization for. It is also an elegant way to defeat GRC segregation-of-duties rules, because the fraud is committed inside the debugger rather than through a controlled screen that GRC monitors. A real incident looks like a fraudulent outbound payment or a silently altered master-data record with no matching change document — usually detected only downstream through financial reconciliation, because the debugger leaves almost no application-layer audit trail. This authorization has no legitimate place in any productive role.
AUTH-001.mitigation:
1. Enumerate offenders precisely: run report RSUSR002 (or SUIM -> Users -> By Authorization Values) selecting object S_DEVELOP with OBJTYPE=DEBUG and ACTVT=02; cross-check with RSUSR008_009_NEW critical-authorization catalog.
2. In PFCG open each offending role -> Authorizations tab -> locate the S_DEVELOP authorization and remove value 02 from ACTVT, leaving 03 (display debugging) only where support genuinely needs to inspect variables.
3. Where change-debugging is truly needed for break-fix, do NOT embed it in a standard role: create a dedicated firefighter role assigned only through GRC Emergency Access Management (or a logged, time-boxed temporary assignment).
4. Confine change-debugging to non-production; on DEV/QA use the newer S_DBG object (ABAP 7.57+, Note 65968) to scope change-debug to specific software components/packages.
5. Save and generate the profile in PFCG (SU24 keeps proposals clean); regenerate any composite roles that include the changed single roles.
6. Enable Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG, legacy SM19) and ensure debugging/replace events are recorded so any future use raises an alert (monitor via RSAU_READ_LOG / SM20).
7. Verify: re-run RSUSR002 for S_DEVELOP DEBUG/02 and confirm zero productive-role hits; have a test user attempt a change-debug and confirm refusal (SU53 shows the missing authorization).
8. Rollout caution: pull the change-debug from support roles only after the firefighter path is live and communicated, otherwise you break existing break-fix workflows; schedule in a change window and test in QA first.

AUTH-002.risk: The role grants S_RFCACL with a wildcard ('*') in the trusted-system/user fields (RFC_USER, RFC_SYSID or RFC_CLIENT) while RFC_EQUSER is not forced to 'Y'. S_RFCACL governs who may log on through a trusted-RFC relationship without presenting a password. With a wildcard, any calling system that is trusted by this system can log on here as ANY user in ANY client — including highly privileged accounts — simply by asserting the identity. SAP Note 1416085 exists precisely because this is so dangerous that SAP strips '*' from RFC_USER during authorization merges and warns on manual maintenance, and S_RFCACL is deliberately excluded from SAP_ALL. In a landscape this is the number-one lateral-movement path: an attacker who compromises a lower-trust system (for example a sandbox, a solution-management system, or a less-hardened satellite that is trusted by production) can pivot into production via SMT1/SM59 trusted destinations and assume a Basis or DDIC-class identity without ever needing a password. Because trusted logon binds to whatever user the caller names, the wildcard turns every trusting system into a skeleton key. In an S/4HANA RISE landscape, where central components (Solution Manager / Focused Run, GRC, SLT, PI/PO) hold trust into the production ERP, one weak node compromises everything downstream. A real incident looks like an interactive or programmatic logon in the production system under a privileged account originating over RFC from a peer system, followed by mass data access or configuration change — with the ticket/trust ticket masking the true origin. This is exactly the 'RFC hopping' scenario auditors and SAP security notes warn about.
AUTH-002.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for object S_RFCACL where RFC_USER, RFC_SYSID or RFC_CLIENT contain '*'; list holders and the roles.
2. In PFCG, for each role, replace the wildcards with the specific calling System ID(s) and client(s) that legitimately need trust, and set RFC_EQUSER='Y' so the trusted logon is bound to the same user only.
3. Review the trust relationships themselves in transaction SMT1 (trusted systems) and the associated destinations in SM59; remove any obsolete or overly broad trust.
4. Migrate legacy trust to the current model per SAP Note 3157268 (How-To-Guide: Migration of Trusted/Trusting Relationships) and its security note 3089413; after migration set profile parameter rfc/allowoldticket4tt = no (check/set via RZ11/RZ10) to block old trust tickets.
5. Ensure the RFC 'step users' behind trusted destinations are of type System/Communication with least-privilege roles, never SAP_ALL.
6. Regenerate profiles in PFCG and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no S_RFCACL wildcard remains in any assigned role.
7. Verify end-to-end: from each trusting system test a trusted call and confirm it only succeeds for the intended same-user/system combination (SU53 / SM59 connection test).
8. Rollout caution: coordinate with interface owners before tightening — mis-scoped trust breaks batch/monitoring interfaces; stage changes in QA, keep a back-out, and change one relationship at a time.

AUTH-003.risk: The role grants S_LOG_COM with COMMAND='*' and HOST='*'. S_LOG_COM authorizes the execution of external operating-system commands defined via SM69 and run via SM49 (or programmatically through function module SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE over RFC). With both COMMAND and HOST wildcarded, the holder can run essentially any external OS command on any application-server host in the system. This is a direct bridge off the ABAP stack down to the operating system running under the <sid>adm service account. An attacker uses it to read or exfiltrate files (the secure store / SSFS, transport files, kernel profiles), write a web shell or cron/at job, add themselves to an OS group, tamper with the database at the file level, or pivot laterally to other hosts sharing the same infrastructure. In an S/4HANA RISE context, even though the hyperscaler/SAP manages the infrastructure layer, OS-command execution inside the managed hosts still exposes the application secrets and can undermine the shared-responsibility boundary. Because the command list in SM69 can also contain parameter substitution, a poorly defined command plus wildcard authorization can be weaponized (argument injection) even without adding a new command. A real incident looks like an unexpected OS process spawned by the SAP work process, an outbound network connection from the app server, or modified files on the SAP mount — with the trigger being an SM49 execution or an RFC call to SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE from a semi-privileged user, mapping straight to CIS/DSAG critical-authorization findings and to a full host compromise.
AUTH-003.mitigation:
1. Find offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_LOG_COM with COMMAND='*' and/or HOST='*'; record roles and holders.
2. In PFCG, restrict S_LOG_COM to the specific, named external commands and target hosts each role must run; never leave COMMAND or HOST as '*'.
3. Review the defined external commands themselves in SM69: remove unused or dangerous commands, and ensure no command allows additional/free parameters that permit argument injection.
4. Tightly restrict who can maintain (SM69) and execute (SM49) external commands; separate the maintain and execute duties.
5. Restrict the RFC path too: ensure S_RFC does not expose the SXPG function group broadly (see AUTH-006) and apply UCON allowlisting to SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE.
6. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no S_LOG_COM wildcard remains in assigned roles.
7. Verify: with a test user, attempt SM49 for a non-authorized command and confirm the authorization is refused (SU53).
8. Enable the Security Audit Log (RSAU_CONFIG) for external-command execution and monitor SM49 usage.
9. Rollout caution: OS commands are frequently used by legitimate batch/monitoring jobs — confirm with the job/interface owners which commands are in use before narrowing, test in QA, and keep a rollback role version.

AUTH-004.risk: The role grants one or more of the role-content control objects with wildcards: S_USER_AUT with AUTH='*' plus create/change/activate activity (01/02/07), S_USER_TCD with TCD='*', or S_USER_VAL at all. These objects govern what a role/authorization administrator may put INTO a role: S_USER_AUT the authorization values, S_USER_TCD which transactions may be inserted, and S_USER_VAL which field values may be maintained. A holder of these with wildcards can define arbitrary authorization values, insert any transaction, and maintain any field value in a role they can edit — effectively minting a SAP_ALL-equivalent authorization for themselves or a confederate, without ever touching S_USER_GRP, S_USER_AGR or S_USER_PRO in a way that looks abnormal. This is the textbook self-escalation / authorization-forging path: a mid-level administrator gradually raises their own privileges, or plants a dormant powerful role, entirely within PFCG. In an S/4HANA RISE production system this defeats least-privilege and, critically, defeats segregation of duties — the person who builds authorizations can build themselves the very access that GRC is supposed to prevent, then use it and roll it back. A real incident is detected (if at all) only through PFCG change-document review or GRC firefighter/mitigation reporting showing a role whose content was expanded to critical objects by someone outside the security team, followed by that person exercising the new access. Combined with even display access to user administration it becomes full compromise of the authorization concept.
AUTH-004.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_USER_AUT (AUTH='*', ACTVT 01/02/07), S_USER_TCD (TCD='*'), and any S_USER_VAL; list holders.
2. Remove S_USER_AUT/S_USER_TCD/S_USER_VAL entirely from any role that is not a genuine security-administration role.
3. For legitimate security-admin roles, scope them: no AUTH='*' and no TCD='*'; grant only the authorization objects and transaction ranges that team is permitted to maintain.
4. Split duties across the S_USER_* family so that user administration (S_USER_GRP), role assignment (S_USER_AGR) and role content (S_USER_AUT/VAL/TCD) are held by different people where feasible.
5. Put all security-role changes under four-eyes / change control; where GRC is present, route role changes through GRC BRM/ARM with approval workflow.
6. Turn on and review PFCG change documents (report RSSCD100 for authorization change docs / table AGR_* change logs) so any content expansion is attributable and reviewed.
7. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm the wildcards are gone from assigned roles.
8. Verify: with a scoped admin test user, confirm they can no longer add an arbitrary critical transaction/authorization to a role (SU53 on the refusal).
9. Rollout caution: coordinate with the security/GRC team so their day-to-day maintenance still works after scoping; pilot on one admin first.

AUTH-005.risk: The role grants S_TCODE with TCD='*', allowing the holder to start every transaction in the system. S_TCODE is the first gate the ABAP kernel checks when a transaction is launched; a wildcard removes that gate entirely, so the only remaining protection is whatever object-level authorizations happen to be present in the role. In practice roles built with S_TCODE='*' are almost always also broad at the object level, so this is effectively unrestricted interactive access and it completely defeats menu-based restriction. From an attacker's perspective, a wildcard S_TCODE is a launchpad: they can reach SE16/SE16N (table data), SM49/SM69 (OS commands), SU01/PFCG (user and role administration), SM59/SMT1 (RFC/trust), SCC4 (client control), and STMS/SE38 (transport and code) — every high-value transaction is one command away, limited only by object authorizations that in such roles are frequently also open. In an S/4HANA RISE production client this is a critical finding: it breaks least-privilege at the most fundamental level, makes SoD analysis meaningless (the user can reach conflicting transactions at will), and is a direct SOX/ITGC deficiency. A real incident looks like a business user navigating directly to administrative or data-browser transactions that their job never requires, then reading or changing data outside any documented process — with the audit trail showing transaction starts that make no sense for the user's role. Wildcard S_TCODE is explicitly called out as a critical authorization in the SAP Security Baseline and DSAG guidelines.
AUTH-005.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_TCODE with TCD='*'; list the roles and their holders, and check RSUSR008_009_NEW.
2. In PFCG, remove the '*' from the S_TCODE authorization and rebuild the role's transaction set from the PFCG role menu, so S_TCODE values are generated from the menu (Authorizations tab -> regenerate).
3. Use an authorization trace (STAUTHTRACE, or ST01 short-term) on representative users to determine the transactions the role actually needs, and add only those to the menu.
4. Let SU24 provide the object-level proposals for the added transactions so the role is scoped at object level too, not just at S_TCODE.
5. Regenerate the profile and re-assign; for composite roles, regenerate them after the single roles are fixed.
6. Verify: re-run RSUSR002 for S_TCODE='*' and confirm zero assigned-role hits; have a test user attempt a transaction not in the role and confirm refusal (SU53).
7. Investigate why the role was built with a full wildcard (often a lift-and-shift shortcut) and correct the role-design standard so it cannot recur.
8. Rollout caution: users on a wildcard role may silently rely on transactions you don't yet know about — run the trace over a full business cycle, communicate, and provide a fast path to add missing legitimate transactions; test in QA before production cutover.

AUTH-006.risk: The role grants S_RFC with RFC_NAME='*' (function-group type), permitting remote invocation of ANY RFC-enabled function module. S_RFC is the authorization checked when a function module is called over RFC; a wildcard removes the per-function-group gate, so any RFC-reachable account holding this role can call the entire library of remote-enabled FMs. That library includes some of the most abusable modules in the system: RFC_READ_TABLE / RFC_READ_TABLE2 (read the contents of virtually any table, including USR02 password hashes, bank data, and configuration), SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE (run external OS commands, see AUTH-003), BAPI_* mass-data modules (create/change master and transactional data), and RFC_PING / system-info modules used for reconnaissance. In an S/4HANA RISE / BTP context this is the primary data-exfiltration and lateral-movement surface: OData services, the Cloud Connector, and integration middleware all reach the ABAP backend over RFC, so an over-broad S_RFC on a service or technical user turns a single credential leak into read/write access to the whole application. The classic attack chain is: obtain a low-value RFC or dialog credential, call RFC_READ_TABLE to dump USR02/USRACL and sensitive business tables, then use a BAPI or SXPG module to escalate or move laterally. A real incident looks like large, unusual RFC traffic from an interface user, bulk reads of sensitive tables with no corresponding application transaction, and is a documented Onapsis/SAP-security abuse pattern. It is a High-severity least-privilege and data-protection (GDPR) exposure.
AUTH-006.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_RFC with RFC_NAME='*'; pay special attention to technical/communication users and roles behind interfaces.
2. Determine what each interface actually calls: run an authorization/UCON trace (STAUTHTRACE, or the UCON RFC monitoring below) over a full processing cycle to capture the real function groups used.
3. In PFCG, replace RFC_NAME='*' with the specific function groups (RFC_TYPE=FUGR) each role/interface requires; scope ACTVT to 16 (execute).
4. Activate UCON (transaction UCONCOCKPIT / UCON RFC scenario): build the allowlist of externally-callable function modules over a logging phase, then switch to active blocking so even authorized users cannot reach modules outside the allowlist.
5. Ensure powerful modules (RFC_READ_TABLE, SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE) are not exposed to broad roles and are covered by UCON.
6. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no assigned role retains RFC_NAME='*'.
7. Verify: from the calling system/middleware, run the interface and confirm it still works with the scoped function groups; confirm a non-allowlisted call is blocked (SM59 test / SU53).
8. Rollout caution: this WILL break interfaces if mis-scoped — coordinate with every interface owner, run UCON in logging mode long enough to capture peak/period-end calls, stage in QA, and keep a documented back-out.
