AUTH-007.risk: The role grants S_TABU_NAM with TABLE='*' and ACTVT=02 (change). S_TABU_NAM authorizes table maintenance by explicit table/view name and was introduced by SAP Note 1481950 as the finer-grained companion to S_TABU_DIS; it is evaluated when the classic S_TABU_DIS (authorization-group) check does not already grant access. With TABLE='*' and change activity, the role grants direct write access to virtually every table in the system through generic maintenance tools such as SE16N, SM30/SM31 and table-view maintenance — completely bypassing the table authorization-group concept that is supposed to compartmentalize sensitive data. This means a holder can directly edit configuration and master/transactional tables without going through the application transactions that enforce business validation, workflow and change documents. Concrete abuse in S/4HANA production: change the contents of finance/customizing tables, alter a user's data, tamper with control tables that govern postings or number ranges, or modify security-relevant tables — all with no business-level audit trail and no four-eyes. This is a severe SoD and data-integrity risk and a direct ITGC/SOX finding because it lets someone circumvent every application control by writing straight to the persistence layer. A real incident looks like altered table rows with no corresponding change document or transaction log, discovered during reconciliation or a table-logging review, where the entry point was a generic table-maintenance transaction rather than the owning business transaction. Broad direct table write is a classic auditor red flag.
AUTH-007.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_TABU_NAM with TABLE='*' and ACTVT=02; list roles and holders.
2. Determine which tables the role legitimately maintains (trace with STAUTHTRACE over a business cycle) and, in PFCG, replace TABLE='*' with those specific table/view names.
3. Prefer display (ACTVT 03) over change (ACTVT 02); grant change only where a documented business need exists and no owning application transaction is available.
4. Ensure sensitive tables carry a proper table authorization group (maintain via SE54 -> Assign authorization group; the mapping lives in table TDDAT) so that both S_TABU_DIS and S_TABU_NAM checks are meaningful.
5. Use report SU24_S_TABU_NAM / SU24 to align default values when introducing name-based table authorizations.
6. Enable table change logging (rec/client parameter and the 'log data changes' flag in SE13/technical settings) for critical tables so any direct edits are recorded (review via SCU3).
7. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm the wildcard is gone from assigned roles.
8. Verify: with a test user, attempt SE16N change on an unauthorized table and confirm refusal (SU53).
9. Rollout caution: some teams genuinely maintain config via SM30 — inventory those needs first, test in QA, and provide the scoped table list before removing the wildcard.

AUTH-008.risk: The role grants S_TABU_DIS with change activity (ACTVT=02) for authorization group '*' or '&NC&'. S_TABU_DIS is the classic table-maintenance authorization that works at the level of table authorization GROUPS: each table is assigned a group and the object grants access to all tables in the granted group(s). Granting group '*' means change access to tables of every authorization group; granting '&NC&' means change access to all tables that have NO authorization group assigned — and in practice a very large number of standard and custom tables carry no group, so '&NC&' with change is nearly as broad as '*'. Either way this is sweeping direct table-maintenance access to most sensitive customizing and master-data tables via SM30/SM31/SE16N, bypassing the business transactions and their controls. In an S/4HANA production client this lets a holder edit configuration and master data directly at the persistence layer without application validation, workflow or reliable change documents — the same integrity and SoD exposure as AUTH-007 but reached through the group mechanism. The '&NC&' case is especially insidious because it is easy to grant 'by accident' (it looks narrow) yet effectively covers the long tail of ungrouped tables, including many custom Z-tables that hold sensitive data. A real incident looks like unauthorized changes to control or master tables with no matching application audit trail, surfaced only through table logging or reconciliation, where the offender used a generic maintenance transaction and their access rode on a broad or ungrouped S_TABU_DIS. Auditors treat '*'/'&NC&' with change as a critical table-authorization finding.
AUTH-008.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_TABU_DIS with DICBERCLS='*' or '&NC&' and ACTVT=02; list roles and holders.
2. Assign proper authorization groups to sensitive tables that currently have none: use SE54 -> Assign authorization group (mapping stored in table TDDAT); this removes tables from the '&NC&' catch-all.
3. In PFCG, replace DICBERCLS='*'/'&NC&' with the specific, narrow authorization group(s) the role must maintain, and prefer display (03) over change (02).
4. Where finer control is needed than groups allow, complement with name-based S_TABU_NAM for individual tables rather than widening the group.
5. Use STAUTHTRACE to confirm which groups/tables the role really needs before narrowing.
6. Enable change logging for critical tables (rec/client + SE13 log flag) and review via SCU3.
7. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no assigned role keeps '*'/'&NC&' with change.
8. Verify: with a test user, attempt to maintain a table outside the granted group and confirm refusal (SU53).
9. Rollout caution: reassigning authorization groups affects everyone who relied on the old grouping — inventory current maintenance, do the SE54 changes in a transport, test in QA across a full cycle, and communicate to config owners.

AUTH-009.risk: The role grants S_TABU_CLI with CLIIDMAINT=X, which authorizes maintenance of client-INDEPENDENT (cross-client) tables. Normal table maintenance is client-specific, so changes affect only the current client; S_TABU_CLI is the additional gate required to change tables whose data is shared across all clients of the system — for example T000 (client table and its protection settings), and many technical/customizing tables that are cross-client by design. Because a single change to a cross-client table takes effect in every client on that system simultaneously, this authorization has system-wide blast radius. The most dangerous combination is S_TABU_CLI together with SCC4 (or S_TABU_* on T000): a holder can alter the client role and, critically, the client-change and cross-client customizing protection flags — turning a 'not modifiable / no changes allowed' production client into one that accepts customizing and repository changes, or opening cross-client customizing that was meant to be locked. That single move disables a fundamental production safeguard for the whole system, enabling further unauthorized changes that would otherwise be blocked. In an S/4HANA RISE production system this is a High-severity change-control and integrity risk and a clear ITGC finding, because production immutability depends on exactly these flags. A real incident looks like the production client's protection settings being flipped, followed by unexpected customizing/repository changes appearing directly in production — with the T000/SCC4 change being the enabling event. Cross-client maintenance must be confined to a very small, controlled administrator population.
AUTH-009.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_TABU_CLI with CLIIDMAINT=X; correlate with who also holds SCC4 and broad S_TABU_DIS/NAM (see AUTH-007/008).
2. Remove S_TABU_CLI from any role that is not a core system-administration role; grant it only to a small, named group of Basis administrators.
3. Confirm production client protection in SCC4: the production client should be 'Not modifiable', with 'No changes to cross-client customizing objects' and 'No changes to repository' set; changes to these settings must be tightly controlled.
4. Enforce four-eyes / change control on any cross-client table maintenance and log it.
5. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm S_TABU_CLI is limited to the intended administrators.
6. Ensure table logging is active for T000 and other critical cross-client tables (rec/client, SE13 log flag) and review via SCU3.
7. Verify: with a non-admin test user, attempt to maintain a cross-client table and confirm refusal (SU53); confirm SCC4 protection flags are unchanged.
8. Rollout caution: some legitimate cross-client customizing (e.g. transport routes, currencies) requires this object during projects — grant it temporarily and controlled rather than permanently; test in QA and keep an approval trail.

AUTH-010.risk: The role grants S_DATASET with FILENAME='*' and PROGRAM='*'. S_DATASET is the authorization checked when ABAP performs OPEN DATASET operations against the application-server file system; its fields scope which physical files (FILENAME) and which programs (PROGRAM) may read/write, together with the activity (33 read, 34 write/delete). With both fields wildcarded, any ABAP program the user can run may read or overwrite ARBITRARY files on the application-server host under the <sid>adm identity. This turns file access into a powerful primitive: read the SecStore/SSFS files and profile parameters, harvest transport data files, tamper with or truncate logs to cover tracks, or write attacker-controlled content into a path that another process will execute or ingest. Combined with the ability to run reports (AUTH-011) or custom Z-programs that call OPEN DATASET, wildcard S_DATASET is a route to disclosing secrets and undermining the integrity of the SAP file layer without ever touching the OS shell directly. In an S/4HANA RISE managed environment this still exposes application-owned secrets and files within the customer's responsibility boundary and can be chained with other weaknesses for escalation. A real incident looks like sensitive files being read or modified by an SAP work process outside any documented interface — for example configuration or credential files being copied to an output directory, or log files being altered — with the enabling factor being a program with unrestricted S_DATASET. It is a High-severity confidentiality/integrity exposure and a common finding in SAP file-handling reviews.
AUTH-010.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_DATASET with FILENAME='*' and PROGRAM='*'; list roles and holders.
2. In PFCG, scope S_DATASET to the specific logical/physical file paths and the specific programs each role needs; never leave FILENAME='*' with write access (ACTVT 34).
3. Constrain the file system itself: define allowed paths via logical file names (transaction FILE) and the physical-path allowlist in table SPTH, so even authorized programs cannot stray outside approved directories.
4. Review custom programs that use OPEN DATASET (search via SE38/Code Inspector) and ensure they use logical file names and validate paths, preventing directory traversal.
5. Prefer read-only (ACTVT 33) where write is not strictly required.
6. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no assigned role retains the FILENAME='*'/PROGRAM='*' combination.
7. Verify: run an authorization trace (STAUTHTRACE) while executing the legitimate file interface to confirm it still works with the scoped values; attempt an out-of-scope file access with a test user and confirm refusal (SU53).
8. Rollout caution: batch/interface jobs frequently read and write server files — inventory those paths with the interface owners first, test in QA over a full cycle, and stage the SPTH/FILE tightening carefully to avoid breaking legitimate file transfers.

AUTH-011.risk: The role grants S_PROGRAM with SUBMIT/BTCSUBMIT activity and P_GROUP='*' or blank, allowing execution of any ABAP report regardless of its program authorization group. S_PROGRAM is checked when a report is started via SA38/SE38 or submitted in background; the P_GROUP field is meant to limit a user to reports of specific program authorization groups. A wildcard or blank P_GROUP defeats that, so the holder can run any executable report — including powerful standard utilities such as the RSUSR* user/authorization reports, mass-maintenance and data-extraction programs, and any custom Z-report — bypassing the transaction-menu restrictions that would normally keep those reports out of reach. This matters because a great deal of sensitive functionality in SAP lives in reports rather than dialog transactions; being able to SUBMIT anything is close to being able to start anything, but through a channel (SA38) that role designers often overlook. Concrete abuse in production: run a report that reads or exports sensitive data (payroll, finance, personal data) outside the owning application, execute a mass-update report that changes data without the usual controls, or run a diagnostic/authorization report to enumerate the landscape for further attack. In an S/4HANA production client this is a High-severity least-privilege and data-protection gap and a frequent SoD bypass, because a user constrained at the transaction level can still reach conflicting or sensitive functionality via SUBMIT. A real incident looks like execution of a report that the user's role has no business reason to run, producing a data extract or mass change with no corresponding transaction footprint.
AUTH-011.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_PROGRAM where P_ACTION includes SUBMIT/BTCSUBMIT and P_GROUP is '*' or blank; list roles and holders.
2. Assign program authorization groups to sensitive reports: set the group in the report attributes (SE38 -> Goto -> Attributes) or use report RSCSAUTH to assign authorization groups en masse (the group is stored in the SECU field of table TRDIR).
3. In PFCG, replace P_GROUP='*'/blank with the specific program groups the role needs, and scope P_ACTION to what is required (e.g. SUBMIT only).
4. Prefer driving report execution through defined transactions in the role menu rather than open SA38 access.
5. Use STAUTHTRACE to confirm which report groups the role genuinely uses before narrowing.
6. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no assigned role retains a wildcard/blank P_GROUP with SUBMIT.
7. Verify: with a test user, attempt to run a sensitive report (e.g. an RSUSR* report) via SA38 and confirm refusal (SU53).
8. Rollout caution: many operational jobs SUBMIT reports in background — inventory those with the job owners, ensure the relevant reports carry the correct groups, test in QA, and communicate before removing the wildcard so scheduled jobs do not fail.

AUTH-012.risk: The role grants S_BTCH_NAM with BTCUNAME='*'. S_BTCH_NAM controls under which user identity a background job STEP may run: when a job is scheduled, the step can be told to execute in the security context of a named 'step user', and S_BTCH_NAM is the authorization that permits choosing that user. With BTCUNAME='*', the holder can schedule job steps that run under ANY other user's identity — including technical batch users or accounts that carry SAP_ALL or other powerful roles. This is background-job impersonation: the attacker does not need to know the target user's password; they simply schedule a step to run as that user and let the SAP batch scheduler execute their chosen program (or a standard report) in the privileged context. The consequence is privileged code execution and data change performed under someone else's identity, which also muddies attribution — the job and its footprint appear to belong to the impersonated step user, not the attacker. In an S/4HANA production client this is a High-severity privilege-escalation and SoD risk: a user with a modest interactive role can reach SAP_ALL-level effect indirectly through a scheduled step, and can defeat GRC SoD analysis because the powerful action is executed by the batch identity. A real incident looks like a background job scheduled by one user but running under a privileged batch user, performing a mass change or data extract, discovered through job-log (SM37) review showing a mismatch between the scheduling user and the step user. Wildcard S_BTCH_NAM is a recognized critical authorization in SAP/DSAG guidance.
AUTH-012.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_BTCH_NAM with BTCUNAME='*'; list roles and holders.
2. In PFCG, restrict S_BTCH_NAM to the specific, legitimate step-user(s) each role needs; never '*'.
3. Make batch step-users dedicated service accounts of user type 'System' (SU01) with least-privilege roles, and never schedule steps under interactive or SAP_ALL-class users.
4. Review existing scheduled jobs (SM37) for steps running under privileged batch users and correct those that impersonate powerful accounts.
5. Separate batch duties across S_BTCH_JOB (schedule/manage jobs), S_BTCH_NAM (run as another user) and S_BTCH_ADM (batch administration) so no single role concentrates them.
6. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no assigned role keeps BTCUNAME='*'.
7. Verify: with a test user, attempt to schedule a step under an unauthorized step-user and confirm refusal (SU53); review SM37 to confirm no unexpected impersonation remains.
8. Rollout caution: legitimate interfaces sometimes run steps under a shared batch user — confirm the required step-users with job owners before locking down, test in QA, and stage the change so scheduled production jobs do not fail.

AUTH-013.risk: The role contains one or more sensitive Basis/administration transactions delivered in S_TCODE — the module flags the presence of high-risk codes such as SE16/SE16N/SM30 (direct table display/maintenance), SE38/SA38/SE80 (ABAP and report execution), SM49/SM69 (external OS commands), SU01/SU10/PFCG/SU02/SU03 (user and role administration), SM59/SMT1/SMGW (RFC, trust and gateway), SCC4/SCC5/RZ10/RZ11 (client and profile-parameter administration), SICF/SMICM (ICF services / ICM), STMS/SE09/SE10 (transport management), SM19/RSAU_CONFIG/SM20/ST01 (audit and trace), SUIM, and DBACOCKPIT/DB02/SM12/SM13 (database, lock and update administration). Each of these grants capability that, in a productive role assigned to non-Basis users, is high-risk on its own and frequently violates segregation of duties. The danger is twofold. First, individually powerful transactions in a business role give ordinary users administrative reach they should never have — e.g. a finance user who also holds SE16N can read data outside their applications, or one holding SM59 can inspect and abuse RFC destinations. Second, and worse, is toxic combination: a role that mixes, say, user administration (SU01/PFCG) with transport control (STMS) or with table maintenance (SM30) enables self-service privilege escalation, change smuggling, or control circumvention, all within one role. In an S/4HANA production client this is a High-severity SoD and ITGC exposure and a recurring SOX audit finding. A real incident looks like a business user reaching an administrative transaction unrelated to their job — browsing tables, editing an RFC destination, or touching a security-audit setting — with the enabling factor being an over-broad role that accreted Basis transactions over time. Even where object-level authorizations are partly restrictive, mere reachability of these transactions is a governance and audit problem.
AUTH-013.mitigation:
1. Enumerate exposure: run SUIM / RSUSR002 for S_TCODE against the sensitive transaction list (SE16, SE16N, SM30, SE38, SA38, SM49, SM69, SU01, PFCG, SM59, SMT1, SCC4, RZ10, SICF, STMS, RSAU_CONFIG, SM20, DBACOCKPIT, SM12, SM13, etc.) and list which roles and users hold each.
2. For every hit, assess it against the role's documented business purpose; a business role should contain no Basis/administration transaction without written justification.
3. In PFCG, remove the unjustified transactions from business roles (rebuild S_TCODE from the role menu) and relocate genuine administrative tasks into dedicated, least-privilege administrator roles.
4. Design those admin roles by duty (user admin, transport admin, RFC/interface admin, DB/monitoring) so no single role concentrates conflicting Basis powers, and gate emergency use through GRC firefighter (EAM).
5. Run SoD analysis (GRC Access Control ruleset, or manual conflict matrix) to catch toxic combinations before and after remediation.
6. Regenerate profiles; where transactions must remain, tighten the underlying object authorizations via SU24 so reachability does not equal full capability.
7. Verify: re-run RSUSR002/SUIM and confirm the sensitive transactions no longer sit in business roles; spot-check with a test user (SU53 on a removed transaction).
8. Rollout caution: some 'Basis' transactions have limited legitimate business use (e.g. SM37 job monitoring) — confirm real needs with process owners, remove in a change window with QA testing, and provide a controlled path for genuine exceptions.

AUTH-014.risk: The role grants S_DEVELOP with create/change activity (ACTVT 01/02) on development object types other than pure debugging — i.e. developer access to create or modify ABAP repository objects such as programs, classes, function modules and dictionary objects. In a properly governed landscape all code and repository changes are made in the development system and moved to production only through the transport management system, so the production and often the QA client are set 'not modifiable' and no one holds developer change access there. Where a role grants S_DEVELOP create/change in a productive system, it breaks that model: a holder can create or alter ABAP directly, introducing untested or malicious code without the review, four-eyes and audit trail that the transport process provides. This is a serious code-integrity and backdoor risk. Concrete abuse: implant a small report or an enhancement/implicit-enhancement that reads or exfiltrates data, weakens an authorization check, or creates a hidden privileged user, then optionally delete it — leaving little trace beyond version history. Even without malice, ad-hoc production code changes cause outages and defeat change control. In an S/4HANA RISE production client this is a High-severity finding and a direct SOX/ITGC change-management deficiency, because the whole point of the transport-based process is that production code cannot be altered outside a controlled pipeline. A real incident looks like an object with a recent local change in production that never came through a transport, or an unexplained enhancement to a standard program, discovered via version management (SE80/SE38 versions) or a repository comparison — the enabling factor being developer change access that should not exist in production at all.
AUTH-014.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: run RSUSR002 for S_DEVELOP with ACTVT 01/02 on non-DEBUG object types in the production (and QA) systems; list roles and holders.
2. Remove development create/change authorizations from all productive-system roles; developer S_DEVELOP change access belongs only in the development system and only for developers.
3. Enforce system-level immutability: confirm the client change option in SCC4 and the global/system change option (SE06 / SE03 -> System Change Option) set production to 'Not modifiable', so repository/customizing changes are blocked regardless of role.
4. Ensure all changes flow through STMS transports from DEV; there should be no local objects created directly in production.
5. Where a rare, controlled production fix is unavoidable, gate it through GRC firefighter (EAM) with logging and a follow-up transport, not a standing role.
6. Regenerate profiles and re-run RSUSR002 to confirm no assigned production role retains S_DEVELOP create/change.
7. Verify: with a test user in production, attempt to change an object in SE38/SE80 and confirm it is refused by both the authorization and the system-change setting (SU53).
8. Rollout caution: some support processes lean on production 'quick fixes' — communicate the transport-only policy, ensure the firefighter/transport path is ready first, and test the SCC4/SE06 lockdown in QA to avoid blocking legitimate customizing where it is still allowed.

AUTH-015.risk: Profile parameter auth/object_disabling_active is set to Y, which enables the global deactivation of individual authorization objects via transaction AUTH_SWITCH_OBJECTS. When an authorization object is globally disabled, the ABAP runtime stops checking it everywhere — every AUTHORITY-CHECK against that object silently succeeds, system-wide, in every program and transaction. This is a powerful maintenance switch that SAP provides for specific, documented situations, but leaving the capability active in production is dangerous because it means a single administrative action can remove an entire authorization control across the whole system without changing a single role. The abuse scenario: an attacker (or a careless administrator) who can reach AUTH_SWITCH_OBJECTS disables a security-relevant object — for example a table-access, RFC, or a custom Z-authorization object protecting sensitive functionality — and thereafter every user passes the check for that object, quietly widening access far beyond what the role design intended. Because roles and profiles look unchanged, this kind of weakening is easy to miss in a normal authorization review; nothing in PFCG reflects it, and SU53/traces show the check 'passing' as if authorized. In an S/4HANA production client this is a Medium-severity control-integrity risk: it undermines the reliability of the authorization concept that SOX/ITGC and GDPR access controls depend on, and a disabled object could mask a breach. A real incident looks like unexplained broad access to a function that role analysis says should be restricted, ultimately traced to a globally-disabled authorization object rather than to any role assignment. The parameter should be off unless a specific, approved object genuinely must be disabled.
AUTH-015.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current setting: check auth/object_disabling_active in RZ11 (dynamic view) and in the instance/DEFAULT profile via RZ10; note whether it is set in the profile or only dynamically.
2. Review what is actually disabled: run transaction AUTH_SWITCH_OBJECTS and list any authorization objects currently globally switched off; investigate why and re-enable any that are not covered by a documented, approved exception.
3. Set auth/object_disabling_active = N in the instance/DEFAULT profile via RZ10 unless a specific object must remain disabled with sign-off; save and activate the profile.
4. Determine the parameter's dynamic status in RZ11; if it is not dynamically switchable, schedule a coordinated instance restart in a change window for the profile change to take effect.
5. After the SU25/upgrade activities that legitimately use object disabling are complete, ensure the switch is returned to N.
6. Restrict who can reach AUTH_SWITCH_OBJECTS (S_TCODE plus the underlying administration authorization) to a minimal Basis group and put its use under change control.
7. Verify: re-check RZ11 shows N (post-restart if required) and AUTH_SWITCH_OBJECTS shows no unintended objects disabled; confirm a previously-disabled object is checked again via an authorization trace (STAUTHTRACE) / SU53.
8. Rollout caution: an object may have been disabled to work around a genuine issue — re-enabling it can surface authorization failures, so validate in QA first, review SU53/traces for fallout, and coordinate with application owners before enabling in production.

STDUSR-001.risk:
Profile parameter login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = 0 leaves the kernel-hardcoded SAP* emergency user active as a fallback. With this value, if the SAP* user master record is ever missing or deleted from a client (via a botched client copy/import, a direct DB delete of USR02, or a freshly created client that has no SAP*), the kernel synthesises SAP* on the fly with the well-known default password PASS.
That synthetic SAP* runs with NO authority checks at all — it is an implicit super-user equivalent to SAP_ALL/SAP_NEW and ignores normal authorization objects, in ANY client on the system.
This is one of the oldest and most reliable full-compromise backdoors in the ABAP stack. It is especially dangerous on RISE/S/4HANA because SAP operates Basis but the customer still owns client-level user administration, so a forgotten client (001/066) or a client copy gone wrong is the customer's liability.
Attack scenario: an insider or attacker who can delete the SAP* record in any client (or finds a client where it never existed) logs on interactively as SAP* / PASS with unrestricted power. They immediately create a dialog user with SAP_ALL, or directly read/export master and financial data, post fraudulent FI documents, or open OS access via SM49/SM69.
Because the fallback works in every client, low-traffic clients such as 000/001/066 are the preferred, poorly-monitored entry point.
Business impact: total system compromise, collapse of segregation-of-duties, SOX ITGC failure, GDPR-reportable breach, and financial fraud. A real incident looks like a sudden SAP* logon in client 066 or 001 off-hours, followed by mass authorization grants and an unexplained super-user session in SM20.
STDUSR-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current value with transaction RZ11 (dynamic view) for login/no_automatic_user_sapstar on every application server.
2. In RZ10 edit the DEFAULT profile and each instance profile, set login/no_automatic_user_sapstar = 1, save and activate.
3. Because this is a static parameter, schedule an instance restart to activate it. On RISE, raise the parameter/kernel change through the SAP-managed-services support channel and coordinate the restart window (customer cannot restart the instance directly).
4. Ensure a real SAP* user master exists in EVERY client including 000/001/066 (transaction SU01); give it a long random password stored in a vault, and assign it to user group SUPER so only super-admins can alter it.
5. Strip SAP_ALL/SAP_NEW from that SAP* master and then lock it (SU01 / SU10) — a locked real SAP* also blocks the kernel fallback.
6. Enable table-change logging on USR02 (rec/client) and monitor SM20 so any deletion of SAP* is detected.
7. Verify after restart: RZ11 shows the value = 1, and report RSUSR003 confirms SAP* exists and is locked in all clients.
Caution: NEVER delete SAP* to 'fix' this. Test the profile change in a non-production system first and confirm scheduled/background jobs do not depend on an unlocked SAP*.

STDUSR-002.risk:
One or more standard/technical users (SAP*, DDIC, SAPCPIC, EARLYWATCH, TMSADM, SAPSUPPORT) still carry their well-known SAP default passwords (for example SAP* = 06071992, DDIC = 19920706, and the kernel SAP* fallback = PASS).
These passwords are published in every SAP hacking guide and are the very first credentials any attacker or automated scanner tries against a discovered SAP instance.
DDIC is not an ordinary account: it owns software logistics, is required in client 000 for upgrades/SPAM/SAINT, and can bypass many application checks. TMSADM is the Transport Management System RFC user that exists identically across the whole transport domain, so one cracked TMSADM equals landscape-wide transport and RFC access. SAPCPIC and SAPSUPPORT are communication users used for RFC/remote support.
Attack scenario: the attacker enumerates clients from the logon screen, tries DDIC/19920706 or SAP*/06071992, obtains a dialog or RFC logon, then escalates by granting SAP_ALL or by calling privileged RFC-enabled function modules. Because TMSADM/SAPCPIC are communication users, they enable programmatic function-module access across systems without a GUI.
Business impact: full compromise, fraudulent postings, mass data exfiltration (GDPR), and a direct SOX ITGC finding because password controls demonstrably failed.
A real incident looks like a successful DDIC logon from an unusual terminal, or a burst of RFC calls authenticating as TMSADM from an unexpected host.
STDUSR-002.mitigation:
1. Run report RSUSR003 (via SA38/SE38) — it lists SAP*, DDIC, SAPCPIC, EARLYWATCH and TMSADM across all clients with their password status (default/initial) and lock status.
2. For each flagged account, set a new strong password in SU01 (or SU10 for mass change); record DDIC/SAP* credentials in a privileged-access vault since they are needed for maintenance.
3. For TMSADM, regenerate the password consistently across the transport domain using report TMS_UPDATE_PWD_OF_TMSADM and follow SAP Note 1414256 so transports keep working.
4. Assign these users to group SUPER (SU01) so only super-admins can maintain them.
5. Lock any standard user not needed for daily operations (see STDUSR-003).
6. Harden the password policy: login/min_password_lng, login/min_password_digits/letters/specials, disable trivial passwords, and login/fails_to_user_lock.
7. Verify by re-running RSUSR003 — every standard user must show 'password changed' and no defaults.
Caution: changing DDIC, TMSADM or EARLYWATCH passwords can break transports, EarlyWatch collection and RFC interfaces. Coordinate with the transport admin and (on RISE) SAP managed services, and test in a non-production system before touching production.

STDUSR-003.risk:
Standard users are not locked. SAP, DSAG and the SAP Security Baseline require SAP*, DDIC, SAPCPIC and EARLYWATCH — and any standard user in clients 000/001/066 — to be locked and unlocked only for controlled maintenance, and SAP*/DDIC must never serve as everyday dialog users.
An unlocked, dialog-capable SAP*/DDIC is a large attack surface: it is a standing target for password guessing, brute force and credential stuffing, and if the default password is also present (see STDUSR-002) it is a trivial takeover.
Clients 000/001/066 are frequently forgotten: 066 is the historical EarlyWatch client, 001 is a template client, and both are low-traffic, weakly monitored places where an attacker prefers to operate.
Even when passwords are strong, leaving these accounts open still increases brute-force exposure and is a direct baseline/audit deviation.
Attack scenario: an attacker runs a slow credential-stuffing campaign against DDIC in client 000; because the account is unlocked and dialog-capable, a single success yields an interactive session that can run any transaction and grant further authorizations.
Business impact: a privilege-escalation foothold, lateral movement across clients, and an audit/compliance finding. A real incident looks like repeated failed logons followed by a successful DDIC or SAP* logon in a rarely used client, off-hours.
STDUSR-003.mitigation:
1. Run RSUSR003 to list the lock status of standard users per client, and RSUSR200 to review last-logon/inactivity.
2. In SU01 (or SU10 for mass action) set an administrator lock on SAP*, DDIC, SAPCPIC and EARLYWATCH in every client, prioritising 000/001/066.
3. Keep DDIC/SAP* out of daily use: do not operate them as Dialog users; unlock only inside an approved maintenance window and re-lock immediately afterwards.
4. If client 066 is unused, delete it following SAP Note 1749142, and lock/close unused client 001 via SCC4 (set it to not-modifiable, no client copies).
5. Assign the accounts to group SUPER so only authorized admins can unlock them.
6. Set login/fails_to_user_lock and login/failed_user_auto_unlock = 0 so brute-force attempts result in a persistent lock.
7. Verify by re-running RSUSR003 (all standard users show 'locked') and review SM19/SM20 security-audit logs for logon attempts.
Caution: never delete SAP* (see STDUSR-001). Locking DDIC during an upgrade or transport import can break jobs — coordinate with Basis and test in non-production first.

TRUST-001.risk:
One or more systems are configured in RFCSYSACL as TRUSTED to log on to this system via trusted RFC. Trust must only ever flow from an equal-or-higher security tier down to a lower one — a production system must NEVER trust a development or QA system.
When production trusts a lower tier, any user who holds a dialog logon plus the S_RFCACL authorization in that lower system can hop into production without knowing a production password, using SM59 trusted destinations or trusted RFC calls. Development and QA typically have relaxed controls, more developers, debug/replace access and weaker passwords, so they are the easy foothold.
The module flags trusted SIDs beginning with D/Q/S/T as likely non-production and raises the severity accordingly.
Attack scenario: an attacker who has compromised (or a developer who abuses) a dev account opens a trusted destination to production, or issues an RFC with trusted logon, and lands in production as their mapped user. If S_RFCACL in production is maintained with wildcards, this becomes arbitrary-user impersonation, up to and including super-users.
Business impact: segregation-of-duties bypass, unauthorized production changes, financial fraud and mass data theft; this dev-to-prod pivot is a classic pattern in real SAP breaches.
A real incident looks like a production logon via trusted RFC that originates from a development host and uses a user that only normally exists in dev.
TRUST-001.mitigation:
1. In SMT1 (or by reviewing table RFCSYSACL) list every system that is allowed to log on to this system via trusted RFC.
2. Delete any trust entry that originates FROM a lower tier (dev/QA into prod). Trust must be one-directional from higher to lower security tier only.
3. For legitimate trust, tighten S_RFCACL in PFCG/SU24 to specific users, the specific calling SID, RFC_EQUSER = Y (same-user propagation) and specific transactions — never S_RFCACL with '*' (cross-reference AUTH-002).
4. Enforce SNC encryption on trusted connections (STRUST/SNC) so trust material cannot be sniffed.
5. Migrate all relationships to the 2020 trust security method (see TRUST-003).
6. Verify: SMT1 shows only approved higher-tier trusting systems, and use SUIM (users by authorization value) to enumerate who actually holds S_RFCACL in production.
Caution: removing a trust relationship can break dependent interfaces, CUA (central user administration) and Solution Manager. Inventory the callers first, coordinate with interface owners, and test the removal in non-production before applying it to production.

TRUST-002.risk:
The system trusts itself for trusted RFC — either rfc/selftrust = 1 or a self-referential RFCSYSACL entry naming the local SID.
Self-trust means a trusted destination that loops back to the same system will accept trusted-RFC identity propagation locally. Combined with the S_RFCACL authorization, a lower-privileged local user can therefore log on as a different, higher-privileged user on the same box without a password.
In effect, self-trust turns any user who holds S_RFCACL into a potential impersonator of privileged users on that system, which is a direct local privilege-escalation path.
Attack scenario: a user with S_RFCACL and the ability to create or modify a self-pointing trusted destination in SM59 calls a function module as an administrative user — for example to grant themselves SAP_ALL, run external OS commands via SM49/SM69, or read sensitive tables. Because the hop is internal, it is easy to overlook in network monitoring.
Business impact: privilege escalation and segregation-of-duties bypass, where a single compromised low-privilege account becomes full administrative control.
A real incident looks like a user gaining privileges with no corresponding administrator action, and RFC traffic looping to the local host but authenticating as another, more powerful user.
TRUST-002.mitigation:
1. Check rfc/selftrust in RZ11. If it is 1, plan to set it to 0 in RZ10 (DEFAULT/instance profile) unless a documented, reviewed scenario genuinely requires it.
2. In SMT1 remove any self-referential trust entry (the local SID trusting itself) that is not required.
3. Schedule a restart to activate the parameter change (coordinate the window with SAP managed services on RISE).
4. Where self-trust is truly needed by an embedded scenario, scope S_RFCACL tightly: specific users only, RFC_EQUSER = Y so only same-user propagation is permitted, and no '*' values.
5. Migrate the relationship to the new trust method (TRUST-003) so even self-trust uses the unique-system-ID mechanism.
6. Verify: RZ11 shows rfc/selftrust = 0, SMT1 shows no self entry, and a SUIM review confirms the reduced population of S_RFCACL holders.
Caution: some embedded/same-system applications rely on self-trust — confirm with the application owners before disabling, and test in non-production first to avoid breaking those flows.

TRUST-003.risk:
Trusted RFC still relies on the legacy trust-ticket method rather than the 2020 security method: either rfc/allowoldticket4tt accepts old tickets, or RFCSYSACL entries have not been migrated (migration method 3).
The legacy trusted/trusting mechanism (pre SAP Note 3089413) used a trust assertion that was not bound to a unique, unforgeable system identity. It is exposed to the capture-replay weakness (CVE-2023-0014) and to improper authentication in the RFC layer (CVE-2021-27610).
An attacker who obtains logon material — by sniffing unencrypted RFC traffic or by reading table RFCSYSACL — can replay or forge a trust assertion and authenticate as a trusted system against any trusting system in the landscape. Researchers demonstrated this as a potentially wormable, landscape-wide chain (SEC Consult / Onapsis).
Attack scenario: capture the trust assertion from one system, replay it against another trusting system, land as an arbitrary or privileged user, then chain onward across every trusting system and use further RFCs for code execution.
Business impact: landscape-wide compromise, remote code execution via follow-on RFC, and mass data breach — all from actively-discussed, already-patched exploit chains, which makes an unpatched estate an audit red flag.
A real incident looks like unexpected trusted logons, replayed tickets, and alerts from an SAP threat monitor about legacy-method trust usage.
TRUST-003.mitigation:
1. Implement SAP Notes 3089413 (kernel + Basis correction, on BOTH the trusted and the trusting systems) and 3157268 (migration how-to); confirm the minimum kernel/SP level.
2. In SMT1 migrate each trust relationship to the new security method (unique system ID), re-creating the relationship on both sides.
3. Once every relationship is migrated, set rfc/allowoldticket4tt = no in RZ10 so legacy tickets are rejected.
4. Enforce SNC encryption (STRUST) on trusted RFC so tickets cannot be sniffed, deny direct table access to RFCSYSACL, and enable table-change logging on RFCSYSACL.
5. Restart after kernel/parameter changes (coordinate with SAP managed services on RISE).
6. Verify with SAP's check report for the note (e.g. zcheck_note_3089413 from the SAP security-services-tools repository) or confirm SMT1 shows every relationship as migrated, and RZ11 shows rfc/allowoldticket4tt = no.
Caution: setting allowoldticket4tt = no before all partners are migrated will break trust. Migrate every partner first in a staged, non-production-first rollout, and watch for certificate-path errors (KBA 3400529) after applying 3089413.

TRUST-004.risk:
One or more trusted RFC destinations (SM59 type 3) are configured with a fixed stored logon user instead of 'current user' propagation.
The entire purpose of trusted RFC is current-user identity propagation with no stored password. Storing a fixed user turns the destination into a stored-credential hop that always executes as that user — which in practice is often a highly privileged technical or dialog account.
Anyone who can execute that destination, or invoke a report/function that uses it, inherits the fixed user's authorizations in the target system, which defeats the trusted-RFC identity model and destroys accountability because every action in the target is logged as the single technical user.
Attack scenario: a low-privileged user invokes the trusted destination (or a program that fronts it) and executes in the target as the stored privileged user, performing actions far beyond their own rights; alternatively an attacker extracts the stored credentials from the destination and reuses them elsewhere.
Business impact: privilege escalation, breakdown of segregation-of-duties and non-repudiation, and an audit failure because activity cannot be attributed to real individuals.
A real incident looks like a stream of business actions in the target system all attributed to one technical user, but actually performed by many different people.
TRUST-004.mitigation:
1. In SM59 review every ABAP (type 3) destination flagged as Trusted System = Yes; use table RFCDES to build the full inventory.
2. For each trusted destination set the logon to 'Current User' and clear the stored user and password — do not store credentials on trusted destinations.
3. If a fixed user is genuinely unavoidable, use a dedicated Communication (type C) user with least privilege — never a dialog user and never SAP_ALL.
4. Restrict who can use the destination via the relevant S_RFCACL / authorization design in the target system.
5. Confirm SNC/TLS is active on the connection.
6. Verify: the SM59 connection/authorization test shows current-user propagation, an RFCDES review shows no stored users on trusted destinations, and a SUIM check confirms the technical users' authorizations are minimal.
Caution: switching to current-user propagation can break batch/background jobs that relied on the technical user's broader rights — confirm each caller's own authorizations in the target first, and test in non-production before changing production destinations.

TRUST-005.risk:
The SAProuter route-permission table (saprouttab) contains permit rules (action P or S) with a wildcard target host and/or target port.
SAProuter is the network gatekeeper between the external world (SAP support, partners, sometimes the internet) and the internal SAP network. A rule such as 'P * * *' lets any external client route to any internal host on any port through the router, effectively turning SAProuter into an open proxy into the internal LAN and bypassing firewall segmentation.
Attack scenario: an internet-exposed SAProuter (commonly on TCP 3299) with a wildcard permit lets an external attacker route to internal dispatcher ports (32NN), the message server (36NN), the RFC gateway (33NN), HANA SQL (3NN15), or even RDP/SSH. From there they exploit unpatched internal services — the message-server/gateway RCE chains (10KBLAZE, CISA advisory AA19-122A) being the textbook examples.
Business impact: full internal-network exposure, remote code execution, data breach, and a common ransomware entry point via an internet-facing SAProuter.
A real incident looks like unexpected SAProuter connections to many internal hosts and ports, with router logs showing the wildcard route being used from external sources.
TRUST-005.mitigation:
1. Locate the saprouttab file referenced by the SAProuter start command (-R option) and review every line. Syntax is: action(P/S/D) source-host dest-host dest-port [password].
2. Replace each wildcard permit (dest-host '*' or port '*') with explicit source, destination host and destination port for the one legitimate flow it serves (for example only the SAP support subnet to a specific app server on a specific service).
3. Make the table deny-by-default: end it with 'D * * *' so anything not explicitly permitted is denied, with the narrow permits listed above it.
4. Prefer 'S' (secure/SNC) entries for external SAP support connections and enforce SNC on the router.
5. Reload the router (saprouter -n) to apply the new table, and keep the SAProuter binary patched.
6. Verify with a route/niping test that a non-permitted host/port is now blocked, and review the router trace (dev_rout) for denied attempts.
Caution: tightening routes can break legitimate SAP support and interface access — inventory required flows with the network and interface owners first, stage the change, and keep a rollback copy of the original saprouttab.

TRUST-006.risk:
The message server does not fully separate its internal application-server channel from external client access (rdisp/msserv_internal = 0), or external administration is enabled (ms/monitor is not 0).
The message server brokers communication between application servers and load-balances SAP GUI logons. Without a dedicated, firewalled internal port, an external client that can reach the message-server port can register itself as a rogue application server, impersonate a legitimate one, and intercept or steal logon tickets and business data — the mechanism behind the 10KBLAZE / CVE-2019 message-server attacks (CISA AA19-122A).
When ms/monitor is greater than 0, external programs such as msmon can administer the message server and change or delete its internal memory.
Attack scenario: an attacker who reaches the external message-server port (36NN) registers a fake application server or manipulates the monitor/ACL, harvests SAP logon tickets, and achieves lateral movement or a denial-of-service outage.
Business impact: credential theft, system takeover and outage of a core ERP service, with a well-documented, actively-scanned exploit history.
A real incident looks like an unknown application server appearing in transaction SMMS, message-server internal memory being manipulated, or a spike in captured logon tickets.
TRUST-006.mitigation:
1. In RZ11 review rdisp/msserv, rdisp/msserv_internal, ms/monitor and ms/acl_info.
2. In RZ10 set rdisp/msserv_internal to a dedicated internal port (for example 39NN) and keep the external rdisp/msserv port firewalled away from clients, per the split recommended in SAP Note 1421005.
3. Set ms/monitor = 0 to block external administration, and configure ms/acl_info to point to a restrictive ACL file that lists only your application-server hosts, denying everything else.
4. On the firewall, allow only application servers to reach the internal port; SAP GUI clients should reach only the external logon port.
5. Restart the instances to activate the static parameters (coordinate the window with SAP managed services on RISE).
6. Verify: SMMS shows only legitimate application servers, RZ11 confirms msserv_internal is set and ms/monitor = 0, and an external test host cannot register.
Caution: a wrong internal port or an incomplete ms_acl_info host list will break application-server communication and load balancing — change it in a maintenance window, test in non-production, and make sure the ACL host list is complete.

TRUST-007.risk:
The UCON (Unified Connectivity) RFC allowlist is not active (ucon/rfc/active is not 1).
By default every RFC-enabled function module (RFM) in an ABAP system is externally callable, and a typical S/4HANA system exposes thousands of RFMs. Many are powerful — RFC_READ_TABLE reads arbitrary tables, RFC_ABAP_INSTALL_AND_RUN and similar modules can execute code or OS commands. Without UCON enforcing a Communication Assembly allowlist, an attacker or an over-privileged interface user who holds S_RFC can call ANY RFM, not just the handful the business genuinely needs.
Attack scenario: an attacker with valid RFC credentials (or arriving via trusted RFC) calls RFC_READ_TABLE to dump the USR02 password-hash table for offline cracking, or invokes a code-execution RFM for remote command execution. The enormous default surface makes both exfiltration and exploitation easy and hard to spot.
Business impact: mass data breach through unrestricted table reads, remote code execution, and a large unmonitored attack surface that is a direct SAP Security Baseline deviation.
A real incident looks like RFC calls to sensitive RFMs the business never uses, and bulk table reads of HR/finance/credential tables from an interface account.
TRUST-007.mitigation:
1. Open transaction UCONCOCKPIT and start the UCON RFC scenario in the Logging phase to record which RFMs are actually called from outside.
2. Let Logging run across a full business cycle, including period-end, so no legitimate interface is missed.
3. Move the recorded RFMs into the Evaluation phase, review them, then promote the vetted set into the Final/active allowlist (Communication Assembly) so only needed RFMs stay externally callable.
4. Set ucon/rfc/active = 1 in RZ10 to enforce the allowlist, and restart to activate.
5. Combine this with tight S_RFC authorizations (per user and function group) and keep UCON logging on to catch new requirements.
6. Verify: RZ11 shows ucon/rfc/active = 1, UCONCOCKPIT shows the active allowlist, and blocked calls appear in the UCON log without breaking business functions.
Caution: activating with an incomplete allowlist will break interfaces — gather enough logging first, involve interface owners, roll out non-production to production in stages, and monitor the UCON block log immediately after go-live.

TRUST-008.risk:
Profile parameter gw/prxy_info is empty and gw/acl_mode_proxy is not 1, so no proxy ACL (prxyinfo) is loaded and the built-in default permits any source gateway to proxy RFC traffic to any destination system.
The RFC gateway can proxy (relay) RFC requests between gateways and systems. Without a prxyinfo ACL, the gateway acts as an open RFC relay: an attacker can use it as a pivot to reach and call RFMs on other systems it could not otherwise contact, defeating network segmentation. Combined with weak reg_info/sec_info ACLs this feeds the well-known gateway relay and RCE chains.
Attack scenario: an attacker who can reach the gateway port (33NN) proxies RFC calls through it to internal target systems, or launders malicious registered-server traffic, expanding reach and evading network controls.
The severity is Medium here because modern kernels with gw/acl_mode_proxy = 1 auto-generate restrictive default proxy rules, which mitigates an empty prxyinfo file; the risk is highest on older kernels or where the mode is off.
Business impact: lateral movement, an expanded RFC attack surface, and defense evasion across the landscape.
A real incident looks like gateway logs (SMGW) showing proxied connections to unexpected destination systems.
TRUST-008.mitigation:
1. In SMGW use Goto -> Expert Functions to review the current ACL/proxy status, and check gw/prxy_info, gw/acl_mode_proxy, gw/reg_info and gw/sec_info in RZ11.
2. Create a prxyinfo ACL file with explicit rules (P/D source-host dest-host) that permit only the required proxy flows and deny everything else by default.
3. In RZ10 point gw/prxy_info at that file and set gw/acl_mode_proxy = 1 (per SAP Notes 910918 and 3224889) so restrictive defaults apply even with a minimal file.
4. Secure the companion gateway ACLs at the same time: gw/reg_info, gw/sec_info and gw/acl_mode = 1 — the gateway ACLs work as a set.
5. Reload the ACLs (SMGW can re-read the ACL files) or restart to activate.
6. Verify: SMGW shows the prxyinfo file loaded, a non-permitted proxy route is denied on test, and gateway logging records the blocked attempts.
Caution: proxy ACLs can break legitimate cross-gateway scenarios (certain SLD/PI and registered-server flows) — inventory proxy flows first, test in non-production, and keep the previous configuration for rollback.

BASELINE-001.risk: The profile parameter auth/rfc_authority_check governs whether the ABAP kernel enforces the S_RFC authorization object when a remote-enabled function module is invoked over RFC. With value 0 the AUTHORITY-CHECK against S_RFC (fields RFC_TYPE=FUGR, RFC_NAME=function group, ACTVT=16) is skipped for EVERY RFC call. The direct consequence is that any authenticated dialog, communication or system user, and critically any caller arriving over a trusted-RFC relationship, can execute ANY RFC-enabled function module irrespective of the roles assigned to them. In S/4HANA RISE landscapes this is severe because systems are densely interconnected: Fiori front-end ABAP servers, SAP PI/PO, SLT, BTP connectivity via the Cloud Connector, SAP GUI and Central User Administration all rely on RFC. A single low-privileged account or one compromised trusted-RFC hop can then call powerful modules such as RFC_READ_TABLE (mass extraction of any table including USR02 password hashes, PA0008 payroll, BSEG finance), BAPI_USER_CREATE1 or SUSR_USER_CHANGE_PASSWORD_RFC (create/reset users), or RFC_ABAP_INSTALL_AND_RUN (arbitrary code). Attack path: attacker obtains any valid credential (phished dialog user, service account in a config file), opens a JCo/RFC session, dumps USR02 and financial tables, then grants itself SAP_ALL and escalates to remote code execution. Business impact is total loss of confidentiality and integrity, collapse of Segregation of Duties, fraudulent postings, exfiltration of GDPR-relevant personal data and a clear SOX ITGC failure. A real incident surfaces as anomalous RFC traffic and unexpected table reads that generate no authorization-failure records because the check itself is disabled.
BASELINE-001.mitigation:
1. Confirm the current value with report RSPARAM (SE38/SA38) or transaction RZ11 for parameter auth/rfc_authority_check.
2. Set the parameter to 9 (check enforced for all function modules) in the DEFAULT/instance profile via RZ10; value 6 is the modern kernel default and acceptable, but 9 is strictest (SAP Note 93254).
3. Because moving from 0 activates checks previously bypassed, first run an authorization trace with STAUTHTRACE/ST01 and the Security Audit Log (SM19/RSAU) over a representative period to learn which S_RFC values interface/service users actually need.
4. Maintain least-privilege S_RFC authorizations (RFC_TYPE=FUGR, RFC_NAME=<needed function groups>, ACTVT=16) on the affected technical users through PFCG roles; never grant S_RFC=*.
5. Update SU24 proposals so future roles inherit correct S_RFC defaults.
6. Although the parameter is dynamic, schedule an instance restart so enforcement is consistent across all work processes.
7. Roll out in DEV/QAS first, run a full interface regression (batch jobs, PI/PO, CUA, BTP Cloud Connector RFCs) and watch ST22 for RFC_NO_AUTHORITY dumps.
8. Verify by re-running RSPARAM on every app server (SM51) and confirming no new RFC_NO_AUTHORITY dumps for legitimate interfaces.
Caution: coordinate with all interface owners; missing S_RFC grants break integrations. Never revert to 0 as a workaround.

BASELINE-002.risk: auth/no_check_in_some_cases is the master switch that activates the Profile Generator (PFCG) and the evaluation of the SU24 check indicators. With value N, the check-indicator logic PFCG uses to derive authorization defaults (which authorization objects are proposed for a transaction/service and whether their fields are pre-filled) is not honoured; the system behaves as if the customer check indicators do not exist. As a result, roles built in PFCG do not receive correct authorization proposals, and administrators compensate with broad manual authorizations or SU53 trial-and-error, which drifts steadily toward over-entitlement. In S/4HANA the authorization model is complex (Fiori catalogs, OData services, CDS-based analytics), so a mis-driven Profile Generator undermines the whole role-design methodology and the ongoing SU24 maintenance during Support Packages and upgrades (SU25 steps 2A-2D). This is not a single technical exploit but a systemic weakness: over-broad roles created because defaults were never enforced produce Segregation-of-Duties conflicts, for example a user who can both create a vendor and post a payment. A malicious insider then exploits that standing privilege for fraud, or an attacker who compromises one such account gains far more than intended. Business and compliance impact includes SoD violations, failed access-control audits (SOX 404 ITGC, ISO 27001 A.9), and an inability to demonstrate least privilege; auditors frequently flag this parameter directly as a Security Baseline deviation. A real incident looks like fraud or a data leak enabled by excessive standing authorizations that trace back to poorly generated roles rather than one dramatic breach.
BASELINE-002.mitigation:
1. Check the value with RSPARAM/RZ11 for auth/no_check_in_some_cases.
2. Set it to Y in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10). This is a static parameter, so schedule an instance restart to activate it.
3. Ensure Profile Generator setup is complete: run transaction SU25 step 1 (initial fill of customer tables USOBT_C/USOBX_C from SAP defaults) if never done, and steps 2A-2D after every Support Package/upgrade to reconcile SU24 check indicators.
4. Maintain SU24 check indicators for custom transactions/services so authorization defaults are proposed correctly.
5. Adjust affected roles in PFCG using Expert mode (read old status and merge with new data) to pull in the corrected proposals, then regenerate profiles.
6. Verify with RSPARAM that value Y is active on all servers (SM51), and confirm in PFCG that authorization proposals now appear for transactions.
7. Run SUIM / report RSUSR008_009_NEW to review critical authorizations and SoD after the change.
Caution: test role generation in DEV/QAS; regenerating production profiles must pass through change control and user regression so that needed authorizations are not inadvertently removed.

BASELINE-003.risk: SNC (Secure Network Communications) provides authentication, integrity protection and encryption for SAP GUI (DIAG), RFC, CPIC and internal RFC traffic. The parameters snc/accept_insecure_gui, snc/accept_insecure_rfc, snc/accept_insecure_cpic and snc/accept_insecure_r3int_rfc govern whether the server still accepts UNprotected plaintext connections while SNC is enabled. A value of 1 (accept insecure) or U (accept insecure where individually allowed) makes SNC effectively optional: even with snc/enable=1, a client can simply omit SNC and connect in clear text, silently. This defeats the purpose of SNC because the landscape believes DIAG/RFC traffic is encrypted while an attacker can force a downgrade to plaintext. Attack scenario: an adversary with network access (a foothold on a jump host, a compromised admin workstation, or a man-in-the-middle position inside the RISE/hyperscaler VPC or on the VPN path to the SAP-managed environment) sniffs a GUI or RFC session that connected without SNC. DIAG carries the user's credentials and every screen field; RFC carries the function payload and, for trusted RFC, the propagated logon identity. The attacker harvests credentials and business content and replays them to log on. Because acceptance is silent, one non-SNC-capable or deliberately downgraded client is enough to expose traffic. Business and compliance impact includes interception of logon credentials and PII/financial data in transit and violation of encryption-in-transit obligations (GDPR Art. 32, PCI-DSS, and the SAP Security Baseline requirement to encrypt). A breach manifests as credential theft with no failed logons, followed by lateral movement using sniffed sessions.
BASELINE-003.mitigation:
1. Inventory the offending parameters via RSPARAM: snc/accept_insecure_gui, snc/accept_insecure_rfc, snc/accept_insecure_cpic, snc/accept_insecure_r3int_rfc.
2. Ensure SNC is fully deployed first: CommonCryptoLib installed, server PSE created and trust set up in STRUST, snc/enable=1, snc/identity/as set, and snc/data_protection_min at least 2 (integrity), ideally 3 (privacy/encryption). Transaction SNCWIZARD gives a guided setup.
3. Confirm all SAP GUI clients, SM59 RFC destinations and CPIC/interface partners are SNC-enabled (e.g. Secure Login Client, Kerberos or X.509).
4. Set each snc/accept_insecure_* parameter to 0 in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10) so only SNC-protected connections are accepted; optionally add the stricter snc/only_encrypted_gui=1 and snc/only_encrypted_rfc=1 to enforce encryption (SAP Note 1690662).
5. These are static parameters, so schedule an instance restart.
6. Verify by attempting a non-SNC GUI/RFC logon and confirming it is rejected, testing SM59 connections over SNC, and reviewing dev_w traces for the SNC handshake.
Caution: do this ONLY after every client and interface supports SNC, otherwise legitimate connections break. Roll out per instance in a maintenance window and coordinate PSE distribution with interface owners and the RISE provider.

BASELINE-004.risk: SAP GUI Scripting is a client-automation API. When the server-side switch sapgui/user_scripting is TRUE, any SAP GUI connected to the system can be driven programmatically (VBScript or other languages) to read every field, table and value shown on screen and to replay keystrokes and transactions as the logged-on user. The API runs with the full authorizations of the interactive user and, by default, without leaving a distinct application-log trail, which makes it an ideal data-exfiltration and unattended-automation vector. Attack scenario: a user is phished into running a malicious script, or malware on the endpoint attaches to the running SAP GUI session (the client WarnOnAttach popup can be suppressed), then the script iterates through transactions such as SE16, PA30 (HR), or FB03 (finance) to scrape master and transactional data and write it out, or performs unauthorized postings, all as legitimate authorized user actions. Because the activity looks like normal GUI use, it evades many detections; combined with a privileged user it enables mass data theft or fraud at machine speed. In RISE/S4 landscapes with remote or BYOD access the endpoint is often outside the customer's full control, increasing the attach/inject risk. Business and compliance impact includes bulk exfiltration of GDPR personal data and trade secrets, fraudulent financial transactions, and an integrity/repudiation gap because the scripted actions are indistinguishable from the user's own. A real incident surfaces as anomalous high-volume transaction sequences from a single session, or business data appearing outside SAP with no corresponding download record.
BASELINE-004.mitigation:
1. Check the value with RSPARAM/RZ11 for sapgui/user_scripting.
2. Set sapgui/user_scripting = FALSE in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10) unless a specific, approved automation use case exists. It is dynamic, so you can switch it immediately in RZ11 and persist it in the profile for the next restart.
3. Where scripting is genuinely required (e.g. an RPA or test tool), do not enable it globally: set sapgui/user_scripting_per_user = TRUE so only explicitly authorized users can script (SAP Note 692245), and restrict it to the specific service accounts.
4. Harden the remaining exposure: set sapgui/user_scripting_disable_recording = TRUE where only pre-built scripts run, and consider sapgui/user_scripting_set_readonly = TRUE to block scripted input where only read automation is needed.
5. Do not suppress the client notifications; keep WarnOnAttach/WarnOnConnection enabled so users see attach attempts.
6. Reference SAP Notes 480149 (introduces the parameter) and 692245 (fine-grained server-side options).
7. Verify with RSPARAM that the value is FALSE on all app servers (SM51) and attempt to start a GUI script to confirm it is refused.
Caution: coordinate with any RPA/test-automation owners before disabling globally, and pilot in non-prod so sanctioned automation is not broken.

BASELINE-005.risk: login/password_downwards_compatibility controls whether the ABAP system, in addition to the strong current hash (CODVN H, salted iSSHA-1 stored in USR02-PWDSALTEDHASH), also generates and stores the old, weak downward-compatible hashes (CODVN B, an unsalted MD5 stored in USR02-BCODE, and related values in PASSCODE). Any value greater than 0 keeps producing these legacy hashes for backward compatibility with old systems and tools. The weak hashes are unsalted and truncate/upper-case the password, so they are trivially recovered offline with rainbow tables or GPU cracking in minutes. The strong hash then becomes irrelevant: if BCODE exists, an attacker cracks that instead, so the system is only as strong as its weakest stored hash. Attack scenario: an adversary who can read USR02 (via RFC_READ_TABLE, a database dump, an over-broad S_TABU_DIS grant, or an unprotected backup) extracts the BCODE column, cracks it offline to recover cleartext passwords, then logs on as those users, including administrators, with valid credentials and no exploit. This is precisely the technique in the public 'All your SAP passwords belong to us' research. Business and compliance impact includes mass credential compromise, privilege escalation, undetectable account takeover, and failure of GDPR/PCI/SOX obligations to protect stored credentials. A real breach looks like legitimate logons from stolen-but-valid passwords, often long after the table was exfiltrated, with no failed-logon trail.
BASELINE-005.mitigation:
1. Confirm the value via RSPARAM/RZ11 for login/password_downwards_compatibility (target value 0).
2. First ensure no connected system still requires old hashes: upgrade or retire any older-release systems that perform password-based logon against this system's hashes, and check CUA/central-system links (SAP Note 1023437).
3. Set login/password_downwards_compatibility = 0 in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10); it is dynamic, so set it in RZ11 and persist it in the profile.
4. Purge the already-stored weak hashes by running report CLEANUP_PASSWORD_HASH_VALUES (SE38) to remove obsolete BCODE/PASSCODE values from USR02 and the password-history tables (e.g. USH02/USRPWDHISTORY).
5. Optionally verify hash strength: report RSUSR003 shows the code version, and USR02 confirms CODVN H is in use.
6. Verify: after cleanup, inspect USR02 (SE16) to confirm BCODE/PASSCODE are blank for user records, and re-run RSPARAM to confirm the parameter is 0.
Caution: run CLEANUP_PASSWORD_HASH_VALUES in non-prod first and take a backup; once old hashes are removed, downward-compatible logon from legacy systems fails by design, so coordinate with interface owners beforehand.

BASELINE-006.risk: sapstartsrv (the SAP Start Service) and the SAP Host Agent expose a SOAP/HTTP management interface (SAPControl) offering web methods such as Start, Stop, RestartInstance, ReadLogFile, ABAPReadSyslog, GetProcessList, ABAPGetTraceFile, ReadDeveloperTrace, GetEnvironment and ListLogFiles. service/protectedwebmethods defines which of these require administrative authentication. If it is not SDEFAULT (the recommended set) or ALL, the sensitive methods are reachable unauthenticated on the sapstartsrv ports (5xx13/5xx14). Unauthenticated read methods leak developer traces, syslogs, log files, environment variables and process lists, a rich source of hostnames, user names, file paths and even secrets that supports further attack; unauthenticated control methods allow Stop/Restart, i.e. denial of service against the SAP instance. Attack scenario: an attacker who can reach the instance-agent port (a flat network segment, an exposed management interface, or a pivot inside the RISE VPC) calls GetProcessList/ReadLogFile to map the system and read traces, then calls Stop to take the instance down, or uses leaked information to attack the database and OS layer. These open interfaces have long been a documented SAP attack surface (ERPScan/Onapsis research). Business and compliance impact includes information disclosure, denial of service against production SAP, and a foothold for deeper compromise, and it violates the Security Baseline hardening of the instance agent. A real incident looks like unexplained instance shutdowns or exfiltrated trace/log files with no ABAP logon record.
BASELINE-006.mitigation:
1. Check the value via RSPARAM/RZ11 for service/protectedwebmethods, and also review the SAP Host Agent host_profile.
2. Set service/protectedwebmethods = SDEFAULT in the instance profile (RZ10); SDEFAULT protects the full sensitive method set. You may extend it (SDEFAULT plus specific methods) but do not reduce it to NONE or a short custom list (SAP Notes 1439348 and 927637).
3. Apply the same setting in the SAP Host Agent host_profile so its sapstartsrv is equally protected.
4. Confirm kernel/Host Agent patch levels support SDEFAULT (per SAP Note 1439348) and update if older.
5. Restart sapstartsrv to activate (sapcontrol -nr <nr> -function RestartService, or restart the instance agent); a full ABAP instance restart is not required for the agent alone.
6. As defense in depth, restrict network access to the sapstartsrv/Host-Agent ports via OS firewall/security groups so only admin jump hosts can reach them.
7. Verify: from an unauthenticated client run sapcontrol -function GetProcessList/ReadLogFile against the port and confirm it now requires credentials or is refused; re-check RSPARAM.
Caution: monitoring tools (SAP Host Agent, Landscape Management, Focused Run) call these methods, so provision proper credentials before locking down and test in non-prod.

BASELINE-007.risk: The RFC Gateway brokers registration of external RFC server programs (registered and started programs) and their access to the system. That access is meant to be governed by the secinfo (who may start/use programs) and reginfo (which external programs may register) ACL files. gw/acl_mode sets the DEFAULT behaviour when those ACL files are absent: value 0 is permissive, so with no reginfo/secinfo the gateway lets external programs register and be started, i.e. an open gateway; value 1 applies a restrictive default (only same-host/internal) that closes the gap when ACLs are missing. An open gateway is the classic critical SAP exposure: a remote, unauthenticated attacker who can reach the gateway port (33xx, or 48xx with SNC) can register a malicious RFC server program or invoke started programs, leading to arbitrary OS command execution on the SAP host. This is the 10KBLAZE / 'SAP Gateway to Heaven' class of attack described in CISA Alert AA19-122A (addressed by secinfo/reginfo ACLs rather than a CVE). Attack scenario: the attacker registers a rogue program via the gateway and calls it to run OS commands as the <sid>adm user, achieving full remote code execution, database access and lateral movement, with no SAP credentials required. Business and compliance impact is unauthenticated RCE on production SAP, total loss of confidentiality, integrity and availability, and a ransomware or data-theft entry point, i.e. a headline-grade breach. It manifests as unexpected registered programs in the gateway monitor (SMGW) and OS processes/commands spawned by the gateway or work process.
BASELINE-007.mitigation:
1. Check the value via RSPARAM/RZ11 for gw/acl_mode and set gw/acl_mode = 1 in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10). Note that gw/acl_mode=1 only takes effect while explicit ACL files are absent; the real control is the ACLs themselves (SAP Note 1408081).
2. Create and maintain the ACL files and point the parameters at them: gw/sec_info = <path>/secinfo and gw/reg_info = <path>/reginfo, using the SAP secinfo/reginfo generator (SAP Notes 1408081 / 1425765 and the CISA AA19-122A guidance).
3. Start deny-by-default, then add only the specific registered/started programs your interfaces need (SAP standard entries, PI/PO adapters, external RFC servers) with explicit host lists.
4. Set gw/reg_no_conn_info appropriately (SAP Note 1444282) and enable gateway logging (gw/logging) to monitor registrations.
5. Use transaction SMGW (Goto > Expert Functions > External Security) to display/reload secinfo/reginfo without a restart; the gw/acl_mode change itself needs an instance/gateway restart.
6. Verify in SMGW that unauthorized program registration is rejected (Logged-On Clients / registered programs list) and review the gateway log for denied registrations.
Caution: mis-scoped ACLs break legitimate RFC server programs (PI/PO, external tax/output systems). Build the lists from a monitored baseline (gw/logging) in non-prod and coordinate with all RFC interface owners before enforcing in production.

BASELINE-008.risk: SAP logon/assertion tickets (the MYSAPSSO2 cookie) and ICF session cookies (SAP_SESSIONID_*) are bearer tokens: possession of the cookie equals an authenticated session, so their transport must be hardened. login/ticket_only_by_https=0 lets the SSO ticket travel over plain HTTP where it can be sniffed on the network. icf/set_HTTPonly_flag_on_cookies not equal to 0 means the HttpOnly attribute is not set on all ICF cookies (the default 3 leaves it off), so client-side JavaScript, for example via an XSS flaw in a Fiori/BSP app, can read the cookie via document.cookie. login/ticket_only_to_host=0 means a ticket is accepted by hosts other than the one that issued it, widening where a stolen ticket can be replayed. Attack scenarios: (a) network sniff, where an attacker on the path captures a MYSAPSSO2 ticket sent over HTTP and replays it to impersonate the user across all SSO-trusted systems until expiry; (b) XSS with no HttpOnly, where crafted script steals SAP_SESSIONID and the attacker rides the session; (c) with ticket_only_to_host=0, a ticket lifted from one system is reused against another, amplifying blast radius. In S/4HANA RISE, Fiori/BTP front-ends rely heavily on HTTP session cookies and SSO tickets, so these weaknesses translate directly into account takeover of business users and admins. Business and compliance impact includes session hijacking, impersonation, unauthorized transactions and data access, and audit/GDPR failures for weak session protection. A real incident shows actions under a victim's identity with no password compromise and no failed-logon trail.
BASELINE-008.mitigation:
1. Review the three parameters via RSPARAM/RZ11: login/ticket_only_by_https, icf/set_HTTPonly_flag_on_cookies, login/ticket_only_to_host.
2. Set login/ticket_only_by_https = 1 so SSO tickets are sent only over HTTPS (requires the ICM HTTPS/SSL service configured in STRUST and SMICM).
3. Set icf/set_HTTPonly_flag_on_cookies = 0 (HttpOnly active for ALL ICF cookies, the preferred value; default is 3) so scripts cannot read session cookies (SAP KBA 2068872).
4. Set login/ticket_only_to_host = 1 so a ticket is accepted only by the issuing host.
5. Complement with HTTP security session management: activate it via transaction SICF_SESSIONS, ensure the Secure flag is set on cookies (HTTPS-only) and set a sensible login/ticket_expiration_time.
6. These are dynamic parameters (settable in RZ11) but persist them in the profile (RZ10); some cookie behaviour needs an ICM restart (SMICM > Administration > ICM > Restart).
7. Verify by capturing a Fiori/GUI-for-HTML login and confirming MYSAPSSO2/SAP_SESSIONID are sent only over HTTPS and carry the HttpOnly (and Secure) attributes; re-check RSPARAM values.
Caution: forcing HTTPS-only requires valid server certificates for every hostname/alias used, so validate the SSL setup in non-prod to avoid locking out Fiori users and coordinate certificate management with the RISE provider.

BASELINE-009.risk: icm/security_log configures the Internet Communication Manager's security log, the web-tier forensic record of HTTP(S) access, TLS handshakes, denied requests and attack indicators at the ICM/Web Dispatcher layer.
