If it is empty or set below LEVEL=3 the system lacks the detailed log needed to detect and investigate web attacks (scanning, injection attempts, admin-URL probing) against Fiori/OData/ICF endpoints. is/HTTP/show_detailed_errors=TRUE makes the ICM return verbose error pages (component and stack details, internal paths, server/version data) to remote clients; this information disclosure hands an attacker reconnaissance about the software stack and internal structure and eases targeted exploitation. Attack scenario: an attacker probes the public Fiori/Web Dispatcher endpoint, detailed error pages reveal kernel/version, internal hostnames and code paths, and the attacker fingerprints the system to select known exploits; meanwhile, with the security log off or under-configured, the reconnaissance and exploitation attempts leave little to no web-tier trail, so the SOC cannot detect or reconstruct the attack. In RISE S/4HANA the ICM/Web Dispatcher is the Internet-facing entry point for Fiori and API traffic, so weak logging combined with verbose errors materially raises breach likelihood and lowers detectability. Business and compliance impact includes impaired incident detection, response and forensics, non-compliance with logging requirements (SAP Security Baseline, ISO 27001 A.12.4, PCI-DSS 10), and easier exploitation via leaked internals. A real incident is one discovered late or left un-attributable because the web-tier evidence was never captured.
BASELINE-009.mitigation:
1. Check both settings via RSPARAM/RZ11: icm/security_log and is/HTTP/show_detailed_errors.
2. Configure icm/security_log to LEVEL=3 with rotation in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10), e.g. LOGFILE=$(DIR_LOGGING)$(DIR_SEP)dev_icm_sec-%y-%m-%d%z,LEVEL=3,MAXFILES=7,MAXSIZEKB=50000,SWITCHTF=day,FORMAT=TABLE (the SAP Security Baseline recommended value).
3. Set is/HTTP/show_detailed_errors = FALSE so production returns generic error pages with no internal detail.
4. Apply the same hardening to any standalone SAP Web Dispatcher fronting the system.
5. These are ICM parameters; activate via an ICM restart (SMICM > Administration > ICM > Restart) or an instance restart.
6. Forward and retain the ICM security log centrally (SIEM), size MAXFILES/retention to policy, and review it alongside the Security Audit Log (SM20/RSAU_READ_LOG).
7. Verify: view active parameters in SMICM (Goto > Parameters), confirm the security-log file is being written under the logging directory, and confirm an intentionally malformed URL now returns a generic error page.
Caution: increased logging consumes disk, so size MAXSIZEKB/MAXFILES and monitor the logging filesystem; test error-page behaviour in non-prod so any custom error handling still works.

BASELINE-010.risk: login/password_compliance_to_current_policy determines whether users whose stored password no longer meets the CURRENT password policy are forced to change it at their next logon. With value 0, legacy passwords set under an older, weaker policy remain valid indefinitely. Consequently, after the organization tightens policy (for example raising login/min_password_lng or adding complexity via a security policy), existing users keep their short or weak passwords; the stronger policy only applies to newly set passwords. The policy is therefore not enforced for the existing population, which is often the majority and includes long-lived administrator and service accounts. Attack scenario: an attacker performs online or offline guessing, or cracks harvested hashes, against accounts that still carry pre-tightening weak passwords, which are exactly the accounts the new policy was meant to protect; a 6-character legacy admin password survives a move to a 12-character policy and is brute-forced, yielding a valid privileged logon. The weakness is silent because no user is prompted and the posture looks compliant on paper (policy parameters are set) while the effective posture is not. Business and compliance impact includes continued exposure to credential guessing and cracking, privilege escalation via weak admin/service passwords, and audit findings that the password policy is not actually enforced (SOX ITGC, ISO 27001 A.9.4, GDPR access control). A real incident is the takeover of an account that should have had a strong password but was never required to update it.
BASELINE-010.mitigation:
1. Check the value via RSPARAM/RZ11 for login/password_compliance_to_current_policy (target value 1).
2. Set login/password_compliance_to_current_policy = 1 in the DEFAULT profile (RZ10) so users with non-compliant passwords are forced to change at next logon; it is dynamic (RZ11) but persist it in the profile.
3. Confirm the surrounding policy is actually strong: review login/min_password_lng, login/min_password_digits, login/min_password_specials, login/min_password_letters and login/password_expiration_time, or the equivalent attributes of a security policy maintained in transaction SECPOL and assigned in SU01, aligned to the SAP Security Baseline.
4. Identify affected users in advance with report RSUSR003 (standard users) and password/last-logon data via SUIM / report RSUSR200 so you can plan communications.
5. Give special attention to technical/service and communication users: a forced change can break interfaces, so proactively set them to compliant passwords via SU01/SU10 and update the consuming SM59 destinations and JCo configurations first.
6. Verify: after activation, confirm a test user with a non-compliant password is prompted to change at logon, and re-check RSPARAM shows value 1.
Caution: schedule around a maintenance window and notify users; handle service accounts and their interfaces first so batch jobs and RFC destinations do not fail on a forced password change.

S4AUTHZ-001.risk: SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR and SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR_MDG are broad SAP-delivered business-role TEMPLATES, not production roles. SAP ships them only for the very first configuration of a freshly provisioned S/4HANA (Cloud) tenant; per SAP KBA 3363028 the role is delivered, non-transportable, and intentionally carries an extremely wide job profile bundling nearly every administrative business catalog. Assigned to a named business user it grants near-complete control of the system: user and role administration, configuration/customizing, output and communication management, and access to most business apps and their data. In a RISE / S/4HANA Cloud context this single identity collapses segregation of duties, because one user can both change configuration and post/approve business transactions and can create or elevate other users. Concrete abuse: an attacker who phishes or brute-forces such an account (or an insider misusing it) can create a new privileged user, reset another user's credentials, weaken logging, alter vendor/bank master data and trigger a payment run, then export sensitive HR and finance data, all under one legitimate-looking account. Because the role is so broad, forensic attribution of which specific privilege was abused becomes nearly impossible. Business impact is severe: SOX/internal-control failure (no SoD), GDPR exposure through mass PII access, and material fraud risk such as a fictitious-vendor payment. Auditors treat any productive assignment of an administrator TEMPLATE as a reportable control deficiency, and a real incident typically surfaces as unexplained master-data changes or user creations traced back to this role.
S4AUTHZ-001.mitigation:
1. Inventory all holders: S/4HANA Cloud use the Maintain Business Roles and Identity and Access Management apps plus the Display Authorization Trace; on-prem S/4HANA use SUIM > Roles by User, report RSUSR070/RSUSR002, or table AGR_USERS filtered on SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR*.
2. Also resolve indirect grants via composite/derived roles and any reference users so no hidden assignment is missed.
3. For every holder, confirm business justification with the role/data owner; assume no named production end user legitimately needs the template.
4. Build a purpose-specific admin role by copying the template into the customer namespace (Z*/Y*) via Maintain Business Roles (Cloud) or PFCG (on-prem), keeping only the business catalogs the job truly needs.
5. Apply scoped restrictions (see S4AUTHZ-002) for org values (company code, cost center, etc.) instead of Unrestricted.
6. Transport the customer role via the Export/Import Software Collection app (Cloud) or standard CTS transport (on-prem); never transport the SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR template itself.
7. Assign the new least-privilege role, then remove SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR* from the user.
8. For genuine emergency super-admin need, use a firefighter/emergency-access model (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance or SAP Access Control EAM) with logging and time-boxed checkout.
9. Schedule a recurring SUIM/RSUSR070 check so the template does not reappear on users.
10. Verify: re-run the user-role report and confirm zero productive assignments; the authorization trace should show the template only on the initial-setup user.
11. Rollout caution: validate the replacement role in the Q/test tenant first and coordinate cutover with the affected admins so operations are not interrupted.

S4AUTHZ-002.risk: In S/4HANA Cloud business roles, data access is scoped through RESTRICTIONS on restriction fields, the modern equivalent of organizational authorization fields (company code, cost center, controlling area, sales organization, profit center, plant, purchasing organization) plus a Read/Write/Value-Help access level. Setting a restriction to 'Unrestricted' is the cloud analogue of placing '*' on an org field in classic PFCG: the user can operate across every organizational unit, not just their own. When the Write access level is Unrestricted, the role can create, change, and delete across the entire enterprise. This silently defeats the data-scoping and SoD controls that business owners and auditors assume are in place. Abuse scenario: a clerk whose role should be limited to company code 1000 but is left Unrestricted can post documents, change master data, or approve transactions in every company code, enabling cross-entity fraud (for example booking to a low-scrutiny entity) or unauthorized access to another region's financial and HR data. An attacker who compromises such an account immediately inherits enterprise-wide write reach with no org boundary to contain them. Impact includes SOX segregation-of-duties and completeness/accuracy control failures, GDPR over-exposure of PII beyond the user's remit, and an inability to demonstrate least privilege at audit. A real incident typically appears as anomalous postings or master-data changes in company codes the user has no business reason to touch, discovered only after the money or data has moved.
S4AUTHZ-002.mitigation:
1. Extract current restriction settings: Maintain Business Roles app > select role > Restrictions tab (S/4HANA Cloud); review every field flagged Unrestricted, prioritizing Write and Value Help.
2. For each org restriction (Company Code, Cost Center, Controlling Area, Sales Organization, Profit Center, Plant, Purchasing Organization) replace Unrestricted with explicit values or ranges matching the actual job scope.
3. Set the Write access level to Restricted with specific values; reserve Unrestricted Write only for genuinely cross-organization roles.
4. Where a role must legitimately span all org units, document the exception and route it through formal risk acceptance / role-owner sign-off.
5. On-prem S/4HANA equivalent: in PFCG maintain Organizational Levels and field values instead of leaving '*', tightening objects such as S_TABU_DIS/S_TABU_NAM, F_BKPF_BUK and K_CCA.
6. Regenerate the role/profile and re-derive any derived roles.
7. Run an SoD analysis (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance or Access Control ARA) to confirm scoping did not leave a toxic combination.
8. Verify: use the restriction view (Cloud) or SUIM/RSUSR070 (on-prem) to confirm no sensitive restriction remains Unrestricted, and test with a representative user that access outside scope is now denied.
9. Document the target values as the role design baseline for future recertification.
10. Rollout caution: apply changes in the Q system first and run a business regression test, since over-tightening org values can block legitimate postings; coordinate go-live with process owners and stage via transport.

S4AUTHZ-003.risk: A business role aggregates business catalogs, each granting Fiori apps together with their underlying OData services and authorization defaults. A role that bundles dozens of catalogs (here more than the configured threshold, default 30) is almost certainly over-provisioned: it grants far more app and data access than any single job function requires and it becomes a magnet for segregation-of-duties conflicts, because unrelated duties such as vendor maintenance and payment release end up combined in one role. Broad, catalog-heavy roles make access recertification and SoD analysis impractical, because reviewers cannot reason about what the role actually permits, so risky combinations pass review unnoticed. This is the classic role-bloat anti-pattern. Abuse scenario: a user assigned such a role can quietly reach functions outside their remit; an insider can combine create-and-approve capabilities to commit and conceal fraud, and an attacker compromising the account gains a very wide blast radius. It also cripples incident response, because after a breach it is hard to determine everything the account could touch. Business impact includes failed SoD and least-privilege controls (SOX), scope-creep of PII and financial data access (GDPR), and audit findings for inadequate access governance. Over time the oversized role becomes the path of least resistance that admins keep assigning, compounding the exposure and steadily degrading the whole authorization concept.
S4AUTHZ-003.mitigation:
1. List catalogs per flagged role: Maintain Business Roles app > Assigned Business Catalogs (Cloud) or PFCG menu / Fiori catalog assignment and table AGR_TCODES (on-prem).
2. Map each catalog to a concrete job task; identify catalogs no current holder actually uses via usage data (Cloud Display Authorization Trace, or on-prem ST03N app usage / STUSOBTRACE).
3. Split the mega-role into smaller task-based business roles aligned to distinct job functions and drop catalogs that are not required.
4. Run an SoD analysis (SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance, or Access Control ARA rule set) on the redesigned roles to ensure no toxic combinations remain.
5. Adjust the scanner threshold max_business_catalogs only with documented justification; 30 is a conservative design guardrail, not a hard limit.
6. Assign the new granular roles and then withdraw the oversized role.
7. Update role-design documentation and recertification scope to reflect the split roles.
8. Verify: re-run the per-role catalog count and confirm each role is under the agreed threshold, and confirm the SoD report shows no new conflicts.
9. Establish a periodic re-check so catalog counts do not creep back up.
10. Rollout caution: pilot with a small user group because splitting roles can temporarily remove needed apps; keep a rollback role, perform the redesign in Dev/Q, transport through the landscape, and coordinate with business process owners.

S4AUTHZ-004.risk: ABAP CDS views can carry the annotation @AccessControl.authorizationCheck. The secure setting #CHECK evaluates the view's DCL (Data Control Language) access control so only rows the user is authorized for are returned. The values #NOT_REQUIRED and #NOT_ALLOWED switch the DCL check off entirely, so every caller sees all rows regardless of authorization; that is acceptable only for provably non-sensitive or purely technical views. The real danger arises when such a view is EXPOSED, that is published as an OData service, bound to a Fiori app or service binding, or released as a C1-stable public API, because the platform then enforces no row-level authorization on it. Any authenticated user, any consuming BTP side-by-side app, or any external API client can read the entire data set. Attack scenario: an adversary enumerates OData/service endpoints, finds one backed by a #NOT_REQUIRED CDS view over financial documents, business partners, or employee data, and bulk-extracts the table using $top/$skip paging, with no per-record check to stop them. Because the check is disabled at the data-model layer, front-end restrictions and launchpad tile-hiding provide no protection. Impact is a mass confidential/PII data breach (GDPR), leakage of financial data (SOX and data-integrity concerns), and an audit finding that authorization is not enforced at the correct layer. A real incident looks like large scripted OData reads returning far more data than the caller's role should ever allow.
S4AUTHZ-004.mitigation:
1. Inventory affected views: in ABAP Development Tools (ADT/Eclipse) search CDS sources for @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED and #NOT_ALLOWED, and correlate with exposure (@OData.publish, service bindings in /IWFND, or released APIs).
2. For each exposed sensitive view, change the annotation to #CHECK to evaluate DCL, or to #MANDATORY to force a runtime error if no DCL exists so access can never silently be open.
3. Create or adjust a DCL access-control (define role ...) that enforces the intended restriction, typically via aspect pfcg_auth bound to the correct authorization object and fields.
4. Where a view is genuinely non-sensitive/technical, document the rationale so the exception is auditable rather than relying on obscurity.
5. Activate and transport the CDS view and DCL objects through Dev > Q > Prod.
6. For OData exposure, additionally protect the service with S_SERVICE (see S4AUTHZ-005) as defense in depth.
7. Verify: log on as a restricted test user and confirm the exposed service now returns only authorized rows.
8. Confirm the check is actually firing using the authorization trace (transaction STAUTHTRACE) while the service is called.
9. Re-scan the CDS source base to confirm no exposed view still has the check disabled.
10. Rollout caution: enabling DCL can suddenly filter data for existing consumers, so validate in Q with real consumer roles, review DCL performance on large tables, and coordinate with app/interface owners before production.

S4AUTHZ-005.risk: In S/4HANA, OData V4 services are grouped into service groups and published to a productive system alias via transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN. Access to a Gateway/OData service is protected by the authorization object S_SERVICE (external service access). A service group that is published against a productive alias but has no matching S_SERVICE authorization means the entity sets it exposes can be called by any authenticated user, because the service-start check is effectively open. OData V4 is a common audit blind spot: reviews historically concentrate on V2 (/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE) and on transaction/Fiori-catalog checks, so a published V4 group can slip through unexamined. Attack scenario: an attacker holding any valid dialog or technical account enumerates published V4 groups, calls an unprotected group's entity sets, and reads or (where the underlying CDS/BOPF permits) writes business data without holding any business role for it, a direct authorization bypass and a ready data-exfiltration channel. If this is combined with a CDS view whose authorization is disabled (S4AUTHZ-004), there is effectively no check at any layer. Impact includes disclosure of confidential, financial, or PII data (GDPR, SOX), possible unauthorized data changes, and a control gap where teams wrongly assume that a user without a Fiori tile cannot reach the data. A real incident appears as API traffic to a service group that no assigned role should grant.
S4AUTHZ-005.mitigation:
1. Enumerate published V4 service groups: transaction /IWFND/V4_ADMIN > Publish Service Groups, noting each group's system alias and publication status.
2. For each published group, confirm an S_SERVICE authorization gates it and that this authorization is granted only through the specific business/PFCG roles that genuinely need the service.
3. Unpublish groups that are published but unused (remove them from the productive alias) to shrink the attack surface.
4. Ensure the ICF nodes behind the services (transaction SICF) are active only where required and are protected.
5. Add or adjust S_SERVICE in the relevant roles (PFCG) with the correct service type/name values and regenerate the profiles.
6. Verify: as a least-privileged test user, call the group's metadata and an entity set and confirm HTTP 403 without the authorization and success only with it.
7. Confirm S_SERVICE is actually being checked using transaction STAUTHTRACE or the /IWFND error log.
8. Cross-check the result against SAP Security Baseline and DSAG Gateway-service protection guidance and align.
9. Document the intended service-to-role mapping for recertification.
10. Rollout caution: tightening S_SERVICE can break legitimate Fiori apps and interfaces that consume the group, so identify all consumers first, test in Q, coordinate with interface owners, and stage the change via transport.

S4AUTHZ-006.risk: SAP Cloud Connector (SCC) links BTP/Cloud Foundry apps to on-premise or RISE private-landscape backends. Each Cloud-To-On-Premise system mapping defines a Principal Type controlling how the caller's identity reaches the backend. Setting Principal Type to 'None' on an HTTPS mapping means no end-user identity is forwarded; the BTP app connects to the S/4 backend as a single shared technical/service user (via the destination's basic-auth or a fixed credential). The consequence is that end-user attribution is lost: every action in the backend appears as that one technical user, so backend authorization checks and the security audit log cannot distinguish individual users, and any BTP app user effectively inherits the technical user's (often broad) backend authorizations. This breaks least privilege and accountability across the RISE landscape boundary. Attack scenario: a low-privileged BTP app user invokes a function that, through the shared technical user, reads or posts data far beyond their own entitlement; an attacker who compromises the BTP app or its destination credentials gains the technical user's full backend reach, and the security log shows only the technical account, hampering detection and forensics. Impact includes loss of segregation of duties and traceability, SOX/GDPR accountability failure (no way to attribute who accessed PII or financial data), and a wider blast radius from a single credential. A real incident looks like backend changes attributed to a generic service account with no way to identify the human actor behind them.
S4AUTHZ-006.mitigation:
1. Review each mapping in the Cloud Connector admin UI > Cloud To On-Premise > system mappings, identifying HTTPS backends with Principal Type = None.
2. Switch Principal Type to X.509 Certificate (principal propagation) for user-facing HTTPS mappings so the end user's identity flows to the backend.
3. Establish the trust chain: configure the SCC CA for principal propagation and import that CA into the backend's trust store via transaction STRUST (SSL server / trusted CA).
4. Map the propagated certificate subject (DN) to the real SAP backend user via transaction EXTID_DN (or CERTRULE) so the certificate resolves to an actual user.
5. Configure the BTP destination to use Authentication = PrincipalPropagation instead of a fixed technical user.
6. Remove or tightly restrict any shared technical-user fallback and pare back its backend authorizations.
7. Verify: run the app end-to-end and confirm the backend security audit log (transaction SM20 / report RSAU_READ_LOG) records the real end user, not the technical user.
8. Confirm in the Cloud Connector monitor that principal propagation is in effect for the mapping.
9. Document the propagation configuration and certificate validity/renewal dates.
10. Rollout caution: certificate and trust setup is delicate, so pilot on one backend in a non-prod SCC, coordinate with the Basis team owning STRUST and system clock/CA validity, and retain a rollback because a misconfigured trust chain breaks the connection entirely.

S4AUTHZ-007.risk: On SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, platform roles (Org Manager, Space Manager, Space Developer) govern the runtime environment itself, separate from the application role collections that govern app functionality. Space Developer is especially sensitive: it permits deploying/pushing applications and therefore executing arbitrary code within the space, binding services, reading environment variables that frequently contain service credentials and secrets, and restarting apps. Org Manager can manage spaces, users, and org-level settings. Over-assigning these roles to many human users (here more than the configured threshold, default 5) dramatically widens who can alter the productive runtime. These platform roles are routinely missed in access reviews because reviewers focus on business/application authorizations rather than the platform layer. Attack scenario: a user with Space Developer, or an attacker who compromises one, pushes a malicious app or modifies an existing one to exfiltrate bound-service credentials (HANA Cloud, XSUAA, destinations), pivots into connected systems, or tampers with productive services, a supply-chain-style compromise of the BTP subaccount; Org Manager abuse can add attacker-controlled users or spaces. Impact includes potential remote code execution in the productive environment, secret theft, lateral movement into S/4 backends, and loss of change control when deployments occur outside CI/CD, all carrying SOX segregation-of-duties and audit implications. A real incident looks like an unexpected app deployment or a credential-harvesting app running in a productive space.
S4AUTHZ-007.mitigation:
1. Enumerate assignments per role and space: BTP cockpit > subaccount > Cloud Foundry > Org/Space Members, or CLI cf org-users ORG and cf space-users ORG SPACE.
2. For each Space Developer / Space Manager / Org Manager holder, confirm a current operational justification; treat routine developers in productive spaces as exceptions.
3. Remove excess grants with cf unset-space-role USER ORG SPACE SpaceDeveloper (and cf unset-org-role ... OrgManager) or via the cockpit.
4. Replace human deployment access with dedicated CI/CD technical users (service accounts / mTLS) so people do not need standing Space Developer in production.
5. Reserve Org Manager for a small named platform-operations team.
6. Separate productive from non-productive spaces so broad developer access lives only in Dev/Test.
7. Adjust the scanner threshold max_cf_privileged_users only with documented approval.
8. Verify: re-run cf space-users / cf org-users and confirm counts are at or below the agreed limit.
9. Confirm CI/CD pipelines still deploy successfully using the technical user before human roles are withdrawn.
10. Rollout caution: coordinate with DevOps so removing Space Developer does not block pipelines, cut over the CI/CD technical user first, then withdraw human roles, and validate the change in a non-prod subaccount before touching production.

S4AUTHZ-008.risk: On SAP BTP, application authorizations are packaged as role collections, and trust configuration can map a role collection to an IdP group. Mapping a role collection to the 'Default' group (or a wildcard / all-authenticated value) turns it into a BIRTHRIGHT assignment: every user who successfully authenticates through the corporate IdP receives that collection automatically, with no explicit provisioning. If the collection carries only genuinely universal, low-privilege access such as a basic launchpad viewer, this is acceptable; if it carries anything beyond baseline, the entire authenticated population is silently over-provisioned. Birthright grants are easy to overlook during recertification because they are not attached to individual users, so the access is invisible in per-user reviews yet fully effective at runtime. Attack scenario: an attacker who obtains any valid federated identity (a compromised SSO account, or an over-broad IdP that lets many people authenticate) immediately inherits whatever the default-mapped collection grants, potentially reaching applications, APIs, or data that should require explicit entitlement, and without any access request to trip an alert. The insider risk is parallel: any employee can reach the function without ever being formally granted it. Impact includes violation of least privilege and need-to-know, GDPR over-exposure if the collection reaches PII, and an audit finding that access is not individually authorized or recertifiable. A real incident looks like users exercising an app they were never explicitly provisioned for, traced back to a default-group mapping.
S4AUTHZ-008.mitigation:
1. List role-collection-to-group mappings: BTP cockpit > Security > Trust Configuration > select IdP > Role Collection Mappings, or CLI btp list security/role-collection; flag any mapped to Default or a wildcard.
2. For each default-mapped collection, review the roles it contains and decide whether that access is truly universal and low-privilege.
3. If it is not universal, remove the Default-group mapping and instead map the collection to the specific IdP group(s) representing the entitled population.
4. Coordinate with the IdP/IAM team so the required groups exist and are populated (via SCIM or SAML assertion attributes) and legitimate users retain access.
5. Keep only a minimal, deliberately chosen baseline collection as a default assignment, and document it as an accepted birthright.
6. Prefer provisioning through SAP Cloud Identity Services / IAS groups or an IGA tool so assignments are attributable and recertifiable.
7. Verify: authenticate as a test user who is NOT in the target group and confirm the collection is no longer granted.
8. Confirm entitled users still receive the collection through their assigned group.
9. Add the default-mapping review to the periodic BTP access recertification.
10. Rollout caution: removing a default mapping can instantly revoke access for many users, so communicate the change, stage it in a non-prod subaccount, and ensure the replacement group mappings are live before removing the Default mapping to avoid an outage.

ARA-P2P-01.risk: This finding means one user account can both maintain the vendor master (FK01/FK02/XK01/XK02/MK01/MK02/BP with F_LFA1_APP and F_LFA1_BUK at ACTVT 01/02) and execute outgoing payments (F110 automatic payment run, or manual F-53/F-58/F-48 with F_REGU_BUK / F_BKPF_BUK at execute level). This is the canonical accounts-payable fraud path: a single person controls both the payee and the disbursement, so no independent party validates that a vendor is legitimate or that a payment is warranted. Attack scenario: a malicious or compromised AP clerk creates a ghost vendor (a shell they control, or a near-duplicate of a real supplier), sets bank details they own, lets or posts an invoice, then runs F110 or F-53 to disburse funds to that account, and afterwards flags the vendor for deletion to erase the trail. A variant redirects a genuine vendor's payment by editing the F110 payment proposal. The business impact is direct, often recurring cash loss kept below review thresholds, material misstatement of accounts payable, and a textbook SOX ITGC/entity-level control failure that auditors will raise as a significant deficiency or material weakness. In S/4HANA RISE the exposure is identical whether access is via SAP GUI, the Manage Supplier Master Data / Manage Automatic Payments Fiori apps, or an RFC/OData technical user; RISE does not shift SoD responsibility, which stays firmly on the customer side of the shared-responsibility model. A real incident usually surfaces only when a bank reconciliation exception or a whistle-blower flags an unknown payee, after several payment cycles have already run.
ARA-P2P-01.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 (Users by Complex Selection Criteria) for tcodes F110/F-53 AND FK01/XK01, or RSUSR008_009_NEW for the critical combination; in GRC Access Control run Access Risk Analysis for the standard Maintain Vendor & Process Payment risk.
2. For each user open SU01 (Roles tab) and PFCG; using table AGR_1251 determine which role grants the vendor-master side (F_LFA1_APP/F_LFA1_BUK) and which grants the payment side (F_REGU_BUK/F_BKPF_BUK).
3. Assign one side per job: keep vendor maintenance with the AP master-data team and F110 with a separate Treasury/disbursement team. In PFCG remove the conflicting transactions from the menu, strip the auth objects, then regenerate the profile (Generate / SUPC).
4. Where F110 must stay broad, restrict F_REGU_BUK by paying company code and split the proposal-edit vs payment-run steps across two users.
5. If separation is impossible, record a formal mitigating control (independent review of the F110 proposal/exception list, positive-pay bank file, periodic review of vendor bank changes via change documents on table LFBK) in mitigating_controls.csv or GRC with an owner and validity date.
6. Verify: re-run RSUSR002 / ARA and confirm zero residual users; confirm change-document logging is active.
7. Rollout caution: test in QA, coordinate with AP and Treasury interface owners (payment-run batch jobs, DME/host-to-host files), and regenerate profiles outside a payment-run window.

ARA-P2P-02.risk: This is the highest-value refinement of the AP fraud path: the same user can change vendor bank details (FK02/XK02/BP editing sensitive fields protected by F_LFA1_AEN at ACTVT 02) and run the automatic payment program F110 (F_REGU_BUK). Bank data (IBAN, bank key, account number) is the single field that decides who receives money, so combining its maintenance with payment execution lets one person point a real vendor's payment at an attacker-controlled account and immediately settle it in the same run, with no second pair of eyes. Attack scenario: the perpetrator waits for a legitimate high-value invoice to become due, silently changes the vendor's bank account to their own just before the payment run, executes F110, then reverts the bank data afterward so a later manual reconciliation shows the correct account. Because the vendor, invoice and approval are all genuine, three-way match and invoice-approval controls do not fire; only bank-change monitoring or dual control on sensitive fields would catch it. Business impact is large single-shot losses (whole invoices diverted, not padded), plus GDPR/data-integrity exposure since bank data is personal/financial data. This is one of the most common real-world SAP payment-diversion frauds and a frequent business-email-compromise landing point where an attacker who phishes an AP user then abuses this exact combination. In RISE/S4HANA the sensitive-field dual-control configuration is a customer responsibility and is often left unconfigured after migration, making this finding especially likely post go-live.
ARA-P2P-02.mitigation:
1. Confirm scope: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for users holding F110 and FK02/XK02 with F_LFA1_AEN ACTVT 02; cross-check GRC ARA for the Maintain Vendor Bank vs Run Payment risk.
2. Enforce dual control on sensitive fields (the primary compensating control): in SPRO -> Financial Accounting -> Accounts Payable -> Vendor Accounts -> Master Data -> Preparations for Creating Vendor Master Data -> Define Sensitive Fields for Dual Control (Vendors), flag the bank fields (LFBK-BANKN, BANKL, IBAN) in table T055F.
3. Assign the confirmation step to a different user: changes then block the vendor for payment until confirmed via FK08 (single) / FK09 (list). Ensure the confirmer is not the changer.
4. In PFCG remove F110 or the sensitive-field maintenance from the offending roles so no single user holds both; regenerate profiles.
5. Add change-document monitoring: schedule report RFKABL00 (vendor master change documents) or the S/4 equivalent for bank-field changes and review before each payment run.
6. Where residual access remains, register a GRC/CSV mitigating control (four-eyes on bank changes + payment-proposal review) with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: make a test bank change in QA and confirm it blocks for payment until independently confirmed; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: coordinate with vendor-master and Treasury teams; test dual-control config in QA as it changes the master-data process flow.

ARA-P2P-03.risk: A single user can both create/change a purchase order (ME21N/ME22N with M_BEST_BSA/M_BEST_EKO/M_BEST_EKG at ACTVT 01/02) and release/approve it (ME28/ME29N holding a release code in M_EINK_FRG). The PO release strategy is the core purchasing approval control that keeps a buyer from committing company funds unilaterally; if the same person raises and releases the PO, that control is defeated and spend is self-authorized. Attack scenario: a buyer creates a PO to a favored or colluding vendor (or to a shell they control), then releases it themselves, skipping the manager/second-buyer approval the release strategy is meant to enforce; downstream goods receipt and invoice verification then treat the PO as fully approved and the payment flows. This enables over-priced buying, kickbacks, splitting POs to stay under value bands, and pure fictitious procurement. Business impact is uncontrolled/unauthorized commitment of funds, inflated procurement cost, and a purchasing-cycle control gap auditors flag under SOX and procurement-fraud reviews. In S/4HANA the same conflict applies to the Fiori Manage Purchase Orders and My Inbox (flexible workflow) approval apps. Note that M_EINK_FRG has no ACTVT field, so merely holding any release code plus PO-create authority is sufficient capability. A real incident often looks like a cluster of just-under-threshold POs to a single new vendor, all created and released by the same user ID over a short window.
ARA-P2P-03.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for tcodes ME21N/ME22N together with ME28/ME29N, and for users holding M_EINK_FRG release codes; confirm in GRC ARA (PO Create vs PO Release).
2. Review the release strategy configuration: SPRO -> Materials Management -> Purchasing -> Purchase Order -> Release Procedure for Purchase Orders (release groups/codes/strategies in tables T16FG/T16FS/T16FC) to confirm approval levels are meaningful and value bands are correct.
3. In PFCG, remove the M_EINK_FRG release code(s) from roles used by buyers who create POs, and remove ME28/ME29N from those menus; keep release authority in a separate approver role. Regenerate profiles.
4. If flexible workflow is used, configure the approval step (transaction SWDD / BRF+ agent determination) to exclude the PO creator as a possible agent.
5. Where a small team must retain both, register a mitigating control (independent review of released POs, monitoring of PO-changes-after-release via change docs table CDHDR/CDPOS on EKKO) in GRC/CSV with owner and validity.
6. Verify: attempt to release an own PO in QA and confirm it is denied or routed elsewhere; re-run ARA for zero residual.
7. Caution: coordinate with procurement process owners; changes to release codes affect existing workflow items, so validate in QA and clear open release-pending POs first.

ARA-P2P-04.risk: A single user can both create a purchase order (ME21N/ME21/ME25 with M_BEST_BSA/M_BEST_WRK at ACTVT 01) and post the goods receipt against it (MIGO/MB01 with M_MSEG_BWA/M_MSEG_WMB at ACTVT 01, typically movement type 101). The three-way match (PO vs goods receipt vs invoice) is the control that ensures the company only pays for what it actually ordered and received; if the same person raises the PO and confirms receipt, two of the three legs are controlled by one individual and the match auto-approves without any real, independent confirmation that goods arrived. Attack scenario: a buyer creates a PO to a colluding vendor, posts a goods receipt for goods never delivered (or over-quantity), and the subsequent invoice verification finds a matching PO and GR, so the fraudulent invoice is released for payment automatically. Variants include booking receipts to inflate inventory, covering shrinkage, or triggering early vendor payment. Business impact is payment for phantom deliveries, inventory misstatement, and a broken procurement control that both internal and external auditors treat as a serious SoD deficiency. In S/4HANA the same risk spans the Post Goods Receipt Fiori apps and Warehouse-managed receipts. A real incident typically appears as GR postings by the same user who created the PO, for a vendor with no independent delivery evidence, followed shortly by invoice payment.
ARA-P2P-04.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for ME21N/ME25 together with MIGO/MB01, restricted to M_MSEG_BWA ACTVT 01; confirm in GRC ARA (PO Create vs Goods Receipt).
2. Assign PO creation to Purchasing roles and goods receipt to Warehouse/Inventory roles; in PFCG remove MIGO/MB01 and M_MSEG_BWA/M_MSEG_WMB (movement type 101) from buyer roles and remove ME21N/ME25 from warehouse roles. Regenerate profiles.
3. Where plants are small and staff few, restrict M_MSEG_BWA by movement type and M_MSEG_WMB by plant/storage location so a user cannot post receipts for the plants they buy for.
4. Strengthen the compensating detective control: run a periodic report/CDS query matching PO creator vs GR poster (change docs on EKKO/MSEG) and review exceptions.
5. Where separation is not feasible, register a mitigating control (independent physical-receipt sign-off, cycle counts, GR/IR clearing account review via MR11) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
6. Verify: in QA attempt a GR against a self-created PO with the restricted role and confirm denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
7. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with warehouse and MM process owners, and validate that movement-type restrictions do not block legitimate receipts (e.g., 101/103/105) before transport to production.

ARA-P2P-05.risk: A single user can both create/change a purchase order (ME21N/ME22N/ME21 with M_BEST_BSA/M_BEST_EKO at ACTVT 01/02) and post the logistics invoice (MIRO/MIR7/MIRA/MIR4 with M_RECH_BUK and F_BKPF_BUK at ACTVT 01). This person originates both the commitment (the PO) and the payable (the invoice), so they can manipulate quantity, price or tolerance on the PO and then post a matching invoice that overpays a colluding or fictitious vendor. It breaks the three-way match from the invoice side: because the invoice references the buyer's own PO, the match succeeds and the block/tolerance controls (OMR6 tolerance keys) do not fire. Attack scenario: the perpetrator raises a PO at an inflated price or with an extra line to a colluding vendor, posts the MIRO invoice themselves, and the payment run later settles it; or they post duplicate/near-duplicate invoices exploiting the fact that the same person controls the reference PO. Business impact is direct overpayment/leakage, inflated procurement spend, duplicate-payment loss, and a SOX-relevant SoD failure in the purchase-to-pay cycle. In S/4HANA the equivalent Fiori Create Supplier Invoice apps carry the identical exposure, and RFC/EDI invoice-posting service users combined with PO-maintenance access reproduce it programmatically. A real incident often shows as invoices posted by the same ID that created the PO, with prices or tolerances that quietly exceed the norm for that material/vendor.
ARA-P2P-05.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for ME21N/ME22N together with MIRO/MIR7 and M_RECH_BUK ACTVT 01; confirm in GRC ARA (PO Create vs Invoice Processing).
2. Split the cycle: keep PO maintenance with Purchasing and invoice verification with Accounts Payable. In PFCG remove MIRO/MIR7/MIRA and M_RECH_BUK from buyer roles (and ME21N from AP roles); regenerate profiles.
3. Tighten tolerances so manipulation is caught: SPRO -> Materials Management -> Logistics Invoice Verification -> Invoice Block -> Set Tolerance Limits (transaction OMR6) and enable stochastic/random blocking (OMRX) so invoices route to independent release (MRBR).
4. Ensure duplicate-invoice check is active (vendor master flag Check Double Invoice; SPRO LIV settings) to blunt duplicate posting.
5. Where a small team retains both, register a mitigating control (independent MRBR release of blocked invoices, periodic price-variance and PO-vs-invoice-creator review) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
6. Verify: in QA post a MIRO against a self-created PO with the split roles and confirm the auth denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
7. Caution: test tolerance and blocking changes in QA (they affect straight-through processing volumes), and coordinate with AP before removing invoice-posting access from mixed roles.

ARA-P2P-06.risk: A single user can both maintain the vendor master (FK01/FK02/XK01/XK02/BP with F_LFA1_APP/F_LFA1_BUK at ACTVT 01/02) and post non-PO (FI) accounts-payable invoices (FB60/FB65/F-43 with F_BKPF_BUK ACTVT 01 and F_BKPF_KOA KOART=K for vendor line items). This covers the direct-FI invoice path that PO-based (MIRO) risks do not: FB60/F-43 postings need no purchase order, no goods receipt and no three-way match, so a user who also controls the vendor master can create a fictitious vendor and book a payable straight to it, which a later payment run settles. Attack scenario: the perpetrator creates a shell vendor with their own bank details, posts an FB60 expense/service invoice against it (services and one-off costs are hard to challenge on receipt), and the open item flows into the next F110 run; because there is no PO or GR to reconcile against, only invoice-approval or vendor-review controls could catch it. Business impact is direct cash loss through fabricated non-PO spend, expense misstatement, and a serious SoD and SOX control gap precisely in the area with the weakest inherent controls (non-PO invoices). In S/4HANA the Create Supplier Invoice (Advanced) / Post General Journal Entries Fiori apps for FI vendor invoices carry the same risk, and it is often overlooked because attention concentrates on the PO/MIRO flow. A real incident looks like FI vendor invoices to a recently created vendor, posted by the same user who created that vendor, with no procurement documents behind them.
ARA-P2P-06.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for FK01/XK01 together with FB60/FB65/F-43 and F_BKPF_KOA KOART=K; confirm in GRC ARA (Maintain Vendor vs Post AP Invoice).
2. Separate duties: vendor master maintenance stays with the master-data team; FI invoice posting stays with AP. In PFCG remove FB60/FB65/F-43 (or F_LFA1_APP/F_LFA1_BUK) from the conflicting roles and regenerate profiles.
3. Route non-PO invoices through document parking + release (FV60 park, FBV0 post) so an independent approver posts what a clerk parks; configure workflow if used.
4. Add detective monitoring: periodic report of FI vendor invoices where the posting user is also the vendor's creator (change docs on LFA1 vs BKPF/BSIK), and review new-vendor + first-invoice pairs.
5. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (four-eyes on non-PO invoices, new-vendor review, payment-proposal review) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
6. Verify: in QA attempt to create a vendor and post an FB60 to it under the split roles and confirm the denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
7. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with AP and master-data owners, and confirm parking/release does not stall time-critical postings before production cutover.

ARA-O2C-01.risk: A single user can both maintain the customer master (FD01/FD02/XD01/XD02/VD01/VD02/BP with F_KNA1_BUK/F_KNA1_APP/B_BUPA_RLT at ACTVT 01/02) and create/change sales orders (VA01/VA02 with V_VBAK_AAT/V_VBAK_VKO at ACTVT 01/02). Controlling both the counterparty and the order means a user can set up a fictitious or altered customer and immediately book sales, deliveries or credit terms to it, with no independent check that the customer is real or creditworthy. Attack scenario: the perpetrator creates a bogus customer (or edits an existing one's ship-to/terms) and raises sales orders that drive deliveries of goods to an address they control, or fabricates revenue/round-tripping to hit targets; because the same person owns the master data, the usual counterparty due-diligence is bypassed. Business impact includes diverted/stolen goods, fraudulent or premature revenue recognition (a financial-reporting/GAAP issue), and channel-stuffing that misstates sales. In S/4HANA the Manage Business Partner Master Data and Manage Sales Orders Fiori apps reproduce this, and RISE go-lives frequently over-provision sales staff with BP maintenance during data cleanup, leaving the conflict live. A real incident looks like new customers created and same-day large sales orders booked by the same user ID, often to unusual ship-to addresses or with atypical terms, followed by deliveries that are never paid.
ARA-O2C-01.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for FD01/XD01/VD01 together with VA01/VA02, checking F_KNA1_* and B_BUPA_RLT; confirm in GRC ARA (Maintain Customer vs Create Sales Order).
2. Separate duties: customer/BP master maintenance belongs to a master-data team, order entry to Sales. In PFCG remove BP/XD01/FD01 and F_KNA1_APP/B_BUPA_RLT from sales-order roles (or VA01 from master-data roles); regenerate profiles.
3. Constrain BP maintenance with the BP role-type/authorization concept (B_BUPA_RLT by BP role, B_BUPA_GRP by grouping) so order-entry staff cannot create payer/ship-to customer roles.
4. Add detective monitoring: periodic review of customers created and first sales order booked by the same user (change docs on KNA1/BUT000 vs VBAK), focusing on new ship-to addresses and credit terms.
5. Where a small team retains both, register a mitigating control (independent new-customer approval, delivery-block on new accounts, credit review) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
6. Verify: in QA attempt to create a customer and immediately raise a VA01 order under split roles and confirm the denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
7. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with SD and master-data owners, and validate BP role-type restrictions do not block legitimate order-to-cash processing before transport.

ARA-O2C-02.risk: A single user can both maintain customer credit limits/risk data (FD32 classic credit management or UKM_BP in FSCM, with F_KNKA_KKB/F_KNKA_MAN at ACTVT 02) and release credit-blocked sales documents (VKM1/VKM3/VKM4 with V_KNKK_FRE at ACTVT 02). Credit management exists to stop the company shipping to customers who exceed their exposure; if the same person can both set the limit and release the block, that control is nullified from both directions. Attack scenario: a sales user facing a credit-blocked order either raises the customer's credit limit/changes the risk category so the block never triggers, or simply releases the blocked document themselves, pushing an order through to delivery and billing for a customer who should not receive further goods on credit. This is used to hit sales targets, favor a colluding customer, or conceal a deteriorating receivable. Business impact is uncontrolled credit exposure, bad-debt losses, understated allowance for doubtful accounts, and a credit-control SoD failure auditors flag in the order-to-cash cycle. In S/4HANA FSCM (Credit Management) the equivalent apps (Manage Credit Accounts, Release Blocked Sales/Documents) carry the same conflict. A real incident looks like credit limits raised or blocks released on the same customers by the same user just before large shipments, with those receivables later going overdue.
ARA-O2C-02.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for FD32/UKM_BP together with VKM1/VKM3/VKM4, checking F_KNKA_KKB/F_KNKA_MAN and V_KNKK_FRE at ACTVT 02; confirm in GRC ARA (Maintain Credit Limit vs Release Credit Block).
2. Separate duties: credit-limit maintenance belongs to a Credit/Finance function; block release to a separate credit controller, and neither to order-entry sales staff. In PFCG remove FD32/UKM_BP (or the release tcodes/V_KNKK_FRE) from the conflicting roles and regenerate.
3. Restrict by credit control area (F_KNKA_KKB) and credit segment (FSCM) so users only act within their authorized scope.
4. Configure credit-management so limit increases require an independent approval step (FSCM case/workflow) rather than direct maintenance.
5. Add detective monitoring: report of credit-limit changes and block releases by user (change docs on KNKK / UKMBP tables) reviewed by an independent credit manager.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent review of all limit changes and released blocks) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA raise a credit-blocked order, then attempt to both raise the limit and release it under the split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA and coordinate with the credit/collections team, since credit-block changes directly affect shipping and customer service.

ARA-O2C-03.risk: A single user can both post/clear incoming customer payments (F-28/FB05/F-32, and reset cleared items with FBRA, using F_BKPF_BUK at ACTVT 01/02 and F_BKPF_KOA KOART=D for customer line items) and maintain the customer master (FD01/FD02/XD01/XD02/BP with F_KNA1_BUK/B_BUPA_RLT). This is the classic cash-misappropriation and lapping conflict: whoever applies cash receipts and also controls the customer account can steal a payment and then conceal the shortfall by manipulating that account. Attack scenario: the perpetrator diverts an incoming customer payment (skimming/lapping), then hides the resulting open item by altering the customer master, applying a later customer's payment to the earlier account (lapping), resetting cleared items with FBRA to re-open and re-manipulate, or writing the balance off; because they control both the cash application and the account, the receivable ledger can be kept looking clean. Business impact is direct theft of customer remittances, misstated accounts receivable and cash, and a high-severity SoD/anti-fraud failure that auditors treat as a material control weakness (cash is the highest-risk asset). In S/4HANA the Manage Incoming Payments / Clear Incoming Payments and BP maintenance apps reproduce it. A real incident is a slow lapping scheme surfaced by a customer complaining their paid invoice is still open, with a trail of FBRA resets and master-data edits by the same user who applied the cash.
ARA-O2C-03.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for F-28/FB05/F-32/FBRA together with FD01/XD01/BP, checking F_BKPF_KOA KOART=D and F_KNA1_BUK; confirm in GRC ARA (Process Customer Payments vs Maintain Customer).
2. Separate duties: cash application belongs to AR/cash-desk, customer master to a master-data team; in PFCG remove BP/FD02 (or the payment/clearing tcodes) from the conflicting roles and regenerate.
3. Tightly restrict FBRA (reset cleared items) — it is a high-risk transaction — to a very small, monitored group and never combine it with master-data maintenance.
4. Enable and monitor change documents on customer master and on reset/clearing activity; review write-offs and FBRA resets independently.
5. Automate cash application (electronic bank statement / lockbox auto-matching, FEBA/FF67) to reduce manual clearing and the opportunity for lapping.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent AR sub-ledger reconciliation, review of manual clearings, dunning-run oversight) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA attempt to clear a receipt, reset it with FBRA and edit the customer under split roles, confirming denials; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with AR/collections, and ensure removing FBRA does not impede legitimate error-correction workflows (route those to a controlled key user).

ARA-O2C-04.risk: A single user can both maintain pricing/condition records (VK11/VK12/VK31/VK32 with V_KOND_VEA/V_KONH_VKS at ACTVT 01/02) and create/change sales orders (VA01/VA02 with V_VBAK_AAT/V_VBAK_VKO). Condition records determine the price, discount and rebate a customer receives; combining their maintenance with order entry lets one person grant an unauthorized price or discount and then consume it on their own orders, with no independent pricing control. Attack scenario: a sales user creates or edits a discount/price condition favoring a specific (possibly colluding) customer, then raises sales orders that apply it, enabling kickbacks, deliberate under-billing of favored accounts, or margin erosion the pricing team never approved; because both actions are theirs, the price appears legitimate on the order. Variants include manipulating rebate agreements to inflate payouts. Business impact is lost margin, revenue leakage, kickback fraud, and inaccurate/uncontrolled pricing that distorts profitability reporting; it is a recognized order-to-cash SoD conflict. In S/4HANA the Manage Prices / Set Sales-Specific Pricing and Manage Sales Orders Fiori apps carry the same exposure, and pricing-condition maintenance is frequently bundled into sales roles for convenience. A real incident looks like new or edited discount conditions immediately consumed on orders raised by the same user, showing as unusually low realized prices or high discounts for particular customers.
ARA-O2C-04.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for VK11/VK12/VK31 together with VA01/VA02, checking V_KOND_VEA/V_KONH_VKS at ACTVT 01/02; confirm in GRC ARA (Maintain Pricing vs Create Sales Order).
2. Separate duties: pricing/condition maintenance belongs to a Pricing/Master-data function, order entry to Sales; in PFCG remove VK11/VK12 and V_KOND_VEA from order-entry roles (or VA01 from pricing roles) and regenerate.
3. Restrict condition maintenance by condition type/application and sales area (V_KONH_VKS) so users cannot maintain the condition types most open to abuse (manual discounts, rebates).
4. Where manual pricing on orders is needed, control it through pricing-procedure configuration (manual condition limits, upper/lower bounds) rather than open condition maintenance.
5. Add detective monitoring: report of condition-record changes vs orders using them by the same user (change docs on condition tables Axxx/KONH), and margin/discount-exception review.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent price/discount approval, periodic realized-margin review) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA attempt to create a discount condition and consume it on an order under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA and coordinate with the pricing team, since condition-type restrictions can affect legitimate quoting and promotions.

ARA-O2C-05.risk: A single user can both post billing documents (VF01/VF02/VF04 with V_VBRK_VKO/V_VBRK_FKA at ACTVT 01/02) and maintain the customer master (FD01/FD02/XD01/XD02/VD01/VD02/BP with F_KNA1_BUK/B_BUPA_RLT). Combining who invoices with who controls the customer account lets one person issue invoices and credit memos to a customer whose terms, bank data, address or very existence they control. Attack scenario: the perpetrator sets up or alters a customer, then posts fabricated invoices or, more commonly, unwarranted credit memos to it — issuing credits that reduce a colluding customer's balance, or invoicing/crediting a shell account they created — routing value to a party they control while the master data masks it. Because both the document and the counterparty are under one user, the usual independent-billing check is absent. Business impact is revenue and receivables misstatement, credit-memo fraud (a very common revenue-side scheme), and diverted value, all a recognized order-to-cash SoD failure with SOX/financial-reporting implications. In S/4HANA the Create Billing Documents and BP maintenance apps reproduce it. A real incident looks like credit memos or invoices to customers recently created or edited by the same user who billed them, often clustered near period-end or tied to specific accounts whose balances are being managed down.
ARA-O2C-05.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for VF01/VF02/VF04 together with FD01/XD01/BP, checking V_VBRK_VKO/V_VBRK_FKA and F_KNA1_BUK; confirm in GRC ARA (Post Billing vs Maintain Customer).
2. Separate duties: billing belongs to an SD billing/AR function, customer master to a master-data team; in PFCG remove BP/XD02/FD02 (or the billing tcodes) from the conflicting roles and regenerate.
3. Tightly control credit-memo creation (billing type, order reason) and require an independent approval/release for credit memos above a threshold via billing-block/release configuration.
4. Restrict billing by sales organization / billing type (V_VBRK_VKO/V_VBRK_FKA) so users only bill within their authorized scope.
5. Add detective monitoring: report of credit memos and invoices to customers created/changed by the same user (change docs on KNA1/BUT000 vs VBRK), reviewed independently, especially at period-end.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent credit-memo approval, AR reconciliation, revenue analytics) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA attempt to edit a customer and post a credit memo to it under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA and coordinate with billing/AR, since billing-block and credit-memo approval changes affect period-end billing throughput.

ARA-O2C-06.risk: A single user can both create/change sales orders (VA01/VA02 with V_VBAK_AAT/V_VBAK_VKO) and release credit-blocked SD documents (VKM1/VKM3/VKM4 with V_KNKK_FRE at ACTVT 02). This is the self-approval variant of the credit control: a sales user whose own order trips the credit check can release that block themselves, so any order the credit-management engine blocks can be pushed straight back into the flow by the person who raised it, nullifying the safeguard through delivery and billing. Attack scenario: the salesperson books an order for a customer who is over their credit limit or on the risk list; the system credit-blocks it; instead of routing it to an independent credit controller, the same user runs VKM3 and releases their own order, letting the goods ship and the invoice post to a customer who should not receive further credit. This is used to hit quota, accommodate a struggling or colluding customer, or avoid the friction of the credit desk. Business impact is uncontrolled credit exposure, avoidable bad-debt, and a defeated order-to-cash credit control that auditors flag; unlike O2C-02 it does not even require limit-maintenance rights, only the ability to release. In S/4HANA FSCM the Release Blocked Sales Documents / Manage Credit apps carry the same exposure. A real incident looks like the same user ID appearing as both the order creator and the block releaser on credit-blocked orders that later become overdue receivables.
ARA-O2C-06.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for VA01/VA02 together with VKM1/VKM3/VKM4 and V_KNKK_FRE ACTVT 02; confirm in GRC ARA (Create Order vs Release Credit Block).
2. Move credit-block release out of sales roles into an independent Credit function; in PFCG remove VKM1/VKM3/VKM4 and V_KNKK_FRE from order-entry roles and regenerate.
3. Configure credit management so blocked documents route to a credit controller worklist rather than being releasable by order entry, and restrict V_KNKK_FRE by credit control area.
4. Where sales must action some releases, enforce that a user cannot release a document they created (custom check / workflow agent exclusion) and keep it above a low value threshold only.
5. Add detective monitoring: report of credit-block releases where releaser = order creator (VBUK/VBAK vs release log), reviewed independently.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent review of all self-adjacent releases, aged-receivable analysis) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA create a credit-blocked order and attempt to release it as the same user, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA and coordinate with credit/collections and customer service, since release-routing changes affect order fulfilment timing.

ARA-R2R-01.risk: A single user can both maintain G/L account master data (FS00/FS01/FS02/FSS0/FSP0 with F_SKA1_BUK/F_SKA1_KTP/F_SKA1_AEN at ACTVT 01/02) and post accounting documents (FB01/FB50/F-02 with F_BKPF_BUK ACTVT 01 and F_BKPF_KOA KOART=S for G/L line items). Controlling both the chart of accounts and the postings to it is a classic financial-statement-fraud and concealment SoD: the same person can create a fictitious, suspense or clearing account and then post fraudulent or covering journal entries to it. Attack scenario: the perpetrator sets up a new G/L account (or unblocks/edits an existing one), posts manual journals that misappropriate funds or misstate results, and uses the account they control to park and later flush the offsetting entries, so the manipulation is buried in an account nobody else scrutinizes; they can also change account settings (e.g., open-item management, reconciliation flags) to make balances behave as needed. Business impact is direct financial-statement misstatement, concealment of fraud, and a top-tier SOX/close-process control failure — manual journal entries plus master-data control is one of the most scrutinized SoD combinations in any audit. In S/4HANA the Manage G/L Account Master Data and Post General Journal Entries Fiori apps reproduce it, and Universal Journal (ACDOCA) postings inherit the same authorization checks. A real incident looks like new/edited G/L accounts followed by manual journals from the same user, especially round-number or period-end entries to unusual accounts.
ARA-R2R-01.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for FS00/FS01/FS02 together with FB01/FB50/F-02, checking F_SKA1_* and F_BKPF_KOA KOART=S; confirm in GRC ARA (Maintain G/L Master vs Post JE).
2. Separate duties: G/L master maintenance belongs to a controlling/master-data function, manual posting to accountants; in PFCG remove FS00/FSS0/FSP0 and F_SKA1_BUK/F_SKA1_KTP from posting roles (or the posting tcodes from master-data roles) and regenerate.
3. Route manual journals through park + post (FV50 park, FBV0 post) so an independent approver posts what a preparer parks; combine with a workflow release if available.
4. Restrict posting by document type/account type and company code, and lock down high-risk accounts (suspense/clearing) with authorization groups.
5. Enable and monitor G/L master change documents and a manual-JE review report (preparer vs poster, new-account postings) as a detective control.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent JE review, account-reconciliation sign-off) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA attempt to create a G/L account and post a manual journal to it under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with the record-to-report/close team, and time role changes to avoid disrupting period-end postings.

ARA-R2R-02.risk: A single user can both maintain G/L account master data (FS00/FSS0/FSP0/FS01/FS02 with F_SKA1_BUK/F_SKA1_KTP at ACTVT 01/02) and open/close posting periods (OB52, which maintains table T001B and is protected by S_TABU_DIS ACTVT 02 and the posting-period authorization group object F_BKPF_BUP). Posting-period control (the OB52 open/closed ranges) is the cutoff mechanism that stops entries being booked into or back-dated to periods that management has closed; if the same person maintains accounts and controls the period windows, they can create/alter an account and reopen a closed period to back-date entries into it, defeating both master-data governance and period-end cutoff at once. Attack scenario: at or after month-end the perpetrator opens a closed period via OB52 (adjusting the F_BKPF_BUP authorization group ranges), books adjusting or concealing entries to accounts they also control, then re-closes the period, so the manipulation looks like it belonged to the prior close. Business impact is cutoff and financial-statement manipulation, defeat of the month-end/year-end close control, and a SOX-relevant deficiency, since posting-period control is a named IT-dependent control in most SOX frameworks. In S/4HANA OB52/Manage Posting Periods carries the identical risk. A real incident looks like posting periods reopened outside the normal close calendar by a user who also edits G/L master data, followed by late entries into the reopened window.
ARA-R2R-02.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for FS00/FSS0 together with OB52, checking F_SKA1_BUK and F_BKPF_BUP / S_TABU_DIS ACTVT 02; confirm in GRC ARA (G/L Master vs Open/Close Periods).
2. Separate duties: posting-period maintenance is a controlled close activity for a small Finance-close team; G/L master maintenance is a separate master-data function. In PFCG remove OB52 / F_BKPF_BUP (or F_SKA1_*) from the conflicting roles and regenerate.
3. Protect the posting-period ranges with a dedicated authorization group in OB52 (the AuGr column) so only the close team's F_BKPF_BUP value can change them, and restrict S_TABU_DIS to display for everyone else on table T001B.
4. Govern period open/close via a documented close calendar and change control; enable change-document/audit logging on T001B.
5. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent approval of every period reopen, review of postings dated in reopened periods) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
6. Verify: in QA attempt to open a closed period and edit a G/L account under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
7. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with the close team so legitimate close/open steps are not blocked, and record who retains OB52 as a named, monitored control.

ARA-R2R-03.risk: A single user can both enter/park journal entries (FV50/FBV1/FBV2/F-65 with F_BKPF_BUK/F_BKPF_KOA at ACTVT 77, the park activity) and post/release parked documents (FBV0/FB01/FB50/F-02 at ACTVT 01). Document parking exists precisely to split preparation from approval so that a second person reviews and posts what a first person prepares — a key four-eyes control over manual journal entries and a named SOX control. A user holding both the park (77) and post (01) activities can prepare a journal and then release it themselves, collapsing the four-eyes control back into one pair of hands. Attack scenario: the perpetrator parks a fraudulent or erroneous manual journal (which normally waits for independent review), then posts/releases it under the same ID via FBV0, so the entry hits the ledger with no genuine second review; the parked-then-posted trail may even look like a controlled process to a casual observer. Business impact is defeat of the manual-JE four-eyes control, unreviewed or fraudulent journals in the financial statements, and a SOX control-design/operating failure specifically on the control auditors most expect to see for manual entries. In S/4HANA the Post/Manage Journal Entries apps and their park/post steps carry the same exposure. A real incident looks like the same user appearing as both the parker and the poster on manual journals that should have required independent release.
ARA-R2R-03.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for FV50/FBV1 (park) together with FBV0 (post parked), distinguishing F_BKPF_BUK ACTVT 77 from 01; confirm in GRC ARA (Park vs Post JE).
2. Split the activities across roles: preparers get park (ACTVT 77) only, approvers get post/release (ACTVT 01) only. In PFCG restrict F_BKPF_BUK/F_BKPF_KOA ACTVT accordingly and remove FBV0 from preparer roles; regenerate.
3. Enforce that the poster cannot be the parker — use SAP document-parking with the completion/release workflow (transaction OBWA/OBWJ area for parked-document release, or the S/4 flexible workflow for journal-entry verification) so release routes to a different user.
4. Consider double-verification / release for critical company codes and account types.
5. Add detective monitoring: report of parked documents where the releasing user equals the entering user (fields in BKPF/VBKPF change/creation data), reviewed independently.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent manual-JE review sign-off) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA park a journal and attempt to post it as the same user under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test the parking/release workflow in QA, coordinate with the close team, and confirm agent determination excludes the preparer before transport.

ARA-R2R-04.risk: A single user can both maintain exchange rates (OB08/OB07/OBBS, which update table TCURR and are protected by S_EXCHRATE at ACTVT 01/02 and S_TABU_DIS ACTVT 02) and post accounting documents (FB01/FB50/F-02 with F_BKPF_BUK ACTVT 01, F_BKPF_KOA KOART=S). The exchange rate drives the local-currency value of every foreign-currency posting and every revaluation; controlling both rate maintenance and posting lets one person set the FX rate that most benefits a manipulation and then post against it, with no independent rate control. Attack scenario: before posting a foreign-currency journal, revaluation or intercompany settlement, the perpetrator edits the relevant rate/rate type in OB08 (e.g., inflates a rate to book a fictitious FX gain, understates a liability, or shifts value between entities), posts the document while the manipulated rate is live, then restores the rate so a later look at TCURR shows normal values. Because FX rates are rarely reviewed transaction-by-transaction, the manipulation can persist. Business impact is misstated FX gains/losses, distorted intercompany balances, and financial-statement manipulation — a recognized record-to-report SoD conflict with SOX and consolidation implications. In S/4HANA the Manage/Import Exchange Rates functions and journal-posting apps carry the same risk, and rate feeds are often maintained manually during month-end, widening the window. A real incident looks like TCURR rate edits by the same user who posts material foreign-currency entries around those edits.
ARA-R2R-04.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for OB08/OB07/OBBS together with FB01/FB50/F-02, checking S_EXCHRATE and S_TABU_DIS ACTVT 02 on TCURR; confirm in GRC ARA (Maintain Exchange Rates vs Post JE).
2. Separate duties: exchange-rate maintenance belongs to a Treasury/close function, posting to accountants; in PFCG remove OB08/S_EXCHRATE (or the posting tcodes) from the conflicting roles and regenerate.
3. Automate rate loading (TBDLS / market-data interface or scheduled TCURR upload) so manual OB08 edits are rare and exception-based, and restrict who can override loaded rates.
4. Protect TCURR with a table authorization group so only Treasury's S_TABU_DIS value can change it; set everyone else to display.
5. Enable change logging on TCURR (data changes / table logging) and review rate changes and rate-type usage as a detective control, especially near period-end.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent review of manual rate changes vs postings) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA attempt to edit a rate and post a foreign-currency journal under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with Treasury and the close team, and ensure automated rate feeds run before postings so operations are not blocked.

ARA-R2R-05.risk: A single user can both open/close posting periods (OB52, updating table T001B, protected by S_TABU_DIS ACTVT 02 and posting-period authorization group object F_BKPF_BUP) and post accounting documents (FB01/FB50/F-02 with F_BKPF_BUK ACTVT 01, F_BKPF_KOA KOART=S). This is the direct cutoff-bypass conflict: the person who controls which periods are open for posting is also the person posting, so they can reopen a closed period to back-date entries or keep a period open to push entries in, then post unauthorized or concealing journals — defeating period-end cutoff and the month-end/year-end close. Attack scenario: after a period is closed the perpetrator uses OB52 to reopen it (adjusting the F_BKPF_BUP authorization-group ranges), books entries dated into the prior period to make results, defer expenses or hide a shortfall, then re-closes the period so the change looks like it was always part of that close; or they simply post into a period that should be closed for their group but which they keep open. Business impact is period-cutoff manipulation, misstated period results, and defeat of a named SOX close control (posting-period management is almost always an in-scope IT-dependent control). In S/4HANA OB52/Manage Posting Periods plus the journal-posting apps carry the identical exposure. A real incident looks like posting periods opened outside the close calendar by a user who then posts back-dated entries into the reopened window.
ARA-R2R-05.mitigation:
1. Identify offenders: SUIM -> RSUSR002 for OB52 together with FB01/FB50/F-02, checking F_BKPF_BUP / S_TABU_DIS ACTVT 02 and F_BKPF_BUK; confirm in GRC ARA (Open/Close Periods vs Post JE).
2. Separate duties: posting-period maintenance is a controlled close activity for a small, named Finance-close team; posting is for accountants. In PFCG remove OB52 / F_BKPF_BUP from posting roles and regenerate.
3. Protect the OB52 period ranges with a dedicated authorization group (AuGr) and restrict F_BKPF_BUP so only the close team can change ranges; set S_TABU_DIS on T001B to display for others.
4. Govern period open/close with a documented close calendar and change control, and enable audit/change logging on T001B.
5. Add detective monitoring: alert on OB52 changes outside the scheduled close windows and review any postings dated into reopened periods.
6. Where separation is impractical, register a mitigating control (independent approval of every reopen, review of back-dated postings) in GRC/CSV with owner and expiry.
7. Verify: in QA attempt to reopen a closed period and post into it under split roles, confirming denial; re-run ARA for zero residual.
8. Caution: test in QA, coordinate with the close team so legitimate open/close steps continue, and treat retained OB52 access as a named monitored control.

ARA-H2R-01.risk: A single user can both maintain pay-relevant HR master data (PA30/PA40 writing infotypes IT0008 Basic Pay, IT0014 Recurring Payments/Deductions, IT0015 Additional Payments, controlled by P_ORGIN with AUTHC W/E/S and INFTY 0008/0014/0015) and execute the payroll driver (PC00_M99_CALC / PC00_M10_CALC, controlled by P_PCLX AUTHC=U on the payroll cluster and P_ABAP). This is the flagship hire-to-retire SoD: whoever sets pay amounts must not be the one who runs payroll, or they can inflate their own or an accomplice's pay and immediately process it into a live payroll result with no review.
